<story>
Halfway down the Rue Saint-Denis, near the Rue du Petit-Lion, stood a charming old house that helped historians reconstruct old Paris. Dilapidated walls bore hieroglyphic-like timber patterns and cracked plaster. A weather-warped triangular roof overhung the street, sheltering the entrance and dormer window; the upper story used overlapping planks to avoid overburdening the fragile structure.On a rainy March morning a young man, cloaked like an antiquary, stood under a shop awning across from the house, studying its sixteenth-century peculiarities. He ignored the first floor’s narrow boarded windows and focused on the third floor’s modest sash windows—poorly crafted and obscured by green-tinted glass. Occasionally he glanced at the shop below, where small amusing details caught his eye.A large wooden beam, supported by four pillars, showed many layers of paint and bore a comical painting of a cat playing rackets. The cat, racket in paw and oversized ball nearby, had become an increasingly absurd caricature. A faded sign read “Guillaume, Successor to Master Chevrel,” a relic of a whimsical past when such signs drew customers.The young man, in a stylish cloak and white silk stockings, looked as if he had come from a wedding or ball; his white gloves and disheveled hair hinted at recent revelry. Despite market gardeners hurrying by, the street felt unusually still. His striking features—a pale face set against a dazzling white cravat—and expressive eyes drew the attention of the shopkeepers at the “Cat and Racket.”Suddenly three apprentices appeared at the dormer window, laughing and teasing him. They sprinkled him with shaving cream; he remained indifferent and brushed it off. Then a young girl’s face emerged, her beauty recalling a Raphael Virgin. Innocent and unembarrassed, she caught sight of him, retreated, and slammed the window shut, leaving him disappointed.Meanwhile the shutters of the “Cat and Racket” opened and a manservant hung a cloth sign: “Guillaume, successor to Chevrel.” The shop’s iron-barred exterior concealed numerous packages wrapped in brown linen. Despite its old-fashioned look, Monsieur Guillaume was known for having the best-stocked store among Parisian cloth merchants and a reputation for honesty—securing profits without flattery, though fellow traders warned against his notary’s heavy discounts.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The old merchant stood at his shop threshold on the Rue Saint-Denis, surveying the street as if waking from a long sleep. Monsieur Guillaume, in traditional attire, watched a stranger who lingered too long, his patient, shrewd face betraying years of experience. A staunch traditionalist, he lamented lost customs and imposed a strict routine on his three apprentices, who feared his keen eye.When the apprentices began their day, Guillaume noticed the stranger’s anxious glances and suspected ulterior motives. The eldest, curious, stepped outside and thought he saw Mademoiselle Augustine hurrying away; he quickly hailed a cab, which relieved them about a prank they had been planning on her. Guillaume soon reproached them for idleness, recalling how he worked faster in his youth. Despite their comfortable backgrounds, he insisted on a rigorous work ethic, in contrast to modern shops where apprentices expected early comforts. The shop itself was kept meticulously, reflecting the order and economy he prized.Though the apprentices paid for board, they were excluded from the family dessert—a mark of Madame Guillaume’s frugality. They attended Mass with the family; the eldest apprentice, Joseph Lebas, earned a modest wage after years of service. Guillaume thought him a suitable match for his elder daughter, Virginie, though Joseph secretly loved the younger daughter, Augustine.The household ran on strict tradition. Virginie and Augustine had been raised under their mother’s austere rule: Virginie resembled her mother—devout and severe—while eighteen-year-old Augustine was delicate, charming, and often overshadowed by maternal expectations. Their lives centered on domestic duties with little exposure to the outside world, so they prized family gatherings and rare visitors.Augustine yearned for something beyond this cloistered existence; secret readings of romantic novels likely kindled her longing for a more passionate life. This longing deepened Joseph’s affection for her, while Virginie, lacking suitors, also felt drawn to him. Amid the monotony of shop life, quiet attachments formed, and Monsieur Guillaume, aware of changing times, wondered about his daughters’ prospects in a world where suitors might grow scarce.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day the old merchant, saddened by his eldest daughter's decline, recalled how he had married Mademoiselle Chevrel under circumstances like Joseph Lebas’s and Virginie’s. Marrying off his daughter would settle a sacred debt by repaying an orphan for a past benefit. Joseph Lebas, now thirty-three, knew the fifteen-year gap between him and Augustine and Monsieur Guillaume’s rigid rule that the elder daughter marry first. The lovesick assistant, warm-hearted yet tall and broad-chested, therefore suffered in silence.To understand, we must rewind a few months. One evening a young painter paused outside the darkened Cat and Racket, captivated by a scene any artist would envy. The unlit shop revealed a dining room glowing from a hanging lamp, its warm light like a Dutch interior. The family—Augustine among them, and a chubby maid—formed a peaceful, modest tableau. Fresh from Rome, the painter craved genuine nature after immersion in grand art; his Italian passion left him longing for a simple, meditative maiden he’d only seen in pictures. The living scene stirred him: Augustine appeared pensive and untouched by food; the lamp made her face almost angelic. Overwhelmed, he left unable to eat or sleep.The next day he retreated to his studio determined to capture the moment. He visited the Cat and Racket repeatedly, even disguising himself to study Augustine. For eight months he lived in love and art, neglecting friends and routine. One morning his friend Girodet broke his isolation, asking about his Salon submission. The artist revealed two masterpieces; Girodet, recognizing the depth of feeling, embraced him. “You are in love?” Girodet asked; the artist nodded. They agreed great works spring from such passion. Girodet warned against showing them at the Salon, doubting public taste, but the works were exhibited and created a sensation. The Interior launched a genre shift, while the portrait became unforgettable, winning acclaim and offers the artist declined.These public events contrasted sharply with the monastic life in Rue Saint-Denis until Madame Guillaume, the notary’s wife, mentioned the exhibition during a visit. Augustine, with her cousin’s help, obtained permission to visit the Louvre. Seeing the portrait, she trembled—recognizing herself, feeling terror and excitement. The artist, nearby, whispered, “You see how love has inspired me,” leaving her stunned.Intent on escaping the crowd, Augustine urged her cousin to leave, but they were drawn closer to the celebrated painting. Overwhelmed, she wept at sight of the work. In a quiet exchange she made a gesture to the artist; he acknowledged her with a nod. This brief connection ignited guilt and exhilaration. The Salon’s chaos, combined with the artist’s gaze, intoxicated her; she felt loved and experienced emotions she had never before known.As they left, the painter radiated joy, and Augustine realized she was loved. Her words echoed, deepening her turmoil. She feigned a headache to avoid discussing the exhibition with her cousin, but her mother’s plan to visit the Salon filled her with dread. That night she reflected, feeling a void in her humdrum life and longing for the elegance she’d always admired. The thought of being a genius’s wife filled her with hope despite her upbringing. The next day her parents returned disappointed: the painter had removed the two pictures, and Madame Guillaume had lost her shawl. Yet the paintings’ disappearance revealed to Augustine a sensitivity women often possess instinctively.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On the morning after a ball, Theodore de Sommervieux, who had won Augustine's heart, struggled to declare his love because her household constrained every approach. Seated in the counting-house between Mademoiselle Virginie and Madame Guillaume, Augustine was closely watched by her mother, making it difficult for Theodore to come near. Fearing a rival among the assistants and feeling hemmed in by the household's eyes, he bribed a maid to facilitate exchanges, and they arranged to meet discreetly during Mass at Saint-Leu.At the Cat and Racket the family was absorbed in stock-taking under Monsieur Guillaume's supervision, verifying inventory and balancing accounts. They were oblivious to Augustine's emotional strain; despite her absent-mindedness, no one noticed her longing for Theodore. After weeks of work the stock-taking finished, and the family relaxed: they attended a performance of Cendrillon while the apprentices enjoyed an evening out.The next morning Guillaume returned to routine and summoned his assistant Joseph Lebas to review the profitable results; he proposed making Joseph a partner in recognition of his contributions. Overwhelmed, Joseph revealed his love for Augustine, but Guillaume misheard and thought he meant Virginie. After some confusion, Guillaume agreed to consider Joseph's feelings for Augustine and suggested he accompany her to Mass.At breakfast tension rose as Madame Guillaume and Virginie exchanged looks at a nervous Joseph. Madame, unaware of Augustine's romance with Theodore, chastised Augustine for inattentiveness during church and, after the service, ordered her to her room, prompting a heated discussion between her parents about their daughter's future.Summoned before them, Augustine confessed her love for Theodore de Sommervieux, stressing his respectable background. Monsieur Guillaume voiced concern about artists, recalling past experience, but Augustine defended Theodore as wealthy and from a good family, which caught her father's interest. At that moment Madame Roguin arrived with news of Theodore's recent honors and increased fortune, advocating the match. Her persuasive account began to sway Monsieur and Madame Guillaume, who had been resistant to their daughter marrying above her station.As the day passed the atmosphere shifted from conflict to acceptance. Monsieur Guillaume informed Joseph of the developments. By evening the family gathered to celebrate the prospect of the union, with Theodore presenting a painting of the Cat and Racket as a symbol of their shared happiness. Monsieur Guillaume consented to the marriage while emphasizing financial prudence and advising Augustine to consult him on money matters. In the end the lovers found solace in mutual affection, and the household embraced a joyful future together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few months after a memorable Sunday, the high altar of Saint-Leu saw two contrasting weddings. Augustine and Theodore radiated happiness, while Virginie, simply dressed, followed her sister. Monsieur Guillaume had tried to have Virginie wed first, but the priests attended to the more elegant bride. Neighbors praised Virginie’s sensible match; others envied Augustine for marrying an artist of rank. An old fan-maker warned such extravagance would bring ruin, and Monsieur Guillaume felt justified in his cautious approach to settlements. After a splendid ball and supper, Monsieur and Madame Guillaume returned home, and the Lebas family resumed their residence. The artist, elated, carried Augustine to their beautifully adorned apartment.The passion between Theodore and Augustine made their first year blissful. Theodore showered her with affection; Augustine, lost in love, believed her devotion would always charm him. She remained blissfully unaware of much around her, speaking the language of love and sometimes revealing her naivety. As the year passed and a child was expected, Theodore felt the need to return to work. He reconnected with friends and frequented the Duchesse de Carigliano’s gatherings, seeking the social validation of being seen with a beautiful wife.Augustine enjoyed society but soon realized her lack of sophistication compared with Theodore’s artistic circle. Her admiration for his work was genuine but shallow, widening a growing disconnect. Theodore, once captivated, began to feel the strain of their differing intellectual worlds. He retreated to his studio, hoping the artistic environment might inspire Augustine, but she struggled to adapt. At gatherings her innocence clashed with the more liberated attitudes of artists, making her increasingly uncomfortable.Over time Theodore’s affection waned and Augustine felt the weight of his indifference. Though she tried to fulfill her duties as a wife, she sensed a growing distance. Three years into their marriage, Augustine, once vibrant, became consumed by sorrow. The artist’s enthusiasm for the Duchesse de Carigliano intensified her despair as she feared being replaced by a more experienced woman.Her heartache led Augustine to examine her shortcomings and the barriers between them. She resolved to educate herself, believing that understanding poetry and art would bridge the gap. But her efforts met frustration; she could not cultivate the natural wit and charm Theodore admired, and her earnestness often backfired, leaving her feeling inadequate.In desperation Augustine sought solace with family, returning to her childhood home. She found sister Virginie and brother-in-law Joseph Lebas managing their drapery business with a steadiness that contrasted sharply with her turmoil. Though their mundane happiness provoked envy, Joseph offered practical, commercially minded advice that only deepened her conflict.Ultimately Augustine returned home, burdened by uncertainty and longing for the love she once shared with Theodore, like a desperate patient trying every remedy to heal a fractured marriage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The elderly couple welcomed their daughter warmly; her visit was a refreshing break from their monotony. For four years they had drifted, dwelling on past business successes and failures—especially the Lecocq bankruptcy. Monsieur Guillaume kept checking on his shop, the Cat and Racket, and talking with former competitors, while Madame Guillaume—now on the Army’s clothing consulting board thanks to her son-in-law’s influence—filled their home with extravagant, tasteless decor. Their nostalgia and stagnation struck Augustine as a stark contrast to her own turbulent life, leaving her both proud and appalled at their vacuous existence.Despite her inner turmoil, Augustine displayed her accomplishments and aired marital grievances, which her parents eagerly absorbed. Madame Guillaume was particularly curious about Augustine’s life with her husband Theodore, a painter. Augustine described his late nights and eccentric habits; her mother disapproved of his lifestyle and profession. Their incredulity grew as Augustine defended him, sparking a heated debate about genius and marital responsibility.When Augustine mentioned divorce, her parents were shocked into action; Monsieur Guillaume offered support. Overwhelmed by their concern and unable to convey the depth of her suffering, Augustine rejected separation and realized she was isolated in her pain—only those who had shared similar trials could truly empathize.Determined to change course, Augustine sought out the Duchess de Carigliano—not to win Theodore back directly but to learn how to captivate him. Approaching the Duchess’s opulent home, she felt envy and admiration for its elegance. Meeting the Duchess, Augustine was struck by her beauty, charm, and a palpable rivalry beneath the civility.The Duchess responded with a mix of sympathy and shrewd counsel, stressing the need to present strength and control with men of genius. She urged Augustine to cultivate mystery and not reveal the full extent of her affections. Though uneasy about the idea of manipulation, Augustine began to accept the lessons, seeing that her love for Theodore might require a new approach.The Duchess offered Augustine a portrait of herself painted by Theodore, believing it could rekindle his interest. Hopeful, Augustine returned home determined to become the image in the painting. When Theodore arrived, she presented the portrait eagerly; his reaction was fury and jealousy, igniting a violent confrontation. The quarrel exposed his passion and insecurity and left Augustine devastated; her dreams were shattered as she faced the true nature of her marriage. The next morning her mother found her in despair amid the remnants of the fight.Overwhelmed by the pressures of her tumultuous life, Augustine died young. Her story is a poignant reminder of the struggles women face pursuing love and fulfillment, especially when entangled with the complexities of artistic genius; the inscription on her grave reflects that enduring tragedy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Comte de Fontaine, a loyal royalist from Poitou, had served the Bourbons during the La Vendée and suffered financial ruin when his estate was confiscated. He married Mademoiselle de Kergarouet, noble but penniless. After the second revolution he moved to Paris with his large family seeking better prospects. Initially disillusioned by the new regime, he unexpectedly received honors — a military promotion and royal decorations — which revived hopes of favor.Still, his sacrifices often went unrecognized as new aristocrats rose. Contemplating a return to his estate, he was drawn back into royal service during the tumult of 1815 and became a trusted adviser to Louis XVIII, prized for wit and political acumen. The Fontaine family then began to prosper: his sons obtained important posts and his daughters made advantageous marriages, typically to lesser nobility.Emilie, the youngest, was especially spoiled and a focus of Parisian society. Her beauty and charm made her sought-after, yet she remained indifferent to suitors and looked down on those of lower birth. Despite the Count’s efforts to arrange matches, Emilie’s high standards and caprice provoked family conflict. She rejected potential husbands for trivial faults, frustrating his plans.As the political scene shifted and the King’s health faltered, the Comte intensified attempts to secure Emilie a husband to cement the family’s standing. Her unreasonable expectations produced a series of refusals that left him increasingly desperate. Family relations strained: Emilie’s arrogance sparked friction among siblings and their spouses, all navigating their new social positions.Throughout these tensions the Comte clung to hope that royal favor and a suitable match for Emilie would vindicate his sacrifices and restore the family’s honor. But as the winter social season advanced, her continued dismissiveness thwarted his ambitions and left the Fontaine family’s future uncertain.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nature had endowed Emilie de Fontaine with every quality to play the part of Célimène. Tall and slender, she could be dignified or playful at will. Her long neck had an air of scorn and impertinence; fine black hair and arched brows lent pride to her features. Emilie mastered feminine gestures that could convey contempt or charm; her melodious voice won hearts or sharply silenced indiscretion. Her alabaster complexion remained calm except for fleeting emotion. Young men she scorned accused her of acting a part; she replied that she only inspired desire to subject it to her whims.Monsieur de Fontaine realized too late that adulation at home had spoiled his daughter. Admiration inflated her pride and self-confidence, nurturing the selfishness common to pampered children. Her youthful charms concealed these flaws, but he saw them and tried to instill life’s wisdom. His gentle advice met her caprice and ironic shrewdness; it slid off her like water from marble. He slowly discerned that much of her affection was condescending, like a child demanding attention and then returning to play.Emilie sometimes showed fondness for her parents but often withdrew, jealous of the mutual love they shared with others, including her siblings. She blamed fate for her solitude, failing to see that happiness comes from within. Determined to resist her sisters’ marriages, she yet felt pangs of envy at their happiness. Her unpredictable behavior led her mother to suspect a touch of madness.Such pride is common among beautiful young women of wealth, who feel misunderstood by mothers and imagine maternal envy. This provokes secret tears, revolts against perceived tyranny, and elaborate notions of a perfect partner who must meet exacting criteria. With experience, many find happiness in unexpected places, learning that ideals often mismatch reality.Emilie’s standards were precise: a suitor must be a peer of France, a soldier, and wear an Order so they'd be treated with respect. He also had to be slender; she despised corpulence, believing only the slim could be romantic. She made clear contempt for those who failed her standards, and Monsieur de Fontaine feared her eccentricities would invite ridicule and that prolonged indecision might have consequences.As the winter of Charles X’s reign progressed, Monsieur de Fontaine intensified efforts to find matches, hosting gatherings to attract suitable suitors. His reputation as a corrupter of legislative integrity spread, yet he believed his intentions honorable. After a season of introductions he felt hopeful Emilie might choose a husband; he soon found her whims unyielding.One morning he summoned her to discuss her future. Arranging his study to present a dignified front, Emilie greeted him with a light kiss that lacked warmth. Monsieur de Fontaine raised the subject of marriage, stressing the importance of a wise choice for her happiness. She interrupted, reminding him of their prior agreement about suitors. He urged her to take matters seriously, explaining that the family had labored to secure prospects. She dismissed his concerns, mocking the idea of settling for anything less than a peer of France, faulting the men he suggested for appearance or background.Exasperated, Monsieur de Fontaine declared he had fulfilled his duty and that the choice now rested with her. He warned that marital happiness depends on mutual respect rather than mere wealth or rank. Moved by her father’s sincerity, Emilie feigned affection and coaxed him, yet her pride remained intact.At a family gathering she again refused marriage, insisting she would only wed a peer. Her uncle jokingly suggested she wait for the Duc de Bordeaux, drawing laughter, but Emilie stayed resolute.As summer approached, the family retreated to their country house, where Emilie asked to attend the Bal de Sceaux, drawn by the crowd’s anonymity. The ball was known for its lively air and mix of classes. To her surprise many upper-class guests attended. Observing the festivities, she noticed a striking young man apart from the throng, exuding elegance and dignity. His bearing sparked her imagination and she felt instant attraction.Determined to learn more, Emilie approached him, but he remained aloof. She tried exaggerated gestures and laughter to draw him in, but he seemed indifferent. Frustrated, she followed his gaze and saw he was watching another dancer, a pale girl named Clara. Intrigued, Emilie trailed the pair as they left the ball. The young man remained attentive to Clara but still caught Emilie’s eye in their parting. She felt jealousy and curiosity, eager to learn his identity.Days later, while riding with her uncle, Emilie met Lady Dudley and Monsieur Vandenesse and discovered the young man she admired was not who she had hoped. Disappointed, she tried to hide her feelings but remained drawn to him. Her uncle, noting her agitation, speculated about her sentiments. Insisting he must be noble, Emilie secured his promise to introduce them. This revelation intensified her interest, and her infatuation grew, fed by imagination and the allure of romance, still dazzled by youthful ideals and unaware of the complexities love and marriage would bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning, before Emilie awoke, her uncle hurried to Chevreuse. Recognizing the young man he had insulted the day before, he approached with the manners of the old court.“Who would have guessed I’d encounter the son or grandson of one of my best friends at seventy-three? I’m a vice-admiral, and I think no more of dueling than smoking a cigar. In my day, young men had to see the color of their blood before becoming friends! I had a bit too much grog last night and ran you down. Let’s shake hands; I’d rather take a hundred rebuffs from a Longueville than cause his family any regret.”Though outwardly cool, the young man could not resist the Count’s cordiality and shook his hand.“You were going out riding,” the Count observed. “Don’t let me detain you, but I hope you’ll join us for dinner at the Villa Planat. My nephew, the Comte de Fontaine, is someone you should meet. I’ll make it up to you by introducing you to five of the prettiest women in Paris. Your brow is clearing! I enjoy seeing young people happy; it reminds me of my youth when adventures and duels were common. Nowadays, you worry over everything as if there were no history.”“But aren’t we right to think this way? The sixteenth century gave Europe religious liberty, and the nineteenth will bring political liberty—”“Let’s not discuss politics. I’m an old woman—ultra, you see. But I don’t stop young men from being revolutionary, as long as they let the King disperse their assemblies.”As they rode into the woods, the Count pointed out a slender birch, took out a pistol, and shot a bullet into the tree from fifteen paces.“You see, I’m not afraid of a duel,” he said with comic gravity.“Nor am I,” replied the young man, promptly aiming his pistol and shooting close to the Count’s bullet.“That’s what I call a well-educated man!” the admiral exclaimed.Throughout the ride the Count quizzed him on the trifles his code deemed essential to a gentleman.“Do you have any debts?” he finally asked.“No, monsieur.”“What, you pay for everything?”“Punctually; otherwise, we’d lose our credit and respect.”“But you must have more than one mistress? Ah, you blush! Manners have changed. All these notions of lawful order and liberty have spoiled young men. You have no Guimard or Duthe now, and you know nothing of heraldry. My dear young friend, you’re not fully fledged. The man who doesn’t sow his wild oats in spring will do so in winter. If I have eighty thousand francs a year at seventy, it’s because I spent my capital at thirty—honorably, of course. However, your imperfections won’t stop me from introducing you at the Pavillon Planat. Remember, you promised to come.”“What an odd little old man!” Longueville thought. “He’s jolly and hale, but while he wants to seem friendly, I won’t trust him too far.”The next day, around four, when the house party gathered, a servant announced “Monsieur de Longueville.” Upon hearing the old admiral’s protégé had arrived, everyone hurried to see Mademoiselle de Fontaine’s expression and judge this remarkable young man who had outshone many rivals.Dressed simply yet elegantly, with an air of ease and polite manners, Longueville won the family’s goodwill. He seemed at home amid the Receiver-General’s luxury. His conversation showed a man of the world with a brilliant education. When the old admiral raised the trivial topic of naval architecture, he answered aptly, prompting a lady to suggest he must have attended the École Polytechnique.“I think, madame,” he replied, “that I may regard it as an honor to have been accepted.”Despite persistent invitations, he politely declined dinner, explaining he was the Hippocrates of his young sister, whose delicate health required care.“Monsieur is perhaps a medical man?” one of Emilie’s sisters-in-law asked ironically.“Monsieur has left the École Polytechnique,” Mademoiselle de Fontaine interjected, her face flushing when she learned the young lady at the ball was Longueville’s sister.“But, my dear, he may be a doctor and still have attended the École Polytechnique—correct, monsieur?”“There’s nothing to prevent it, madame,” he replied.All eyes turned to Emilie, who gazed with uneasy curiosity at the captivating stranger. She breathed more easily when he added with a smile, “I do not belong to the medical profession; I even gave up going into the Engineers to preserve my independence.”“And you did well,” the Count said.Longueville made his visit neither too long nor too short, leaving when he saw he had pleased everyone and piqued their curiosity.“He’s a cunning rascal!” the Count remarked after seeing him out.Mademoiselle de Fontaine, who had dressed to attract Longueville’s attention, felt disappointed when he didn’t give her the attention she expected. The family was surprised by her sudden silence; Emilie usually displayed all her charms for newcomers, but now she seemed simple and natural, perhaps more beautiful. Some read her restraint as a refined strategy to dazzle him later.During dinner, as everyone speculated about Longueville’s qualities, Mademoiselle de Fontaine remained silent until a sarcastic remark from her uncle prompted her to quip that such heavenly perfection must conceal a great defect, adding she would be cautious in judging such a gifted man at first sight.“Those who please everybody please nobody,” she concluded, “and the worst fault is to have none.”Like all girls in love, Emilie tried to hide her feelings, but after a fortnight every family member knew. When Longueville called for the third time, Emilie believed it was chiefly for her sake, which thrilled her but stung her pride. Accustomed to being the center of attention, she now recognized an outside force that attracted her, and despite attempts to resist, she could not shake the image of the young man from her heart.Anxiety crept in as two of Longueville’s qualities—unexpected modesty and discretion—conflicted with her curiosity. He never spoke of himself or his family, skillfully evading hints. If she discussed painting, he answered as a connoisseur; if she played music, he proved a good pianist. One evening he charmed the party by singing a duet with Emilie, but when they tried to learn if he was a professional singer, he preserved the mystery of his social status.Distracted by his reserve, Emilie hoped to extract more information from Clara, aided by her uncle’s maneuvers. The family expressed a strong desire to meet Clara Longueville, proposing an informal dance to entertain her. Despite clouds of suspicion and curiosity, joy illuminated Emilie’s soul as she felt intimately connected to someone outside herself. She began to understand life’s relationships; whether happiness made her better or she was simply too occupied to torment others, she became gentler and more indulgent.This change enchanted her family, suggesting her selfishness was transforming into love. She eagerly awaited the arrival of her bashful admirer, and though they hadn’t spoken of passion, she sensed his affection. She led him to reveal varied information about himself, realizing he was also studying her and striving to improve her education.The day of the dance arrived, with Clara and her brother as principal guests. For the first time, Emilie felt pleasure in another young girl’s triumph, lavishing Clara with sincere attention. However, Clara proved more adept at conversation, steering talk away from personal topics while charming Emilie, who felt both envious and judged.“Mademoiselle,” Clara said, “I’ve heard so much about you from Maximilien that I wanted to know you out of affection for him; but isn’t wanting to know you a wish to love you?”“My dear Clara, I feared I might have displeased you by speaking of those not of noble birth.”“Oh, be at ease. Such discussions are pointless these days. It doesn’t affect me; I’m beside the question.”Ambitious as her answer seemed, Emilie took it as encouragement. She danced with renewed spirit, watching Longueville, whose grace surpassed her ideal. She felt satisfaction believing him to be well-born, and her eyes sparkled as she danced, feeling a connection with him.As autumn arrived, the couple surrendered to the tide of sweet sentiment, strengthening their bond through countless small incidents. They studied each other, as lovers do, and enjoyed aimless conversations that masked deeper feelings. They admired sunsets together, gathered daisies, and sang passionate duets, expressing secrets through music. Each little shared pleasure—the casual talk, the music, the walks—deepened their intimacy and confirmed the affection each had been careful to conceal.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################There is a particular twilight hour that draws reflective artists outdoors; in that hour a young painter, finishing a large canvas, falls from his ladder, strikes his head, and loses consciousness. He is roused by the voices of a young woman and her mother who rushed to his aid. As he recovers he notices the girl’s beauty—the delicate features matching the feminine ideal he had imagined—and the older woman’s refined, marquise-like air. The mother tends him carefully and offers gentle advice on his recovery. The two, naturally reserved, withdraw without pressing for acquaintance.After they leave, the porter’s wife identifies them as Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, longtime residents of the building. The painter, Hippolyte Schinner, has just moved into the attic studio and begun to earn some recognition. He hopes to secure a better life for his own mother, who sacrificed much for him. Despite modest success, he remains guarded and does not reveal his private affairs easily.During convalescence Hippolyte replays the encounter, especially the image of the young girl, which lingers. Returning to his studio he is eager to visit them, feeling a kinship born of imagined shared misfortune; this sense of common ground draws him to their door.In their modest home he notices signs of poverty but feels only empathy, not disdain. Mademoiselle Leseigneur greets him with a polite bow; he notes the contrast between their humble surroundings and the elegance they maintain. The room holds faded traces of past luxury—worn finery kept meticulously clean.Conversation begins awkwardly but soon turns to art, allowing Hippolyte to speak openly about his work and feelings. Madame Leseigneur reveals that a cherished portrait of her late husband, a naval officer, is decaying. Hippolyte offers to paint a new portrait, hoping the gesture will deepen their acquaintance. They accept with grace, and he senses the emotional weight behind the request.While they talk, Adelaide briefly leaves and returns with candles to brighten the room. The mood shifts when a visitor arrives—someone who clearly knows Adelaide. His presence awakens a quick jealousy in Hippolyte, who wants to understand their relationship. The newcomer is an older gentleman in a coat recalling a bygone fashion; his confident entry suggests nobility mixed with libertinism and hints at a complex past. The exchange between him and the women reveals layers of history and social position, leaving Hippolyte to consider the dynamics at play.Throughout the evening Hippolyte wrestles with his feelings for Adelaide—torn between admiration and uncertainty. The contrast between their social standings weighs on him, yet he is drawn to her spirit and grace. As night deepens he becomes increasingly aware of the fragile boundary between their worlds and of the possibility of connection amid the shadows of their circumstances.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A remarkable figure appeared beside the “Louis XIV’s light infantry” specimen, a Bonapartist nickname for these remnants of the Monarchy. This lean, aged man was a shadow of his former self: worn coat, faded powdered hair, and diminished intellect—a faded reflection, as Rivarol’s quip about Champcenetz suggested.The silent old man puzzled the painter Chevalier; no one addressed him. His relation to the gallant man—friend, relative, or companion—was unclear, and his presence raised questions about any past link to the gallant’s deceased wife without offering answers.One of the least dilapidated men approached Madame de Rouville, kissed her hand, and sat beside her; the other bowed and took a seat nearby. Adelaide leaned over the old gentleman’s chair in a pose recalling a famous painting, though his familiarity seemed to annoy her.“Are you sulky with me?” he asked, casting a shrewd glance at Schinner the painter, as if gauging his status.“This is our neighbor,” Madame de Rouville said, introducing Hippolyte, the celebrated painter. The old man noted Hippolyte’s red ribbon of distinction and contrasted it with his own hard-earned honors.Hippolyte, captivated by Adelaide’s beauty, became lost in admiration and momentarily forgot the squalor around them. Absorbed in contemplation, he answered briefly. The old gentleman lamented his losing streak at cards; Adelaide offered help, but he insisted she stay where he could see her.When the game ended, the gentleman voiced frustration at his losses. The silent figure echoed the hour, prompting Hippolyte to take his leave, still entranced by budding feelings.The next day Hippolyte longed to see Adelaide again. He delayed until afternoon before visiting Madame de Rouville, awkwardly requesting the Baron’s portrait. Adelaide, alone in the drawing-room, asked him to fetch it, revealing her modesty. Their shared glance spoke volumes; Hippolyte took the portrait, promising to return it soon.In the following days their relationship blossomed with laughter and shared thoughts. Schinner, eager to learn piquet, often lost, while the unspoken bond between Hippolyte and Adelaide deepened. They became inseparable; Adelaide’s affection grew and Hippolyte’s jealousy flared when the old Count showed familiarity with her.Adelaide worried that Hippolyte’s social life harmed his health and expected more from him. Their love flourished without grand gestures, relying on mutual trust and delicate exchanges. Within two months their lives were intertwined: Hippolyte visited daily, their connection deepened, the old gentleman’s visits faded, and Hippolyte took his place at the card table, sharing in the losses.Still, Hippolyte could not shake concern about Madame de Rouville’s finances. He spent two months on the Baron’s portrait, considering it among his finest works, but the Baroness’s silence about payment puzzled him.One day Adelaide visited his studio and was awestruck by the portrait; their moment brimmed with unspoken feeling. Overwhelmed, Hippolyte expressed his desire to marry her, but their moment was interrupted by the arrival of the old vice-admiral, who praised the portrait and hinted at financial support for both commissions.That evening Hippolyte noticed Madame de Rouville’s unusual excitement during cards, stirring suspicions about her gambling and the nature of her dealings. After a night of turmoil he discovered his purse missing and, in his pain, suspected Adelaide and her mother of theft.Despite heartbreak he clung to hope for a misunderstanding. The next day a friend mentioned rumors surrounding Adelaide and her mother, unsettling him further. Torn between love and doubt, he resolved to confront his feelings and visit Madame de Rouville.Seeing Adelaide, he was struck by her pallor and sadness; their conversation revealed mutual longing, and he felt ashamed of his earlier suspicions. The evening turned tender as they played cards, but his fears reemerged when he noticed Adelaide’s secretive glances at her mother.During the game Adelaide discreetly placed a new purse before Hippolyte, a thoughtful gesture to replace the one he had lost. Overwhelmed, he realized the depth of their connection and impulsively declared his intention to marry her.Just as they began to discuss their future, the old vice-admiral arrived with news of a promotion and expressed support for the young couple. He praised the portrait and offered assistance, dispelling lingering doubts. The evening concluded with a renewed sense of unity as the families came together.This tale of love, trust, and misunderstanding follows characters navigating the complexities of their relationships and, through gestures of faith and reconciliation, finding solace in shared bonds and the promise of a brighter future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In October 1800, a foreigner named Bartolomeo di Piombo stood outside the Tuileries Palace with his wife and young daughter. The man, over sixty and somber, was deeply troubled; his wife, though sad, tried to keep calm for their child. Their despair drew onlookers, but the stranger’s fierce gaze discouraged curiosity.After a pause, Bartolomeo made a desperate choice. He handed a dagger to his wife, declaring he would confront the Bonapartes. Approaching the palace, a soldier barred him without a written request. Bartolomeo insisted, producing a tense standoff until Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, arrived, recognized him, and brought him inside to meet his brother, Napoleon.Napoleon dismissed his aides to speak with Bartolomeo alone. The Corsican sought asylum and admitted he had killed the Portas — the family that had burned his vineyard and murdered his son. He described avenging his son by killing all seven Portas, shocking Napoleon. Bartolomeo bitterly reminded the brothers how he had once protected their family.Now a national leader, Napoleon felt bound by law and could not endorse private vengeance. Still, he acknowledged the Corsican tradition of vendetta and offered Bartolomeo a place to live in Paris, promising financial help while warning that the law would not tolerate private justice in the city.Bartolomeo accepted, pledged loyalty to the Bonapartes, and expressed gratitude for their recognition of his misfortune, appearing hopeful about life in Paris. Seeing a potential ally, Napoleon hinted at needing trustworthy friends later.Afterwards Lucien, learning the family had arrived with nothing, gave money and promised further assistance, noting that Bartolomeo’s Corsican property would not suffice to support them in Paris. Fifteen years later, events would unfold that required the context of these struggles and decisions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Servin, a distinguished artist, was the first to open a studio for young girls who wished to paint. About forty pupils, strict in morals and devoted to art, attended his classes. He was married to the dowerless daughter of a general. At first mothers accompanied their daughters; reassured by Servin’s principles and dedication, they soon sent them alone.Determined to avoid social disputes, he taught only young ladies from wealthy families and refused girls who wanted to become professional artists, since that would demand more thorough instruction. His careful approach and skill won him a stellar reputation among the aristocracy. When a girl showed talent, mothers invariably recommended Servin. He became synonymous with feminine art much as Herbault was with bonnets and Leroy with gowns. A young woman trained by him was assumed capable of critiquing museum pictures, painting striking portraits, or handling genre scenes. Though connected with Paris’s elite, Servin retained an independent, patriotic attitude and a witty, half‑ironic tone.The studio itself was arranged with care. The attic above his apartment was sealed and reachable only by a small spiral stair. The large, light room had big glass panes veiled by green linen shades; dark‑gray walls displayed caricatures and plaster models, producing a curious mixture of artistic disorder and harmony.On a bright July day the studio hummed with girls at their easels, each with different expressions and styles. Among them sat a modest, simply dressed girl, Ginevra Piombo, apart and seemingly anxious. The group split into two cliques: daughters of wealthy merchants and aristocrats, led by Amelie Thirion, vain and pretentious. Despite their differences they were absorbed in work, laughing and chatting. Ginevra, the most talented and beautiful, drew the aristocrats’ jealousy. Intent on ostracizing her, Amelie pushed her easel away, provoking whispers about the insult.The political climate after the Bourbons’ second return sharpened tensions. Ginevra, a devoted Bonapartist, met disdain from royalist girls; Amelie’s act was both a personal affront and a reflection of the era’s divisions. When Ginevra arrived she found silence where she expected warmth; her beauty and grace commanded attention, but her politics made her a target. Unmoved, she focused on her work, unaware of the full extent of the surrounding tension.During the session a faint sound behind a partition drew Ginevra’s attention. She discovered Servin was sheltering a political fugitive, an officer of the Imperial Guard. The revelation filled her with both excitement and concern for his safety. Resolving to protect him, she preferred to stay near the fugitive rather than risk his exposure. Her indifference to Amelie’s slight only increased the latter’s jealousy and baffled the other girls.Madame Servin’s arrival interrupted the brewing conflict; she announced her husband could not attend and seemed aware of the hidden man. Sensing danger, Ginevra began humming a Corsican song to help mask the officer’s breathing. As the session continued, she sketched the officer’s head with surprising fervor. Servin praised her talent; other girls, including Amelie, were drawn to her work. Amelie, suspicious, tried to uncover the mystery but was thwarted by Ginevra’s quick thinking. Servin, attuned to the tension, engaged each pupil while keeping a close eye on Ginevra. Their shared awareness deepened their bond, and Ginevra felt empowered to assist in protecting the fugitive.By day’s end Ginevra’s resolve to keep the secret and help the officer escape had strengthened. Political turmoil loomed outside, but within the studio the girls navigated rivalries and friendships, united by their art.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the painter Servin and Ginevra believed they were alone, Servin admitted a tall young officer in an Imperial uniform, wounded and pale. He recoiled at the sight of Ginevra, but Servin soothed him, introducing her as the daughter of Baron di Piombo, a loyal friend of the Emperor. Recognizing her patriotism, the soldier relaxed.Ginevra noticed his wound; he dismissed it as healing. Cries in the street then announced a death sentence—“Condemned to death!”—and the officer turned ashen at the name “Labedoyere,” collapsing on a stool. In despair he lamented that while Labedoyere faced execution for their cause, he was hiding. He felt abandoned after losing his protector and believed an honest life countenanced more than traitors’ lives; he wondered how to sell his life to the executioner.Ginevra stopped him as he tried to leave and asked whether he expected to restore the Emperor, whether he could raise “that giant who could not maintain himself.” He replied helplessly. Alarmed, she offered her savings, saying her father was wealthy and would not blame her for helping a soldier. She urged him to wait out the storm, escape, and serve elsewhere.Her maternal grace and hopeful persuasion moved him deeply. He admired her beauty and called her an “angel of goodness,” though his thoughts kept returning to Labedoyere. A quiet understanding passed between them; their brief acquaintance deepened into a bond. Servin asked if he could save Labedoyere; the officer could only avenge him, revealing a devotion to Napoleon that roused Ginevra.As the sun set on the officer’s face, Ginevra felt a sudden, almost magical connection, recalling childhood memories. She left smiling, a spark of hope kindled.The next day, the officer—now named Luigi—recounted the retreat from Moscow and the disaster at Waterloo. Ginevra listened, admiration growing as they conversed in Corsican, which strengthened their intimacy. Days passed; her classmates noticed her absence from social gatherings and attributed it to Luigi. Mademoiselle Thirion, worried by the rumor, withdrew from the class, and attendance declined. Ginevra remained absorbed by thoughts of Luigi.Laure, a fellow student, later confided that her mother forced her to leave the studio because of rumors about Ginevra and Luigi. Ginevra was shocked but reassured Laure they would meet again; Laure departed sadly. As Ginevra and Luigi’s bond deepened, they recognized each other’s worth in silence. One evening Luigi signaled to her but, spotting Laure, hesitated. Ginevra, unaware of underlying tensions, learned her classmates had left because of the scandal.Confronting Servin, she revealed the young ladies’ falsehoods; Servin dismissed the gossip and focused on his upcoming exhibition. Luigi arrived, joking that he had caused Servin to lose students; the painter playfully suggested marriage. Their ensuing silence—more eloquent than words—marked the first public acknowledgment of their love. Servin celebrated the joy of love and urged their union. Ginevra proposed helping Luigi secure a pardon, confident in her father’s influence.Meanwhile, Ginevra’s parents grew anxious at her late return. Bartolomeo, her father, paced, uneasy despite being used to her late hours; he had always prioritized his family’s well-being over appearances. As they waited, they reflected on their close-knit household, built on love, mutual respect, and shared political loyalty to the Emperor—values Ginevra had inherited.When she finally returned, Bartolomeo embraced her, relieved. The family shared a warm moment that revealed their support. In the studio, Ginevra and Luigi’s relationship blossomed amid scandal and danger; their love, forged in adversity, became a source of strength, uniting them and embodying the resilience of their shared ideals.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Here she is, my Ginevra, Ginevrettina, Ginevrola, mia Ginevra bella!” cried the old man.“Oh, father, you hurt me!” Ginevra laughed, then explained to her mother it had been a trick to slip away. The baroness recovered; Piombo, rubbing his hands, showed courtly joy. The two aged parents seemed renewed.“Now, to dinner!” Piombo called, offering his hand to Ginevra. “Your mother says you’ve been at the studio more. Painting takes you from us.”“Oh, father!” she protested.“Ginevra prepares a surprise,” her mother suggested.“A picture of your own! Will you show us?” Piombo asked eagerly.“Yes, I am busy at the studio,” she replied slowly. Her mother fretted over her pallor. “No! It shall never be said Ginevra Piombo acted a lie,” Ginevra declared, then confessed, lowering her eyelids, “I love a young man.”Piombo’s irony and his wife’s tremor met the revelation. “Is he a prince?” the baron asked.“No, father, a young man without fortune,” she answered gently.“Is he handsome?”“He is very unfortunate,” she said.“He?” Piombo pressed. “What is he?”“Labedoyere’s comrade; he was proscribed and hidden by Servin,” she explained.“Servin is a good fellow,” Piombo said, “but you do wrong to love any man but your father.”“It does not depend on me to love or not,” Ginevra replied softly.“I thought my Ginevra would remain faithful to me until I die,” Piombo lamented. “I did not expect our love to have a rival.”“Did I ever reproach you for devotion to Napoleon?” Ginevra countered. “You left me months on missions. I endured your absence. Life has necessities.”“Ginevra!” he cried.“You don’t love me for myself; your reproaches show your egotism,” she said.“Dare you blame your father’s love?” he shouted.“Father, I don’t blame you,” she answered. “You have grounds for your egotism, as I have for my love. I have never left your side for fifteen years; it has been my pleasure to charm your life. Is it ingratitude to desire a husband who will protect me?”“What! You reckon benefits with your father?” Piombo asked dangerously. Silence followed. Bartolomeo finally cried, “Oh! stay with us! I cannot bear you love another man. Ginevra, you will not have long to await your liberty.”“But, father, I need not leave you; we will both love you. You will come to know the man to whom you bequeath me,” she said.“Oh! Ginevra, why did you not marry when Napoleon offered?” Piombo lamented.“They loved me to order,” she replied. “I did not wish to leave you alone.”“You don’t wish to leave me alone, yet you marry! I know you, my daughter; you would cease to love us,” he said.Elisa, the baroness, sat motionless. “We have no longer a daughter; she wishes to marry,” Piombo declared, hands raised to heaven, bowed by sorrow. Ginevra expected a violent outburst but met gentleness.“Father,” she said, “you shall never be abandoned by your Ginevra. Love me for my own sake. If you knew how he loves me! He would never make me unhappy!”“Comparisons already!” Piombo shouted. “If he loved you as you deserve, he would kill me; if not, I would kill him.”Ginevra alone could withstand his gaze, her eyes aflame. “Oh! to love you! What man is worthy of such a life?” Piombo continued. “Who is worthy to be your husband?”“He,” Ginevra replied, “he of whom I am not worthy.”“How can he know you enough to love you?” he asked.“Whether he loves me or not, if I love him—” she began.“You love him?” Piombo interrupted.Ginevra nodded.“You love him more than you love us?” he pressed.“The two feelings cannot be compared,” she said.“Is one stronger?” he asked.“Yes, I think it is,” she admitted.“You shall not marry him,” Piombo declared, shaking the windowpanes.“I shall marry him,” Ginevra replied calmly.“Oh, God!” cried her mother. “How will this quarrel end?”Piombo, now seated, tried a softer tone. “Say nothing more tonight—let me think otherwise. Do you wish to see your father on his knees, his white hairs prostrate before you? I supplicate you—”“Ginevra Piombo does not break her word,” she answered. “I am your daughter.”“She is right,” the baroness said. “We are sent into the world to marry.”“Do you encourage her in disobedience?” Piombo demanded of his wife, who sat petrified.“Refusing an unjust order is not disobedience,” Ginevra asserted.“No order can be unjust from your father’s lips,” Piombo replied. “Why do you judge me? My repugnance may be counsel from on high, to protect you from great evil.”“The only evil would be if he did not love me.”“Always he!” Piombo exclaimed.“Yes, always,” she affirmed. “He is my life, my good, my thought. Even if I obeyed you, he would remain in my soul. To forbid me to marry him is to make me hate you.”“You love us not!” Piombo shouted.“Oh!” Ginevra shook her head.“Then forget him; be faithful to us. After we are gone—you understand?”“Father, do you wish me to long for your death?” she asked.“I shall outlive you. Children who do not honor their parents die early,” he warned.“All the more reason I should marry and be happy,” she replied.Piombo’s anger cooled as Ginevra sprang to his knee and embraced him. “Oh, yes, yes, let me die first! I could never survive you, my father!” she cried.“Oh! my Ginevra, my own Ginevra!” Piombo answered, affection melting his fury. They shared playful moments, but Ginevra’s attempts to introduce Luigi were met with her father’s teasing refusals.The next day Ginevra was more tender to her father, thanking him as if for a consent that did not yet exist. A week later, her mother motioned her close. “I have persuaded your father to receive him,” Elisa whispered. Ginevra rejoiced.That day she returned on Luigi’s arm. The officer, who had emerged from hiding, had been helped by Ginevra’s appeal to the Duc de Feltre — his name was restored on the officers’ list. Warned that her father might disapprove, Luigi was anxious to meet him, and his fear deepened Ginevra’s love.“How pale you are!” she said as they reached her home.“Oh! Ginevra, if it concerned my life only—” he began. Bartolomeo sat stern and silent.“Father,” Ginevra said, “I bring you someone you will be pleased to see—a soldier who fought beside the Emperor at Mont-Saint-Jean.”Piombo rose, glancing at Luigi. “Monsieur is not decorated,” he remarked.“I no longer wear the Legion of Honor,” Luigi replied timidly.Embarrassed by her father’s rudeness, Ginevra drew a chair for Luigi. Madame Piombo, noting her husband’s softening, tried to engage. “Monsieur’s resemblance to someone we knew in Corsica, Nina Porta, is surprising,” she noted.“Nothing could be more natural,” Luigi answered. “Nina was my sister.”“Are you Luigi Porta?” Piombo asked.“Yes,” Luigi confirmed.The effect was instantaneous. Bartolomeo staggered, supported by his wife. The two old parents left the room in silence, abandoning their daughter in horror.Luigi, bewildered, looked at Ginevra, who had turned pale, staring at the door through which her parents had exited. The air filled with dread. “What misery in a word!” she exclaimed.“In the name of our love, what have I said?” Luigi asked.“My father never spoke of our tragic history, and I was too young to know,” Ginevra explained. “My mother told me the Portas killed my brother and burned our house. My father then massacred their family. How did you survive? You were tied to the bed before they set fire to the house.”“I do not know,” Luigi replied. “I was taken to Genoa at six and raised by an old man named Colonna. I was warned I had enemies and advised to take Luigi as my surname to evade them.”“Go, go, Luigi!” Ginevra urged. “Stay here; you are safe in my father’s house, but once you leave, danger awaits! My father has two Corsicans in his service, and they will hunt you.”“Ginevra,” he asked, trembling, “does this vendetta exist between us?”She smiled sadly and nodded. “Oh, Luigi, our love must be pure and sincere to give me strength for the path ahead. But it promises happiness for our lives, does it not?”Luigi squeezed her hand. Ginevra realized true love must endure more than words. Resolute, she escorted him to his modest lodging and ensured he reached it safely.Returning home, she greeted her parents, who were preparing dinner, with gentle composure, noticing her mother’s tear-streaked face. They rose in silence. In the gloomy salon Piombo attempted speech but faltered; his strength seemed gone. He rang for a servant. “Pietro, light the fire; I am cold,” he ordered.Ginevra trembled, watching his internal struggle. Both suffered in silence, nursing pride and pain. The love that had bound them was now a burden between reproach and sorrow.On Ginevra’s birthday, in a final attempt to reconcile, her mother gathered them in Bartolomeo’s study. Ginevra divined her mother’s plan and smiled sadly. At that moment two notaries arrived with witnesses. Bartolomeo eyed them warily. The older notary began to speak of Ginevra’s marriage to Luigi Porta.“Monsieur le baron, we have come to fulfill the law regarding your daughter’s marriage,” he stated.Piombo erupted. “Are there laws in France that destroy paternal authority? Which tear a daughter from her father? Which deprive an old man of his last consolation?” He drove the notary silent. In a sudden movement he seized a dagger from the wall and rushed toward Ginevra. Witnesses intervened, but Piombo pushed them aside, fury driving him.When Ginevra saw him approach she knelt before him. “No, no! I cannot!” he cried, flinging the dagger away. “Have mercy! Give me Luigi! I ask your consent upon my knees,” she pleaded, voice trembling.Bartolomeo pushed her away. “Go. The wife of Luigi Porta cannot be a Piombo. I have no daughter. My Ginevra Piombo is buried here,” he said, clutching his heart. “Leave my house, unhappy girl,” he added, ushering her out.“Luigi!” Ginevra cried, entering her lover’s humble abode. “My Luigi, we have no fortune but our love.”“Then I am richer than kings!” he exclaimed.“My father and mother have cast me off,” she said sadly.“I will love you in their place,” he promised.“Then let us be happy—we WILL be happy!” she declared, voice full of mingled joy and dread.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day after Ginevra was expelled from her father's house, she sought refuge with Madame Servin until her marriage to Luigi. Madame Servin received her coldly, upset by the trouble Ginevra had caused her husband. Proud yet shocked by this selfishness, Ginevra rented a room near Luigi, who devoted himself to her and painted a bright future that lifted her spirits.One morning Ginevra received trunks of essentials from her mother, who included a letter urging her to reconsider the marriage. The letter, full of love and sorrow, deeply moved Ginevra. Just as she felt compelled to return home, Luigi arrived and her resolve to abandon him faded; she chose to bury her grief for the sake of their love.On the wedding day Ginevra was alone, and Luigi brought ordinary men as witnesses, a stark contrast to typical wedding gaiety. Ginevra's beauty silenced them, creating a somber atmosphere. As they walked to the mayor's office, they passed other joyful wedding parties, highlighting the difference in their situation.Inside, their love was overshadowed by the festive mood around them. The mayor's clerk noted the absence of Ginevra's parents, and when the mayor inquired about their protest the clerk confirmed it. The bureaucratic formality felt oppressive, reminding Ginevra of her family's disapproval; the ceremony was reduced to a legal act devoid of celebration.After signing the registers, Ginevra and Luigi left the office with a mix of relief and sadness. Luigi expressed his gratitude, and Ginevra felt a quiet happiness despite the circumstances. They hurried to the church, where an old priest married them in a modest chapel amid the grandeur of other weddings. The priest's remarks about their parents' absence stung, but a blessing from a sergeant comforted Ginevra.Once married, they moved into a modest apartment where Luigi embraced Ginevra, calling it their true wedding. They explored their new home, which was filled with artistic supplies for Ginevra. She was delighted by the thoughtful arrangements, and they reveled in their shared space, feeling they had found paradise despite its limitations.In the early days of marriage they indulged in each other’s presence, but reality soon set in and they realized they needed to work. Ginevra began copying paintings and gained clients, while Luigi used his handwriting skills to offer copying services. His efforts paid off, and soon he had enough work to hire assistants, providing a stable income for both.This period marked the happiest time of their lives, filled with love and labor. They enjoyed evenings together; Ginevra always smiled and never complained. Each worked for the other, finding joy in shared effort. Yet Ginevra occasionally felt pangs of remorse for the happiness she had sacrificed by leaving her family, haunted by thoughts of her parents' sorrow.

Augustine Guillaume's cousin's tale is worth mentioning. Élodie-Rose, the widow of Maelien, lived a very miserable life. She was born in the same city, Paris, in the same year as Augustine, and grew up happily alongside her, until she got married. She married very early at 16, to a skinny man exactly twice her age with the same hair color and height,  and moved to Albi with him immediately afterward. The last time Augustine and Élodie-Rose saw each other was at Élodie-Rose’s wedding. Élodie-Rose became single again very shortly after and had to continue her life in a town far from her family. She endured a life full of hardship and pain. The six feet tall lady aged very quickly—her dark black hair turned gray, she developed stage 4 glioblastoma, the worst type of brain cancer, and her beautiful hazel eyes grew sad. She lived in Albi for two decades, most of it alone,  and died there a lonely death.


To commemorate their anniversary, Ginevra painted a portrait of herself for Luigi—an embodiment of their wedded bliss. Another year passed in contentment; no significant events disrupted their quiet happiness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the beginning of 1819 picture-dealers urged Ginevra to produce originals instead of copies as competition grew. Madame Porta regretted not pursuing genre painting earlier; she tried portraiture but faced stiffer rivals. Despite modest savings, Luigi and Ginevra worried about their future.That winter Luigi labored relentlessly in a crowded, poorly paid writing market. He could no longer afford help and had to work longer for the same pay. Ginevra painted commendable pieces, but dealers shunned lesser-known artists, and even low prices brought few sales.Their household grew dire; love and happiness were overshadowed by poverty. They concealed worries from each other: Ginevra lavished affection while Luigi hid his fears. One night Ginevra woke to find Luigi gone and discovered him working late to avoid burdening her. Moved, she resolved to help by coloring lithographs, working through the night while Luigi toiled in his garret. At dawn they pretended to sleep, each shielding the other from distress.On another night Luigi, exhausted and feverish, opened his window and saw Ginevra’s lamp across the courtyard, realizing she too worked. Confronting her, he learned she could not idle while he suffered. They shared a tender moment, watching dawn together and feeling tentative hope.Their struggle deepened when Ginevra bore a son, Bartolomeo. The joy of parenthood briefly masked their burdens, but soon they sold possessions and jewels. Poverty stripped them of comfort and pride, pushing them toward desperation.Months later Ginevra, frail and worn, struggled to care for a sickly child. Luigi, feeling helpless, sought help and found none. He sold Ginevra’s paintings and their remaining furniture for a pittance, hardly easing their plight. Ginevra displayed remarkable strength, managing the household and tending their son while often starving herself to feed Luigi.One evening she offered Luigi her last piece of bread and urged him to eat. Realizing her sacrifice, he was engulfed by despair and left to seek aid. In desperation he decided to sell himself as a military substitute, hoping the advance would save Ginevra and their child.After obtaining the money, he hurried home, finding Ginevra asleep with their child. When he woke her, she smiled, then noticed Bartolomeo was cold and lifeless. Luigi, horrified, sought help, but it was too late. In delirium Ginevra professed her love for Luigi and lamented their lost child. She died in his arms, leaving him devastated.As Luigi mourned, Ginevra’s parents sat in their home, remembering the daughter they had not seen in years. Elisa Piombo spoke of her, hoping to rekindle Bartolomeo’s affection for Ginevra. Just as Bartolomeo seemed to soften, a man entered with the tragic news of Ginevra and Luigi’s fate. The old couple were shattered, and Bartolomeo’s heart broke at the loss of his beloved child. The cycle of suffering had claimed another victim, leaving only memories and grief.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Many tales, rich in plot, can be rendered simply without losing charm. But some human experiences require a delicate touch: certain details—anatomical, one might say—demand a nuanced rendering of thought. Portraits need a soul; some things can only be articulated through secret harmonies born of time, circumstance, or an inner moral inclination.Such revelations are essential to tell this simple story, intended for those who are grave and reflective, who draw sustenance from tender emotions. If the writer, like a surgeon at a dying friend’s side, feels reverence for the subject, should not the reader share that feeling? It is easy to resonate with the vague sadness that surrounds us, a gentle suffering oddly pleasing. If the reader has mourned a dear one, is alone, or the day is waning, they may continue; if not, they should set this book aside. Those who have never buried a beloved relative will not grasp these pages, which may seem fragrant to some and colorless to others. The reader must have known the luxury of tears and the silent pangs of memory, evoking regret for what is lost and smiles for past joys.The writer assures this narrative is a true story, worthy of the reader's emotional investment, if they possess any.In contemporary France, language mirrors as many idioms and idiosyncrasies as there are individuals. It is entertaining to hear differing accounts of the same person from Parisians. Ask a Practical man about Madame Firmiani and he will describe wealth and standing: a fine house in the rue du Bac, well-furnished salons, an income of a hundred thousand francs, and a husband once a receiver-general—solid people, nothing to criticize.A Lounger knows her as a hostess: respectable house, excellent tea, gatherings of the best society. An Egotist dismisses her salons as dull, lamenting that possessing a mind should exempt one from trivial conversation. A Student calls her enchanting and wishes to introduce you. A Fop notes faded beauty but admits a lingering charm.A Connoisseur praises her collection of paintings, while a Spiteful woman warns against visiting her, labeling her a dangerous siren who arouses jealousy. An Attache remembers her beauty a decade ago. A Distinguishing type remarks on elite connections despite modest living. A Duke pretends not to know her, recognizing only those presented at court. A Ninny mistakenly claims she used to sing at the Opera. Two old ladies gossip about her lineage, revealing ties to the noble Cadignan family.An Original declares her salon safe since any scoundrels present are of high quality. An Observer describes a beautiful woman who rarely rises to greet guests, suggesting she is a mystery married to a mythical husband; he notes her charm and conversational skill, hinting at many admirers but asserting none favored. A Contradictor will claim she was once Murat’s mistress, while an Envious person dismisses her wealth, calling her a coquette who ruined a young man’s prospects—an oft-seen, puzzling spite.Thus Madame Firmiani emerges through many lenses—each account revealing more about the observer than the observed. These varied testimonies, shaped by personal bias and experience, invite readers to explore interpretations while reflecting on the deeper emotional truths that connect them all.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In January 1824, opinions about Madame Firmiani varied so widely that no clear portrait emerged. She might be a widow or a wife, wise or foolish, virtuous or otherwise, rich or poor, beautiful or plain. This range of interpretations showed how society distorted a reputation, like lithographic blocks producing different images from slight variations.That winter Monsieur de Bourbonne, an elderly country magnate from Touraine, grew intrigued. A provincial planter used to managing an estate and tenants, he was shrewd and accustomed to practical affairs. He came to Paris to learn more, but gossip only increased his confusion. A man of honor, he had a beloved nephew, Octave de Camps, in whom he had high hopes. Octave had recently sold an estate without consulting him, which angered the old man. A distant relative then claimed Octave had squandered his fortune on Madame Firmiani and was now teaching mathematics to survive—a revelation that deeply troubled Monsieur de Bourbonne.Refusing to accept the worst, he went to Paris to investigate Octave’s situation. Using his connections in the faubourg Saint-Germain, he learned more about Madame Firmiani and resolved to meet her under the name de Rouxellay, hoping to discern the truth of her relationship with Octave. He chose a time when he knew Octave would be occupied, and soon confirmed that the rumors of his nephew’s financial distress were true.Monsieur de Rouxellay was no typical provincial uncle; once a member of the King’s guard, he understood society’s manners and politics. Entering Madame Firmiani’s salon with the Marquise de Listomère, he expressed pity for Octave, wondering how a woman of such luxury could be linked to someone in such straits. Yet on seeing Madame Firmiani his anger melted. Her beauty, heightened by the salon’s atmosphere, captivated him and made him reconsider his initial judgments.Madame Firmiani, said to be twenty-five but closer to twenty-eight, had married the invisible Firmiani at sixteen. At her height she combined beauty, wealth, musical talent, intelligence, and wit. Admired in high society and respected by rivals, her popularity made her a target for malicious gossip; many envied her apparent happiness and propriety. Though the rumors were unfounded, they persisted, revealing the dangers of societal suspicion.This account hints at Madame Firmiani’s true nature: a complex blend of qualities—beauty, grace, charm—that inspired admiration and intrigue.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The former guardsman lingered after the other guests had left; Madame Firmiani found him still seated before her as the clock struck two in the morning.“Madame,” he began as she rose, signaling he should go, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”Madame Firmiani sat back down, visibly moved. The old man, Monsieur de Bourbonne, could not tell whether her pallor was shame or pleasure—provincial men often misread reserved women. She gave him a sharp look that puzzled him.“Someone in my province tells me my nephew has ruined himself for you, living in a garret while you enjoy wealth,” he said bluntly. “Forgive my rustic honesty; you should know these rumors.”“Stop, monsieur,” she interrupted. “I know of them. You are too polite to press and too gallant to suspect me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe me when I say I scorn money; I married without fortune. Your nephew’s wealth or poverty matters not; I welcome him as a friend. All my friends respect one another; I cannot accept those I do not esteem. My guardian angel has kept me from gossip and dishonesty.”“Madame,” Monsieur de Bourbonne said, trembling, “I am old, almost a father to Octave. Pardon me for one question. I swear as a gentleman your answer shall remain here.” He placed his hand on his heart. “Do you love Octave?”“To any other man I would answer with a look,” she replied. “To you I ask what you would think of a woman who answered such a question. To confess love is the lover’s reward. To tell another—” She left the sentence unfinished, rose, bowed, and withdrew to her private rooms.He left thinking her either sly or angelic.The next morning about eight, Monsieur de Bourbonne found Octave in his garret. The old man berated him for squandering the advantages of a comfortable uncle who meant to leave him an estate in Touraine. “Have you been gambling? Lost at the Bourse? Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll hug you. But if you lie, I’ll return to my youthful ways.”“I saw your Madame Firmiani yesterday,” he went on, approvingly. “Have you ruined yourself for her?”“Yes, uncle,” Octave admitted.“I guessed as much. In my day women were cleverer at ruining men—this one is a piece of the last century,” the old man said.“You are mistaken, uncle. Madame Firmiani deserves esteem and adoration,” Octave said tenderly. He produced a letter from her, given in a portfolio she had handed him. “Read this; it explains almost everything.”Octave began to read aloud: “‘My beloved—’”“Then you are still intimate with her?” his uncle interrupted.“Of course,” Octave said. “We are married.”“Heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Then why do you live in a garret?”“Let me continue.” Octave read, sometimes pausing with feeling: “‘My beloved Husband, you ask why I am sad. I cannot deceive you; a wife should be gay and caressing. I long to love you forever; a woman’s glory is in the man she loves. Esteem, consideration, honor—must they not belong to him who receives our all? Well, my angel has fallen. The tale you told me has tarnished my past joys. I feel humiliated in you—you whom I thought the most honorable of men. I must have deep confidence to make this avowal, which costs me much. How can you, knowing your father unjustly deprived others of their property, keep it? You told me of this in the room where our love began; you are a gentleman, you think yourself noble, and I am yours. I find some excuses in your youth and thoughtlessness, but I cannot respect a man who knowingly is smirched for a sum of money.
