Five francs stolen at play or five times a hundred thousand won by a legal trick equally dishonor. Love, to a woman, means boundless confidence and reverence. If you come to me poor, my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. I do not wish you to make restitution because I urge it; consult your conscience. A man who loves a woman sincerely respects the sanctity of her trust too deeply to dishonor himself. I am your wife, not your mistress. I blame myself for preaching, but scold me if you must—only a little. Dear, between us the power is yours—you alone must perceive your faults.’”There was silence. Octave wiped his eyes. “There’s more,” he said, but the rest he smiled away as a lover’s secret.“You teach mathematics, and you have your uncle’s capital?” Monsieur de Bourbonne asked. Octave explained that the letter had sparked a revolution in him. Haunted by conscience—every pleasure felt like theft—he rushed to Madame Firmiani. Together they calculated what was due to the Bourgneufs, whom Octave’s father had wronged. “I condemned myself to pay three percent interest,” he said, though his means did not cover the full amount. Madame Firmiani, whose position had made her careful, offered her savings and he accepted.“You mean that adorable woman actually saves money?” the uncle exclaimed.“Do not laugh at her,” Octave said. “Her husband went to Greece in 1820 and died three years later. Legal proof of his death and his will, leaving everything to her, were lost or stolen during the troubles there. Unsure whether she might lose her fortune, she lived prudently. I wanted to acquire property of my own to provide for her if she lost hers.” He added that he gave lessons to avoid living at anyone’s expense and that the act of restitution had brought him joys beyond millions. He found the Bourgneufs living miserably: an old father agent, daughters keeping books and house, a sick mother. To bring them relief felt like being Providence; words could not convey the happiness of making amends. He called Madame Firmiani “my dear conscience”—she inspired a delicacy of feeling he thought he had lacked—and he hoped to become rich by his own industry.Touched, the old man said, “Ah, my boy, you have your mother’s soul.” Then, from the street, the sound of a carriage announced Madame Firmiani. She entered, slightly annoyed to find Monsieur de Bourbonne there but smiling. “I came to humbly entreat my husband to accept my fortune,” she said. “The Austrian Embassy has sent me a document proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet kept safe for me. Octave, you can accept it all; you are richer than I, for you have treasures here”—she laid her hand on his heart—“to which none but God can add.” She then laid her head on his breast in unrestrained happiness.Monsieur de Bourbonne concluded, “In my day we made love; in yours, you love. Women are all that is best in humanity; they are not even guilty of their faults, for they come through us.”MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, once a dark, winding lane near the Hôtel de Ville, ended at the Rue Martroi and was closed by a turnstile until 1823. Less than six feet wide at its broadest, it often pooled gutter water and remained muddy and damp; sunlight barely reached it except briefly in summer. Lamps were lit by five in June and kept burning through winter. Approaching the Marais felt like passing through cellars, as many old Paris streets did. A corner house at Rue du Tourniquet and Rue de la Tixeranderie showed its age: iron rings remained from chains once used for public safety.Built to combat humidity, the house had cellars raised above ground and an entrance reached by three steps. The door had a closed arch with a keystone carved as a female head. Three high windows, protected by iron bars, lit a small dwelling. Inside, two beds draped in green serge and an old woman tending a brazier preparing a porter’s-wife stew were visible. The kitchen-dining room was sparsely furnished but kept with evident cleanliness and thrift.The old woman’s pale, wrinkled face seemed part of the house itself. She sat by the fireplace with an expression of mischief mixed with resignation while her daughter worked at an embroidery frame. With slow fingers and antiquated spectacles, the mother occasionally glanced out the window; the daughter bent over her work with focused modesty.Their modest home reflected the lives of working-class Parisian women. Despite poverty, the girl retained youthful charm—rosy cheeks and bright eyes that suggested a spirit not wholly dimmed by hardship. The old woman speculated about passersby and hoped for a better future for her daughter; the girl, absorbed in embroidery, usually kept her gaze down.One August day in 1815 the mother noticed a new face among the regular passersby: a tall, thin man in black whose solemn bearing intrigued her. She fancied he was a clerk from the Préfet’s office, and her excitement was plain. Caroline, roused from indifference by her mother’s remarks, glimpsed the stranger as he passed.The old woman eagerly awaited his return, convinced he would come by again. When he did, Caroline caught another glimpse and felt an unfamiliar sympathy, noting traces of suffering on his face. This was the first time a passerby had stirred such feeling in her; she typically regarded passersby with indifference, focused only on work and routine. The man’s presence left a lasting impression, and as she resumed her embroidery Caroline found herself looking forward to another sight of him, intrigued by the possibility that this new acquaintance might alter her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For two months the “Black Gentleman” behaved erratically, varying the time he passed the Rue du Tourniquet. Madame Crochard noticed his distracted manner—often lost in thought and seldom acknowledging her or Caroline. One mid-September morning Caroline’s lively presence caught his eye; their brief, silent glances suggested a quiet understanding.Weeks later he began passing twice daily; their meetings became a routine they both anticipated. Their silent exchanges conveyed mutual concern: Caroline sensed when he was troubled, and he could tell when she was anxious about work. Both remained cautious, fearing greater intimacy might bring complications.Madame Crochard, annoyed by her daughter’s indecision, turned her displeasure toward the Gentleman, whom she had once mildly approved. As winter neared, life’s hardships wore on Caroline, and the Gentleman observed how they affected her spirit. One night he overheard a mother-and-daughter conversation that revealed their financial struggles. Touched, he secretly left money for them; Caroline found it the next day, and a moment of silent gratitude passed between them.In early May Caroline proposed a trip to Montmorency, and the Gentleman—more somber than usual—unexpectedly joined. During the journey Madame Crochard pretended to sleep, giving Caroline and the Gentleman a chance for light-hearted talk that revealed their growing affection. The countryside’s beauty and their warm exchange lifted his spirits; he began to see Caroline differently.Their bond deepened as they shared personal stories. Caroline spoke of her family’s hardships and her mother’s sacrifices. The Gentleman, now called Roger, was drawn to her honesty and resilience. The day in Montmorency brimmed with laughter and joy, a sharp contrast to their earlier meetings.As evening approached, Caroline voiced her newfound happiness, and Roger reflected on the possibility of more than a single bright day. Preparing to leave, they playfully held hands—an unspoken promise and a hopeful step toward a brighter future together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The adventures of Roger and Caroline continue in modern Paris, where newly built apartments suit honeymooners. In a pristine building on Rue Taitbout, Caroline has lived for over a month in a charming second-floor apartment, furnished with care and style. The rooms, freshly painted and elegantly appointed, reflect current trends influenced by English design.Her bedroom has gray hangings trimmed with green silk, comfortable chairs, and a writing table for love letters. A bronze clock showing Love crowning Psyche and a Gothic-patterned carpet complete the cozy setting. As she prepares for Roger’s visit, her hairdresser flatters her, reminding her of his admiration.On a chilly September day in 1816, Caroline wears a green grenadine gown trimmed with chinchilla and eagerly steps onto the balcony to watch for Roger; her heart races as she anticipates his arrival. After a quarter-hour of waiting, impatience replaces excitement, but she brightens when she finally sees him approaching.Roger arrives and they embrace passionately, rejoicing in their reunion after three long days apart. He then tells her he must attend a meeting with the Attorney-General, which disappoints Caroline; she playfully objects to the idea of his enjoying the theatre without her and declares her deep affection.Despite the setback, Caroline returns to the joy of being with Roger. She expresses her wish to take his name—Caroline de Bellefeuille—and, perched on his lap, speaks of using her earnings to help the poor, underscoring her gratitude for their life together. As she plays a sonata, Roger, mindful of obligations, prepares to leave. They exchange a playful farewell; she watches him drive away, savoring the moment and longing for his return.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years after Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille settled in her Rue Taitbout home, she sits serenely with her four-year-old son Charles, who plays with a rocking horse. Now twenty-four, Caroline’s beauty reflects happiness and fulfillment. She has embraced motherhood, devoted to raising children and cultivating their appreciation of goodness and beauty. Content with simple pleasures, she remains indifferent to social ambition, never craving luxury or status.Rocking her infant daughter Eugenie, Caroline recalls joyful visits to Bellefeuille with Roger. Their love has flourished in the simplicity of shared life, free from social complication; yet she wonders why Roger seeks happiness in secrecy and what binds him to such arrangements.On May 6, 1822, the anniversary of a pivotal day for them, Caroline prepares for dinner expecting Roger. He arrives with a document detailing Eugenie’s marriage portion and they discuss different provisions for their children. Roger argues Charles, as a boy, should learn self-sufficiency, while Eugenie, as a girl, deserves a fortune. Their evening is warm and lively, ending with a magical lantern show for Charles. As night deepens, Roger must leave; Caroline feels the familiar pain of separation. He reassures her he has three days off, but duty still looms.Soon after that anniversary, Caroline hastens to her mother, Madame Crochard, who is gravely ill. The old widow’s acquaintances, unaware that Caroline is her daughter, have summoned a priest to offer last rites. Caroline arrives amid confusion and deceit: the old women hope to benefit from Madame Crochard’s wealth. Despite their intentions, Caroline’s love shows as she cares for her mother.In her final moments Madame Crochard expresses regret over their estrangement and reveals that the priest has compelled her to confess about Caroline’s benefactor—an admission that hints at a complex web of relationships and secrets to follow.To explain those connections, the narrative backtracks to events before Madame Crochard’s death. In November 1805 a young barrister, Monsieur de Granville, finds himself precariously placed after leaving a gathering at the High Chancellor’s residence and gets a chance to advance his career through his uncle’s influence. He receives a letter from his father urging a swift return: a possible marriage to Mademoiselle Angélique Bontems could secure his future. The letter details the financial advantages despite the social stigma attached to the Bontems family.Torn between ambition and feeling, Granville recalls childhood affection for Angélique, whom he has not seen in years. The next day he travels to Bayeux, greeted by his father and taken to the Bontems’ home, stifled under the widow’s strict religious observances. Granville dreads the match, yet his father’s insistence weighs on him.During a church service Granville glimpses Angélique; her beauty captivates him anew. Their brief interaction rekindles his feelings, while family constraints remain. As the service ends his impatience drives him to approach her, setting the stage for a reunion that could alter both their lives.The intertwined stories of Caroline and Granville reveal the tensions of love, duty, and social expectation as each navigates personal desire against familial obligation and ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For about two weeks after the Vicomte de Granville’s engagement to Mademoiselle Bontems, he visited her regularly in her dreary drawing-room. He watched Angelique marking her trousseau; she never initiated religious discussion. When Granville playfully handled her rosary or mocked religious practices, she quietly returned it to its bag with a serene smile. She was firmly committed to the Church’s teachings, insisting a mother should have a religion and questioning how one could judge between disbelievers and God.Granville, captivated by her conviction, sometimes mistook his desire for her as a wish to share her beliefs. Angelique reconciled feeling and duty with ease, leaving Granville unsure of his own emotions. He was grateful to Madame Bontems for instilling those principles, believing they would help Angelique face life’s hardships.On the day of their marriage contract, Madame Bontems made Granville promise to respect Angelique’s religious practices and allow her freedom of conscience. Angelique’s innocent gaze convinced him to agree; the Abbe Fontanon, who guided their consciences, approved. Shortly after their wedding the couple moved to Paris, where Granville became public prosecutor.Angelique persuaded him to take a large ground-floor apartment in the Marais, near a church and chapel, arguing it would benefit their future children. Granville, who had preferred a more fashionable neighborhood, yielded. He set up his study and left the home decoration to Angelique, who delighted in furnishing their new house. But Granville was dismayed by the cold, solemn atmosphere she created, a reflection of her austere upbringing.Despite trying to understand her choices, he grew increasingly frustrated by the lack of warmth and charm. He blamed her Bayeux background for a less refined taste and tried to comfort her by saying happiness came from her love and gentleness rather than material things.From April through winter the couple appeared harmonious; Granville stayed busy with work and remained largely unaware of Angelique’s strict observances—Lenten abstinence she disguised as carelessness. He accompanied her to church on Sundays but did not grasp her fervor until a significant event disrupted their lives.In November 1808 the Canon of Bayeux, Angelique’s spiritual guide, arrived in Paris alarmed by what he saw as her moral decline and sought to reclaim her from a lax lifestyle. Angelique adopted stricter practices, causing misunderstandings, particularly over social obligations. Granville grew irritated by her frequent excuses to avoid events, especially balls.After a tense evening he confronted her about her demeanor and attire, urging her to fulfill her social role. Arguments escalated: Angelique cited her director’s prohibitions; Granville insisted her priest was overstepping. The dispute turned theological and led Angelique to write to the Pope for guidance.The Pope’s reply affirmed Granville’s view, saying a wife should accompany her husband even if it meant compromising her virtue. Rather than settle the matter, this deepened the divide; Angelique and her director dismissed the Pope’s advice. Frustrated by her strict observances, Granville began to assert his preferences more forcefully—ordering meat daily despite her objections.As the home grew oppressive, Granville withdrew from social life, weighed down by Angelique’s bigotry. The household became stifling and gloomy, affecting everyone including servants. Angelique’s beauty was overshadowed by austerity; duty had replaced love, leaving little room for affection. Their children suffered under strict rules: Granville sent his sons to school early and resolved to marry off his daughters as soon as possible.Increasingly isolated, he sought solace in work and attempted to write a book on law, but Angelique’s attempts to reconvert him only added to his frustration. The relationship deteriorated: Granville felt alienated from his wife and their home, now cold and rigid rather than warm and companionable.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The history of the de Granville household between 1806 and 1825 was outwardly uneventful. Madame de Granville, having lost her husband's affection, persisted in paying for Masses and scrutinizing her faults in hopes of restoring their marriage. Granville, now a prominent judge, avoided her by living in the entresol. Each morning a housekeeper relayed messages; he continually declined breakfast invitations, citing important court business.At thirty-five and looking older, Madame de Granville waited for his return, ready with narrow remarks. When Granville refused the presidency of a provincial court, friends and her confessor suspected an illicit affair; the refusal seemed to signal a liaison, which horrified her because she believed him incapable of such sin.During Lent in 1822 her health declined from stress and fever, made worse by his indifference. Her attempts at affection were often undone by recurring bigotry. By late May, as she recovered, she sought guidance from the Abbe Fontanon, who delivered devastating news: Granville had lived with a concubine for seven years, fathering two children and squandering their fortune.In disbelief, she wrestled with faith and betrayal. The Abbe urged forgiveness and patience, predicting Granville’s eventual enlightenment, but she was consumed by turmoil. After a tumultuous day she left home, determined to confront him.Meanwhile Roger and Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille engaged in a tense exchange: she declared hatred for his wife, whom she blamed for their unhappiness, and he reassured her of hopes for a shared future. Their conversation was interrupted when Madame de Granville suddenly fainted. On regaining consciousness she confronted Granville, questioning his love and fidelity. He accused her of sacrificing his happiness to piety, claiming she had failed to be a loving wife.Their ensuing exchange exposed deep faults. Granville argued that true love requires sacrifice and understanding; he said he had sought happiness elsewhere because she had neglected their relationship through religious devotion. Madame de Granville clung to moral righteousness, her jealousy and anger revealing irreconcilable differences. His coldness and admission of loving another devastated her. Despite her tears and pleas for forgiveness, he remained resolute, saying he could not rekindle a love faded by years of neglect.The dialogue ended in a stark acknowledgment of their failed marriage: love cannot be commanded or revived at will. Madame de Granville was left to face the reality of his emotional detachment and the consequences of their choices.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in December 1833 a white‑haired man, his features worn more by grief than years, walked the Rue Gaillon at midnight. He paused before a modest two‑story house whose attic windows showed a dim, flickering light, some patched with paper. A young man emerged; recognizing him, he approached with a jesting courtesy.“What, is it you, Monsieur le Président? Alone at this hour, so far from the Rue Saint‑Lazare. Allow me to give you my arm. The pavement is so greasy this morning that if we do not hold each other up, we shall find it hard to escape a tumble.”The Comte de Granville answered dryly that at fifty‑five he was still hale and strong.“You must be in waiting on a lady,” said Horace Bianchon. “You are not in the habit of walking Paris. When a man keeps such fine horses—”“Still, when I do not visit in the evening, I commonly return from the Courts or the club on foot,” Granville returned.“And with large sums of money about you, perhaps!” Bianchon exclaimed. “It is a positive invitation to the assassin’s knife.”“I am not afraid of that,” Granville said with melancholy indifference.“Then do not stand about,” urged Bianchon, drawing him toward the boulevard. “A little more and I shall believe you are bent on robbing me of your last illness, dying by some other hand than mine.”“You caught me playing the spy,” Granville admitted. “For some time I have watched at a third‑floor window of your house the shadow of a person who seems to work with heroic constancy.”He paused as if in pain. “I take as great an interest in that garret as a citizen of Paris must in the finishing of the Palais‑Royal.”“Well,” Bianchon began eagerly—“Tell me nothing,” Granville interrupted. “I would not give a centime to know whether the shadow is that of a man or a woman, nor whether the inhabitant is happy or miserable. Though I was surprised to see no one at work there this evening, I stopped only to indulge in the idle conjectures of an idler. For nine years I have ceased to wonder at old men who amuse themselves with growing flowers and planting trees; life has taught them disbelief in all human affection. I grew old within a few days. I will no longer attach myself to any creature but unreasoning animals, plants, or superficial things. I think more of Taglioni’s grace than of all human feeling. I abhor life and the world in which I live alone. Nothing, nothing can move or interest me.”“But you have children?” Bianchon asked.“My children!” Granville repeated bitterly. “My eldest daughter is the Comtesse de Vandenesse. The other, through her sister’s connections, will make a good match. My sons have succeeded; the Viscount was public prosecutor at Limoges and is now President of the Court at Orléans; the younger is public prosecutor in Paris. My children have their own cares, their own anxieties. If one of those hearts had been devoted to me, if one had tried to fill the void I have here”—he struck his breast—“that one would have failed in life, sacrificing it to me. And why should he? To bring sunshine into my few remaining years—and would he have succeeded? Might I not have accepted such generosity as a debt? But, doctor,” he added with bitter irony, “it is not for nothing that we teach them arithmetic and how to count. At this moment perhaps they are waiting for my money.”“O Monsieur le Comte, how could such an idea enter your head—you who are kind, friendly, humane! If I were not myself a living proof of the benevolence you exercise so liberally—”“To please myself,” Granville replied. “I pay for a sensation, as I would pay a pile of gold to recover the most childish illusion that would make my heart glow. I help my fellow‑creatures for my own sake, just as I gamble; I look for gratitude from none. I should see you die without blinking; and I beg of you to feel the same with regard to me. The events of life have swept over my heart like the lavas of Vesuvius over Herculaneum. The town is there—dead.”“Those who have brought a soul as warm and living as yours to such a pitch of indifference are indeed guilty!” Bianchon exclaimed.“Say no more,” Granville said, shuddering with aversion.“You have a malady which you ought to allow me to treat,” Bianchon insisted.“What, do you know of a cure for death?” Granville cried irritably.“I undertake, Monsieur le Comte, to revive the heart you believe to be frozen.”“Are you a match for Talma, then?” Granville asked satirically.“No, Monsieur le Comte. But Nature is as far above Talma as Talma is superior to me. Listen: the garret you are interested in is inhabited by a woman of about thirty, and her love is carried to fanaticism. The object of her adoration is a young man of pleasing appearance but endowed with every conceivable vice. He is a gambler, addicted to wine and women, and has committed acts deserving legal punishment. To him this unhappy woman sacrificed a life of ease, a man who worshiped her, and the father of her children.”“What is wrong, Monsieur le Comte?” Granville asked.“Nothing. Go on,” he replied.“She has allowed him to squander a perfect fortune; she would give him the world if she had it; she works night and day; and many times she has seen the wretch rob her even of the money saved for her children’s clothes and food. Only three days ago she sold her hair—the finest I ever saw; he came in, and she could not hide the gold quickly enough. For a smile, for a kiss, she gave up the price of a fortnight’s life and peace. Is it not dreadful, and yet sublime? But work is wasting her; her cheeks are hollow. Her children’s crying has broken her heart; she is ill, and at this moment on her wretched bed. This evening they had nothing to eat; the children had not strength to cry; they were silent when I went up.”Bianchon stood still; Granville, despite himself, put his hand into his waistcoat pocket.“I can guess, my young friend, how it is that she is yet alive if you attend her,” Granville said.“O poor soul!” Bianchon cried. “Who could refuse to help her? I only wish I were richer, for I hope to cure her of her passion.”“But how can you expect me to pity a form of misery of which the joys to me would seem cheaply purchased with my whole fortune!” Granville exclaimed, withdrawing his hand empty of the notes Bianchon had assumed he was feeling for. “That woman feels; she is alive! Would not Louis XV have given his kingdom to rise from the grave and have three days of youth and life? And is not that the history of thousands of dead men, thousands of sick men, thousands of old men?”“Poor Caroline!” Bianchon lamented.At the name Granville shuddered and gripped the doctor’s arm.“Her name is Caroline Crochard?” he asked, voice breaking.“Then you know her?” Bianchon replied, astonished.“And the wretch’s name is Solvet. Ay, you have kept your word!” Granville exclaimed. “You have roused my heart to the most terrible pain it can suffer until it is dust. That emotion, too, is a gift from hell, and I always know how to pay those debts.”They had reached the corner of the Rue de la Chaussée‑d’Antin. A night scavenger with a shriveled face stood at the curb.“Do you ever pick up a thousand‑franc note?” Granville asked.“Now and then, master,” the scavenger replied.“And you restore them?”“It depends on the reward offered.”“You're the man for me,” Granville said, handing him a thousand francs. “Take this, but remember, I give it to you on condition of your spending it at the wineshop, getting drunk, fighting, beating your wife, blacking your friends’ eyes. That will give work to the watch, the surgeon, the druggist—perhaps to the police, the public prosecutor, the judge, and the prison warders. Do not try to do anything else, or the devil will be revenged on you sooner or later.”Granville pointed out the scavenger’s grotesque face to Bianchon, who stood stupefied. “Now I have squared accounts with hell and had some pleasure for my money,” he said. “As for Caroline Crochard—she may die of hunger and thirst, hearing the heartrending cries of her starving children, convinced of the baseness of the man she loves. I will not give a sou to rescue her; and because you have helped her, I will see you no more—”He left Bianchon standing like a statue and walked briskly to the Rue Saint‑Lazare, where a carriage waited at his door.“Monsieur, your son, the attorney‑general, came about an hour ago,” the servant said. “He is waiting for you in your bedroom.”Granville dismissed the servant and entered. “What motive can be strong enough to make you infringe the order I have given my children never to come to me unless I send for them?” he asked his son.“Father,” the younger man replied tremulously, “I hope you will forgive me when you have heard me.”“Your reply is proper,” Granville said. “Sit down.” Then, after a pause: “But whether I walk up and down or take a seat, speak without heeding me.”“Father,” the son continued, “this afternoon, at four o’clock, a very young man who was arrested in the house of a friend of mine, whom he had robbed, appealed to you. He says he is your son.”“His name?” Granville asked hoarsely.“Charles Crochard.”“That will do,” Granville said, waving his hand imperiously.He paced in silence; his son waited.“My son,” he began, voice suddenly mild and fatherly—startling the young lawyer—“Charles Crochard spoke the truth. I am glad you came to me tonight, my good Eugène.” He handed him a bundle of banknotes. “Here is a considerable sum of money; you can make any use of it in this matter. I trust you implicitly and approve whatever arrangements you may make, either now or in the future. Eugène, my dear son, kiss me. We part perhaps for the last time. I shall tomorrow crave my dismissal from the King, and I am going to Italy.“Though a father owes no account of his life to his children, he is bound to bequeath to them the experience Fate sells him dearly—is it not part of their inheritance? When you marry,” he continued, involuntarily shuddering, “do not undertake it lightly; that act is the most important society requires of us. Remember to study the character of the woman who is to be your partner; consult me too, I will judge of her myself. A lack of union between husband and wife, from whatever cause, leads to terrible misfortune; sooner or later we are always punished for contravening the social law. But I will write to you on this subject from Florence. A father who presides over a supreme court of justice must not have to blush in the presence of his son. Good‑bye.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The incident in this sketch took place in late November 1809, at the height of Napoleon’s empire. The echoes of the Battle of Wagram still lingered in Austria as peace with the Coalition was being negotiated. Kings and princes orbited Napoleon, who reveled in his power and paraded Parisian entertainments around his marriage to an Austrian archduchess. The opulence of the French aristocracy—diamonds and gold on women and uniforms—contrasted sharply with the Republic’s earlier austerity. Military leaders indulged in wealth gained through the sacrifices of countless soldiers.Many women adopted a carefree, Louis XV–style attitude. Whether influenced by the fallen monarchy or the Imperial family, men and women embraced a hedonistic lifestyle that seemed to foreshadow doom. The craze for military men, encouraged by the Emperor’s frequent calls to arms, made every peace treaty feel temporary. Women’s affections shifted fast; many moved from mistress to widow within weeks.The appeal of soldiers sprang from prospects of early widowhood, the lure of historical legacy, and the romanticized notion of courage. Women were attracted by the idea that their passions could remain secret, buried on the battlefield. This fascination produced a culture where bravery and spectacle were prized: diamonds, fireworks, and jewels—worn ostentatiously by men following examples like Murat—dominated fashion.Comte de Gondreville, formerly Citizen Malin, had risen to prominence and postponed a grand entertainment to outshine other courtiers and impress Napoleon. His drawing rooms brimmed with ambassadors, influential figures, and a few princes, all expecting the Emperor. A private dispute between Napoleon and Josephine—hinting at divorce—kept him from the festivities, though the guests remained unaware.The most beautiful women in Paris flocked to Gondreville’s event, embodying luxury and elegance. The financial elite, proud of their fortunes, sought to rival the military’s splendor, using grand balls to introduce daughters to Napoleon’s inner circle and hoping to convert wealth into favor. Women confident in their beauty attended to test their allure, their true intentions masked by smiles. Amid the gaiety, many trusted friends less than rivals, since the pursuit of wealth and power underpinned social life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################These remarks are necessary to explain the incidents surrounding the little imbroglio that is the subject of this study, reflecting the tone prevalent in Paris drawing-rooms at the time.“Look toward the pedestal supporting that candelabrum—do you see the young lady with her hair a la Chinoise? She’s in the corner to the left, with bluebells in her chestnut curls. She’s so pale you might think she’s ill, delicate-looking, and very small. Now she’s turning her head; her almond-shaped blue eyes seem made for tears. She’s leaning forward to see Madame de Vaudremont below the crowd.”“I see her now. You only had to mention her lovely complexion; I would have recognized her. She has the whitest skin here, but she’s either a prude or a coquette, as her bodice barely hints at her beauty. What shoulders! What lily-whiteness!”“Who is she?” asked the first speaker.“I don’t know.”“An aristocrat! Do you want to keep them all to yourself, Montcornet?”“You of all people to tease me!” replied Montcornet with a smile. “Do you think you can insult a poor general like me just because you’re a rival of Soulanges? Or is it because I’ve only just arrived in this Promised Land? Come, Monsieur le Maitre des Requetes, let us glean in the field you can only hold precariously. We have a right to live! If you knew the German women, you might help me with the Parisian you love best.”“Well, General, since you’ve noticed that lady, have you seen her dance?”“Where have you been? There’s a triple rank of the most undaunted coquettes of Paris between her and the swarm of dancing men. It’s a miracle anyone could spot her in that corner, buried in gloom despite the candles. Between her and us is a sparkle of diamonds and glances, so many floating plumes and fluttering lace that it’s impossible for any dancer to detect her among those stars. How could you think she’s the wife of some sous-préfet from Lippe or Dyle?”“Oh, he will be!” exclaimed the Master of Appeals.“I doubt it,” replied the Colonel of Cuirassiers, laughing. “She seems as raw in intrigue as you are in diplomacy. I bet you don’t know how she got there.”The lawyer looked at the Colonel with a mix of contempt and curiosity.“Well,” Montcornet continued, “she probably arrived punctually at nine, the first of the company, and embarrassed the Comtesse de Gondreville, who can’t think straight. Repulsed by the mistress of the house and driven into the corner by jealous ladies, she became a victim of their coalition against her. Each of those sweet faces would have conspired to keep the men from taking her out. That’s how those tender-looking women would have formed a coalition against the stranger without saying a word beyond, ‘Do you know that little woman in blue?’ If you care to run the gauntlet of flattering glances and inviting questions, try to get through the rampart defending that Queen of Dyle, Lippe, or Charente. You’ll see how even the dullest woman will invent a wile to keep the plaintive stranger in the shadows. Doesn’t she look like an elegy?”“Do you think so, Montcornet? Then she must be married?”“Why not a widow?”“She would be less passive,” said the lawyer, laughing.“She might be the widow of a gambler,” replied the handsome Colonel.“Of course; since the peace, there are many widows of that sort!” said Martial. “But my dear Montcornet, we are simpletons. That face is too innocent; there’s too much youth and freshness for her to be married. What splendid flesh-tints! Nothing has sunk in the modeling of her nose. Her lips and chin are as fresh as a white rosebud, though her expression is veiled by clouds of sadness. Who could make that young creature weep?”“Women cry for little,” said the Colonel.“I don’t know,” replied Martial; “but she’s not crying because she’s left without a partner; her grief is not new. It’s clear she beautified herself for this evening with intention. I would wager she’s in love already.”“Bah! She’s perhaps the daughter of some German princeling; no one talks to her,” said Montcornet.“Dear! How unhappy a poor child may be! Can there be anything more graceful and refined than our little stranger? Yet not one of those furies around her, who think they can feel, will say a word to her. If she would only speak, we’d see if she has fine teeth.”“Bless me, you boil over like milk at the least increase of temperature!” cried the Colonel, a bit nettled at finding a rival in his friend.“What!” exclaimed the lawyer, ignoring the Colonel’s question. “Can nobody here tell us the name of this exotic flower?”“Some lady companion!” said Montcornet.“A companion! Wearing sapphires fit for a queen and a dress of Malines lace? Tell that to the marines, General. You wouldn’t shine in diplomacy if you jump from a German princess to a lady companion.”Montcornet stopped a man by taking his arm—a fat little man with iron-gray hair and clever eyes, mingling unceremoniously with various groups.“Gondreville, my friend,” said Montcornet, “who is that charming little woman sitting under that huge candelabrum?”“The candelabrum? Ravrio’s work; Isabey made the design.”“Oh, I recognized your lavishness and taste; but the lady?”“Ah! I don’t know. Some friend of my wife’s, no doubt.”“Or your mistress, you old rascal.”“No, on my honor. The Comtesse de Gondreville is the only one capable of inviting people no one knows.”Despite this acerbic comment, the fat little man’s lips retained a smile from the Colonel’s suggestion. Montcornet returned to the lawyer, who had rejoined a neighboring group, intent on asking, but in vain, for information about the fair unknown. He grasped Martial’s arm and said in his ear:“Mind what you’re about. Madame de Vaudremont has been watching you for some time with ominous attentiveness; she can guess by the movement of your lips what you say to me. Our eyes have already told her too much; she’s thinking even more than we are about the little blue lady.”“That’s too old a trick in warfare, my dear Montcornet! However, what do I care? Like the Emperor, when I make a conquest, I keep it.”“Martial, your fatuity cries out for a lesson. You, a civilian, and so lucky as to be the husband-designate of Madame de Vaudremont, a widow of two-and-twenty with four thousand napoleons a year—who slips such a diamond on your finger,” he added, taking the lawyer’s left hand, which the young man complacently allowed; “and you affect the Lovelace, as if you were a colonel obliged to maintain the military reputation! Fie! Just think of all you may lose.”“At any rate, I shall not lose my liberty,” replied Martial with a forced laugh.He cast a passionate glance at Madame de Vaudremont, who responded only with a smile of unease, having seen the Colonel examining the lawyer’s ring.“Listen to me, Martial. If you flutter around my young stranger, I shall set to work to win Madame de Vaudremont.”“You have my full permission, my dear Cuirassier, but you will not gain this much,” and the young Maitre des Requetes put his polished thumb-nail under an upper tooth with a mocking click.“Remember that I am unmarried,” said the Colonel; “that my sword is my whole fortune; and that such a challenge is setting Tantalus down to a banquet he cannot devour.”“Prrr.”This defiant roll of consonants was the only reply to the Colonel’s declaration, as Martial looked him over before turning away.The fashion of the time required men to wear white kerseymere breeches and silk stockings at a ball. This costume showcased Montcornet’s fine shape. At thirty-five, he attracted attention with his stalwart height, emphasized by the handsome uniform of the Cuirassiers of the Imperial Guard. A black moustache highlighted his soldierly countenance, with a broad forehead, aquiline nose, and bright red lips. Montcornet’s manner, marked by a certain superiority from his command experience, might please a woman sensible enough not to aim at making a slave of her husband. The Colonel smiled as he looked at the lawyer, one of his favorite college friends, whose small figure made it necessary for Montcornet to look down slightly as he answered his raillery with a friendly glance.Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon was a young Provencal favored by Napoleon; his fate might lead to a splendid embassy. He had won the Emperor with his Italian suppleness, a talent for intrigue, and a knowledge of manners, which are good substitutes for the higher qualities of a sterling man. Though young and eager, his face had acquired the rigid brilliancy of tinned iron, a characteristic of diplomatists that allows them to conceal emotions. The heart of a diplomat may be an insoluble problem, as the most illustrious ambassadors of the time were distinguished by enduring hatreds and romantic attachments.Martial, however, was one of those men capable of reckoning on the future amid their enjoyment; he had learned to judge the world and hid his ambition under the guise of a lady-killer, cloaking his talent under the commonplace of mediocrity as he observed the rapid advancement of those who gave the master little umbrage.The two friends parted with a cordial handshake. The introductory tune, signaling the ladies to form squares for a fresh quadrille, cleared the men from the space they had filled while talking in the large drawing-room of Gondreville’s mansion. Their whispered conversation had taken place during the usual interval between dances. The chandeliers and flambeaux on the chimney-shelf illuminated their faces, failing to conceal the faint expressions of their feelings from the keen-sighted countess or the artless stranger. This espionage of thoughts is perhaps one of the pleasures idle people find in society, while many disappointed individuals are bored without daring to admit it.To fully appreciate the interest of this conversation, it’s necessary to relate an incident that would serve as an invisible bond, drawing together the actors in this little drama, currently scattered through the rooms.At about eleven o’clock, just as the dancers were returning to their seats, the company observed the entrance of the handsomest woman in Paris, the queen of fashion, the only person missing from the brilliant assembly. She made it a rule never to appear until a party reached a pitch of excitement that doesn’t allow women to preserve the freshness of their faces or dresses. This brief hour is the springtime of a ball. An hour later, when pleasure falls flat and fatigue sets in, everything is spoiled. Madame de Vaudremont never committed the blunder of remaining at a party to be seen with drooping flowers, hair out of curl, tumbled frills, and a face courting sleep—not always without success. She took care not to let her beauty be seen drowsy, as her rivals did; she cleverly maintained her reputation for smartness by always leaving a ballroom in brilliant order, as she had entered it. Women whispered with envy that she planned and wore as many different dresses as parties she attended in one evening.On this occasion, Madame de Vaudremont was not destined to leave when she wished. Pausing on the threshold, she shot swift, observant glances at the women present, hastily scrutinizing their dresses to assure herself that her own eclipsed them all.The illustrious beauty presented herself to the admiration of the crowd at the same moment as one of the bravest colonels of the Guards’ Artillery and the Emperor’s favorite, the Comte de Soulanges. The transient association of these two had an air of mystery. Upon hearing the names announced, a few women sitting by the wall rose, and men hurried in from side-rooms, pressing forward to the principal doorway. One of the jesters in the assembly remarked that “women have as much curiosity about seeing a man faithful to his passion as men do in studying a woman difficult to enthrall.”Though the Comte de Soulanges, a young man of about thirty-two, possessed a nervous temperament that gives rise to fine qualities, his slender build and pale complexion were not initially attractive. His black eyes revealed great vivacity, but he was taciturn in company, and nothing in his appearance hinted at the oratory that would later distinguish him in the legislative assembly under the Restoration.The Comtesse de Vaudremont, a tall woman, somewhat plump, with dazzlingly white skin and a small head carried well, inspired love through her gracious manner. She was one of those beings who keep all the promise of their beauty.The pair, for a few minutes, became the center of general observation, but did not long give curiosity a chance to exercise itself. The Colonel and the Countess seemed to understand that accident had placed them in an awkward position. Martial, as they approached, hastened to join a group of men by the fireplace, watching Madame de Vaudremont with the jealous anxiety of a first flame of passion from behind a barrier of heads. A secret voice warned him that the success he prided himself on might be precarious. However, the coldly polite smile with which the Countess thanked Monsieur de Soulanges and her little bow of dismissal as she sat down by Madame de Gondreville relaxed the rigidity of his face, which jealousy had made tense. Seeing Soulanges still standing near the sofa where Madame de Vaudremont sat, apparently unaware of the glance that conveyed to him that they were both playing a ridiculous part, the volcanic Provencal knitted his brows and smoothed his chestnut curls to maintain his composure, watching the faces of the Countess and M. de Soulanges while still chatting with his neighbors. He then took the hand of Colonel Montcornet, who had just renewed their acquaintance, but he listened without hearing him; his mind was elsewhere.Soulanges gazed calmly at the women, sitting four ranks deep around the immense ballroom, admiring the display of diamonds, rubies, gold, and shining hair, which almost outshone the blaze of waxlights, the cut glass of the chandeliers, and the gilding. His rival’s indifference unsettled the lawyer. Unable to control his impatience, Martial approached Madame de Vaudremont with a bow. Upon seeing the Provencal, Soulanges cast him a covert glance and impertinently turned away his head. A solemn silence reigned in the room, where curiosity peaked. Eager faces wore strange mixed expressions; everyone anticipated an outbreak that men of breeding carefully avoid. Suddenly, the Count’s pale face turned as red as the scarlet facings of his coat, and he fixed his gaze on the floor to hide his agitation. Spotting the unknown lady humbly seated by the pedestal of the candelabrum, he moved away with a melancholy air, passing in front of the lawyer, and took refuge in one of the cardrooms. Martial and the company thought Soulanges had publicly surrendered his position out of fear of the ridicule that follows a discarded lover. The lawyer proudly raised his head and looked at the strange lady; then, as he took his seat near Madame de Vaudremont, he listened so inattentively that he missed her words spoken behind her fan:“Martial, please don’t wear that ring you took from me. I have my reasons and will explain them when we leave. You must give me your arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s.”“Why did you come in with the Colonel?” asked the Baron.“I met him in the hall,” she replied. “But leave me now; everybody is looking at us.”Martial returned to the Colonel of Cuirassiers. It was then that the little blue lady became the object of curiosity that agitated the Colonel, Soulanges, Martial, and Madame de Vaudremont.When the friends parted after their challenge, the Baron flew to Madame de Vaudremont and led her to a place in the most brilliant quadrille. Favored by the intoxication dancing produces in a woman and the turmoil of a ball, where men appear in all the trickery of dress, which enhances their attractions as much as it does women’s, Martial thought he might yield to the charm that drew his gaze to the fair stranger. Though he managed to hide his first glances towards the lady in blue from the Countess’s watchful eyes, he was soon caught in the act; and while he excused himself once for his absent-mindedness, he could not justify the unseemly silence that followed the most insinuating question a woman can ask:“Do you like me very much this evening?”The more dreamy he became, the more the Countess pressed and teased him.While Martial danced, the Colonel moved from group to group, seeking information about the unknown lady. After exhausting the good humor of even the most indifferent, he resolved to take advantage of a moment when the Comtesse de Gondreville seemed at liberty to ask her the name of the mysterious lady. He noticed a little space cleared between the pedestal of the candelabrum and the two sofas, which ended in that corner. The dance had left several chairs vacant, forming rows of fortifications held by mothers or middle-aged women; the Colonel seized the opportunity to make his way through this palisade of shawls and wraps. He began by making himself agreeable to the dowagers, and from one to another, through compliments, he finally reached the empty space next to the stranger. At the risk of catching on to the gryphons and chimeras of the huge candelabrum, he stood there, braving the glare and dripping wax, to Martial’s annoyance.The Colonel, too tactful to speak suddenly to the little blue lady on his right, began by addressing a plain woman seated on the left:“This is a splendid ball, madame! What luxury! What life! Every woman here is pretty! You’re not dancing—because you don’t care for it, no doubt.”This vapid conversation aimed to induce his right-hand neighbor to speak; however, she, silent and absent-minded, paid no attention. The officer had prepared several phrases leading to: “And you, madame?”—a question from which he hoped for great things. But he was surprised to see tears in the strange lady’s eyes, which seemed wholly absorbed in gazing at Madame de Vaudremont.“You are married, no doubt, madame?” he asked hesitantly.“Yes, monsieur,” replied the lady.“And your husband is here, of course?”“Yes, monsieur.”“And why, madame, do you remain in this spot? Is it to attract attention?”The mournful lady smiled sadly.“Allow me the honor, madame, of being your partner in the next quadrille, and I will ensure not to bring you back here. When so many are ready to ascend the throne, and Royalty is the mania of the day, I cannot imagine you will refuse the title of Queen of the Ball that your beauty may claim.”“I do not intend to dance, monsieur.”The curt tone of her replies was so discouraging that the Colonel found himself compelled to withdraw. Martial, guessing what the officer’s last request had been and the refusal he had met with, began to smile, stroking his chin and making the diamond on his finger sparkle.“What are you laughing at?” asked the Comtesse de Vaudremont.“At the poor Colonel’s failure, who just put his foot in it----”“I begged you to take your ring off,” said the Countess, interrupting him.“I did not hear you.”“If you can hear nothing this evening, at least you see everything, Monsieur le Baron,” said Madame de Vaudremont, vexed.“That young man is displaying a very fine diamond,” the stranger remarked to the Colonel.“Splendid,” he replied. “The man is the Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of my most intimate friends.”“I thank you for telling me his name,” she said; “he seems an agreeable man.”“Yes, but he is rather fickle.”“He seems to be on the best terms with the Comtesse de Vaudremont?” asked the lady, looking inquiringly at the Colonel.“On the very best.”The unknown turned pale.“Hallo!” thought the soldier, “she is in love with that lucky devil Martial.”“I fancied Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to M. de Soulanges,” said the lady, recovering from the suppressed grief that had clouded her beauty.“For a week past, the Countess has been faithless,” replied the Colonel. “But you must have seen poor Soulanges when he came in; he’s still trying to disbelieve in his disaster.”“Yes, I saw him,” said the lady. Then she added, “Thank you very much, monsieur,” in a tone that signified dismissal.At that moment, the quadrille was coming to an end. Montcornet had only time to withdraw, telling himself, “She is married.”“Well, valiant Cuirassier,” exclaimed the Baron, drawing the Colonel aside into a window-bay to breathe the fresh air from the garden, “how are you getting on?”“She is a married woman, my dear fellow.”“What does that matter?”“Oh, deuce take it! I’m a decent sort of man,” replied the Colonel. “I have no intention of courting a woman I cannot marry. Besides, Martial, she expressly told me she did not intend to dance.”“Colonel, I will bet a hundred napoleons to your gray horse that she will dance with me this evening.”“Done!” said the Colonel, putting his hand in the coxcomb’s. “Meanwhile, I’m going to look for Soulanges; he might know the lady, as she seems interested in him.”“You’ve lost, my good fellow,” cried Martial, laughing. “My eyes have met hers, and I know what they mean. My dear friend, you owe me no grudge for dancing with her after she has refused you?”“No, no. Those who laugh last, laugh longest. But I’m an honest gambler and a generous enemy, Martial, and I warn you, she is fond of diamonds.”With these words, the friends parted; General Montcornet made his way to the cardroom, where he saw the Comte de Soulanges sitting at a bouillotte table. Though there was no friendship between the two soldiers beyond the superficial comradeship from the perils of war and service, the Colonel of Cuirassiers was alarmed to see the Colonel of Artillery, known to be prudent, playing a game that could lead to ruin. The heaps of gold and notes piled on the fateful cards showed the frenzy of play. A circle of silent men stood around the players at the table. Occasionally, a few words were spoken—pass, play, I stop, a thousand Louis, taken—but the five motionless men seemed to converse only with their eyes. As the Colonel, alarmed by Soulanges’ pallor, approached him, the Count was winning. Field-Marshal the Duc d’Isemberg, Keller, and a famous banker rose from the table, completely cleaned out of considerable sums. Soulanges looked gloomier than ever as he swept up a quantity of gold and notes; he didn’t even count it; his lips curled with bitter scorn, defying fortune rather than being grateful for her favors.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Courage,” said the Colonel. “Courage, Soulanges!” He thought he was helping by pulling him from the gaming table. “Come with me. I have good news, but on one condition.”“What is that?” asked Soulanges.“That you will answer a question.”The Comte de Soulanges rose abruptly, gathering his winnings into his handkerchief; his expression was so fierce no one opposed him; the players seemed relieved to see his morose face leave the table.“Those soldiers are always as thick as thieves at a fair!” remarked a diplomat who took Soulanges’ place. A tired face turned to the newcomer and said, “When we say military men, we do not mean civil, Monsieur le Ministre.”“My dear fellow,” Montcornet said to Soulanges, leading him aside, “the Emperor praised you this morning, and your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”“The Master does not love the Artillery.”“No, but he adores the nobility, and you are an aristocrat. He said that men who married in Paris during the campaign are not in disgrace. Well?”Soulanges looked stunned.“And now I hope you will tell me if you know a charming woman sitting under a huge candelabrum----”At this the Count’s face brightened; he seized the Colonel’s hand. “My dear General,” he said, voice altered, “if anyone else had asked me that, I would have cracked his skull with this gold. Leave me, I beg you. I feel more like blowing out my brains tonight than— I hate everything I see. I am going. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”“My poor friend!” Montcornet replied gently, squeezing his hand. “You are too vehement. What would you say if I told you that Martial thinks so little of Madame de Vaudremont that he is quite smitten with that little lady?”“If he speaks to her,” Soulanges shouted, trembling, “I will thrash him flat, even if he’s in the Emperor’s lap!”He sank into an easy chair; Montcornet left slowly, realizing Soulanges was too furious for consolation.Back in the ballroom Montcornet first noticed Madame de Vaudremont, agitated. He sat beside her.“I wager something has vexed you?” he said.“A mere trifle, General. I want to leave for a ball at the Grand Duchess of Berg’s, but first I must stop at the Princesse de Wagram’s. Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon amuses himself by flirting with the dowagers.”“That’s not the whole secret. I bet a hundred louis you will stay here the whole evening.”“Impertinent man!”“Then I’ve hit the truth?”“Well, tell me, what am I thinking?” she asked, tapping his fingers with her fan. “I might reward you if you guess right.”“I won’t accept the challenge; I have too much advantage.”“You are presumptuous.”“You are afraid of seeing Martial at the feet—”“Of whom?” she exclaimed, feigning surprise.“Of that candelabrum,” he replied, glancing at the fair stranger and scrutinizing the Countess.“You have guessed it,” she said, hiding her face. “Old Madame de Lansac, as malicious as an old monkey, told me that Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is flirting with that stranger, who sits there like a skeleton at a feast. I would rather see a death’s head than that face, so cruelly beautiful and pale. She is my evil genius. Madame de Lansac, who only goes to a ball to watch everything while pretending to sleep, has made me miserable. Martial shall pay dearly. Urge him, since he is your friend, not to make me unhappy.”“I just left a man who promises to blow his brains out if he speaks to that lady. And he is a man, madame, to keep his word. But I know Martial; such threats encourage him. Besides, we have wagered—” The Colonel lowered his voice.“Can it be true?” asked the Countess.“On my word of honor.”“Thank you, my dear Colonel,” Madame de Vaudremont replied, her glance inviting. “Will you do me the honor of dancing with me?”“Yes; but the next quadrille. During this one, I want to see what comes of this little intrigue and find out who the little blue lady is; she looks intelligent.”Understanding Madame de Vaudremont wished to be alone for the moment, the Colonel retired, pleased at the start he had made.At entertainments like this, women such as Madame de Lansac gather like old sailors watching younger mariners struggle. She noticed the Countess’s agitation at once. The younger woman might fan herself, smile at young men, and use all arts to hide emotion, but the Duchess—clear-sighted and mischievous—read her heart. Madame de Vaudremont felt real sorrow beneath the mask: she had believed Martial a man who could adorn her life with power, but now she knew her passion was likely mistaken, cutting her reputation and self-esteem. Her sudden love for Martial, though recent, caused acute pain, like losing a part of herself.The old Duchess, eager to intervene, dismissed her Ambassador; a lover’s quarrel outshone every other interest. She shot a sardonic glance at the Countess that made the younger woman fear her fate in the dowager’s hands. There are looks between women that are like torches at a climax; none unfamiliar with the Duchess could miss the terror her expression inspired.Madame de Lansac was tall and, despite rouge and jewelry that hid age, displayed a face known for epigrams. Her polite manners softened her mischievous mind and kept her from outright malice. A triumphant glance and a smile implying “I promised you as much!” crossed the room, bringing a blush of hope to the pale cheeks of the stranger under the chandelier. The alliance between Madame de Lansac and the stranger did not escape Madame de Vaudremont, who sensed a mystery and aimed to uncover it.The Baron de la Roche-Hugon, having questioned the dowagers without learning the blue lady’s name, turned in despair to the Comtesse de Gondreville, who said only, “A lady whom the ‘ancient’ Duchesse de Lansac introduced to me.”By chance the Baron caught the Duchess’s knowing look toward the stranger. Despite their poor relations, he decided to speak. The Duchess, seeing the jaunty Baron approaching, smiled with sardonic irony and looked at Madame de Vaudremont, prompting Montcornet to laugh.“If the old witch pretends to be friendly,” thought the Baron, “she intends some spiteful trick.” “Madame,” he said, “you have, I am told, undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”“Do you take me for a dragon?” said the old lady. “But of whom are you speaking?” she added sweetly, reviving Martial’s hopes.“Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”“Yes,” said the Duchess. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time ago, a woman of good birth, whom you men do not know; she goes nowhere.”“Why does she not dance? She is such a pretty creature. May we conclude a treaty of peace? If you tell me all, I promise to urge a petition for restitution of the woods of Navarreins by the Commissioners of Crown Lands on the Emperor.”The Baron’s promise was trickery: the younger branch of Navarreins bears the arms of Lansac, and the Duchess’s liaison with Louis XV had once earned her husband a title. Asking to petition for the elder branch’s estate was a specious bribe.“Monsieur,” the old woman said with deceptive gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont to me. I promise to reveal the mystery of the interesting unknown. You see, every man in the room shares your curiosity. All eyes are turned toward the corner where my protégée has modestly placed herself; she is reaping homage the women meant to deny her. Happy the man she chooses for partner!” She fixed her eyes on Madame de Vaudremont with a look that read, “We are talking of you.” “I imagine you would rather learn the stranger’s name from the lips of your handsome Countess than from mine.”There was such defiance that Madame de Vaudremont rose, approached, and took the chair Martial placed for her. Without acknowledging him she said, “I can guess, madame, that you are talking of me; but I admit my lack of perspicacity; I do not know whether it is for good or evil.”Madame de Lansac pressed the young woman’s pretty hand in her dry, wrinkled fingers and replied low and compassionately, “Poor child!”The two women regarded one another. Madame de Vaudremont realized Martial was in the way and dismissed him with an imperious “Leave us.”The Baron, displeased to see the Countess under the dangerous sibyl’s spell, gave one of those looks a man can give—potent over a blinded heart but ridiculous in a woman beginning to scrutinize her suitor.“Do you think you can play the Emperor?” she said, giving him an ironical sidelong glance.Martial, a man of the world with wit and prudence, would not risk falling out with a woman favored at Court whom the Emperor wished to marry. He relied on provoking jealousy to uncover her coolness’s secret and withdrew willingly as a new quadrille began.Making room for dancing, the Baron leaned against a marble console, folded his arms, and watched the two ladies. Comparing the Countess with the new beauty—made alluring by mystery—he was seized by the self-interest common to opportunistic seducers, torn between a fortune within reach and indulging his caprice. The light highlighted his anxious, sullen face against white silk, making him seem like an evil genius. Observers likely remarked, “There is another poor wretch who seems to be enjoying himself!”Meanwhile the Colonel, leaning in the doorway between ballroom and cardroom, laughed under his moustache. He enjoyed watching the turmoil: the Countess’s and Martial’s agitation, Soulanges’ gloomy look, and the unknown’s expression of alternating hope and dread. Montcornet stood like the king of the feast, receiving inviting smiles from many women; as a Colonel of the Imperial Guard, he was one of the best matches in the army.It was nearly midnight. Conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, rivalries, and scheming had reached a pitch that made a young man exclaim, “A fine ball!”“My sweet little angel,” Madame de Lansac said to the Countess, “you are now at an age when I made many mistakes. Seeing you endure a thousand deaths, I thought I might give you some charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty spoils your future, as if tearing the gown you must wear. Later we learn to go about in it without crumpling it. Go on, making clever enemies and friends without sense of conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you will lead!”“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do you not think?” the Countess eagerly said.“My child, at your age, you must learn to choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is not wise enough to be a good husband, nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he is the man to squander your fortune; still, that would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do you not see how aged he is? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left. In three years he will be a wreck. Then he will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed. I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, who may have business acumen and gossip agreeably, but he is too presumptuous for real merit; he will not go far. Besides—look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this very moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman, but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he is calculating you as if you were an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position who is halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should not be a trivial love affair. A new alliance should be a good speculation on your part; in marrying again, you ought at least to hope to be addressed as Madame la Maréchale!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As she spoke, both women naturally focused on Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “then you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But please, don’t disturb families or ruin wives’ happiness. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women, dear—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passions. I came here to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why are you looking at me in amazement? If you want to play with men, toy only with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, for instance, whom you’ve turned into a lovesick fool for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married for two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has experienced remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come and see the result of your actions.”The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.“There,” said Madame de Lansac, indicating a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child gives her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”The Countess looked away, lost in sad thoughts.The Duchess led her to the card-room door, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!”The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. Players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amid festivity, struck the Countess with foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not completely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac whispered to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.“Oh! I will talk to him!” she declared to Madame de Lansac.“Do nothing of the sort!” the old lady exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.“Well, madame, do you finally know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess when they were alone.“Yes,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, her expression playful.Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp leading travelers astray. Martial, believing she still loved him, adopted a charming demeanor. “Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?”“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”“But she loves her husband.”“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”“But she is married.”“A whimsical objection!”“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”“You deserve that I should send you to her.”“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning.“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”This thought stirred her pride more than curiosity to unravel the intrigue. In her distraction, she lost control. The Colonel, misreading her embarrassment, became more ardent. Old diplomats, entertained by the unfolding drama, had never witnessed so many simultaneous intrigues. The passions of the two couples were evident in the crowded rooms, reflected in the faces around them, amplifying their own feelings of impotence.Finally, the Baron found a seat beside Madame de Soulanges. He admired her beauty up close, captivated by her fresh neck and graceful figure. At that time, women wore sashes tied under the bosom, a style that flattered those with perfect figures. As he stole glances at the Countess, Martial was enchanted by her perfection.“You haven’t danced once this evening, madame,” he said in soft, flattering tones. “Not for lack of a partner, I presume?”“I never go to parties; I’m quite unknown,” Madame de Soulanges replied coldly, unaware of her aunt’s signal to attract the Baron.Martial, adjusting the diamond on his hand, felt a spark of hope as he caught the Countess’s eye. She blushed and looked at him with an indefinable expression.“Do you enjoy dancing?” he asked to continue the conversation.“Yes, very much, monsieur.”Their eyes met, and the earnestness in her voice stirred something in him. “Then, am I too bold in offering to be your partner for the next quadrille?”Artless confusion colored her cheeks.“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”“Was it that tall cavalry colonel over there?”“Precisely.”“Oh! He’s a friend of mine; don’t worry. Will you grant me the favor I hope for?”“Yes, monsieur.”Her tone revealed a new and deep emotion that touched Martial’s world-weary heart. Overcome with shyness, he lost his confidence, and his southern charm ignited. He tried to speak, but his words felt clumsy compared to Madame de Soulanges’ bright replies. Fortunately, the quadrille was forming, and standing beside her eased his nerves. For many men, dancing is a way to express themselves, believing they can win a woman’s heart through their physical grace. Martial likely intended to showcase all his charms, judging by his pretentious movements and gestures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################He led his conquest to the quadrille, where the most brilliant women prized dancing above all. As the orchestra played, the Baron felt immense pride surveying the ladies forming the square. Madame de Soulanges’ dress rivaled even Madame de Vaudremont’s, who stood opposite him and the lady in blue. All eyes turned to Madame de Soulanges; a flattering murmur showed she was the topic among the men. The admiration and envy made the young woman blush, enhancing her charm. When she raised her eyes, she looked at her partner as if wishing to transfer the glory of admiration to him, showing she valued his opinion above all others. She blended innocence and vanity, displaying the budding affection youthful hearts possess. Though modest and inexperienced in ballroom arts, she knew how to glance at him at just the right moments.When a new figure, invented by a dancer named Trenis, brought Martial face to face with the Colonel, he joked, “I have won your horse.” Montcornet retorted, “Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” Martial dismissed the concern: “What do I care? Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”After the quadrille, whispers circulated. The less attractive women made moral comments about Martial and the Comtesse, while the more beautiful marveled at her apparent ease in surrendering to him. The men were baffled by the Baron’s luck, not finding him particularly charming. A few sympathetic women warned against judging the Countess harshly, suggesting young wives would be ruined if a mere glance or graceful dance could compromise a reputation.Only Martial understood his happiness. During the last figure, as the ladies formed the moulinet, he clasped the Countess’s hand, feeling her response through the thin glove. “Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not return to that corner where you’ve been hiding. Is admiration the only benefit you gain from your jewels? Come and enjoy the scene and yourself.”Madame de Soulanges yielded to his charm, and they walked through the crowded rooms. She hesitated at each door, peering in before entering, only reassured when he said, “He is not here.” They reached a grand picture gallery, where a lavish collation awaited three hundred guests. As supper approached, Martial led the Countess to an oval boudoir overlooking the garden, draped with flowers and blue hangings. The noise faded. Initially startled, the Countess resisted following him but eventually sat on an ottoman after glancing in a mirror, perhaps reassured they could be seen.“This room is charming,” she remarked, admiring the decor. “All here is love and delight!” Martial, deeply moved, noticed her face in the dim light: a mix of unease, modesty, and eagerness that captivated him. She smiled, which seemed to quell her internal struggle. Flirtatiously she took his left hand and removed the ring he wore, exclaiming, “What a fine diamond!”Martial, intoxicated by her touch, looked at her with eyes as bright as the gem. “Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—” He trailed off, captivated by her rapture.
