She asked, “You give it to me?” with surprise. “I wish I had the whole world to offer you!” he replied. “Will you accept only my diamond?” She pressed, “You will never take it back?” “Never,” he assured her.She placed the ring on her finger, and as Martial prepared to embrace her, she suddenly stood and said clearly, “I accept the diamond, monsieur, with less scruple because it belongs to me.” The Baron was taken aback. “Monsieur de Soulanges took it from my dressing-table and told me he had lost it.”“You are mistaken, madame,” Martial said, irritated. “It was given to me by Madame de Vaudremont.” “Precisely,” she replied with a smile. “My husband borrowed it, gave it to her, and she gifted it to you. This ring has made a little journey, that is all. It may reveal secrets to me.” She touched a spring in the ring, revealing a lock of M. de Soulanges’ hair, then fled into the crowded rooms, leaving Martial confounded. He noticed the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont laughing nearby, and the Colonel teased, “Will you have my horse to chase after your prize?” Martial took their banter in stride, grateful for their silence regarding the evening’s events, as he exchanged his horse for a young wife.As the Comtesse de Soulanges drove across Paris to her home in Faubourg Saint-Germain, she was consumed by anxiety. Before leaving the Hotel Gondreville, she searched for her aunt and husband, who had departed without her. Disturbing suspicions plagued her mind. A silent witness to her husband’s torment since Madame de Vaudremont had ensnared him, she had hoped for his repentance. Now she feared she had made a grave mistake by agreeing to her aunt’s scheme.The evening darkened her innocent spirit. Initially alarmed by her husband’s suffering, she grew more distressed upon seeing her rival’s beauty, feeling the weight of societal corruption. As she crossed the Pont Royal, she discarded the hair from the diamond, a token of pure affection. Tears flowed as she recalled her long-standing grief, and she shuddered at the thought that a woman seeking peace must bury her pain without complaint.“Alas!” she pondered, “What can women do when they do not love? What sustains their devotion? I cannot believe, as my aunt claims, that reason alone is enough.”Still sighing, she arrived home and rushed upstairs, startled to find her husband by the fire. “How long has it been since you attended balls without telling me?” he asked, his voice strained. “You know a woman is out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself by hiding in the dark corner.”“Oh, my dear Leon,” she coaxed, “I couldn’t resist the joy of seeing you without your knowing. My aunt took me to the ball, and I was very happy there!”Her words softened the Count’s stern demeanor, for he had been dreading her return, fearing she would learn of his infidelity. In a bid to deflect her anger, he had tried to be the first to criticize her. Delighted to see him smile and grateful for his presence in a room he had frequented less often, the Countess looked at him tenderly, blushing and lowering her eyes. Her kindness further enchanted Soulanges, especially after the misery he had endured at the ball. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully; gratitude often accompanies love.“Hortense, what is that on your finger that hurt my lip?” he asked, laughing. “It’s my diamond, which you said you lost, and which I have found.”General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding, as she became one of the victims of the tragic fire that marred the ball hosted by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In September 1835 Mademoiselle du Rouvre, only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. Though the Marquis had squandered much of his fortune, Clémentine was a substantial heiress through her mother’s family; settlements by relatives ensured her a steady annual income on marriage.Comte Adam, an exile and not a charge on the French government, came from an illustrious Polish family but faced prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was poorly regarded. At first he was seen as a student enjoying Paris after losing political prospects. The Polish émigré community in France was divided between republican and noble exiles, and Adam became the butt of Parisian humor, with some questioning his wealth and status.In 1834 Adam bought a house in the rue de la Pépinière, and by 1836 he was established in Parisian society, having fallen in love with Clémentine. Their marriage elevated his standing; he was soon recognized as fashionable. The couple’s home was lavish, blending various architectural styles and filled with exquisite decor that reflected the tastes and wealth of the new bourgeoisie.Clémentine, now a countess, began to notice Captain Paz, a factotum who managed their affairs yet remained largely invisible. She questioned Adam about Paz, who was a constant presence, handling everything from finances to horse care. Adam explained that Paz came from a poor branch of the Paz family, had fought bravely for Poland, and had become a close friend through shared hardships.Paz’s loyalty and discretion were evident; he ran the household with remarkable efficiency. Adam admired Paz’s character, insisting their friendship rested on mutual respect and understanding. Despite social hierarchies, Adam regarded Paz as an equal; once he offered Paz money, which Paz declined—an example of their proud bond.As the couple prepared for a day out, Clémentine’s affection for both men grew, illustrating themes of loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th-century Paris, where personal ties could defy social norms.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Paz lived a reclusive life, largely unnoticed in Paris until the Comtesse Laginska was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne. Clémentine insisted that Thaddeus join them for dinner; he dressed for the occasion but behaved like a subordinate, listening and answering minimally. Adam urged him to be more lively, frustrated by Thaddeus’s dull demeanor, unaware that Thaddeus was struggling to conceal deep feelings for Clémentine.After dinner they went to the Opera, where Thaddeus remained detached. Clémentine remarked on his lack of engagement; Adam called him disagreeable. Thaddeus’s coldness stemmed from a painful, unrequited love intensified after Clémentine returned from a trip. He took a bittersweet pride in being indispensable to their household, knowing the home would falter without his management, and found both joy and suffering in that role.Clémentine, now more beautiful and secure in her marriage, concentrated on her social standing in Paris and had little space for thoughts of Thaddeus. Still, she once noticed him while driving home and invited him to join them, an invitation that flustered him. Their interactions were charged with unspoken emotion: Thaddeus felt gratitude and longing but kept his distance, preferring silent devotion to overt expression.As days passed, Thaddeus settled back into routine, often overlooked while Clémentine was absorbed by social obligations and rivalry. He found solace in managing their affairs, yet his feelings remained a source of inner turmoil and reflection on the nature of unreciprocated love. When Clémentine held a reception, his absence went unnoticed; he returned to solitary habits, content in serving from afar while privately aching over the dual joy and pain his attachment brought.Clémentine’s Parisian life was a constant contest for attention. Distracted by rivals and the strain of maintaining her status, she often neglected Adam. Thaddeus remained a steadfast presence, quietly overseeing their household while wrestling with his emotions.In May, as Clémentine prepared to leave for her uncle’s estate, she again encountered Thaddeus; their brief exchange revived a curiosity in her. She insisted he join them for dinner. Though hesitant, he complied and attempted to engage more, but shyness and duty constrained him. As the evening progressed, Clémentine began to see him differently; their conversation revealed a deeper connection and Thaddeus felt momentarily emboldened. Still, loyalty to Adam restrained him from pursuing his desires openly.Clémentine voiced dissatisfaction with Adam’s behavior and a yearning for more passion. Thaddeus, torn between love for her and friendship with Adam, recognized the complexity and felt the urge to confess but hesitated. Her insecurity and vulnerability gave him hope, yet he remained cautious; their talk deepened and the silence between them became charged.To deflect the risk of revealing his true feelings, Thaddeus invented a story about being in love with a circus performer named Malaga — a captivated, agile contrast to Parisian women. The ruse allowed him to express longing for something simpler and more genuine without exposing his love for Clémentine. He visited Malaga as an emotional escape, providing for her comfortable living but maintaining no romantic entanglement. Though Malaga’s presence eased his solitude, his heart remained with Clémentine.As Malaga’s circumstances shifted, she began to question Thaddeus’s intentions, causing misunderstandings and tension. Despite this, he continued as her benefactor while grappling with his own conflicted heart. Eventually he received a heartfelt letter from Malaga expressing despair over their separation and longing for his companionship. The letter struck him, echoing the depth and pain of his own unreturned love for Clémentine, and underscored the unwavering devotion that defined his life amid the intertwined demands of love, loyalty, and sacrifice.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner the count and countess discussed Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam worried over Thaddeus’s misfortune, fearing he would lose everything because of his attachment to a circus performer; Clementine dismissed such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus, pointing out the sacrifices he had already made for Malaga.Paz had asked Adam for money to help Malaga, and the countess regarded Paz as a possible ruin for Adam. Adam, though uneasy, believed Thaddeus would abandon Malaga if it suited him; the countess remained skeptical.Soon Paz noticed a change in Clementine’s manner toward him and sensed her contempt, which plunged him into despair. Convinced he had caused her to despise him, he nevertheless found solace in admiring her silently at social gatherings and devoted himself to her comfort and the household. His love was deep, but he felt unworthy of her respect; the emotional strain briefly made him ill, though he later recovered.After Adam lost a significant sum gambling, he asked Thaddeus to cover it and to blame the loss on Malaga. Thaddeus, feeling the weight of obligations, reluctantly complied. When Clementine discovered Thaddeus’s financial generosity toward Paz she expressed frustration and questioned his loyalty; Thaddeus, burdened by expectations and the need to manage her affairs, resolved to teach her household management so she might be more self-sufficient.As spring arrived Adam fell seriously ill; Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him while Clementine sought the best medical care. Through attentive, unceasing care Thaddeus managed to keep the count alive. After weeks Adam improved and Thaddeus received praise, but he remained tormented by his love for Clementine and the knowledge that he could never possess her. Concluding that leaving was the only way to escape his unrequited passion, he decided to go to the Caucasus.Before departing Thaddeus wrote a heartfelt letter to Clementine, confessing his deep feelings and the sacrifices he had made for her happiness, and acknowledging that remaining in Paris would only prolong his pain. Clementine, on receiving the letter, reflected on her feelings and the loss of a devoted friend. Adam, unaware of the full extent of Thaddeus’s sacrifices, lamented the departure, convinced their bond might have withstood trials.Years later Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, wondering about his fate. Unaware of his true feelings and depth of devotion, she was eventually drawn into a new romance, yet his memory lingered—a bittersweet reminder of a love that could never be.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY — CENTRAL PART############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################The Marquise de Listomere embodies the Restoration spirit, balancing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she preserves a virtuous façade to support his political prospects. While some women wait to judge him only when he reaches the peerage, the marquise’s conduct is strictly principled.The marquis himself is a bland figure—an uncharismatic deputy who votes correctly but inspires little admiration, earning the nickname “dull weather.” His unremarkable nature makes him a safe match for a woman of integrity, unlikely to provoke folly.Though reserved, the marquise commands both respect and scorn from would-be suitors, returning contempt to advances. She converses with agreeable men without inciting gossip, not out of coyness but from genuine principle.The narrator, an admirer, sketches her appearance: neither plain nor conventionally pretty, yet possessing a captivating presence. Animated eyes suggest a deeper soul; she charms without seeking it. This quality is evident in a recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever young man who mixes modesty and vivacity.At a ball Rastignac engages her on topics from opera to women’s duties; their exchange shows mutual interest before he abruptly departs. The next morning he is still preoccupied, writing letters in a comfortable setting and imagining the pleasures of romance, until his friend Horace interrupts their talk of a possible expedition to Morea.Meanwhile the marquise, preparing for the day, receives a letter from Rastignac that begins with affectionate terms, provoking curiosity. Though tempted to discard it, she reads on to gauge his feelings; its closing leaves her fatigued as she contemplates the impertinence and implications of his tone.The moment captures the tension between her virtuous exterior and an emerging emotion, foreshadowing the complexities of her character and future relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame de Listomere forbade her porter to admit Monsieur de Rastignac, determined to show him more than disdain for what she deemed intolerable insolence; she had thought of keeping a letter he sent but ultimately burned it."Madame received a fine love-letter," Caroline told the housemaid, surprising her.That evening Madame de Listomere went to a party at the Marquis de Beauseant expecting Rastignac; by two in the morning she realized he would not come, despite having resolved to confront him with coldness. Stendhal called this mental process "crystallization."Four days later Rastignac scolded his valet Joseph for misplacing letters. Distracted, Joseph recalled one was for Madame de Listomere and another for Rastignac's lawyer. When Rastignac learned the letter was not for Madame de Nucingen he laughed, recognizing his heart had strayed. His errors began with a misguided plan to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the blunder and worsened by his subsequent absence for several days, which let her resentment crystallize.When he finally called, the porter said Madame de Listomere was out. The Marquis de Listomere, unaware of the tension, invited him in. As they climbed the stairs Rastignac noticed social blunders. On seeing him, Madame de Listomere blushed; he thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere, absorbed in his newspaper, was oblivious.Madame de Listomere quickly regained composure. When Eugene asked after her health she said she was well; he suggested his visit might be unwelcome because of the marquis's introduction, and she replied that her husband did not share all her secrets, hinting at a complex marriage. To ease the mood he said silence would excuse his visit; she promised forgetfulness while maintaining a dignified air. When he mentioned the letter she feigned offense but could not hide amusement. Eugene insisted no offense was intended and that the letter was not meant for her; she pretended to be offended yet warned him against further mistakes.Monsieur de Listomere, still engrossed in his paper, interrupted to mention a mutual acquaintance's death, prompting a discussion that revealed the marquise's irritation with Eugene's earlier remarks. He naively suggested she might be trying to extract a secret from him, which only increased her curiosity. When Eugene brought up Madame de Nucingen the marquise's demeanor shifted: she rang for wood, signaling that it was time for him to leave, and coldly confronted him about the letter, asking how his pen could have written her name. Embarrassed, Eugene stumbled away.Days later Madame de Listomere confirmed his claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society; the marquis explained her absence as a stomach ailment, while her physician recognized it as a nervous condition she used to justify seclusion. The episode underscores the complexity of social interaction and misunderstandings in romantic contexts, revealing characters' emotional responses and social roles.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At Paris balls, two gatherings commonly occur: the formal party of superficial pleasantries, and a later, more intimate meeting where genuine conversation and laughter flourish. The latter, hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches, preserves the old spirit of French wit and allows free exchange of ideas and camaraderie.One memorable evening at Mademoiselle des Touches' salon gathered notable figures, including the Minister de Marsay, in an atmosphere of wit and ease. Anecdotes and confessions wove a lively tapestry of social interaction until the talk turned to de Marsay's recollections of early love—particularly a passionate affair with a woman six years his senior. He described youthful infatuation marked by intense devotion, secrecy, and a practiced indifference to shield their liaison from society.He portrayed their relationship as a careful dance of appearances and feeling: riding past her carriage, exchanging silent signals through the flowers she carried, reveling in mutual admiration and the thrill of concealment, all while remaining unaware of any betrayal. The turning point came when, ill, he sent a note to cancel a meeting but, consumed by jealousy, went to her nonetheless. There he found her with another man, which ignited a storm of emotion; yet her calm demeanor and tender concern briefly soothed his doubts.The narrative darkened as de Marsay confronted the reality of betrayal. He felt innocence end and a more cynical view of love begin, wrestling with thoughts of revenge and the tangled nature of passion, and ultimately deciding that true love must rise above the urge for vengeance. His account moved those present, prompting reflection on love, jealousy, and the social expectations that shape relationships—reminding the salon of trust’s fragility and love’s transformative power.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Cured of my cold and my pure love, I began an affair with a charming heroine whose beauty sharply contrasted with that of my deceptive angel. I avoided quarrels with this clever woman, doubting whether true love could match the delights of her skilled deceit. Such refined hypocrisy can be as good as virtue.I sought a hair artist in the Rue Boucher to make a keepsake for my new angel. He specialized in hair mementos and showed me work surpassing fairy tales, mentioning trends like marking linen with hair. Suspicion rose when I recognized a handkerchief he had worked on, confirming doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections. I left the shop believing in pleasure but skeptical about love.Two months later, in the boudoir of the ethereal being, I held her hand as we explored the depths of sentiment. In a moment of tenderness, I seized the chance to draw out her most delightful lies. Charlotte professed love, insisting I was the only man in her world and that she had been seeking ways to bind me to her eternally.The women listening to de Marsay seemed offended by his portrayal of their airs. Just as I might have believed her sweet falsehoods, I asked, “When are you to marry the Duke?” The directness startled her; her reaction revealed guilt. She feigned astonishment, but I pressed on, suggesting she should marry him, as he was rich and devout.Charlotte fell to her knees, insisting she loved no one but me. I urged her to be truthful, and she swore before God. I confessed doubts about her fidelity, which she quickly turned against me, claiming I no longer loved her. She sought to escape the confrontation, but curiosity compelled her to ask what I had seen. I revealed that I had noticed her attending mass with the Duke several times; she dismissed it as jealousy.I explained that her marriage to the Duke would elevate her status and advised her to embrace the role, as he was virtuous and would expect propriety. Charlotte, in tears, lamented that if I had only spoken sooner we could have been happy. I maintained it was too late and she had gone too far with the Duke.She worried about the Duke’s suspicions; I reassured her he likely had none but that she needed to prove devotion. As she paced the boudoir, she shifted demeanor, held out her hand, and declared I was noble and charming, vowing never to forget me.Her tactics were admirable, and I played the distressed lover. She then asked what would become of me, and I revealed I had made advances to another woman. This startled Charlotte, who accused me of being unfaithful. I countered that she had too much wit for her heart to suffer.Charlotte expressed disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I acknowledged her anger but pointed out that taking love seriously in society was like romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal; I feigned submission and asked if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess.Her bitterness confirmed she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully distanced myself. I had studied the lives of successful men with women and believed I had mastered the art of retreat.The women in the room reacted with sympathy for Charlotte, while de Marsay maintained a slight smile. Conversation shifted to the decline of social distinctions. Lady Dudley noted that women often did not see their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of the aristocracy, saying duchesses and marquises were disappearing and the Civil Code had leveled social hierarchies.Blondet remarked that aristocracy now began with viscountesses, while de Marsay observed that countesses might survive in some form. He reflected on how the last great ladies had vanished with the splendor of the previous century, and the Code had inadvertently created a new class of women.The Comte de Vandenesse commented on how the current structure allowed anyone to claim nobility, while Lord Dudley noted property had not been organized, accelerating the aristocracy’s decline.Joseph Bridau observed that the beauty of womanhood had diminished and the great lady’s fan was broken. Daniel d’Arthez said the aristocracy had retreated, leaving behind women who might otherwise have shaped society.The discussion turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the era, embodying good taste and grace but lacking the grandeur of past great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questioned the notion of progress, while Madame de Nucingen explained that a woman’s worth was no longer tied to lineage but to character and presentation.Count Adam Laginski asked what defined a “perfect lady.” Émile Blondet described her as exuding elegance and refinement, effortlessly commanding respect and admiration. He depicted her as graceful in bearing, charming in conversation, composed at carriage and table, and able to captivate without theatricality.The dialogue highlighted modern femininity’s complexities: the “perfect lady” navigates societal expectations with skill and charm, blending beauty and intellect to captivate those around her. Guests considered whether this new type of woman compensated for the vanished greatness of earlier aristocratic women, or merely reflected a reshaped society where merit and presentation replaced inherited splendour.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“You will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet began, gravely observing the ways of contemporary women. Even when a lady is married she artfully conceals her husband; he appears only as a briefly noticed middle-aged man who bows and leaves. At a ball she can be radiant and unattached; at home she is prudent and exacting. Blondet sketched the modern lady’s household with care: she receives no one before four, keeps her house warm and orderly, renews her luxury so nothing looks neglected, arranges flowers as deliberate gifts, and surrounds herself with fashionable trifles that are for living use, not museum display. At home she sits by the fire in a low chair and lets the visitor do the honors; playful at a ball, she becomes the hostess whose wit you owe. She creates an intimate world where she can shine, supported by conversation that often skirts generalities.De Marsay agreed that Blondet had detected a flaw of the age: the epigram now indexes momentary events rather than striking at enduring people or objects. Blondet went on: a modern lady’s intelligence, if present, shows itself chiefly in skepticism. The townswoman is steady in virtue; the fashionable lady hesitates and debates her virtue. That uncertainty, Blondet suggested, is among the few graces left by the confused moral climate of the time. She may rarely go to church yet will debate religion; if you dissent, she will try to convert you with the pious, politically slanted phrases typical of the class—claims that religion preserves society and family against selfish individualism, couched in a neo-Christian morality that is neither clearly Catholic nor Protestant but very “moral” in a muddled, modern way.The women laughed at Blondet’s satire, but he pressed the point: the “perfect lady” of the day is a mixture of intellectual and political confusion, surrounded by ostentatious yet ephemeral goods produced by an industry that constantly replaces its wares. She feigns curiosity and ignorance to extract confidences, arranging her passions discreetly, like notes on a musical score, with rests and pauses. Whereas the great ladies of old displayed their affairs boldly, the modern woman stages them with calculated restraint. Name, position, and fortune no longer provide immunity; the aristocracy no longer shields indiscretions. Rather than being aggressively triumphant, she navigates compromises; she fears servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. Free and dazzling in public, she becomes a slave at home—independent only in private or in theory. Her first duty is to preserve her position as a lady. In a society where a repudiated wife, stripped of carriage or opera box, becomes neither wife, maid, nor townswoman but an adrift chattel, a woman must weigh potential ruin and the uncertainties of lovers and convents. Thus the modern lady may provoke gossip but rarely slander.Blondet summarized the era as producing a system between English hypocrisy and eighteenth-century frankness—a transitional, bastard regime that yields no clear new model. He doubted that any woman, however high-born, could acquire before twenty-five the encyclopedic familiarity with social nuance, the practiced maneuvering, the delicate combination of artifice and candor needed to be “perfect.” When Mademoiselle des Touches asked where the female author would fit in this scheme, Blondet replied, with a teasing epigram attributed to Napoleon, that a woman without genius is a woman “comme il n’en faut pas”—not the right sort. Canalis interposed, defending literary genius and segueing into a vivid encomium of Napoleon.Canalis painted Napoleon as a phenomenon of will and contradiction: a man of action and thought, the most concentrated force of his age, capable of creating empires, laws, and public works while also improvising. He juxtaposed Napoleon’s military and political genius with his human contradictions—hypocrisy and generosity, love of both simplicity and pomp, protection of the arts despite poor taste, a strong practical genius that nevertheless failed to secure his final position. Canalis admired Napoleon’s capacity to do everything he willed, likening him in youthful vigor to Caesar and Cromwell but emphasizing his tragic, improvisational mastery that ultimately left Europe altered and himself undone. De Marsay lauded Napoleon as both despot and dispenser of justice, the genuine sovereign of decisive moments.This appreciation of strong, decisive characters led the company to remark on the value of conversation itself—how invaluable those brilliant flashes of wit are, like rare performances that cannot be repeated. Joseph Bridau observed that if one had to pay for the charms of such conversation as one pays for music, one’s fortune would be inadequate. The Princesse de Cadignan, skeptical of claims that the age had diminished women, defended contemporary passions and capabilities: public splendor may have receded under a regime run by grocers, but the human heart remains unchanged. She argued that devotion and magnificent suffering still exist without the old publicity; that her contemporaries—marquises, dancers, singers, poets—match or exceed the glories of the past; that the Empire’s age, like Louis XV’s, had its own stamp and splendor. The Comte de Vandenesse chimed in, agreeing that present women can rival the heroines and beauties of earlier ages and that Paris boasts a thousand great women who write better than the solitary Madame de Sévigné, although they publish not. Blondet’s portrait of the modern lady, Vandenesse conceded, described many fascinations but did not preclude heroism where needed. He had seen providential effects even amid the casualness of women deemed light by society.The group’s banter and compliments—Madame de Vandenesse’s hopes, Madame de Serizy’s teasing, Baron de Nucingen’s relish for the talk—led to General Montriveau’s turn. He prefaced his tale with a solemn note: in his campaigns he had, involuntarily, been the cause of a frightful disaster, a drama useful to Doctor Bianchon, who studied the mind as well as the body, for it bore on the will.General Montriveau recounted the disordered retreat from the Beresina in 1812. The army had dissolved into a mixed, suffering mass. On a bleak evening he sought shelter and found a wretched Polish farmhouse where officers were gathered to share meager food—potatoes, horseflesh, frozen beets. He sat down among comrades and remembered an extraordinary Italian colonel of his regiment: tall, powerfully built, with abundant black curls and a complexion so fair as to be almost womanly, and with a strikingly irascible temperament. The colonel’s face could be angelic one moment and terrifying the next; his voice had a guttural roll that lent force to every command. He was a man of contrasts, gifted with an authority and presence that made women yield to him as glass yields to a blower’s pipe. Yet he was neither a louche seducer nor indifferent to female influence—rather, he enacted a fierce, possessive, almost primitive brutality when roused.Montriveau recounted a scene where the colonel, witnessing a quarrel between two batteries whose collision had crushed one captain’s leg, had turned suddenly ferocious and, in two seconds of outburst, killed the other colonel by splitting his skull. The victimized captain’s Sicilian wife, Rosina, was beautiful despite hardship—dark, small, with almond eyes and the remnants of female grace. She had captured the colonel’s attention, nurturing a dangerous liaison; her husband, an impassive Piedmontese gentleman, appeared oblivious to their connection but betrayed a cruel, restless underlip that made Montriveau distrust him.That night at the farm the colonel called for “Rosina,” and the little wife, after distressed gestures, went to him. Montriveau, with a laugh that broke the tense air, apologized to the husband for some perceived impropriety, and the men slept. Next morning they resumed marching. Some distance on, as they looked back toward the farmhouse, they heard terrifying sounds—the din of fire and a woman’s weak scream. The farmhouse had been barricaded and burned. The captain, who had been walking calmly among the column, pointed to his breast and toward the fire with a single gesture: “It was I.” None asked further; they resumed their march in silence, as the Englishman’s phrase in the group remarked: there is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep. The image of Rosina’s fate—the burning of the house and the dreadful explanation—haunted them. Madame de Montcornet confessed she would dream of it.Blondet and others turned to the medical testimony. De Marsay suggested Bianchon, who had seen the dying woman, should speak. Bianchon affirmed the scene’s tragic beauty: the Duke and he had spent a night by a dying Duchess’s pillow. She was consumed by pulmonary disease, but retained a sublime, pallid serenity. The Duke for a moment fell asleep; upon waking he sat beside his wife, and she, with a last tenderness, took his hand and murmured, “My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?” and died looking at him. The company noted the profound, sweet impression such intimate deathbed scenes left, and Mademoiselle des Touches rose, moved.An appended catalogue followed, listing the principal characters and where else they appear in Balzac’s Human Comedy: Bianchon; the Blondets; Bridau; Canalis; Lord Dudley; the Marquise d’Espard; Comte Adam Laginski; Marsay; the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse; General Montriveau; the Nucingens; the Portenduere vicomtesse; Rastignac; the Ronquerolles; the Serizy comtesse; the imaginary mistress saga; Mademoiselle des Touches; and Comte Félix de Vandenesse—each with references to other novels and tales in which Balzac revisits them, indicating the interwoven unity of his social panorama.Throughout the conversation and narratives, the recurring themes are clear: the modern woman’s performance of virtue and sentiment; the precarious social position that forces delicate public artifice and private constraint; the decline of old aristocratic protections; the moral muddle of a society caught between English prudery and eighteenth-century frankness; the place of genius and heroism amid triviality; and the brutal realities of war that expose character and precipitate tragedy. The dialogue alternates satire, admiration, and melancholy—satire of contemporary manners, admiration for strength and talent (as in Napoleon), and melancholy at examples of human suffering and the small cruelties of social life. The General’s anecdote and the doctor’s deathbed scene provide poignant counterpoints to the conversation’s lighter wit, grounding the assembled company’s reflections in the human costs of passion, pride, and misfortune.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“Ah! madame,” replied the doctor, “I have appalling stories, but each has its hour—you know Chamfort’s jest to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” said the mistress.“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” they cried.The doctor bowed and fell silent.“At about a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the banks of the Loir,” he began, “stands an old brown house with very high roofs, so isolated that nothing stands near it, not even a tannery or tavern. In front is a garden running down to the river where once-clipped box shrubs now straggle. Willows rooted in the stream form a half-hidden fence. Weeds have taken the bank; fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, their suckers forming a thicket. Espaliers look like a copse; the gravel walks are overgrown—there is no trace of a path.“From the hilltop where the ruins of the old Dukes of Vendôme cling, you can see this must once have been a country gentleman’s delight, devoted to horticulture, especially choice fruit. An arbor’s wreck and a partly standing table remain. The garden suggests provincial peace, like an epitaph for a worthy tradesman. One wall bears a sundial with the motto, ‘Ultimam cogita.’“The roof is dreadfully dilapidated; the shutters are always closed; swallows hang nests from the balconies; the doors are shut. Grasses green the flagstones; the ironwork is rusty. Moon and sun, winter and snow have eaten into the wood, warped the boards, peeled paint. The silence is broken only by birds, cats, polecats, rats and mice. An invisible hand has written over it all: ‘Mystery.’“From the street you see a large arched gate full of holes made by children. I later learned the door had been blocked for ten years. Through these breaches the courtyard side is as ruined as the garden: weeds on paving-stones; walls cracked; blackened coping laced with pellitory. Steps are disjointed; the bell-cord rotten; the gutter-spouts broken. What fire from heaven or what curse has fallen on this dwelling? Reptiles crawl over it but reply not. This empty house is a vast enigma.“It was once a small domain, held in fief, called La Grande Bretèche. During my stay at Vendôme, where Despleins had left me to tend a rich patient, this strange dwelling became one of my keenest pleasures. It was better than a ruin; ruins keep memories, but this house, decaying though still roofed, held a secret, an unrevealed thought. More than once I climbed the wild hedge, braved scratches to enter the ownerless garden, and lingered for hours amid its disorder. I would not have traded the story that produced this scene for any gossip. There I wove romances and indulged a melancholy that intoxicated me. Had I known the reason for the neglect, I would have lost the unwritten poetry that enchanted me. To me the place represented phases of human life shadowed by misfortune: sometimes a graveyard without the dead, sometimes a leper’s house, sometimes a home like the Atridae; above all the contemplative provincial life. I often wept there; I never laughed.“Sometimes the dull hum of a passing wood-pigeon gave me involuntary terrors. The earth is dank; you must watch for lizards, vipers, frogs; above all you must not fear cold, for an icy cloak can settle on your shoulders like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck.“One evening the wind turned an old rusty weathercock and its creak sounded like a cry from the house. I returned to my inn absorbed in gloomy thoughts. After supper the hostess entered with an air of mystery: ‘Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.’“‘Who is Monsieur Regnault?’ I asked.“‘What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,’ she said and left.“A tall, slim man in black appeared, hat in hand, entering with a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face like dirty water. You would have taken him for an usher. His coat was old and worn, but he had a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.“‘Monsieur,’ I said. He sat by my fire, placed his hat on my table and, rubbing his hands, said: ‘Dear me, it is very cold.—Monsieur, I am Monsieur Regnault.’“I encouraged myself to question.“‘I am,’ he continued, ‘notary at Vendôme.’“‘Delighted, monsieur,’ I said. ‘But I am not in a position to make a will for reasons best known to myself.’“‘One moment!’ he said, raising a hand. ‘Allow me, monsieur! I am informed you sometimes walk in the garden of La Grande Bretèche.’“‘Yes, monsieur.’“‘One moment!’ he repeated. ‘That constitutes a misdemeanor. As executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, I beg you to discontinue the practice. I am not a Turk and do not wish to make a crime of it. Besides, you may be ignorant of the circumstances which compel me to leave the finest mansion in Vendôme to fall into ruin. You must be a man of education and know the laws forbid trespass on enclosed property. A hedge is the same as a wall. But the state in which the place is left may excuse your curiosity. For my part, I should be content to allow you to come and go; but being bound to respect the testatrix’s will, I beg you not to enter the garden. I myself, since the will was read, have never set foot in the house, which is part of the late Madame de Merret’s estate. We have done nothing there but verify doors and windows to calculate taxes I must pay annually from the funds she left. Ah! my dear sir, her will made a great commotion in town.’“He paused to blow his nose. I respected his volubility—administering Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life. Forced to leave my reveries I asked the reasons for such eccentricity.“At these words pleasure spread across the lawyer’s countenance. He drew up his collar, took his snuffbox, offered me a pinch; when I declined, he took a large one and was happy. A man without a hobby does not know life’s pleasures.“‘Monsieur,’ said Regnault, ‘I was head-clerk in Monsieur Roguin’s office in Paris. A first-rate house—you may have heard of it? No! An unfortunate bankruptcy made it famous. Not having money to purchase a practice in Paris at the inflated prices of 1816, I came here and bought my predecessor’s business. I had relations in Vendôme; a wealthy aunt allowed me to marry her daughter. Three months after being licensed by the Keeper of the Seals, one evening, before my marriage, I was sent for by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her Chateau of Merret. Her maid, now a servant in this inn, was waiting at my door with the Countess’ own carriage. Ah! one moment! I ought to tell you Monsieur le Comte de Merret had gone to Paris to die two months before I came here. He came to a miserable end, plunging into every dissipation. You understand?“‘On the day he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted La Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it. Some say she burnt the furniture and hangings—in short, everything used to furnish the premises now let by the said M.—(Dear, what am I saying? I beg your pardon, I thought I was dictating a lease.)—In short, they say she burnt everything in the meadow at Merret. Have you been to Merret, monsieur?—No,’ he answered himself, ‘Ah, it is a very fine place.’“‘For about three months before, the Count and Countess lived very eccentrically; they admitted no visitors; Madame stayed on the ground floor, Monsieur on the first. When the Countess was left alone she appeared only at church. At home she refused to see friends. She was already much altered when she left La Grande Bretèche for Merret. That dear lady—I say dear, for she gave me this diamond, though I saw her but once—was very ill; she had, no doubt, given up hope, for she died without choosing to send for a doctor; many fancied she was not quite right in her head. Well, sir, my curiosity was excited when I heard she needed my services. Nor was I the only one interested. That very night all the town knew I was going to Merret.“‘The waiting-woman replied vaguely; nevertheless she told me her mistress had received the Sacrament that day and seemed unlikely to live through the night. It was about eleven when I reached the chateau. I went up the great staircase. After crossing large, lofty, dark rooms, diabolically cold and damp, I reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. From the rumors about this lady (monsieur, I should never end if I repeated them), I had imagined a coquette. Imagine, then, that I had great difficulty seeing her in the great bed where she lay. To light this enormous, old-fashioned room thick with dust, she had only an old Argand lamp. Ah! you have not been to Merret. The bed is of the old world, with a high tester hung with flowered chintz. A small table by the bed held an ‘Imitation of Christ,’ which, by the way, I bought for my wife, and the lamp. There was also a deep armchair for her maid and two small chairs. There was no fire. That was all the furniture, scarcely enough to fill ten lines in an inventory.“‘My dear sir, if you had seen that vast room, papered and hung with brown, you would have felt transported into a romance. It was icy—nay, funereal,’ and he lifted his hand theatrically.“‘At last I saw Madame de Merret under the lamp’s glimmer. Her face was as yellow as wax and as narrow as two folded hands. She wore a lace cap showing abundant hair, white as linen thread. She sat up, keeping upright with difficulty. Her large black eyes, dimmed by fever and half-dead, hardly moved beneath the bony arch of her eyebrows—there,’ he added, pointing to his brow. ‘Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands were like bones covered with soft skin; veins and muscles perfectly visible. She must have been very handsome; now disease had made her a phantom. Her pale-violet lips barely moved when she spoke.“‘Though my profession has brought me to many deaths to record last wishes, the families in tears and agonies I have seen were as nothing compared with this lonely woman in her vast chateau. I heard no sound; I perceived not the breathing that ought to move the sheets, and I stood motionless, absorbed. In fancy I am there still. At last her large eyes moved; she tried to raise her right hand, but it fell back, and she uttered words like a breath, for her voice was no longer a voice: “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose to her cheeks. It was a great effort to speak.“‘“Madame,” I began.’ She signed to me to be silent. The old housekeeper rose and whispered, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise.”“‘I sat down. A few moments later, Madame de Merret mustered all her strength, slipped her right hand under the bolster, paused, and with a last effort withdrew a sealed paper, drops of perspiration on her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” and that was all. She clutched a crucifix, lifted it to her lips, and died.“‘The expression of her eyes makes me shudder. She must have suffered much! Joy was in her last glance and remained stamped on her dead eyes.“‘I brought away the will, and when opened found she had appointed me her executor. She left most of her property to the hospital at Vendôme except a few legacies. But these were her instructions regarding La Grande Bretèche: she ordered me to leave the place, for fifty years from her death, in the state it was at her death, forbidding anyone, whoever he might be, to enter the apartments, prohibiting any repairs whatever, and even settling a salary to pay watchmen if necessary to secure fulfillment. At the expiration of that term, if the will has been duly carried out, the house becomes my heirs’ property, for, as you know, a notary cannot take a bequest. Otherwise La Grande Bretèche reverts to the heirs-at-law, but on condition of fulfilling certain terms set forth in a codicil not to be opened until the fifty years expire. The will has not been disputed, so—’ Without finishing his sentence the lanky notary looked at me with triumph; I made him happy by offering congratulations.“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘you have so vividly impressed me I fancy I see the dying woman whiter than her sheets; her glittering eyes frighten me; I shall dream of her tonight.—But you must have formed some idea as to the instructions contained in that extraordinary will.’“‘Monsieur,’ said he with comical reticence, ‘I never allow myself to criticize the conduct of a person who honors me with the gift of a diamond.’“Nevertheless I soon loosened the discreet notary’s tongue, though his digressions and the town’s conflicting gossip nearly put me to sleep. Happily he went away.“‘Ah, ha, monsieur,’ he called on the stairs, ‘a good many persons would be glad to live five-and-forty years longer; but—one moment!’ and he laid his finger to his nostril with a cunning look, as much as to say, ‘Mark my words!—To last as long as that—you must not be past sixty now.’“I closed my door, roused by this last speech which he thought funny; then I sat down with my feet on the fire-dogs. I had lost myself in a Radcliffean romance built on Monsieur Regnault’s legal base, when my landlady pushed the door open. She was a buxom, florid Fleming, always good-humored, a woman who had missed her calling.“‘Well, monsieur,’ she asked, ‘Monsieur Regnault has no doubt been giving you his history of La Grande Bretèche?’“‘Yes, Madame Lepas.’“‘And what did he tell you?’“I recounted the creepy story of Madame de Merret. At each sentence my hostess leaned forward with the scrutiny of an innkeeper, constable and merchant combined.“‘My good Madame Lepas,’ I said, ‘you seem to know more about it. If not, why have you come up to me?’“‘On my word, as an honest woman——’“‘Do not swear; your eyes are big with a secret. You knew Monsieur de Merret; what sort of man was he?’“‘Monsieur de Merret—well, you see he was a man you never could see the top of, he was so tall! A very good gentleman from Picardy, who had, as we say, his head close to his cap. He paid everything down to avoid difficulties. He was hot-tempered, you see! All our ladies liked him very much.’“‘Because he was hot-tempered?’ I asked.“‘Well, maybe,’ she said; ‘and you may suppose a man had to have something to show before marrying Madame de Merret, who, without reflection on others, was the handsomest and richest heiress in our parts. She had about twenty thousand francs a year. All the town was at the wedding; the bride was pretty and sweet-looking, quite a gem. Oh, they were a handsome couple in their day!’“‘And were they happy together?’“‘Hm, hm! so-so—so far as can be guessed, for, as you may suppose, we of the common sort were not hail-fellow-well-met with them.—Madame de Merret was a kind woman and very pleasant, who had no doubt sometimes to put up with her husband’s tantrums. But though he was rather haughty, we were fond of him. After all, it was his place to behave so. When a man is a born nobleman, you see——’“‘Still, there must have been some catastrophe for them to part so violently?’“‘I did not say there was any catastrophe, sir. I know nothing about it.’“‘Indeed. Well, now, I am sure you know everything.’“‘Well, sir, I will tell you the whole story.—When the Emperor sent Spaniards here, prisoners of war and others, I was required to lodge at the Government’s charge a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Notwithstanding his parole, he had to show himself every day to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—a name in os and dia, something like Bagos de Férédia. I wrote his name in my books; you may see it if you like. Ah! he was a handsome young fellow for a Spaniard, who are all ugly they say. He was not more than five feet two or three in height, but so well made; and he had little hands that he kept so beautifully! Ah! you should have seen them. He had as many brushes for his hands as a woman has for her toilet. He had thick, black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion, which I admired. He wore the finest linen I have ever seen, though I have had princesses to lodge here, among them General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. He did not eat much, but he had such polite and amiable ways that it was impossible to dislike him, though he scarcely spoke; if spoken to, he often did not answer—a way, a mania perhaps they have.“‘He read his breviary like a priest and went to mass regularly. And where did he post himself?—we found this out later.—Within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. As he took that place the first time, no one imagined purpose. Besides, he never raised his nose above his book, poor young man! And then, monsieur, of an evening he went for a walk on the hill among the ruins of the old castle. It was his only amusement, poor man; it reminded him of his native land. They say that Spain is all hills!’############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening, shortly after he arrived, he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait for him. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys thought he had seen the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he returned, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being observed.Eventually, one morning, we found he hadn’t come back. Searching his things, I discovered a note in his drawer, with fifty doubloons—about five thousand francs—and a sealed box of diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note said that if he did not return, he left the money and diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.At that time my husband went searching for him. He returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He went early, so no one saw him. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced that he had escaped.The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas believed the Spaniard had drowned. I thought otherwise, suspecting his involvement in what happened with Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was of ebony and silver; early in his stay Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, do you think I should feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague, sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread—as if entering a dark church where a faint light glimmers beneath a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, suddenly appeared vividly in my mind. I felt compelled to explore the mysterious dwelling to uncover the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Observing her, I sensed an inner thought beneath her blooming health. There was an element of sorrow or hope in her soul; her demeanor suggested a secret, like devout souls who pray excessively or a woman haunted by a past crime. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of criminality; one would deem her innocent seeing the large checked kerchief tucked into her lilac-and-white-striped gown. “No,” I resolved, “I will not leave Vendôme without knowing the full story of la Grande Bretèche. To achieve this, I will make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.”“Rosalie!” I called one evening.“Your servant, sir?”“You’re not married?” She flinched slightly.“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, quickly regaining composure; every woman, from the highest lady to the inn-servant, possesses presence of mind.“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”This reply seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to the affair; she seemed tied to its core. It wasn’t a matter for ordinary courtship; this girl contained the final chapter of a romance, and from that moment all my attention was directed toward her. By studying her, I noticed admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm desire can bestow upon a woman. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning, I said to Rosalie:“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a particular attitude of confidence is necessary for storytelling. The best tales are told at a certain hour—just as we are all here at table.If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarcely suffice. As the event she recounted stands between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it concisely.The room at la Grande Bretèche where Madame de Merret slept was on the ground floor; a small built-in cupboard about four feet deep served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been seriously ill, so much so that her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. By chance, he returned home two hours later than usual from the club, where he had been reading the papers and discussing politics. His wife assumed he was in bed and asleep. The evening’s animated discussions and his losses at billiards had delayed him.For some time Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to inform him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative responses led him to his room, trusting her completely. But that evening he decided to check on Madame de Merret to share his misfortunes and perhaps seek comfort. He had noticed her looking particularly lovely at dinner and thought her convalescence had enhanced her beauty, a realization that often comes too late for husbands.Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. His instincts were piqued by a nagging doubt; he noticed an anxious expression in her eyes.“You’re very late,” she said, her voice slightly husky.Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing the room, arms crossed.“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He didn’t respond.“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She sensed disaster in her husband’s demeanor and wanted to be alone with him. Once Rosalie left, or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage, Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, stating coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”This “No” pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he didn’t believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand, stopping him, and said with strange emotion, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”The extraordinary dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could be legendary on a grander stage.“No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” At these words, Madame de Merret looked at him with a haggard expression. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in there; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,” examining the ebony and silver crucifix.“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.”“Indeed,” said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.He waited for her to arrive. “Go to bed, all of you,” he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss the billiards game and the club’s discussions. When Rosalie returned, she found them conversing amiably.Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the reception rooms on the ground floor. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a considerable quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.“Tell him to come in,” he replied aloud.Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.“Gorenflot,” said her husband, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning, I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, which won’t be paid until your wedding day, contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!”“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”Her husband quietly paced the room, keeping an eye on the door, the mason, and his wife, without displaying any suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”Monsieur and Madame de Merret remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door. This silence was intentional on the husband’s part; he didn’t want to give his wife the chance to say anything ambiguous. On Madame de Merret’s side, it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason took advantage of his master’s back being turned to break one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to Gorenflot. They all three then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling “Hope.”At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.The next morning, he casually mentioned, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy.“He will go to Duvivier’s,” she thought.As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”In an instant, Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with an intensity beyond words, she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”“No, monsieur.”“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. “Jean,” he added, turning to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he said without allowing her to speak, “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”After this story, all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some among them had shivered at the last words.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################During the Restoration one of the few drawing-rooms frequented by the Archbishop of Besançon was Baronne de Watteville’s, because of her devoutness.Monsieur de Watteville, a descendant of the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming. After a quiet life in La Franche-Comté he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, heiress of the Rupt family; the match was arranged in 1802 and formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. Following her grandparents’ deaths, Madame de Watteville grew more devout, adopting a pious life that influenced Besançon’s upper circles.Monsieur de Watteville, thin and plain in appearance, occupied himself as a turner and collector of local geological specimens. Observers detected a certain nobility of soul in him: realizing early he would not dominate his wife, he threw himself into work and hobbies.The Rupt residence reflected both families’ standing—grand rooms, elegant furnishings, and fine cuisine—with Monsieur sometimes acting as his own butler. Madame de Watteville’s fortune was considerable; her husband’s estate was modest. Their intimacy with the Archbishop attracted several notable abbés to their home.At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 guests received Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit. The Vicar-General said the legal success had been unexpected given the political climate and credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron, for the decisive role. Curious, Madame de Watteville asked about Savaron’s origins; the Vicar-General replied he lived nearby but his background was unknown, which piqued the guests’ interest. Amedée de Soulas, present that evening, would later prove significant to the story that followed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often skirting customs. What is "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual borrowing. For example, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts, first derided, became a dominant European fashion for decades. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, yet French styles soon shifted in response.More recently England supplied French with terms like "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset, sparked a trend of stylish young men in Paris that spread to the provinces. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt ill at ease in the dull, military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained an ostentatious appearance, keeping a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and lavished expense on his own wardrobe while subsisting on scant income.Though seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was adept at economizing, often dining out to avoid household costs. His costly grooming and clothes were a façade aimed at impressing the local aristocracy and winning Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His travels and Parisian knowledge helped him cultivate a reputation as well-informed despite financial strain.Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy, "the Colony," kept strict social hierarchies. Amedee’s unusual fashions—like wearing trouser-straps—set him apart and bolstered his status as a "lion." The town’s nobles wished to appear modern to visiting Parisians, and Amedee enabled that image.Amedee’s chief ambition was to marry Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict, religious household with little contact with the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye and instructed more in domestic skills than social polish. Sheltered from modern influences, she lacked awareness of the social expectations surrounding courtship.Despite a passive exterior, Rosalie had a resolute spirit from her lineage. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake. This produced tension as Amedee courted Rosalie: maternal jealousy and Rosalie’s uncertainty complicated matters.As Amedee pursued Rosalie, he navigated Besançon’s social intricacies to secure a favorable match while protecting his reputation. Gossip of a possible engagement circulated among the nobility, but Rosalie’s upbringing and her mother’s influence hindered his efforts. Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus illustrates the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraints as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville’s heart.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Madame,” Monsieur de Soulas began to the Baroness as he waited for his soup to cool, “one morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the Mairie as ‘ALBERT SAVARON’ and left his card with the local officials.”“The name Savaron is well-known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville said; “it’s one of the oldest and richest families in Belgium.”“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas returned. “If he claims the Savaron arms, he must add a bar-sinister. The only remaining member of the Brabant family is a rich heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”“The bar-sinister marks a bastard,” Rosalie put in, “but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble.”“Enough, mademoiselle!” the Baroness cut in. “You insisted she learn heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville murmured; “she knows it well.” They urged Monsieur de Soulas to continue.“In a town like Besancon, where everything is cataloged, Albert Savaron was accepted without fuss by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he wanted, especially since he sent his card to the judges instead of calling. Three days later, he was forgotten. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron remained unnoticed because no one saw him.”“Does he not go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“He attends the early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two in the morning, works until eight, breakfasts, continues working, walks in his garden, dines, and goes to bed around six or seven.”“How do you know all this?” she asked.“I live on Rue Neuve, overlooking his house, and my servant communicates with Jerome.”“And you gossip with Babylas?” someone joked.“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar-General.“The President assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man innocent and exposed the real culprits, leading to their arrest. His defense impressed the Court and jury. A merchant then entrusted him a difficult case, which he won. Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ Savaron given our situation; I met him, felt confident—and I was right.”“Is he truly extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied. “The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey went on, “he received me in a modest outer room—old oak, law books, a plain writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, a green carpet. He looked young but matched his setting: black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”“The devil’s colors!” Madame de Watteville exclaimed.“He has a magnificent head,” the Abbe continued. “Black hair streaked with gray, thick and curly; a round white throat; a splendid forehead furrowed by thought; an olive complexion; a square nose; fiery eyes. Despite passion, he is calm, with a sweet voice that can thunder when needed. Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”“The second time I visited,” the Abbe added, “he received me in his shabby bedroom. The third time, he was having a frugal breakfast in his library; I observed him closely. There is much behind that terrible yet gentle face; he seems to carry a heavy burden.”“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besancon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”“After studying his head and hearing him parry a prominent Parisian lawyer,” the Abbe replied, “I believe he will eventually make a significant impact.”They moved on to other topics, but the Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron captivated Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone who seemed exceptional—unlike the mundane Count who had courted her. Albert Savaron presented enigmas: “Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him, replaying the Abbe’s words and fixating on his description. “What a fine forehead!” she thought, comparing him in her mind to the men at the table.“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.“I’m not hungry, mamma,” Rosalie replied, lost in thought about the prelate’s hands. She recalled a lighted window seen from her bed and realized it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.The education of girls, the narrator notes, poses real difficulties: mothers shape nations, and whether girls should be informed about everything is a long-debated question. Religion often imposes restraint; enlightening them too much risks premature maturity, whereas suppressing thought can produce emotional outbursts. Rosalie’s crisis was triggered by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron.The next morning, while dressing, Rosalie looked out to see Albert walking in the garden beside the Hotel de Rupt. “At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered. Having seen him, she resolved to enter his house, uncover his mystery, hear his voice, glimpse his eyes. She would achieve it.Throughout the day she embroidered with the concentration of someone appearing idle but deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession. The next morning, after Mass, she arranged to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service, hoping to encounter Albert.She visited her father in his workshop and suggested he turn larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. He delighted in the idea; she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding. Rosalie envisioned a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.Sunday came. Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled. “It’s the first time I’ve seen you so fidgety,” Mariette remarked.“Perhaps you too are more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing. As she crossed the courtyard, her heart raced; she had never walked the streets like this before, feeling as if her mother could read her thoughts and would forbid her confession. She felt invigorated, as if walking on fire. She arrived at church early, prayed briefly, and checked if Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered.No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression. His solemn demeanor suggested a heavy burden, and his deep gaze conveyed intense thought. The Vicar-General’s words finally made sense. Those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. Boldly, she stood in his path to exchange glances, feeling a rush of warmth.After Albert sat, Rosalie found a place to observe him during her confession. When Mariette announced Monsieur Giroud’s arrival, the time in the confessional felt fleeting. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended and Albert had gone.“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besancon? I must know everything! But how?”With this new desire, Rosalie masked her preoccupation with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother.
