From that Sunday, after their glance, she eagerly supported the belvedere plan.“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he’ll see us—perhaps remarks will be made—”“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie, thinking you know more about life and propriety than we do?”“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentioned a cool room in the grotto where we can take coffee.”“Your father has had an excellent idea,” Madame de Watteville said, going to inspect the columns.She approved the design, choosing a spot in the garden that could not be seen from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but would allow them to see into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior was to be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor; Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier.“The Wattevilles are creating something lovely in their garden,” rumor spread in Besancon.“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—”“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” an Abbe added.“No,” the young man corrected, “he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.”“You know the least about what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt snapped, glancing at her daughter, who had been waiting to marry for a year.Mademoiselle de Watteville felt a flush of pride at the Belvedere’s success, sensing superiority over others. No one suspected the true motive: the girl thought to be a witless goose had devised the plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, increasing the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal skill.On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was also launching a new venture. Through quiet connections among local merchants, he was starting a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each subscribed by his first ten clients, convinced that Besancon needed promotion as a trade hub.To compete with Strasbourg, Besancon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas appealed to merchants eager to believe in their own enterprise.Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, letting his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The Review appealed to various regions, and with a low subscription price it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers.To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Bouchers’ eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while letting him think he was gaining experience. The commercial community, pleased with the Review’s success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.After the third issue the Review gained recognition among French publications. Albert began reading other journals at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, which sparked discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though higher circles dismissed it for liberal views.“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besancon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie who had shown him so much affection, subscribed and lent her the four issues already published. That night Rosalie devoured the tale—the first story she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact on her was striking. She hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages, she became convinced it reflected him, as the critics suspected Albert had drawn from his own life in this confession.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1823 two young men set out from Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland, planning to tour the lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered striking mountain, river, and meadow views. As they passed the hamlet of Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay while his friend Leopold continued the tour.Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together. Leopold was headed to be a notary in Paris; Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more troubled background. His mother had sacrificed everything for his education, hoping to secure his future. Rodolphe was passionate and sensitive, often driven by desire, in contrast to Leopold’s calm composure.In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with the couple who rented to the Lovelaces and learned the woman was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. They were of modest means; Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.The next day Rodolphe sought permission to visit the Bergmanns’ gardens, who rented to the Lovelaces. After hesitation he was allowed to explore and noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, whose background seemed unusual. Eager to meet Fanny, he encountered resistance from the protective Bergmanns.That evening Rodolphe heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later encountered her and Gina, but their conversation was interrupted when Rodolphe was attacked and wounded by an unknown assailant. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken back to safety.While he recovered Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, yet he found solace in Fanny’s companionship. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.Fanny later revealed that her husband was not as old as he had seemed and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his feelings and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing he had great potential.Their relationship deepened, but Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past, sent news and eventually arrived. Tito’s presence stirred rivalry and fear in Rodolphe of losing Fanny’s affection.Despite these challenges their connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, yet Rodolphe’s ambition, Fanny’s loyalty to her husband, and social constraints loomed, forcing him to confront the tensions between love, aspiration, and the sacrifices required for happiness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For twenty days Rodolphe stayed with the Bergmanns, watching Francesca almost unconsciously. He perceived in her a mix of youthful imprudence and resolute character: sometimes resisting love, sometimes yielding. Her gratitude toward the old man showed an innate nobility that intrigued him; the whole situation seemed an enigma. Those days brought secret joys and melancholy, quarrels as pleasing as harmony. Rodolphe became more captivated by Francesca’s constant tenderness and grew jealous over trifles.One evening, when Francesca said she wished to leave Gersau because it lacked luxuries, Rodolphe asked if she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating love of luxury with love of art and beauty, insisting she had never complained in hardship and mischievously suggesting ordinary people naturally aspire to greatness. She then insisted Rodolphe not accompany them to Geneva, citing town gossip and her wish to protect her husband’s reputation. She professed love for Rodolphe but demanded it remain hidden; she kissed him and left him astonished.The next day the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed early. Restless, Rodolphe went to Vevay and then to Geneva by the end of October, renting a room in Eaux-Vives to avoid town discomforts. His first inquiry was about Italian refugees, particularly a bookseller named Lamporani, but the landlord said the only recent arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini. On his first walk he came to Villa Diodati and was struck by its beauty; there he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady, a sight that left him dizzy and lifted a veil on their past interactions. He feared he had been a mere whim confronted by her noble lineage.He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by a man posing as Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from asking to see the Princess and returned to Eaux-Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety. That evening his passion intensified; he felt the need to raise his social standing to match Francesca’s. At the concert she was radiant, and their eyes met in an electric moment. Her beauty and grace captivated him; he felt a profound connection and realized she had remained true to their past despite her rank.After the concert Rodolphe was introduced to the Colonnas and Francesca, who playfully called herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of their time in Gersau; he was overwhelmed by her voice and the intimacy of their proximity. As days passed Francesca acknowledged Rodolphe’s deep love, calling their bond natural and insisting it should remain faithful and persistent, whatever distance separated them. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition—he wanted to achieve greatness so she could be proud of him.Francesca, with her blend of gaiety and tenderness, had a tragic past: born destined for a cloister, family misfortunes forced her into marriage with Prince Gandolphini to preserve their status. Despite her noble background she preferred independence, held liberal ideas, and sympathized with revolutionary sentiments. Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva, time passing swiftly in Francesca’s company. Yet as spring neared he felt inner turmoil: her calm contrasted with his unrest, and he longed for more demonstrative feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive.One day on the terrace, Rodolphe voiced bitterness about others’ happiness, particularly that of a noblewoman. Francesca, wounded, drew back; he followed and found her in tears. In a moment of passion he kissed her, but she rebuffed him and asserted her dignity. She then ordered Rodolphe to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Though pained, he complied, recognizing the strength of her will.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Returning to Paris, Rodolphe found a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, an artist who had refused several other commissions. Rodolphe doubted anyone could persuade him, but Francesca charmed Schinner into painting an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She announced this in a delightful letter, beginning a regular correspondence between her and Rodolphe—their sole indulgence.Driven by ambition and love, Rodolphe poured his resources into a venture that failed by deceit, costing him three years of struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace and renewed courage, he turned toward politics but first went to Naples to recover at the Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored to their posts. Three months there revived his hope and steadied him before new trials.He then tried to rebuild his fortune. His talents were acknowledged and a high post was promised as reward for his dedication, but the storm of July 1830 again thwarted his ambitions.Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave efforts of this young man—possessing fine qualities but lacking fortune’s favor. Sustained by love, he returned to face fresh challenges, guided by an ever-supportive presence and a faithful heart.Lovers! Pray for him!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her tale, Rosalie’s cheeks burned and tears fell. The novel, a man’s own account of his love, was her first exposure to that voice, and it stirred a jealous conviction that Francesca Colonna was Albert’s beloved.Consumed by curiosity and a fierce desire to win Albert despite insecurities about her looks and lack of musical talent, Rosalie became determined to learn the truth. Her imagination supplied schemes typical of a young woman raised under strict rules: sneaking into Albert’s garden, writing to him, or visiting her father’s land to contrive an encounter.From Albert’s window she observed a scene promising insight: Jerome, Albert’s servant, being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noted Mariette’s plainness but also her relative financial security, which made her appealing to Jerome. The sight taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson—that her mother’s constraints could be bypassed by servants.The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the opportunity to question her about Jerome. Confronting Mariette about their secret meetings, Rosalie unexpectedly offered help in exchange for access to letters Albert sent by Jerome. Relieved by Rosalie’s discretion, Mariette agreed.Rosalie’s obsession deepened when she read a letter Albert had sent to his friend Leopold. It revealed his noble lineage, past struggles, ambition, and constancy—an image of a man striving for success while devoted to the woman he loved, Francesca. She was enthralled by his character and ideals.As she read more correspondence, Rosalie grew increasingly aware of her inadequacy and the moral cost of her actions. She struggled with guilt over invading Albert’s privacy but rationalized it as proof of love. Her passion hardened into a dangerous infatuation that led to penances and self-imposed suffering intended to demonstrate devotion.One evening Mariette brought another letter from Albert to Francesca, plunging Rosalie into jealousy and doubt. Torn over whether to read it, she ultimately yielded to the desire to know Albert’s feelings. The letter confirmed his ongoing love for Francesca and outlined his ambitions and hopes for their future.The revelation made Rosalie’s heart ache as she confronted the depth of Albert’s devotion. Though she had at first resolved to respect their relationship, she found herself caught between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing. In a clear moment she decided to step back, recognizing the nobility of their bond and her own shortcomings. As she returned the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on sorrow, resignation, and a yearning for the selfless, enduring love she had witnessed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil; by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In solitude that struggle fed her passion and dark plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist; her story is a cautionary tale.That winter Albert de Savarus advanced his position in Besançon, eager for the Chamber’s dissolution. He had secured backing from a wealthy contractor linked to the moderates. The Romans had long invested in town water supplies; Besançon drew water from the Doubs, though Arcier, a distant hill, was promoted as a source of “Roman” water. Restoring an aqueduct to bring Arcier water seemed absurd to some, yet if citizens were persuaded they would authorize large expenditures that would benefit the contractor. Savarus denounced the river water and promoted Arcier; the local Review echoed him, and townspeople across parties came to favor the Romans’ water. The issue became a focal point, driven by hidden interests. Opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned while Savarus’s projects dominated debate. After eighteen months he had stirred the stagnant town, gaining influence though not popularity.Throughout the winter he won several lawsuits for local priests; ambition consumed him. His apparent disinterest in personal gain masked a kind of moral usury: he expected a much larger reward. In October 1834 he aided a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, which also qualified him for election. The Abbé de Grancey, seeing Savarus’s designs, called him remarkable and suggested he had taken a wrong turn in life.Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, at first content merely to observe. The Watteville estate, les Rouxey, produced ten thousand francs a year but was poorly managed by the Baron, who left it in the charge of an old servant, Modinier. The Baron’s indifference stemmed from his wife’s substantial income; they often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot shaped by the famous Watteville, who had built a dam and lake. Yet Watteville’s heirs pressed a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, long entitled to the land, and the Baron's death left a legacy of usurpation that his descendants continued to assert.Rosalie’s maneuvers were subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey, enchanted by the lake and associating it with love. During a suggested boat trip her father confided legal troubles with Riceys over land rights. Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for their estate. She argued a lawsuit would add excitement and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey; she clearly envisioned fixing her future there and creating beautiful gardens.Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbé urged him to consider political advantages, especially the potential votes the case could bring.Rosalie, determined to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election, even wrote to the préfet revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the préfet disregarded her. She understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it to her advantage.As the election approached, a meeting of influential men in Besançon decided to nominate Savarus, much to his surprise; he had been unaware of the political machinations. The Abbé, observing the situation, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past. Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and captivated his audience. However, the préfet worked to undermine him, eroding his voter base. Despite his efforts, the political tide shifted against him.On the day of the first voting the results disappointed Savarus; he felt the weight of his ambitions and the election’s pressure and returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future. Just as he neared hopelessness the Abbé arrived with news of hope. The priest had labored behind the scenes to secure support, urging Savarus to visit the Wattevilles and promising their case would be resolved in time for the election. Winning the Rouxey lawsuit could secure crucial votes, the Abbé said, and he even encouraged Savarus to consider marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to solidify his standing. Savarus was initially resistant but recognized the potential benefits. The Abbé’s guidance proved invaluable; Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, aware that his future depended on choices he would make in the coming days.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By nine o’clock the next evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were full of Besançon’s aristocracy, discussing the unexpected decision to present the daughter of the Rupts to the polls. It was known that the former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, would be proposed. Madame de Chavoncourt attended with her younger daughter Sidonie, while her elder sister, secure in her relationship, had not bothered. The Abbé de Grancey moved through the groups, listening and offering sharp comments that steered the talk.“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” They spoke of Berryer, isolated and uncertain; of the Duc de Fitz-James’s nomination in Toulouse; and how voting for Monsieur Savarus might drive Republicans toward them rather than toward the Moderates.By nine-thirty Albert had still not appeared, and Madame de Watteville took his tardiness as an insult. Madame de Chavoncourt urged her not to let small matters distract from serious issues, suggesting Albert might be detained by a consultation. Rosalie, watching her mother’s exchange, noted her leniency toward Albert and hinted at a possible match between Sidonie and him.At ten Albert remained absent and impatience grew. Some gentlemen began to play cards; the Abbé, perplexed, went to the window where Rosalie had withdrawn, exclaiming, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.“Jérôme!” Rosalie called to the servant below. When he confirmed that Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbé declared, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s face betrayed a sudden joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but pretended not to, wondering at her involvement.Back in the drawing-room Monsieur de Watteville announced the shocking news of Albert’s unexplained departure. By half-past eleven only fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbé de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbé de Grancey, Rosalie, Amédée de Soulas, and a retired magistrate who had supported Albert’s election. The Abbé stationed himself near the Baroness to observe Rosalie, whose normally pale face now showed agitation.“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. A servant then brought a letter for the Abbé on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did, he noticed Rosalie turn pale; he recognized the handwriting. He folded the letter, discreetly pocketed it, and their exchanged glances hinted at her feelings.“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie stopped him: “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” When he questioned how she knew the writing, her passionate reply—“I love him! What is the matter?”—revealed her. He told her that Albert was withdrawing from the election. Stunned, Rosalie begged him to keep the news secret, realizing that the election’s end would destroy the prospect of her sister’s marriage to Albert.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette how Albert had suddenly vanished. An elderly Parisian had visited Monsieur Savaron at the Hotel National; Savaron turned pale at the visitor’s card, they met for over an hour, and the visitor left with a large package. Savaron, distraught, went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage, ordered post horses, left his keys, and told his servant to seek other work.Savaron’s abrupt departure shook Besançon; the Prefect quickly granted him a passport. The affair stirred local prejudice against outsiders, and Albert was soon forgotten by most—except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville urged Rosalie to marry, noting how much she had changed. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness fretted over finances. A match with Monsieur de Soulas was arranged; Rosalie refused, insisting she was content as she was. Conflict between Rosalie and her mother escalated, and they spent a tense summer at les Rouxey. After Mariette and Jerome wed, the Baron invested in the estate—actions that only increased the Baroness’s resentment. The Vicar-General tried to mediate.One day Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had sabotaged Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo: she had intercepted letters and caused their separation. The Vicar-General was horrified and urged her to seek forgiveness and face the consequences.Rosalie’s guilt deepened when a Paris clerk, acting for Albert, came to sell his belongings and revealed that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received a letter in which Albert expressed complete forgiveness for Rosalie and a desire to remain in solitude.When the Vicar-General conveyed this, he urged Rosalie to reconcile with her mother and accept Monsieur de Soulas. She remained defiant, unwilling to sacrifice her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General’s death left a void; soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support.Now of age and financially independent, Rosalie entered Parisian society determined to confront the Duchess. At a ball she publicly revealed her role in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken. Rosalie returned to Besançon having exposed the truth, but the emotional cost weighed heavily—this tale of love, betrayal, and ambition left a lasting impact on all involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her les Rouxey estate, riding, hunting, and improving the land, making her notable in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, looks younger, while her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, has visibly aged. He remarked to young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”Mademoiselle de Watteville was known for her eccentric “vagaries.” Each year she visited the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps recalling a grand-uncle who once broke monastery walls to gain freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon—reportedly to marry, though the true reason remains unclear. She returned after a steamboat boiler explosion on the Loire that cost her the use of her right arm and left leg and scarred her face. Her health deteriorated; in constant pain, she now devotes herself to religious practices at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.PARIS, May 1842.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot, The Deserted Woman- Genovese: Massimilla Doni- Hannequin, Leopold: Beatrix, Cousin Betty, Cousin Pons- Jeanrenaud: The Commission in Lunacy- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman- Rhetore, Duc Alphonse de: A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Letters of Two Brides; The Member for Arcis- Savaron de Savarus: The Quest of the Absolute- Savarus, Albert Savaron de: The Quest of the Absolute- Schinner, Hippolyte: The Purse; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; A Start in Life; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists- Tinti, Clarina: Massimilla Doni############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########### ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyLETTERS OF TWO BRIDESBy Honore de BalzacTranslated by R. S. ScottDEDICATIONTo George SandYour name, dear George, adds a reflected radiance to my book—not from self-interest, but to bear witness to our enduring friendship, which has triumphed over the world's malice. This bond is unchanged. The names attached to my works give me pleasure amid the vexation of their growing number. Each new book provokes fresh annoyance, as if my pen could rival the world that supplies my models. Would it not be wonderful if future antiquarians found only great names and generous hearts among my friends? I take pride in this possession rather than in unstable popularity. For those who know you well, it is happiness to sign myself,Your friend,  DE BALZAC.  PARIS, June 1840.LETTERS OF TWO BRIDESFIRST PARTI. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBE. PARIS, September.Sweetheart, I too am free! I am the first to write, unless you have already sent to Blois.Raise your great black eyes from my opening sentence and save your excitement for the account of my first love. Why always "first?" Is there to be a second?Do not press; let me tell how I escaped the convent vows. My deliverance was plain: an alarmed conscience overcame prudence. After you left, melancholy seized me; my aunt did not wish me to fade away, my mother relented, and a lady companion with Philippe came from Paris to fetch me.If you had seen me when we parted, you would have known how deeply you had touched me. We had lived so close that our imaginations had become one; absence, however, made me truly Carmelite—dreary, listless, and sick of life. I had been ready for death rather than take vows alone at Blois. The convent routine had earlier been transfigured by our flights of fancy into a world of play; without you, it returned to monotonous duty.My aunt, who had created a small paradise in her two-acre convent, could not understand my revolt. A religious life suits either great simplicity or heroic self-sacrifice; she had sacrificed for family, but she could not ask me the same for an unknown ideal.I buried my reflections and wrote to you as a poor substitute for our talks. I imagine you have begun your journal too, and that soon I shall be at home in your Gemenos valley as you will in my Paris.On the morning of my rescue I left with a lady companion and Philippe. My aunt kissed my forehead with that consuming fire that had blackened her life; I kissed her hands and promised never to return unless heartbroken. She laughed and called me madcap, saying there was more of Montespan in me than La Valliere.At Beaugency I still felt stunned. In Paris, no one received me; my mother was at the Bois de Boulogne, my father at council, my brother late, and Miss Griffith and Philippe conducted me to my rooms. The suite belonged to my beloved grandmother, the Princess de Vauremont, whose memory filled me with grief on seeing the rooms left as when she died. I was to sleep in her bed; sitting on the sofa I burst into tears, overwhelmed by recollection.Philippe told me the Princess's furniture had been left to me in her will, and that my father delayed refurbishing the house until restitution laws might restore lost property—an estimate of three hundred thousand livres by the king's architect. This thought of money and neglect stung: would my father rather renovate than arrange my marriage?Seeing no flesh-and-blood welcome, I consoled myself with the Princess's shade and prepared to keep our pledge to tell one another the smallest domestic details. Send me a faithful picture of your surroundings; omit nothing—even the sunset through the tall trees.October 19th.I arrived at three; at half-past five Rose told me my mother had returned. My mother's suite lies under mine, and a secret staircase serves both. I descended to meet her uncertain of how to behave toward a woman who had shown little maternal feeling. She received me neither with theatrical warmth nor chilling reserve, but as a sincere friend, kissing my forehead and saying: you would be better with your family than dead in a convent; I should like to be a sister to you. She was beautiful at thirty-eight—dark-blue eyes, silk lashes, smooth brow, pink and white complexion, and a splendid figure. I responded like a good child, and her manner grew more engaging; she even put her arm about my neck and kissed me on the forehead.We had people coming to dinner; she advised I not make my first appearance in society until I had seen the dressmaker. I agreed. My father then entered. He played the affectionate father so well that I was persuaded by the performance: he clasped my hands, kissed my forehead, called me his rebel daughter, and presented my brother Rhetore, who greeted me politely. Fearing strangers, I withdrew to prepare my rooms and write to you. Tired from travel and emotion, I went to bed convent-fashion at eight after supper.II. THE SAME TO THE SAME November 25th.Next morning, my rooms were dusted and flowers put in by Philippe. I found convent hours unsuitable to Paris life—Rose complained about my morning appetite—and settled into domestic arrangements. At one, my father came in while I was writing. He handed me a purse containing twelve thousand francs as my annual allowance, and spoke frankly of my fortune: my grandmother left me five hundred thousand francs in government stock, yielding about forty thousand francs a year. He had meant to endow my younger brother with this sum, but my choice upended those plans; he trusted my good sense and reminded me of the family dignity, with a warning about slander.I sat staring at the purse, pleased and vexed. Philippe spent the morning in shops: Victorine the dressmaker, a corset-maker, the shoemaker Janssen, a glovemaker—all busy to fit me for society. My father had refused a ministerial post and preferred an embassy; this preoccupation explained his absent manner. My parents lived like two perfectly matched people with nothing in common beyond name: united for the world, separate at heart.A dancing-master will come daily; I must be ready for a ball in a month. My mother proposed a governess; I kept Miss Griffith—the English-woman recommended by the ambassador. She is thirty-six, well-bred, daughter of a clergyman, and will act as chaperon, sleeping in Rose's room. She is cautious yet kind, and I suspect she will be governed by me.Our household life is a series of fragments: lunch is the time when family meets, otherwise my father and brother are out, my mother absorbed in toilette and receptions. Mornings see her invisible till her drive; evenings are given to theatres, balls, and concerts—Paris holds twelve theatres. It is a strange discovery that members of one family may live side by side and know little of one another. Already I have not walked the streets; my mother has a box at the Italian opera, which I long to see.Miss Griffith, having judged me not a complete ignoramus, warns me not to be dazzled by society and especially by what attracts me most. Her counsel is simple: be on your guard. She has revealed little else.III. THE SAME TO THE SAME December.My Darling,—I am ready to make my bow to the world. I have been rehearsing my transformation—stays, shoes, curls, dress, ornaments. In privacy, I tested my air of triumph; Griffith was my sole spectator. I tried to know myself, and I like what I find.Permit a brief portrait. I am no shy, insignificant thing; there are faults—angular elbows, narrow shoulders—but a bountiful health and vigor, delicate joints, and Greek-correct features. My eyes are insolently blue with pure white and long lashes; my hair ripples pale gold with darker center tones and playful stray locks. The nose is slender and imperious, the neck long and dignified, the mouth full of expression. I have a certain gait and voice—imitative of my grandmother's charm—able to command laughter or a Madonna-like melancholy. In short: lovely and charming, versatile in coquetry.My trousseau is complete—ribbons, shoes, perfume, dressing-case, fan, prayer-book, gold chain, cashmere shawl—thanks to my father, who also promises riding lessons and dancing. Tomorrow evening I am to come out at a ball: white muslin, a garland of white roses in Greek style. I shall wear my Madonna face and play simpleton to win the women.My mother does not suspect these inner combustions; she flatters herself I am destitute of mind. My brother honors me with polite contempt. He is handsome but melancholy, jealous of his father perhaps, with no post in the State. In our house each member pursues separate habits: my father absorbed in affairs and amusements, my mother in social duties, my brother in moods. I have plenty of hours for reflection, far more than they do.I begin to see what strong bonds society exercises—keeping people out until the small hours, consuming money and strength. When I longed for it I could not foresee how it separates lives. Perhaps my father sees the isolation and chooses to look away; there is mystery enough here to explore.Thus, sweetheart, I enter Paris life with curiosity and caution. Keep to me in your letters, tell me every little domestic detail of Maucombe, for in these trifles we sustain one another.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yesterday at two I drove through the Champs-Élysées and the Bois de Boulogne on a Loire-like autumn day; I finally saw Paris. Place Louis XV impressed—a testimony to human artistry.I was impeccably dressed, pensive, and poised, yet received not a single smile. The carriage moved slowly, matching my demeanor. Only one person, a duke—my father—acknowledged me with satisfaction; my mother blew a butterfly kiss. Griffith scanned everyone indiscriminately. A young woman should know where to look.I was furious. A man inspected my carriage without seeing me—probably a carriage-maker. I had misjudged my appeal; beauty, it seems, is common in Paris. Hats were tipped to less deserving people while my mother drew admiration. I resolved to understand why.The men, in general, were unattractive, with a few poor imitations among them. Their fashion lacks the elegance, color, and comfort of former times. Hats, like truncated columns, sit awkwardly; it seems easier to incite a revolution than to design a stylish hat. Yet, despite their fickle reputation, Frenchmen cling to these absurd styles.Their faces were worn, reflecting thwarted ambition and bruised vanity. A fine forehead was rare. “And these are the products of Paris!” I said to Miss Griffith. “Most cultivated and pleasant men,” she replied. I was silent; a thirty-six-year-old spinster’s heart is a well of tolerance.That evening I went to a ball, staying close to my mother, who received all attention. I served as a pretext for compliments aimed at her. My partners all noted the heat and beauty of the ball as if I were freezing and blind; each professed it was his first time seeing me.My dazzling dress paled beside the extravagant attire of married women, each surrounded by admirers and watching one another closely. A few shone with real beauty—my mother among them. At a ball a girl is merely a dancing machine, insignificant.The men impressed me no more there than on the Champs-Élysées: exhausted, bland, and uniform. The proud, vigorous men of our ancestors seem vanished. One man, however, stood out for his beauty yet failed to stir me. I neither knew his works nor his birth; a commoner, however gifted, could not touch me. He seemed absorbed in himself, confirming my suspicion that intellectuals view women as objects. If they love, they should abandon their writing; otherwise their love is not genuine. I learned he is a professor and author, driven by ambition.I resolved not to quarrel with society for failing to recognize my merits and instead embraced the simple joy of dancing, which I thoroughly enjoyed. I overheard much trivial gossip about people I did not know; perhaps my ignorance preserved my pleasure, though many seemed to relish such talk. Society is a web of intrigue, and I am perceptive.Returning home pleasantly tired, I began to share my experiences with my mother. She interrupted, advising me to confide only in her. “My dear child,” she said, “it requires as much tact to know when to be silent as when to speak.”Her counsel revealed feelings that should remain concealed even from a mother. I perceived the vast realm of feminine duplicity. In our innocence we would seem astute gossipers; a mere gesture or glance can teach deep lessons. Suddenly I felt overwhelming shyness. Must I never again express the joy of dancing? What of deeper emotions?I went to bed sorrowful, reeling from the clash between my candid nature and society’s harsh rules. Already the hedges of my life are flecked with white wool. Farewell, beloved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################How deeply your letter moved me, especially when I think of our divergent paths. You are entering a brilliant world; I will end my modest life in a peaceful retreat.In the Castle of Maucombe my room is as I left it, now with a splendid view of the Gemenos valley that I once saw without understanding. Shortly after my arrival my parents took me to dine with M. de l'Estorade, a wealthy old gentleman who prospered by frugality.M. de l'Estorade lost his only son to Bonaparte's conscription in 1813. After evading the draft the son joined the Emperor's bodyguard but was not heard from after the Battle of Leipzig. The Baron clung to hope and hoarded his income for him. Eventually I realized the son's unexpected return explained my own: who could have imagined my future husband was making his way back from Russia, Poland, and Germany? His fortune turned in Berlin, where the French Minister facilitated his return. The Baron, a small landowner in Provence with an income of about ten thousand livres, has not become famed enough to draw attention to his son's adventures.The combined income from the late Mme. de l'Estorade's estate and the father's savings amounts to around two hundred and fifty thousand livres, excluding property—quite a fortune in Provence. The Baron recently bought a poorly managed estate, intending to plant ten thousand mulberry trees. Now that he has found his son, his sole desire is to marry him to a girl of good family.My parents supported the idea once they learned that Renée de Maucombe would be acceptable without a dowry and that her future inheritance would be recognized in the marriage contract. Similarly, my younger brother Jean de Maucombe signed a document acknowledging an advance on his inheritance. This is how the nobles of Provence navigate the restrictive Civil Code of Bonaparte, which sends many girls into convents rather than into marriage.The dinner was the first meeting between your sweetheart and the exile. My father-in-law-to-be has no castle, only a simple country house at the entrance of a beautiful valley. The house is unpretentious, with four pebble walls and a red-tiled roof. The garden is typically Provençal, enclosed by low walls of round pebbles and showing neglect.For all its simplicity, the house of l'Estorade did its best for the dinner, serving us on old silver. The exile, my dear, is like the railing—emaciated and pale, marked by suffering. At thirty-seven he looks much older, with streaks of white in his once-beautiful hair. He is a little deaf, like the Knight of the Sorrowful Countenance.Despite this, I have graciously agreed to become Mme. de l'Estorade with a dowry of two hundred and fifty thousand livres, on the condition that I can transform the grange into a park. I have requested a water grant from my father to help this. In a month I will be Mme. de l'Estorade, having made a good impression. After enduring Siberia, Louis de l'Estorade seems pleased to marry the fair Renée de Maucombe.While you prepare to enjoy Paris's vibrant life, I, Renée, descend from our shared dreams into the humble realities of country life. I am determined to bring joy to this young man, who has known little youth, having gone straight from his mother's arms to war and the harshness of Siberia.I will find pleasure in the quiet country, seeking to enhance the oasis around our home with fine trees and greenery. I envision a park on the hillside, perhaps with a kiosk from which I can glimpse the Mediterranean. I will cultivate orange and lemon trees and embrace the poetry of the surrounding nature. My father-in-law and the Chevalier share my religious sentiments.My life unfolds like a smooth highway, free from the turmoil of another Bonaparte. If I have children, they will be mine to nurture and guide. If you remain true to your destiny, your children will have a devoted protector in me.Thus I bid farewell to the romances and adventures we once imagined. My life’s milestones will now revolve around the care of the young Masters de l'Estorade and the mischief they bring. I will embroider their caps and be cherished by a frail man in the Gemenos valley. Perhaps one day I may visit Marseilles, but the dangers of provincial life are minimal. We will take an interest in the silkworms whose mulberry leaves we sell, and we will share in the ordinary ups and downs of Provence. Quarrels will be unlikely, for M. de l'Estorade has promised to leave household matters to me, and I will ensure he keeps that promise.You, dear Louise, will provide the romance in my life. Share all your adventures, your balls and parties, your attire, and the men you meet. Your other self will always be with you, listening and feeling the excitement. If only I could enjoy some Parisian fun while you managed the household at La Crampade, the name of our grange. Poor M. de l'Estorade thinks he is marrying one woman—will he discover there are two?I am rambling, and since I can only be foolish through you, I should conclude. One kiss on each cheek—my lips remain untouched, as he has only taken my hand. Our propriety is quite amusing, I assure you. I must go now. Goodbye, dear.P.S. I have just opened your third letter. I have about one thousand livres to spend; please buy me pretty things we cannot find here or even in Marseilles. While you pursue your affairs, think of the recluse at La Crampade. Remember, neither family has anyone with taste in Paris to make purchases. I will answer your letter later.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################VI. DON FELIPE HENAREZ TO DON FERNAND PARIS, September.The heading shows the head of your house is safe. The massacre at the Court of Lions forced us into a double identity, but it taught me Arab cunning that may have saved my life.Ferdinand's fear made his acting persuasive enough that Valdez believed him; without my intervention the Admiral would have died. The Liberals still misunderstand kingship. I know this Bourbon well: his repeated promises of protection confirm my doubts. A true Spaniard need not repeat assurances; excess words often signal deceit.Valdez left on an English ship. When I saw our cause falter in Andalusia, I put my escape in motion. I instructed my steward and sent coral fishers to meet me on a coast; while Ferdinand tried to seize me, I was already in my barony of Macumer among brigands who defy authority.The last Hispano-Moorish family of Granada has taken refuge in an African desert; even a Saracen horse remains on an estate inherited from Saracens. Those brigands, who once feared my authority, now shelter me from the King’s vendetta, proud to defend the Duc de Soria, a Henarez—the first of my line to visit them since the Moors ruled.I had hoped to live off this estate’s income, but Queverdo was right. He had twenty-two lives at my disposal but no true establishment: vast prairies without houses, virgin forests with no furniture. A million piastres and a resident steward for fifty years would be needed to make these lands profitable. I must address this.The conquered have time to reflect. Seeing my noble country reduced to a corpse for monks to exploit moved me to tears; it foreshadows Spain’s grim future.In Marseilles I learned of Riego's fall. It struck me my life would be a prolonged unnoticed martyrdom. Is existence worth living when one can neither die for country nor live for a woman? To love and conquer—this was our law. Yet my sabre is broken, the palace in ruins, the living spring dried in barren sand.Here is my last will and testament.Don Fernand, this is why I restrained your enthusiasm and urged loyalty to the rey netto. As your brother and friend I implore you; as your master I command: approach the King and claim my dignities, property, offices, and titles. He may hesitate, but tell him you are loved by Marie Heredia and she can marry only a Duc de Soria. This will please the King: the Heredia fortune has long blocked my ruin. Your proposal will seem to deprive me of a final resource, and he will gladly hand over my spoils.You will then marry Marie. Her secret love for you was no secret to me; I have prepared the old Count for your ascension. Marie and I have fulfilled our family duties; everything favors you. You are handsome and possess Marie's heart. I am an ill-favored grandee for whom she harbors an unconfessed aversion; any reluctance she might feel over my misfortunes you will overcome.As Duc de Soria, you will not regret my predecessor nor lose a maravedi. I will keep my mother's diamonds for my independence; the gap they leave can be filled by Marie's jewels. Send them with my nurse, old Urraca—the only servant I shall retain. No one else makes my chocolate as she does.During our brief revolution my needs were bare and covered by my salary. You will find the income from the last two years with your steward. This sum is mine, but a Duc de Soria cannot marry without expense, so we will share it. You will not refuse this wedding gift from your brigand brother; I intend it so.The barony of Macumer, not Spanish territory, remains mine. Thus I still have a country and a name should I wish to re-enter the world.Thank Heaven, this concludes our business, and the house of Soria is saved!Just as I resign myself to being merely Baron de Macumer, French cannon announce the arrival of the Duc d'Angouleme. You will understand why I must stop here.October.When I arrived I had not ten doubloons. A poor leader finds means to live amid calamity: for the vanquished Moor, a horse and the desert; for the frustrated Christian, the cloister and a few gold pieces.My resignation is weariness, but I am not ready to abandon all thoughts of life. Ozalga gave letters of introduction, including one to a bookseller who serves our compatriots as Galignani does the English. He found me eight pupils at three francs a lesson. I teach every other day, earning twelve francs—more than I need. When Urraca arrives I will pass this connection to another Spanish exile.I lodge in Rue Hillerin-Bertin with a poor widow who takes boarders. My room faces south, a small garden beyond. It is quiet; I have green trees to gaze on, and I spend one piastre a day. I am amazed at the calm pleasure of this life, reminiscent of Dionysius at Corinth. From sunrise until ten I smoke and enjoy chocolate, contemplating two Spanish plants—a broom rising from jessamine—gold on white, colors that thrill any scion of the Moors. At ten I give lessons until four, return for dinner, then read and smoke until bed.I can endure this life of work, meditation, solitude, and society for a long time. Be happy, Fernand; my abdication brings no regrets. Five days have passed since I wrote my will; they feel like five centuries. Honor, titles, wealth—as if they never existed.Now that the conventional barriers of respect are down, I open my heart to you, dear boy. Though outwardly grave, my heart is full of tenderness and devotion—never given to any object, not even she who was to be my bride since childhood. This is the source of my political enthusiasm: Spain took the place of a mistress and received my homage. Now Spain is lost. Beggared of all, I gaze upon the ruins of my former self and ponder the mystery of my existence.Why was life given to this carcass, and when will it depart? Why did a race famed for chivalry infuse all its virtues into this last offshoot only for the seed not to sprout—no stem, no flower to scatter its Eastern perfumes? What crime did I commit before birth to inspire no love? Was I destined to be stranded, a useless hulk on a barren shore? In my soul I see deserts where my ancestors roamed under a scorching sun that shrivels life. Proud remnant of a fallen race, vain force, love wasted—an old man in the prime of youth—I shall await death's last grace. Alas! under this murky sky no spark will rekindle these ashes. Thus my last words may echo Christ's: "My God, Thou hast forsaken me!"—a cry of agony and terror whose depth few mortals have explored.You can now understand, Fernand, the joy I find in living through you and Marie. I will watch you with the pride of a creator satisfied with his work. Love each other well and continue to do so; any discord between you would pain me more than it would you.Our mother had a premonition that events would one day fulfill her wishes. Perhaps a mother's longing is a pact with God. Was she not one of those beings who converse with Heaven and return with visions? How often I read in her brow that she coveted for Fernand the honors and wealth of Felipe! When I mentioned this she would weep, revealing a heart that rightly belonged to both her sons, yet was given to you alone by an irresistible passion.Her spirit will hover above you as you bow at the altar. Mother, have you no caress for your Felipe now that he has yielded to your favorite even the girl you regretfully thrust into his arms? What I have done pleases our womenfolk, the dead, and the King; it is God's will. Do not resist, Fernand; obey and remain silent.P.S. Tell Urraca to call me nothing but M. Henarez. Do not mention me to Marie. You must be the sole living soul to know the secrets of the last Christianized Moor, whose veins carry the blood of a great family that began in the desert and is now about to die out in solitary exile.Farewell.VII. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBEWhat! Married so soon? Unheard of. After a month you become engaged to a stranger about whom you know nothing. He may be deaf—there are many kinds of deafness! He could be sickly, tiresome, insufferable!Don’t you see, Renée, what they want from you? You’re needed to carry on the glorious stock of the l’Estorades, that is all. You will be buried in the provinces. Are these the promises we made each other? Were I you, I’d rather set off to the Hyères islands in a caique, risk capture by an Algerian corsair, and be sold to the Grand Turk. Then I could be a Sultana and make a stir in the harem while I’m young—yes, and afterwards too!You are leaving one convent for another. I know you; you are a coward and will submit to family life with docility. But I will guide you; come to Paris. There we shall drive men wild and hold court like queens. Your husband may join the Chamber in three years. I know how those members work; you will work that machine well; you can live in Paris and become, as my mother calls it, a woman of fashion. Oh! do not think I will leave you in your grange!Monday.For a fortnight I have lived the life of society: one evening at the Italiens, another at the Grand Opera, and always a ball afterward. Society bewitches. The Opera’s music enchants me; while my soul is immersed, I am the focus of admiration and opera glasses. But a single glance can make the boldest youth drop his eyes.I’ve seen charming young men, yet none have stirred me; not one has evoked the emotion I feel when listening to García in his splendid duet with Pellegrini in Otello. How jealous Rossini must have been to express jealousy so well! What a cry in "Il mio cor si divide!" I speak Greek to you, for you’ve not heard García, but you know how jealous I am!What a wretched dramatist Shakespeare is! Othello wins battles and leaves Desdemona meekly neglected. Such a sheep deserves slaughter. Let the man I love beware of thinking of anything but loving me!I prefer the long trials of old chivalry. That lout of a young lord who took offense because his sovereign lady sent him among the lions to fetch her glove was impertinent. Doubtless the lady had some exquisite prize for him, which he lost—the puppy!But I ramble. My father is likely to represent our master the King in Madrid. I say "our" master, for I shall be part of the embassy. My mother wishes to remain; my father will take me to have a woman with him.This seems simple, but there are complications. In a fortnight I have probed the household’s secrets. My mother would accompany my father to Madrid if he took M. de Canalis as secretary. But the King appoints secretaries; the Duke cannot offend the King, who hates opposition. The cunning diplomat thinks he cut the knot by leaving the Duchess here. M. de Canalis, the great poet of the day, cultivates my mother’s society and studies diplomacy with her from three to five. Diplomacy must be a fine subject, for he keeps at it as regularly as a gambler at the Stock Exchange.The Duc de Rhetore, our elder brother, solemn and capricious, will remain in Paris. Miss Griffith has discovered Alphonse is in love with a ballet girl at the Opera. How can one fall in love with legs and pirouettes? Our brother attends only when Tullia dances; he applauds and leaves. Two ballet girls in a family are more destructive than the plague. My second brother is with his regiment; I have not seen him. Thus I must act as the Antigone of His Majesty's ambassador. Perhaps I may marry in Spain; perhaps my father imagines a marriage without a dowry. He asked if I’d accompany him and offered me the use of his Spanish master."Spain, the country for castles in the air!" I exclaimed. "Perhaps you hope it will mean marriages for me!"He only gave a knowing look. For days he has teased me at lunch; he watches me, and I dissemble, playing cruelly with him as father and ambassador in petto. He asked my opinion of some young men and girls. I answered with inane remarks about hair, faces, and figures. He seemed disappointed by my crassness and inwardly blamed himself for asking."Still, father," I added, "don’t suppose I’m saying what I really think: mother made me afraid the other day that I had spoken too frankly about my impressions.""With your family, you can speak quite freely," said my mother."Very well, then," I continued. "The young men I’ve met are too self-centered to excite interest; they’re taken up with themselves. They put on an expression to talk to us, then drop it, apparently satisfied that we don’t use our eyes. The man as he converses is the lover; silent, he is the husband. The girls are so artificial that it’s impossible to know them, except from their dancing; their figures are not a sham. But the alarming thing is society’s brutality. Small incidents when supper is announced give one an idea—comparing small things with great—of what a popular uprising might be. Courtesy is a thin veneer over general selfishness. I imagined society to be very different. Women count for little in it; perhaps this is a survival of Bonapartist ideas.""Armande is coming on extraordinarily," said my mother."Mother, did you think I would never get beyond asking to see Mme. de Staël?"My father smiled and rose.Saturday.I left out one thing. The love we pictured is extremely well hidden; I have seen no trace. True, quick exchanges of glances occur in drawing-rooms, but how colorless it all is! Love as we imagined it—a world of wonders, glorious dreams, charming realities, sorrows that awaken sympathy, smiles bringing sunshine—does not exist. The bewitching words, constant interchange of happiness, misery of absence, joy of the beloved’s presence—where are they? What soil produces these radiant flowers of the soul? Which is wrong? We or the world?I have seen hundreds of men; not one has moved me. No proof of admiration or devotion on their part, not even a sword drawn in my defense, would have touched me. Love is so rare that one might live a lifetime without meeting the being who can make one happy. The thought chills; if found too late, what then?For days I have trembled at the thought of women’s destinies and begun to understand why so many wear sad faces beneath social excitement. Marriage is mere chance. Look at yours. A storm of wild thoughts swept me. To be loved every day the same, yet with a difference, to be loved as much after ten years as on the first day—such love demands years. The lover must languish; curiosity must be piqued and satisfied, feelings stirred and reciprocated.Is there a law for the inner fruits of the heart, as for nature's visible fruits? Can joy be made lasting? In what measure should love mix tears with pleasure? The convent’s monotony seems the only real life; while wealth, splendor, tears, delights, triumph, joy, and the satisfaction of an equal, shared, sanctioned love appear idle visions.I see no room in this city for the gentle ways of love—precious walks, the full moon on water, the suitor pleading in vain. Rich, young, and beautiful, I have only to love, and love would be my life; yet in three months nothing has appealed to me among the bright, covetous eyes here. No voice has thrilled me, no glance brightened my world.Music alone has filled my soul; it has even taken the place of our friendship. At night I linger by my window, summoning the future from its mystery. Sometimes, setting out to drive, I dismount and walk the Champs-Élysées imagining the man who will awaken my slumbering soul is near. Instead I meet mountebanks, vendors, jugglers, and secret lovers. I am tempted to stop them, ask, "You who are happy, tell me what is love." But I repress the impulse and return to my carriage, swearing to die an old maid. Love is an incarnation, and how many conditions must be met before it can occur! We are not certain we shall never quarrel with ourselves; how much less certain with another? Only God can solve this.I begin to think I’ll return to the convent. If I stay in society I may do foolish things, for I cannot reconcile what I see with my sense of delicacy and principle.Ah! my mother is the happiest, adored by Canalis, her great little man. I sometimes crave to know what goes on between them. Griffith tells me she has passed through such moods; she has longed to lash out at women whose happiness is evident and blackened their characters. According to her, virtue is burying savage instincts in one’s heart. But then what becomes of the heart? It becomes a sink for the worst in us.It is humiliating that no admirer has yet appeared for me. I am marriageable, but having brothers and a family sensitive about honor may keep men away. If that is why, they are cowards.The parts of Chimène in the Cid and the Cid himself delight me. What a marvelous play! Farewell.VIII. THE SAME TO THE SAME, January.Our master is a poor refugee, hiding because of his part in the revolution quelled by the Duc d’Angouleme—a triumph that has led to splendid fêtes. Though a Liberal and likely a man of the people, he has piqued my interest; I suspect he was condemned to death. I make him talk to uncover his secret, but he has a truly Castilian taciturnity, proud as if he were Gonsalvo di Cordova, yet angelic in patience and gentleness. His pride is not irritable like Miss Griffith’s; it belongs to his nature, compelling civility through perfect manners and keeping us at a distance with the respect he shows.My father says much nobility dwells in Señor Henarez, whom he humorously calls Don Henarez. A few days ago I took the liberty of thus addressing him. He raised his eyes—usually cast down—and flashed a look that abashed me; he certainly has the most beautiful eyes imaginable. I asked if I had offended, and he replied in grand, rolling Spanish:"I am here only to teach you Spanish."I blushed and felt snubbed. I nearly retorted sharply, but remembering what our dear mother used to say, I instead replied:"It would be kind to tell me if you have any complaints."A tremor passed through him; the blood rose in his olive cheeks. He answered with some emotion:"Religion must have taught you, better than I can, to respect the unhappy. Had I been a don in Spain and lost everything in Ferdinand VII.'s triumph, your witticism would be unkind; but if I am only a poor language teacher, is it not heartless satire? Neither is worthy of a young lady of rank."I took his hand, saying:"In the name of religion, I beg you to pardon me."He bowed, opened my Don Quixote, and sat.This little incident disturbed me more than a harvest of compliments and glances. During the lesson I watched him closely, which I could do safely, as he never looks at me.From my observations, the tutor, whom we assumed to be forty, is under thirty. My governess remarked on the beauty of his black hair and pearly teeth. His eyes are velvet and fire, but he is plain and insignificant. Though Spaniards are often called unclean, this man is meticulously groomed, with hands whiter than his face. He stoops slightly and has an odd, large head. Smallpox scars give his ugliness a piquancy; his prominent forehead and shaggy brows give a harsh, frowning, plaintive look, like a sickly child whose life depends on superhuman care. As my father noted, his features are a shrunken Cardinal Ximenes. The natural dignity of his manners disconcerts the dear Duke, who dislikes him and never feels at ease before superiority.Once my father knows enough Spanish we will go to Madrid. When Henarez returned two days after his reprimand, I said to show gratitude:"I have no doubt you left Spain because of political events. If my father is sent there, as seems likely, we might help you obtain a pardon, if you are under sentence.""It is impossible for anyone to help me," he replied."But," I pressed, "is that because you refuse help or because the situation is impossible?""Both," he said, bowing, in a tone that closed the matter.My father's blood boiled. I was offended by this haughty posture and promptly dropped Señor Henarez.Yet something admirable lay in his refusal of aid. "He would not accept even our friendship," I thought while conjugating a verb. Suddenly I stopped and spoke to him in Spanish. Henarez replied that equality of sentiment is necessary between friends, which did not exist here, making the matter pointless."Do you mean equality in feeling, or equality in rank?" I demanded, eager to provoke him.He raised those awe-inspiring eyes, and mine fell. Dear, this man is a hopeless enigma. He seemed to ask whether my words implied love; the mixture of joy, pride, and agonized doubt in his glance moved me. It was clear that advances, taken lightly in France, might complicate with a Spaniard, and I retreated, feeling foolish.The lesson ended; he bowed, and his eyes expressed a humble prayer: "Don't trifle with a poor wretch."This sudden variance from his usual grave dignity made a deep impression. It seems horrible to think and say, but I can’t help believing there are treasures of affection in that man.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################All is over, my dear child, and it is Mme. de l'Estorade who writes to you. Between us there is no change; it is only a girl the less.Don't be troubled; I did not give my consent recklessly. My life is now mapped out, and the certainty of my path suits me. A great moral power has removed chance from my life. We have property to develop and a home to beautify; I have a household to manage and a husband to reconcile to life. I shall likely have children to care for and educate.Everyday life cannot be cast in a heroic mould, yet I can still explore the infinite in our familiar craft. My humble duties do allow for passion; restoring faith in happiness to those who have suffered is a noble task that will relieve the monotony of existence. I foresee little suffering and much good to be done. My love for Louis de l'Estorade does not make my heart race, but there is nothing in him that offends me.You may wonder about my instincts for greatness and the mental energies that bind us. That thought has troubled me, but these faculties remain mine, even if concealed and employed in secret for the welfare of our family. The time for a woman to seek admiration is short; if my life is not grand, it will at least be calm and free from shocks.Nature has favored our sex with a choice between love and motherhood. I have made mine. My children will be my gods, and this spot of earth my Eldorado.I can say no more today. Thank you for all you have sent me. Please look at my needs on the enclosed list. I am determined to live in an atmosphere of refinement and luxury, taking from provincial life only what adds to its charm. In solitude a woman is never vulgarized—she remains herself. I count on your kindness to keep me fashionable. My father-in-law is delighted and refuses me nothing; he is turning his house upside down. We are bringing in workmen from Paris to renovate everything.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE January.Oh, Renée, you have made me miserable! Your beauty, proud features, graceful manner, noble soul, and exquisite heart are doomed to be wasted in a dull marriage you will come to despise. I already loathe the idea of your children; they will be monsters!You seem resigned, without hope or fear. Yet what if some glorious morning the man who could awaken you appears? The thought chills me.At least you will have a friend. You will become the guardian angel of your valley and learn its beauty until it becomes part of you. As you walk with your husband and children, I foresee your letters: the valley, hills, and meadows reflecting the monotony of your soul. I will be there, a heart untouched by social pettiness, all your own.Monday.My Spaniard is adorably melancholic, with a calm, severe dignity that intrigues me. His solemnity and silence provoke my curiosity. How strange that a language teacher captivates me more than any nobleman or officer I’ve met.His coldness amuses and irritates me. He keeps his distance, wrapped in pride and mystery, while I am bold. What is a man, a Spaniard, a teacher to me? I regard no man as significant, not even a king. I jest that I would have made a slave of Napoleon!Yesterday I made a remark to M. Henarez that must have struck him; he did not respond, and the lesson ended with a bow that implied he would not return. That suits me; I do not want a new Nouvelle Héloïse. I recently read Rousseau and found it distasteful—passion that argues and moralizes seems detestable. Clarissa delights in her long letter, and my father praises Richardson’s portrait of English women, while Rousseau reads like a philosophical sermon in letters.To me, love is a subjective poem. Everything written about it is both false and true. Since you will be an authority on married love, I feel called to remain unmarried and pursue a grand passion, to enrich whatever experience we share.Tell me every detail of your first days with that strange creature called a husband. I promise the same if I ever find love.Farewell, poor martyred darling.XI. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU La Crampade.Your Spaniard and you make me shudder, my darling. Dismiss him. Everything you say fits the dangerous adventurer who risks everything. He cannot be your husband or lover. I will write more about my married life when my heart is less shaken by your last letter.XII. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE February.This morning my father visited, more thoughtful than usual. He asked me to sit and then flattered me, admitting he had misjudged my intelligence and claiming I alone could grasp his political concerns. He launched into a sober account: France is in a critical state, understood by the King and a few others; the King is a head without arms, and nobles lack authority over elected men who refuse to cooperate.He described the Revolution as still active in laws and opinions, lamented that the family has ceased to exist in France, replaced by individuals and the State treasury. He argued that a country cannot be stable without paternal authority and urged me to consider the role of family in building a strong state. He warned of calamities if their party fails. When I asked how I could help, as I felt no vocation for martyrdom, he playfully called me a little hussy and laughed. He then asked my help persuading him to sacrifice my inheritance for my younger brother's estate. I questioned whether I could still enjoy life if I gave up my fortune. He insisted my future husband would not take me without a dowry; perhaps an older peer might be an option. We parted in good humor.Your last letter made me reflect on the abyss you warned about. I took precautions regarding Henarez, whose gaze unsettles me. I hesitated to tell my father I wanted no more lessons, but I find myself drawn to him.His voice is thrilling, his manners simple and genuine. When he left, he almost reached for my hand to kiss it but stopped, terrified. I smiled, understanding the courage it takes for a plebeian to love a noblewoman. My smile encouraged him; I handed him his hat with gravity. His eyes were wet with unshed tears, and in that moment I felt a strange happiness. I cannot fully explain it, but he has fascinated me.Friday.He is handsome and speaks admirably, with remarkable intellectual power. He has revealed a new world of thought. He told a story about a valet who died for a queen's glance, and I was captivated.At a ball, M. de Vandenesse told me Henarez is of noble descent—the Duc de Soria—whose titles were confiscated by King Ferdinand. This left me in suspense until our next lesson.During that lesson I confronted him, suspecting he was the Duc de Soria. He denied it, saying he was merely the servant of the King of Spain. His reticence provoked me, and I devised a practical test: I asked him to translate a letter I wrote describing my ideal lover.He was visibly moved by my words and asked to keep the letter as a memory. He left asking to speak with my father, and from my father I learned Henarez is now the Baron de Macumer, with property in Sardinia.It has been two weeks since I last saw him, and I wonder what he is doing. I feel he has become necessary to me, and I am torn between love and the realities of my situation.Sunday.At the Italian Opera, I felt his gaze upon me; his fiery eyes drew me. He has a genius for love.XIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU LA CRAMPADE, February.My dear Louise, I waited to write until I had learned much for your good. The difference between a girl and a married woman is vast; the girl cannot comprehend it, nor can the married woman return to girlhood.I chose to marry Louis de l'Estorade rather than return to the convent. The seriousness of marriage filled me with terror, but I realized friendship might be the key to a happy union. I accepted his love, believing it would not be slavery but a reign. We agreed to separate marriage from marital rights, to keep our independence. I watched Louis closely, wondering if suffering had softened or hardened him. I concluded his love was a passion, and I could risk much.I proposed a friendship that would let us bear life's burdens together. I asked him to leave me free, promising to consider his feelings. He was overjoyed, and we married soon after.I am nearly happy now, but I must conceal my happiness from him, as he is sensitive. For three months we lived as before, and I studied our relationship. I saw how my presence transformed him, and I became accustomed to him.I realized I could bring new life to my husband before bearing him children. His happiness brightened our home; even my father became generous. I manage the household with economy, aiming for pleasure without excess.I have ambitious plans for Louis, hoping to see him in politics. He has embraced my plans, showing the depth of his affection.To conclude, here is a letter he wrote, expressing his love and admiration: he sees me as a treasure, and I feel proud of our bond.XIV. THE DUC DE SORIA TO THE BARON DE MACUMER MADRID.My dear brother, I did not make you Duc de Soria to betray the name. I cannot tolerate that you are in exile while we enjoy happiness. Marie and I will not marry until you accept the money Urraca offers.We have prayed for your happiness; I believe Heaven will send you the love you seek. I have taken Marie, and I am grateful to you for that.You can write to us with confidence through the Nuncio. Farewell, dear brother. Be proud of the happiness you have brought us.XV. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE March.Ah, my love, marriage is making a philosopher of you! Your views on life and duty trouble me. Do you think you can convert me to matrimony with your cautious arithmetic?We left Blois innocent, armed with the weapons of meditation, and now those weapons have turned against you! If I did not know you were pure, I would call your calculations vice.You have made a holocaust of your youth, becoming a miser before your time. If Louis loves you, he will never realize you act from necessity. Love is the first principle of all virtues; I cannot believe you are justifying your role as a married woman with such prudent measures. You are acting a man's part, and Louis will have to play the woman!Your letter plunged me into reflection. I see now the convent cannot replace a mother. Please, do not turn away from my cries!Monday.Last night I looked at the stars and saw my Spaniard in a tree, admiring my window. I was overwhelmed with joy and fear.He had blown a letter to me through the window, but I burned it without reading; it felt improper. At the opera he was present, and I learned he had performed a noble action by renouncing a rich heiress for another man. I was wretched for burning his first letter—what would he have said?
