To be loved in secret, to know a man is devoted—how can I resist?When I returned home, I opened my window hoping he would be there. He was, and I received another letter, which I read and cherished.XVI. THE SAME TO THE SAME March.I am dressed in white, camellias in my hair, and have a strange longing to delay my decision and make him wait.What a vision of beauty! I drove my barouche, and when I saw him, I felt a thrill. I complimented him on his horse; he said it was a gift from his steward.Henarez is no longer a simple man; he is becoming more distinguished.Oh, my dear, do my love affairs amuse you? I am a little off my head and must stop.XVII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE CHAULIEU April.My angel, you have grieved me. You have not realized the force of the word indissoluble in marriage.Each marriage has its own laws, shaped by place and character. A wife of a leader must resign to his guidance, while a wife of a fool must take control. You speak of vice, but passion can be admirable when it springs from the heart. Sooner or later, you will understand we are answerable only to God and ourselves for our happiness.You will find a wife’s calculation necessary to maintain happiness. I have reflected on the duties of a wife and mother, and I believe a sublime hypocrisy—small, conscious sacrifices and concessions—is required to attain the ideal of a perfect woman within society.I persist in my principles, believing virtue varies with surroundings. If I wished to make Louis unhappy, I would leave the helm in his hands; instead I help steer. I may not have married a man of distinction, but I hope to help him on that path. Five years from now we will see if I was mistaken.You have taught me what a woman sacrifices in marriage. Regret is not remorse, but I feel the loss of freedom. I have read Bonald and seen the strength of the family.Farewell, my dear friend.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Well, my Renee, you are a wonderful woman, and I admit we can be virtuous only by cheating. Will that satisfy you? The man who loves us is our property; we may make a fool or a genius of him as we please—though the fool is common. You will make yours a genius and keep the secret—two heroic acts!If there were no afterlife, how nicely you would be sold, for this is the martyrdom you choose. You want to make him ambitious and keep him in love! Child, surely the latter alone is enough.Tell me, to what extent is calculation a virtue, or is virtue calculation? You won’t say? No matter—we have Bonald to refer to. We are, and mean to remain, virtuous; yet I think you, with all your little knavery, are a better woman than I.Yes, I am shockingly deceitful. I love Felipe and hide it from him with hypocrisy. I long to see him leap from his tree to my balcony—and if he did, how I would wither him with my scorn! I confess this to you.What restrains me? What mysterious power prevents me from telling Felipe how supremely happy he has made me with his pure, overflowing love?Mme. de Mirbel is painting my portrait, which I intend for him. Love animates life; every hour, every trifle becomes charged, blurring past, present, and future. Is happiness soothing or exciting? I am restless, drawn to him against reason and propriety. At least now I better understand your feelings.Felipe’s happiness is feeling himself mine; his aloofness and strict obedience irritate me, as his profound respect did when he was my Spanish master. I want to cry, "Fool, if you love me as a picture, what will it be when you know the real me?"Oh! Renee, you burn my letters, don’t you? I will burn yours. If anyone else read these thoughts, I would send Felipe to silence them—perhaps even worse.Monday.How can one fathom the heart of man? My father should introduce me to M. Bonald; I would ask him. I envy God, who can read the heart’s undercurrents.Does he still worship? That is the question. If I ever catch the least decline in the respect he once showed me as my instructor, I feel I could end it. "Why such fine words, such grand resolutions?" you will say. I will tell you why.My charming father, who treats me with the devotion of an Italian cavaliere servente, had my portrait painted by Mme. de Mirbel. I had a copy made for the Duke and sent the original to Felipe with these lines:"Don Felipe, your single-hearted devotion is met by a blind confidence. Time will show whether this is to treat a man as more than human."It was a high reward—both promise and challenge, intended without full commitment. If his reply contained "Dear Louise!" or even "Louise," he would be done for!Tuesday.No, he is not done for. The constitutional minister is perfect as a lover. Here is his letter:"Every moment away from you has been filled with ideal pictures of you; my eyes closed to the world, fixed on your image. Henceforth my gaze will rest upon this wondrous ivory—this talisman—since your blue eyes sparkle with life when I look, and paint becomes flesh and blood. If I delayed writing, it was because I could not tear myself from your presence, which wrung from me what I must normally keep secret."Closeted with you all last night and today, I have for the first time surrendered to complete happiness. If you could see where I have placed you—between the Virgin and God—you might comprehend the agony of my night. I would not offend you by speaking of it; a glance from you, stripped of tender sweetness, would be torture. Queen of my life and soul, could you grant me but a fraction of the love I bear you!"This was the burden of my prayer; doubt ravaged me as I swung between belief and despair. A criminal awaiting verdict is not more racked than I, in owning my temerity. The smile on your lips, which alone can calm my dread of displeasing you, haunts me. From birth, no one, not even my mother, has smiled on me. The girl meant for me rejected my heart for my brother. In politics all my labors have failed. In the king's eyes I read thirst for vengeance; he has been my enemy since childhood, and the Cortes' vote that placed me in power felt like an insult."Such trials might breed despondency in the stoutest heart. I have no illusions; I know my graceless person and how hard it is to show the heart within this rugged shell. To be loved had ceased to be more than a dream when I met you. Thus, when I bound myself to your service, I knew that devotion alone could excuse my passion."But as I gaze at this portrait and hear your smile whisper of rapture, shafts of hope pierce the gloom, only to be veiled again by mists of doubt and fear of displeasing you. It is impossible you should love me yet—I feel it; but in time, as you see the strength, constancy, and depth of my affection, you may yield me some foothold in your heart. If my daring offends, tell me kindly, and I will resume my old place. But if you consent to try and love me, be merciful and break it gently to one who has placed his happiness in serving you."My dear, as I read these last words he seemed to rise before me, pale as the night when the camellias told their tale and he understood his offering was accepted. These humble words differed from lovers' usual flowery rhetoric; a wave of feeling passed over me—it was the breath of happiness.The weather has been atrocious; impossible to go to the Bois without arousing suspicion. Even my mother, who often braves rain, stayed home.Wednesday evening.I saw him at the Opera; he was another man. He came to our box, introduced by the Sardinian ambassador.Having read from my eyes that his audacity was welcome, he seemed awkward, suddenly conscious of his limbs, addressing the Marquise d'Espard as "mademoiselle." A light, brighter than the chandeliers, flashed from his eyes. He left with the air of a man unsure of his next step."The Baron de Macumer is in love!" exclaimed Mme. de Maufrigneuse."Strange, isn’t it, for a fallen minister?" my mother replied.I kept composure, regarding Mmes. de Maufrigneuse and d'Espard and my mother with curiosity, as if they spoke a foreign tongue; inwardly my soul sank in waves of intoxicating joy. There is one word for what I felt: rapture. Such love as Felipe's makes him worthy of mine. I am his very breath; my hands guide his thoughts. I long to see him surmount every obstacle and stand before me, boldly asking for my hand. Then I would know whether this storm of love would calm at a single glance.Ah, my dear, I stopped writing because I was trembling. As I wrote, I heard a noise outside and rose to see. From my window I saw him coming along the wall at the risk of his life. I went to the bedroom window and made him a sign; it sufficed. He leaped from the wall—ten feet—and ran along the road, showing he was unharmed. That he thought of my fear at that moment moved me so I am still crying; I don’t know why. Poor ungainly man! What was he coming for? What had he to tell me?I dare not write all my thoughts and will go to bed joyful, imagining what we would say together. Farewell, fair silent one. I have no time to scold you for not writing; it has been over a month since I heard from you! Does that mean you are finally happy? Have you lost the "complete independence" you once boasted of—which nearly betrayed me tonight?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################If love is life’s essence, why do philosophers dismiss it in marriage? Society demands women sacrifice for family, breeding marital discord. That discord was predicted, prompting men to devise defenses against women; without them, their theories might be overturned by love or aversion.Marriage, as practiced, contains two opposing forces the law should reconcile. Your letter challenges foundations laid by the sage of Aveyron, under which I once found comfort. Laws fashioned by men accept conjugal love stripped of passion, permitting women to yield without love’s sanction, reflecting Nature’s emphasis on procreation.Once a person, I now feel like property. I have swallowed many tears alone, aching for a comforting smile. Why are our fates so different? You thrive in lawful love, where virtue aligns with pleasure, while I confront an uncertain future.You are loved and adored. Embrace this noble romance that has long been our dream. Your beauty, refined and spiritual, was made to enchant. Guard your heart’s secret and test Felipe’s love, but, above all, test your own. Novelty and the illusion of happiness easily mislead.You remain independent, dear friend; I urge you not to rush into marriage without assurance. In intimate conversation, a gesture or word can reveal hidden depths. You have the courage to explore where others falter.I watch you with anxious sympathy from afar; my heart beats with yours. Write to me about everything; your letters ignite passion in my otherwise mundane life. I oscillate between despair and hope—perhaps expecting too much. In youth we often believe that ideals and reality will align.Seated beneath a rock in my park, I concluded that love in marriage is a fortunate accident, not a universal rule. The philosopher rightly views the family as society’s unit, historically placing women in subservience. The resolution to our predicament lies in motherhood, which I yearn for to channel my energy and heart.Louis remains cheerful; his love is active while mine is passive. He enjoys the flowers of my affection without acknowledging the toil behind them. I play the devoted mother, sacrificing for his happiness, which in turn gives me joy. I await the rewards of these sacrifices, hoping motherhood will fulfill my aspirations and compensate for what I surrender.---Dear wedded friend, your letter arrived just as I contemplated a bold step. I feel a strange yearning for the unknown, revealing a conflict between societal and natural laws within me. I want to speak with Felipe alone, beneath the lime trees in our garden. This suits the modern girl I’ve become, a fact my father recognizes.There is method in this seeming madness: I wish to reward Felipe for his nights beneath my window while testing his reaction to my boldness. If he respects my folly, he may imagine me as his wife; if not, farewell, Don Felipe.Meeting him this way seems less risky than encounters in society where prying eyes abound. I cannot express the agitation this idea brings me, nor how I longed for your company to share the anticipation of our first clandestine meeting.I sent Felipe a tracing of the garden key with a note urging him to stop his reckless behavior, warning that if he fell it would tarnish my reputation. Yesterday I took Griffith to the garden, and after a delicious moment of tension, I saw him approach like a shadow.When he reached the garden, I introduced him to Griffith, who withdrew a few steps. The night was mild and heavy with scent; I felt intoxicated by the thrill of being almost alone with him. We savored the moment before I urged him to abandon his reckless climbing, fearing scandal.His pallor revealed fear; I reminded him that if he had been caught, suspicion would fall on my family. He apologized, and I reassured him my actions were not as mad as they seemed.After a few moments of silence he expressed joy and reverence for me, acknowledging that my actions were sacred. I told him I was on the path to loving the man who understands me, but that my heart would belong only to someone who truly knows it.He expressed gratitude for my acceptance; I noted he should prove himself worthy of my affection. We walked and talked; his tenderness reminded me of divine love. His low, melodic voice conveyed his feelings beautifully.I dismissed him, aware of our meeting’s implications. Griffith remarked on my commitment, but I assured her that in France my intentions were merely exploratory.Once again I met Felipe; he appeared timid and reverent, which I found appealing. He seemed to sense my power, yet remained unaware of the depth of my feelings. I am still fond of him, but I keep my composure.Love appears in two forms: one that commands and one that obeys. Can both coexist? Will Felipe ever become my master? These questions unsettle me.He remains oblivious to my true nature. If I were in his place, I would see Mlle. de Chaulieu as a calculating coquette. I still care for Felipe, but I feel calm and in control now. The thrill of uncertainty has faded, and I fear I may have lost the excitement of our initial encounters.Is suspense always more pleasurable than fulfillment? Do we exaggerate our feelings by letting imagination run wild? These thoughts haunt me as I consider another secret meeting in the garden—this time without Griffith. How far can I go? Imagination knows no bounds, but pleasure has its limits. How can a woman reconcile these two aspects of her existence?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I am displeased with you. If you did not weep over Racine's Berenice and regard it as a profound tragedy, we have no connection and should part. Forget me unless you answer suitably; otherwise you will be M. le Baron de Macumer to me, or cease to exist.At Mme. d'Espard's yesterday your self-satisfied air disgusted me; your confidence of success alarms me. You have lost the humility of your first letter and, instead of the absent-mindedness of a lover, you polished epigrams. That is not the behavior of a true believer, who should always stand in awe of his divinity.If you do not see me as the very breath of your life, a being above others, then I am less than a woman in your eyes. You have bred a spirit of mistrust that drowns my tenderness. Reflecting on our past, I feel justified in my suspicions. Understand, Prime Minister of all the Spains, that I have contemplated the vulnerable state of our sex. My innocence has lit my path, and I have emerged unscathed.Let me repeat what youth taught me: in most matters deceit and broken promises are punished, but love follows its own code—the victim is also accuser and judge. The most heartless betrayals remain secret, known only to the two hearts involved. How can the victim reveal them without harming herself? Love administers its own justice, which the world ignores.I have resolved never to forgive a serious offense; in love, what is not serious? Yesterday you looked like a man who had already succeeded. If that certainty has stripped you of the charming uncertainty that once characterized you, I would blame you. I want you neither timid nor presumptuous; I do not want you to fear losing my affection—that would be an insult—yet I also do not want you to treat your love lightly. Your heart should never be freer than mine. If you cannot grasp the torment a single stab of doubt can inflict, beware the lesson I might teach you.In a single glance I entrusted my heart to you, and you understood it. The purest feelings of a young girl are yours. My reflections have enriched my mind; yet if a wounded heart seeks counsel from reason, it may ally itself with the darker aspects of knowledge and daring.I swear to you, Felipe, if you love me as I believe you do, and if I sense even the least decline in the fear, obedience, and respect you have shown, or if the pure flame that ignited my heart grows dim, expect no reproaches. I would not burden you with letters of weakness, pride, or rage. But if I became silent, my face would announce that death was near. I would not perish, however, until I had branded you with infamy and sown eternal sorrow in your heart; you would behold the girl you loved dishonored and lost, condemned to suffering.Therefore, do not make me envy the old, happy Louise, the object of your pure worship, whose heart thrived in happiness, as Dante wrote: Senza brama, sicura ricchezza! I have searched the Inferno for the most terrible punishment, a mental torture to link with divine vengeance.Yesterday, as I observed you, doubt pierced me like a cold dagger. I mistrusted you, and the pain was unbearable. If my service is too exacting, leave it; I would not retain you. I do not need proof of your cleverness. Keep your wit for me. Do not present a polished façade to others for flattery or praise. Come to me burdened with scorn or hatred, the target of calumny, told that no woman loves you; then you will discover the treasures of Louise's heart.We are truly rich only when our wealth lies so deep the world could trample it without knowing. If you were merely handsome, I doubt I would have noticed you or found the myriad reasons for my love. We understand little of those reasons, as little as we know why the sun makes flowers bloom and ripens fruit. Yet I can reveal one cause dear to me.The character and individuality that give meaning to your face are a mystery to all but me. My love transforms you into the most lovable of men, and that is why I wish to keep your mental gifts for myself. To others they should be as meaningless as your eyes and features. Let it be mine alone to ignite your intelligence, as I bring light to your eyes. I envision you as a proud Spanish grandee, cold and haughty, a monument to be admired from a distance—ruins of barbaric power—rather than a figure of Parisian affability.Have you forgotten my ideal portrait? Your excessive cheerfulness betrayed your love. Had I not restrained you, you would have revealed to the sharpest salons of Paris that your inspiration came from Armande‑Louise‑Marie de Chaulieu.I believe too much in your greatness to think your love is merely strategic; yet if you did not show childlike simplicity with me, I could only pity you. Despite this initial fault, you are still deeply admired by LOUISE DE CHAULIEU.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When God sees our faults, He also sees our repentance. My beloved mistress, you are right; I felt I had displeased you but did not know how. Now that you have explained, I find new reasons to adore you. Like the God of Israel, your jealousy is a holy and precious sentiment. Be jealous of your servant, Louise; the more you strike, the more contrite I will be, proving my devotion to you.If the effort it took to overcome my timidity was invisible to you, will Heaven reward it? It was no small feat to show you my true self, as I was before love. In Madrid, I was seen as a good talker, and I wanted you to witness my few gifts. If this was vanity, I have been punished.Your last glance unnerved me. I quailed, even more than when I faced peril at Cadiz. I sought the cause of your displeasure; the lack of sympathy between us was terrible. I wish only to act by your will, think your thoughts, and share your joys and sorrows. The anguish lay in the breach of our shared feelings."I have vexed her!" I repeated, distraught. My noble, beautiful Louise, your words have illuminated my feelings. If this is chastisement, what sweetness lies in your rewards?For me, it was happiness enough to be your servant. You have given me a life I thought lost. I no longer draw a useless breath; I have something to devote myself to, even if it means suffering for you. I will always be as I was when I first offered myself as your bondman. If you were dishonored, my heart would cling to you even more, seeking to heal your wounds and pray for your innocence.Did I not tell you my feelings for you are not just those of a lover? I will be your father, mother, sister, brother—anything or nothing, as you wish. Is it not your desire that has confined my devotion to a lover's feelings? Forgive me if the father and brother sometimes fade behind the lover; they are still there, though hidden. If you could read my heart when you appear before me, radiant in beauty, you would know how free from selfishness my pride is when I hear praises of your loveliness. When you greet me, I feel as if God's blessing rests upon me, and the joy lingers long after.Can you be unaware of how you are loved? After seeing you, I return to my study, and the glitter of its ornaments pales before your portrait. I lose myself in thoughts of you, linking my happiness to verse. Have you never heard the whispers of my prayers reaching you?Through silent contemplation of your face, I have deciphered every feature and its connection to your spirit's grace, penning sonnets in Spanish about your dual beauty. You will never see them, for my poetry is unworthy of you. Not a moment passes without thoughts of you; my whole being is bound to you, and without you, I would ache.Now, Louise, can you realize the torture of knowing I displeased you without knowing why? My ideal life was cut short, and I feared you had ceased to love me. Your letter turned my despair into joy. Oh, might I listen forever to such chiding!Once, a child, after falling, turned to his mother and said, "Forgive me." Hiding his hurt, he sought pardon for the pain he caused her. Louise, I am that child, and I remain so. Here is the key to my character, which I humbly place in your hands.But fear not, there will be no more stumbling. Keep tight the chain that binds me to you, so that a touch may communicate your lightest wish to your devoted slave, FELIPE.---My dear friend, how can you, who married a broken-down invalid to mother him, understand the shifting drama of love, where death lies in a glance or a light reply?I reserved one last test for Felipe to see if his love was like a Royalist's for his King, who can do no wrong. He walked with me all night, and not a shadow of suspicion crossed his mind. The next day, he loved me more, but with reverence and humility. He is a true Spaniard, scaling my wall to kiss my hand from my balcony, risking his life.But this is nothing; Christians suffer for heaven. I spoke to my father, the royal ambassador-to-be, with a smile, saying some friends claim he is marrying Armande to an ambassador's nephew for wealth and title. Your daughter weeps but bows to her father's authority. Some say she hides a worldly soul behind her tears.We are going to the gentleman's box at the Opera tonight, and M. le Baron de Macumer will visit us there."Macumer needs a spur," my father said, smiling at me."You mistake Clarissa Harlowe for Figaro!" I replied, scornfully. "When you see me with my right hand ungloved, you will mark your displeasure at this gossip.""I can rest easy about your future. You have no more a girl's head than Jeanne d'Arc had a woman's heart. You will be happy, love no one, and allow yourself to be loved."This was too much; I laughed."What is it, little flirt?" he asked."I tremble for my country's interests..."Seeing him blank, I added, "At Madrid!""You have no idea how this little nun has learned to make fun of her father," he told the Duchess."Armande makes light of everything," my mother replied."What do you mean?" I asked."You are not even afraid of rheumatism on these damp nights," she said, glancing at me meaningfully."Oh!" I answered, "the mornings are so hot!""It's high time she were married," my father said. "It had better be before I go.""If you wish it," I replied demurely.Two hours later, my mother, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, and Mme. d'Espard were blooming in the box. I sat sideways, unseen, to observe everything.Macumer arrived, putting his opera-glasses to look at me comfortably.In the first interval, the young man I call "king of the profligates," Comte Henri de Marsay, entered with an epigram in his eyes and a smile. He greeted my mother and the others, then turned to me, saying, "I do not know whether I shall be the first to congratulate you on an event that will make you the envy of many.""Ah! a marriage!" I cried. "Is it left for me, a girl fresh from the convent, to tell you that predicted marriages never come off?"M. de Marsay whispered to Macumer, and I sensed he was warning him about me.Macumer treated him to a glance of noble scorn, replying he was "not in love with any little coquette." His demeanor delighted me, and I caught sight of my father, ready to act.Felipe showed no fear or doubt. His faith is in me; society's lies cannot hurt him. Not a muscle of his face stirred, nor did his olive cheek flush.The two young counts left, and I laughed at Macumer, saying, "M. de Marsay has been treating you to an epigram on me.""He did more," he replied. "It was an epithalamium.""You speak Greek to me," I said, smiling at him.My father was talking to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, exclaiming about the scandalous gossip surrounding me. He intended to take a turn in the promenade to avoid suggesting the marriage.The Duchess and Mme. d'Espard exchanged glances, sensing something. Love is the least mysterious of all mysteries; it exudes from women like perfume.Having enjoyed Felipe's response, I signaled him to come to my window by the dangerous route. Later, I found him, upright as a statue, studying the light in my room."My dear Felipe," I said, "You acquitted yourself well tonight; you behaved as I would have if told you were marrying.""I thought you would hardly tell others before me.""And what right have you to this privilege?""The right of one who is your devoted slave.""In truth?""I am, and shall ever remain so.""But suppose this marriage was inevitable; suppose I had agreed..."His eyes flashed, calculating the danger of a fall. A power stronger than passion held him back."An Arab cannot take back his word," he said. "I am your slave; my life is not mine to destroy."I placed my hand on his as I said, "Felipe, my beloved, from this moment I am your wife in thought and will. Go in the morning to ask my father for my hand. He wishes to retain my fortune, but if you acknowledge receipt of it in the contract, his consent will be given. I am no longer Armande de Chaulieu. Leave me; no breath of scandal must touch Louise de Macumer."He listened, pale and trembling, then cleared the ten feet to the ground in safety. It was agonizing, but he waved goodbye and disappeared."I am loved then," I thought, "as never woman was before." I fell asleep, content, my destiny fixed.The next day, my father summoned me to his private room, where I found the Duchess and Macumer. After civilities, I said that if my father and M. Henarez agreed, I had no reason to oppose their wishes. My mother invited the Baron to dinner, and we drove in the Bois de Boulogne, where I smiled ironically at M. de Marsay as he passed.Felipe has reprinted his cards as follows:HENAREZ(Baron de Macumer, formerly Duc de Soria.)Every morning, he brings me a splendid bouquet, hidden within which is a letter containing a Spanish sonnet in my honor.To keep this letter concise, I send you only the first and last of these sonnets, translated for your benefit:FIRST SONNETMany a time I've stood, clad in thin silken vest,  Drawn sword in hand, with steady pulse,  Waiting the charge of a raging bull,  And the thrust of his horn, sharper-pointed than Phoebe's crescent.I've scaled, on my lips the lilt of an Andalusian dance,  The steep redoubt under a rain of fire;  I've staked my life upon a hazard of the dice  Careless, as though it were a gold doubloon.My hand would seek the ball out of the cannon's mouth,  But now meseems I grow more timid than a crouching hare,  Or a child spying some ghost in the curtain's folds.For when your sweet eye rests on me,  Any icy sweat covers my brow, my knees give way,  I tremble, shrink, my courage gone.SECOND SONNETLast night I fain would sleep to dream of thee,  But jealous sleep fled my eyelids,  I sought the balcony and looked towards heaven,  Always my glance flies upward when I think of thee.Strange sight! whose meaning love alone can tell,  The sky had lost its sapphire hue,  The stars, dulled diamonds in their golden mount,  Twinkled no more nor shed their warmth.The moon, washed of her silver radiance lily-white,  Hung mourning over the gloomy plain, for thou hast robbed  The heavens of all that made them bright.The snowy sparkle of the moon is on thy lovely brow,  Heaven's azure centers in thine eyes,  Thy lashes fall like starry rays.What a gracious way to say to a young girl that she fills your life! Tell me what you think of this love, which lavishes treasures of earth and soul. Only in the last ten days have I grasped the meaning of that famous Spanish gallantry.Ah, dear, what is happening at La Crampade? How often I stroll there, inspecting our crops! Do you have news of our mulberry trees and last winter's plantations? Does everything prosper as you wish? While buds open on our shrubs, have they also blossomed in the bosom of the wife? Does Louis continue his policy of madrigals? Do you enter each other's thoughts? I wonder if your wedding peace is better than the raging torrent of my love! Has my sweet lady professor taken offense? If so, I would send Felipe post-haste to plead for my pardon or her head. Sweet love, my life here is a splendid success, and I want to know how it fares with life in Provence. We have just added a Spaniard with the complexion of a Havana cigar to our family, and your congratulations still await.Seriously, my sweet Renee, I am anxious. I fear you may be eating your heart out in silence, afraid to cast a gloom over my sunshine. Write to me at once, naughty child! Tell me your life in every detail; tell me whether you still hold back, whether your "independence" stands erect, or has fallen on its knees, or is sitting comfortably, which would indeed be serious. Can you suppose the incidents of your married life are without interest for me? I muse over all you have said. Often, at the Opera, while absorbed in watching the dancers, I think, "It is half-past nine; perhaps she is in bed. What is she doing? Is she happy? Is she alone with her independence, or has it gone the way of other dead and cast-off independences?"A thousand loves.---Saucy girl! Why should I write? What could I say? While your life is varied by social festivities and the passions of love, mine is as monotonous as a convent.We go to bed at nine and rise with daybreak. Meals are served with maddening punctuality. Nothing ever happens. I have adjusted to this routine, which may be the nature of things. Where would the universe be without the fixed laws that govern it?I have imposed certain rules of dress, which occupy my mornings. I feel it is my duty as a wife to look charming, and it pleases both Louis and the good old man. After lunch, we walk. When newspapers arrive, I disappear to manage household affairs or read—often writing to you. I return an hour before dinner, and after, we play cards or receive visits. Thus, my days pass between a contented old man, who has done with passions, and the man who owes his happiness to me. Louis' happiness is so radiant that it has warmed my heart.For women, happiness cannot consist solely in satisfying desire. In the evenings, when I can relax, I imagine your life, its riches and passions, and wonder what such a stormy preface can lead to. May I not swallow the book itself? For you, my darling, love's illusions are possible; for me, only the facts of homely life remain. Your love seems a dream!I find it hard to understand why you insist on romanticizing it. Your ideal man must have more soul than fire, more nobility and self-command than passion. You demand sacrifices for the pleasure of rewarding them, testing Felipe to see if desire, hope, and curiosity endure. But behind your stage scenery lies the altar where everlasting bonds are forged. The day after your marriage, the graceful structure raised by your strategy may fall before the reality that transforms a girl into a woman, a gallant into a husband. Remember, there is no exemption for lovers. For them, as for ordinary folk like Louis and me, the great "Perhaps" of Rabelais lurks beneath wedding rejoicings.I do not blame you for your rashness in speaking with Felipe in the garden or spending a night with him; but you play with life, and I fear it may turn the tables. I dare not give you the counsel my experience suggests, but let me repeat from my valley that the viaticum of married life lies in resignation and self-sacrifice. Despite your tests, I see that marriage will mean to you what it has been to me. The greater the passion, the steeper the precipice we have hewn for our fall—that is the only difference.Oh, how I wish to see the Baron de Macumer and talk with him! Your happiness lies so near my heart.---As Felipe has generously acknowledged my fortune, my mother has become even friendlier, calling me sly-boots and a woman of the world."But, dear mama," I said before the contract was signed, "you attribute cunning to what is truly the outcome of the most unselfish love! I assure you I am not the 'woman of the world' you think.""Come, Armande," she said, drawing me close, "you did not want to return to the convent or die an old maid. Like a noble Chaulieu, you recognized the need to build up your father's family." I glanced at my father, who was listening, and said, "I love Felipe de Soria with all my soul. Though this love sprang up without my knowledge, I swear I never yielded until I recognized in him a character worthy of mine.""But, my dear," she interrupted, "he is as ugly as...""As anything you like," I retorted, "but I love his ugliness.""If you love him, Armande," my father said, "and can master your love, do not risk your happiness. Happiness in marriage depends largely on the first days—""Days only?" my mother interrupted. "You had better leave us to talk.""You are to be married in three days," the Duchess began, "so I must give you the serious advice every mother owes her daughter. You are marrying a man you love, and there is no reason for pity. I have only known you for a year, but it is long enough to love you. You have gratified maternal pride and shown yourself a sweet daughter. I promise you will always find a staunch friend in me.""You smile? Alas! it often happens that a mother who has lived well with her daughter as a girl comes to cross purposes with her as women.""I want your happiness, so listen. The love you feel is that of a young girl, natural to us all, for it is a woman's destiny to cling to a man. Unfortunately, there is but one man for a woman! Sometimes, the man fate has marked for us is not the one we choose as husband. If we cannot love the man we have chosen, the fault lies with both or circumstances beyond our control. Yet, there is no reason why the man chosen by our family should not be the one we can love. The barriers that arise between husband and wife often stem from a lack of perseverance on both sides. Transforming a husband into a lover is as delicate as making a lover into a husband, which you have just proved marvelously successful."Your happiness is my object. Never forget that the first three months of marriage may bring misery if you do not submit to the yoke with the same forbearance, tenderness, and intelligence you showed during courtship. You have indulged in the innocent pleasures of clandestine love. If the culmination of your love begins with disappointment, come and tell me. Do not hope for too much from marriage; it may bring more discomfort than joy. The happiness of your life requires as much patient cherishing as the early shoots of love."If you lose the lover, you will find in his place the father of your children. This is the secret of social life. Sacrifice everything to the man whose name you bear, whose honor cannot suffer without involving you in frightful consequences. Such sacrifice is not only a duty for women of our rank; it is also wise policy. This is the mark of great moral principles—they hold good from every aspect. But I need say no more."I fancy you are jealous, and if you knew how jealous I am! But do not be foolish about it. To publish your jealousy is like playing politics with your cards on the table; those who let their game be seen learn nothing of their opponents. Whatever happens, we must know how to suffer in silence."She added that she intended to have a serious talk with Macumer the evening before the wedding.I kissed her hand, dropping a tear, moved by her words. In her advice, I saw high principles worthy of us both, true wisdom, and a tenderness unspoiled by society's narrow code. Above all, I saw she understood my character. Her words summed up the lessons life had taught her, perhaps at a heavy price."Dear little girl, you've got a nasty crossing before you. Most women, in their ignorance or disenchantment, are as wise as the Earl of Westmoreland!"We laughed, but I must explain the joke. A Russian princess had told us of this gentleman, who suffered from sea-sickness and turned back from Italy upon hearing of "crossing" the Alps. "Thank you; I've had quite enough crossings already," he said.You will understand, Renee, that your gloomy philosophy and my mother's lecture revived fears that had disturbed us at Blois. As marriage approached, I needed all my strength, resolution, and affection to face this passage from maidenhood to womanhood. All our conversations returned to my mind, and I re-read your letters, sensing a vague sadness.This anxiety had one advantage; it helped me adopt the regulation expression for a bride. Consequently, on the day of signing the contract, everyone said I looked charming and quite right. This morning, at the Mairie, it was informal, with only witnesses present.I write this tail to my letter while they prepare my dress for dinner. We shall be married at midnight at the Church of Sainte-Valere, after a very gay evening. I confess my fears give me a martyr-like air to which I have no right, but which will be admired—why, I cannot conceive. I am delighted to see that poor Felipe is as timid as I; society grates on him, and he feels like a bat in a glass shop."Thank Heaven, the day won't last forever!" he whispered innocently.In his bashfulness, he wished for no one there.The Sardinian ambassador presented me with a pearl necklace linked with six splendid diamonds—a gift from my sister-in-law, the Duchess de Soria. Along with it was a sapphire bracelet engraved with the words, "Though unknown, beloved." I could not accept these without consulting Felipe."For," I said, "I would not want you to wear ornaments from anyone but me."He kissed my hand, moved, and replied, "Wear them for the inscription and the sincere feeling."Here, then, my poor Renee, are the last words of your girl friend. After the midnight Mass, we set off for an estate Felipe bought in Nivernais, on the way to Provence. My name is now Louise de Macumer, but I leave Paris in a few hours as Louise de Chaulieu. However I am called, there will always be but one Louise for you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I have not written to you, dear, since our marriage nearly eight months ago, and not a line from you! You are inexcusable.We set off in a post-chaise for the Castle of Chantepleurs, which Macumer has bought in Nivernais, on the banks of the Loire, sixty leagues from Paris. Our servants, except my maid, were there before us, and we arrived the next evening after a rapid journey. I slept from Paris to beyond Montargis, resting my head on Felipe's shoulder. His tender affection touched me deeply; he kissed me twice on the forehead.At Briare we breakfasted in the carriage and talked as at Blois, admiring the Loire. By half-past seven we entered the avenue to Chantepleurs. We dined at eight and retired to our charming Gothic bedroom, comfortable with modern luxuries. Felipe, often thought ugly, appeared beautiful to me in his kindness. Throughout the journey he felt like an old friend, though he later confessed to storms of passion he had concealed beneath calm."So far, I have found nothing terrible in marriage," I said, gazing at the moonlit park."Why fear it?" he replied. "Have I ever proved false to my promise? Why should I in the future?"His words and glances were masterful, thrilling my heart. I reflected on duty and the constraints placed on a woman's delicate feelings. What force seeks to crush a woman's sensitivity under obligation? Love should guard its rights, blooming freely under its warmth.Oh, my noble Renee! I understand you now. The woman who has not used marriage merely to legalize her heart's choice has only motherhood left to turn to. When earthly ties fail, the soul seeks the divine.A hard truth emerges: only truly great men know how to love. They balance sensual and spiritual forces, while weaker men confuse them. Great souls express natural instincts with grace; their passion is tempered by reverence for women. The depth of feeling corresponds to the caliber of intellect, which is why only men of genius possess a woman's delicacy. When mind, heart, and senses unite, we soar to heaven, however briefly.This is the philosophy of my first three months of marriage. Felipe is angelic, another self; I can think aloud with him. His greatness surpasses me, and our bond deepens with possession. I foresee years of marriage strengthening his affection and our connection. What joy!Pleasure invigorates me, leaving sunshine that becomes part of my being. The intervals between ecstasies are like brief nights separating long summer days. What happy chance has given me such a destiny? My mother's fears were unfounded; your fears and mine have vanished.We spent seven and a half months at Chantepleurs, like runaway lovers in blissful solitude. One morning, in particular happiness, I reflected on my life and thought of your prosaic marriage. I grasped the meaning of your life. Oh, my sweet, why do we speak different languages? Your marriage of convenience and my love match are worlds apart. You walk the earth, while I soar. Love crowned me queen, while you reign by duty. My heights are so lofty that a fall would shatter me.But I won't dwell on this. I hesitate to describe love's vibrant springtime as we know it.For ten days we have been in Paris, staying in a lovely house in the Rue du Bac, prepared by the architect who decorated Chantepleurs. I have been listening to Rossini's divine music, which once soothed my restless heart. They say I am more beautiful now, and I take childish pleasure in being called "Madame."Friday morning.Renee, my dear, my happiness draws me back to you. I feel I can be more to you than ever. I have studied your married life alongside my own, and you emerge as great, noble, and splendid in your goodness. I declare myself your humble admirer and friend. My marriage has been a joy; I would have perished had it been otherwise. And you live! What feeds your heart? I will never mock you again. Mockery stems from ignorance; we jest at what we do not understand. "Recruits laugh where veterans are grave," said the Comte de Chaulieu, whose experience has been limited to marches from Paris to Fontainebleau.I suspect you have not shared everything. You hide wounds, and I believe you suffer. From afar I wove romantic theories about you: "She has merely experimented with marriage," I thought, "and what is happiness for me has brought her only pain." Your sacrifice seems unrewarded, and you would not make it greater than necessary. The axioms of social morality cloak your disappointment. If self-denial is noble, what of my joy, sheltered by the church's canopy and witnessed by the mayor's seal? Is it unnatural?For the honor of the law and to complete my happiness, I long to see you content. Oh, tell me you see a dawn of love for Louis, who adores you! I return to this midday blaze, fearing it may consume me.Dear Renee, do you remember how you once said, "I love you so, Louise, that if God appeared to me, I would pray for all your sorrows to be mine"? Now I take up your prayer, imploring Heaven to grant you a share in my happiness.I have an idea. I suspect you are nurturing plans for Louis de l'Estorade. Very well; help him get elected deputy in the upcoming election. He will be nearly forty then, and the Chamber won't meet for six months, so he will qualify for a seat. You will come to Paris—wouldn't that be enough? My father and future friends will come to know and admire you. If your father-in-law consents to start a family, we could secure the title of Comte for Louis. That is something! And we shall be together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, your letter left me dizzy with joy and a touch of envy. I read it at sunset beneath a barren rock overlooking the Mediterranean—where I’ve planted climbers to one day cover it—calling the spot Louise's seat. It both symbolizes my solitude and reminds me I am not alone.As I read, the child within me stirred: a blend of warning, delight, pain, promise, and fulfilment that convinces me I will one day know laughter and love. I now understand my purpose: to bring forth life. The certainty of motherhood has turned my sacrifices into profound joy, eclipsing any former personal passion. For you, love reflects Felipe’s ardor; my love will return in my children. Your golden harvest may fade, but my delayed motherhood will be enduring and renewing. Love is a beautiful gift taken from Nature, but motherhood is Nature’s purest joy. A smile has dried my tears; while love makes Louis happy, it is marriage that has made me a mother, and that gives me happiness.I returned to my white grange to share these thoughts. For five months the miracle of nature has unfolded within me, yet emotionally I remain detached. My family is excited, but I feel cold and am hesitant to confess how abstract motherhood still seems. Louis was equally surprised by my condition, which shows how little the father’s role appears to matter. My doctor thinks chance governs childbirth, though he believes children born of passionate love are often more fortunate. Perhaps motherhood will hold joys for you I may never feel, since your love for Felipe differs from my sense of duty.These reflections trouble me as I anticipate family life. I live in a state of expectation, coupled with physical discomfort: nausea and odd cravings, including a puzzling desire for rotten fruit. While Louis brings home the finest oranges, I seek the half-rotten ones and find them delicious despite their smell. This craving has eased my sickness and made me feel stronger, suggesting a natural process common to women. As my condition advances, I plan to stay within the grounds to avoid being seen. I am eager to know when the real sense of motherhood will begin and hope it will not arrive with suffering.Farewell, my fortunate friend. Your love story inspires me, and I long to hear every detail—remain faithful to our bond, and I promise to share everything. Yet after rereading your letter a dread came over me: your happiness seems to challenge Providence, and I fear calamity may take offense. Remember to pray and do good; religion has become more important to me since my marriage, and your letter’s silence on this worries me. My concern comes from deep affection. I trust you and Felipe attend church together. My fears may seem primitive, but they arise from a friendship that cares. You deserve happiness, and I cherish our bond. God bless you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame,—My wife wishes you to hear first from us about an event that has brought us immense joy. Renée has given birth to a healthy boy; we are postponing his baptism until your return to Chantepleurs. We sincerely hope you will come to La Crampade and agree to be godmother to our firstborn, whom we have named Armand‑Louis de l’Estorade.Renée endured considerable pain but showed remarkable patience; the thought of bringing happiness to us all sustained her through this trying experience.I can say without new‑father exaggeration that little Armand is a beautiful infant, resembling Renée in both features and eyes, which suggests intelligence.The physician assures us Renée is now out of danger. She proves an excellent nurse, and we are free to express our joy. This happiness has revitalized our home and transformed my wife’s life; I hope you will soon share it.Renée has prepared a suite of rooms for your stay; though I wish they were more splendid, we hope the warmth of our welcome will compensate.I thank you for your kind thoughts, which are so timely. The birth of a grandson has reconciled my father to sacrifices that weighed on him. He has recently purchased two estates, and La Crampade now yields an annual rental of thirty thousand francs. He intends to seek the King’s permission to make it an entailed estate; if you could assist him in obtaining the title you mentioned, it would greatly benefit your godson.I will follow your suggestions to facilitate your reunion with Renée during the Chamber sessions. I am working diligently to become a specialist, and nothing would encourage me more than your interest in little Armand. We earnestly invite you to come and, with your beauty, grace, playful spirit, and noble heart, be a good fairy to my son and heir. In doing so you will win our everlasting gratitude.Your very humble, obedient servant,LOUIS DE L’ESTORADE.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################XXX. LOUISE DE MACUMER TO RENEE DE L'ESTORADEJanuary 1826.Macumer just woke me with your husband's letter. Yes, we will go to Chantepleurs at the end of April. I am delighted to travel, to see you, and to be godmother to your first child. I must have Macumer as godfather; sharing this role would be unbearable. If you could see the look he gave me, you would know how dearly he cherishes me!I look forward to visiting La Crampade; I might have a child there too. Can you imagine me torn between you and an infant? If I ever saw anything take my place in your heart, I can't promise how I'd react. Medea might have had a point!So, my dear, you have the fruit without the flowers; I the flowers without the fruit. Our lives contrast, but we have enough philosophy to find meaning yet. Ten months married is too soon to lose hope.We live happily, as happy people do. Society now finds the Baronne de Macumer prettier than Louise de Chaulieu; love is a wonderful cosmetic. Driving along the Champs-Élysées on a crisp January morning, I reflect on how far we've come since last year.If I lack a mother's joys, you can share them with me; I will learn by sympathy. Still, I cannot imagine anything surpassing the rapture of love. You may laugh, but I often wish to die at thirty, leaving life untainted by disappointment, having lived fully in love. To carry a young heart in an aged body, surrounded by cold faces, would be torment.Felipe and I quarrelled about this. I insisted he should have the strength to end my life in my sleep at thirty, so I might pass from one dream to another. He refused, turning pale. This distinguished statesman is surprisingly childlike. Now that I share such thoughts with him, we continually surprise each other.Felipe and I want to send you a gift, dear mother. We wish to choose something that will bring you joy and remind you of us. Tell me what you would like. I thought of a luncheon service with baby figures, since lunch is our happiest meal. Let me know soon; we must order it.Farewell, dear nursing mother. May you enjoy all the delights of motherhood! I eagerly await your first letter describing your experiences. Some parts of your husband's letter touched me; I will tell my godson how dearly he must love you. Much love, my sweet one.XXXI. RENEE DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE MACUMERIt has been nearly five months since the baby was born, and I have found no moment to write. When you become a mother, you will understand. Please write more often! Share your joys; I am happier than you can imagine.I went to the parish church for the Mass of recovery from childbirth, supported by both grandfathers. I knelt before God, overwhelmed with gratitude. I have so much to tell you, but one memory stands out: my prayer in the church.Transformed into a joyful mother, I felt the Virgin on the altar bow her head, pointing to the infant Christ, who smiled at me. With a heart full of love, I held little Armand for the priest to bless, anticipating his baptism later.My child—how sweet that word is! In the last two months I dragged myself through the gardens, scarcely conscious of the precious burden I carried. Gloomy forebodings haunted me, overshadowing curiosity about motherhood. My heart did not respond to the thought of the little one, though he made himself known with lively kicks.When the moment came, I summoned all my strength and braced for suffering. I bore the agony marvelously, sinking into a stupor. I felt like two beings—my body in pain and my soul at peace. The pain formed a halo around me, and at the climax I screamed, finding strength against the torture. Suddenly the newborn's cries silenced my pain.They laid me back in bed, and it felt like paradise despite my exhaustion. Happy faces pointed to the child, and I exclaimed in terror, "It looks like a little monkey! Are you sure it's a child?"My mother, now my nurse, reassured me, "You've got the finest baby in the world. Don't worry; focus on being a mother." I fell asleep, resolved to let nature take its course.Waking from the first days' pain was heavenly. A ray of light pierced the darkness, and my heart and soul emerged from suffering as a flower opens to the sun. The moment the little one latched onto my breast was pure joy—this is the dawn of motherhood!Here is happiness, though it comes with pangs. Oh, my sweet jealous soul, you will relish this delight that exists only for us, the child, and God. For this tiny creature, all knowledge is in its mother's breast. Its wants and thoughts revolve around this source of life, and its lips bring a sweetness beyond words.Nursing is a miracle of transformation. The cries go to your heart; the child's eyes and lips speak volumes. What else matters? The father? You could kill him if he disturbed the baby! The bond between mother and child is profound; the joy of nurturing is unparalleled.My little one has become the prettiest darling in five months. He cries little now, content in the love that surrounds him. I strive to envelope him in my affection.Dear, my feelings for Louis have evolved into a tender fondness, perhaps surpassing love. This unselfish gratitude reflects the joy of motherhood, lighting the future and recalling fond memories of the past.The old l'Estorade and his son have redoubled their devotion to me; I feel like a new person. Each time they see me it brings them joy, which touches me deeply. Louis is ecstatic about having a son and spends hours watching him sleep. He feared childlessness, but now he works harder, driven by fatherhood.I grow happier every moment. Each hour strengthens the bond between mother and child. My feelings are natural and enduring, unlike romantic love, which can wane. A mother's love grows with the child's needs, embracing passion, duty, and joy. Here lies a woman's true sphere, where self-sacrifice flourishes without jealousy.Society aligns with nature in this regard, reinforcing the maternal instinct. A mother cherishes the child who opens up these joys and teaches her the art of motherhood. The right of the eldest child, once part of the natural order, should never have been questioned. A mother learns so much from her child, and her character flourishes in fulfilling life's duties and pleasures. A woman without children is incomplete.Hasten, my sweetest, to fulfill your mission. Your happiness will multiply with the wealth of my delights.23rd.I had to leave you because my godson was crying; I can hear him from the garden. I wanted to say farewell. I reread my letter and am surprised at how ordinary my feelings seem; every mother must have felt this way. But I am as happy now as I once was miserable. This place is my land of promise. The desert is past. A thousand loves, darling. Write to me, for I can now read about your happy love without tears. Farewell.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Do you know, dear, it has been over three months since I last wrote? I’m to blame for not answering your last letter, but surely you won’t mind?Macumer and I took your silence as consent about the baby-wreathed luncheon service; it’s bound for Marseilles after six months’ work. When Felipe asked me to see it before packing, I realized we hadn’t exchanged words since your letter that revealed a mother’s heart.My excuse is this dreadful Paris. Society is a whirlpool—didn’t I tell you one must conform? It drives out sentiment, monopolizes time, and can consume your heart. Moliere’s Celimene is the perfect portrait of the society woman—timeless and apt.Without my breastplate—the love I have for Felipe—I would be lost. This morning I told him he is my salvation. My evenings are filled with parties, but at night my heart is restored in the embrace of passionate love.I dine at home only with friends and spend one afternoon a week—Wednesday—there. I have entered the social fray, competing with Mmes. d'Espard and de Maufrigneuse; my house is lively. I allowed myself to become fashionable for Felipe’s pleasure. My mornings belong to him; from four in the afternoon until two in the morning I belong to Paris. Macumer is an admirable host—witty, dignified, courteous. No woman could help but love such a husband.My parents have gone to Madrid. With Louis XVIII gone, the Duchess secured my father’s appointment as attaché. My brother, the Duc de Rhetore, now acknowledges me. My younger brother, the Comte de Chaulieu, owes me for the fortune I spent to secure him an estate and a marriage with Mlle. de Mortsauf, an heiress. The King will confer the de Lenoncourt and de Givry titles to preserve those names. Mlle. de Mortsauf, sole heiress of the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, is expected to inherit over one hundred thousand livres a year. My father insisted the de Chaulieu arms appear on the de Lenoncourt escutcheon, making my brother Duc de Lenoncourt. The young de Mortsauf is gravely ill, so the marriage will wait until next winter after mourning.My father’s plan has justified itself: it earns me compliments and smooths over the explanation of my marriage to society. The Prince de Talleyrand, out of affection for my grandmother, has become warm to Macumer. Society that once criticized me now admires us.I now reign where I was once insignificant. Macumer is envied as the husband of “the most charming woman in Paris.” Many women vie for that title; men murmur sweet nothings and gaze longingly. The chorus flatters vanity, and I see why women pay dearly for such transient privileges. This triumph is intoxicating; I understand how women become selfish and frivolous under adoration. Society’s demands consume your spirit; you give your all for smiles and jealousies and receive only flattery. You know the cost, yet cannot escape the vortex.Ah, how I crave a true friend! Felipe’s love and devotion are precious to me, and my heart longs for you. We are joyfully preparing to move to Chantepleurs, where we can escape the farce of Parisian society. After rereading your letter, I realize no fashionable woman in Paris can be a good mother.Goodbye for now, dear. We will be at Chantepleurs for only a week and will see you around May 10th. We will finally meet again after more than two years! How we have changed! We are both matrons now—one in love, the other in motherhood.If I haven’t written, it isn’t because I’ve forgotten you. How is my little godson? He must be over nine months now. I long to witness his first steps, though Macumer says even precocious infants don’t walk at ten months.We will have delightful gossip and see if a child truly spoils a pattern.P.S.—If you reply, address it to Chantepleurs. I’m just leaving.---My child, if you become a mother, you’ll find it impossible to write during the first two months of nursing. Mary, my English nurse, and I are exhausted. I had resolved to do everything myself—sewing baby clothes, embroidering caps. I am a slave, day and night.Master Armand-Louis eats whenever he pleases, and I must constantly change, wash, and dress him. I love watching him sleep, singing to him, and carrying him about, but there is little time for myself. Society has been to you what motherhood is to me!I cannot express how rich my life has become; I long for your visit so you may see. I fear he will soon be teething and may be fussy. So far he hasn’t cried much because I’m always attentive. Babies cry when their needs aren’t met, and I’m vigilant. My heart has expanded while yours shrinks under society’s demands! I eagerly await your arrival, wanting your thoughts on l’Estorade, just as you’re curious about Macumer.Write to me from your last stop. The gentlemen want to meet our distinguished guests. Come, Queen of Paris, to our humble home, where love will greet you!---The name on this letter shows my petition has been granted. Your father-in-law is now Comte de l'Estorade. I wouldn’t leave Paris until I secured this for you; I write in the presence of the Keeper of the Seals, who has just informed me the patent is signed.Goodbye for now!---I am ashamed that my sudden departure will surprise you. But I must be honest: I was horribly jealous.Felipe’s eyes rested too often on you. Your little talks at the foot of your rock tortured me; your beauty reminded him of his homeland and Marie Heredia, and I can be jealous of the past too. Your magnificent features stabbed me. I tried to enhance my appearance, but everything paled beside you.Felipe doted on the child, whom I began to resent. Yes, I confess I wanted that exuberance of life for myself. I saw regret in Macumer’s eyes and wept for two nights. I was miserable in your home. You are too beautiful and triumphant for me to endure.You complained of your lot, but L’Estorade is presentable, charming, and likely to become a deputy. His exile has given him a calm, dignified air, essential for a politician. Macumer, too, should achieve high position.Having satisfied myself of your happiness, I am off to Chantepleurs, where Felipe must fulfill his aspirations. I won’t receive you there without a fine baby to match yours.I know I deserve your scorn. I am a fool, a wretch, an idiot. That is jealousy. I am not angry with you, only miserable; you will forgive my escape. Two more days and I would have made a scene.Despite my jealousy, I am glad to have seen you in the pride of motherhood. Even here in Marseilles I feel proud of you as a splendid mother.You are born for motherhood, while I am not. A good mother and a passionate wife both require intelligence and grace. I watched you closely and admired you! Your children will be happy, not spoiled, with your tenderness and reason.Tell Louis the truth about my departure, but find a decent excuse for your father-in-law and family. Felipe doesn’t know why I left, and he never will. If he asks, I’ll invent a pretext—perhaps that you were jealous of me! Forgive my little fib.Goodbye. I write in haste, wanting you to receive this at lunch. The postilion is here, waiting.Kisses to my dear godson. Be sure to come to Chantepleurs in October. I’ll be alone there while Macumer is in Sardinia, planning improvements for his estate. That gives him a sense of control, which unsettles him when he shares it with me. Goodbye!---Dear sister, we were all astonished at lunch when we learned you had left, especially after receiving your letter. You were happy, and there was no justification for your objections. My sentence is that you return at my first summons. Your letter didn’t mention your next stop, so I’m addressing this to Chantepleurs.Listen, dear sister of my heart. My focus is your happiness. Your husband commands respect, not only for his gravity but for the power revealed in his plainness and the fire in his eyes. It took me time to converse easily with him. He has been Prime Minister and adores you, which makes him a profound dissembler. Extracting secrets from this diplomatic soul requires skill.You know my role is reason, while yours is imagination. I am a simple country vicountess, ambitious for my family’s prosperity. Macumer, the Duc de Soria, has a name, and you, a duchess by right, reign in Paris, where it’s hard to maintain power. You have a considerable fortune, which may double if Macumer develops his Sardinian estates. If anyone has the right to be jealous, it’s not you. Thankfully, we both have generous hearts that place our friendship above pettiness.I know you must feel ashamed of fleeing. But your flight won’t spare you from my words today. Read carefully, as this concerns you more than Macumer.Firstly, you do not love him properly. In two years you will tire of adoration. You will never see Felipe as a husband; he will always be a lover to you. You do not revere him as a woman should revere her god. I have studied love, and this is not love.You cannot escape the fate of all queens. One day you will long to be treated as a dangerous, overwhelming lover, to be swept off your feet by a strong man who won’t worship you. Macumer loves you too much to resist or criticize. Sooner or later you will scorn this excessive devotion. He spoils you, as I did at the convent; you are enchanting, and there’s no escaping your charms.Worse, you are candid, and our happiness often relies on social hypocrisies you won’t embrace. Society won’t tolerate a wife’s power over her husband. The convention is that a man must not show himself as a lover to his wife, nor may a married woman act as a mistress. You both disregard this.The unpardonable sin in society is to be openly happy. If happiness exists, it must remain hidden. More importantly, equality between lovers should never appear in marriage, or it undermines society. It is painful to see a man made a nonentity; worse to see a capable man reduced to that. You will reduce Macumer to a shadow, and your household will consist of one imperfect person. You will regret it, but by then it will be too late. Women lack the qualities essential for family life. Macumer senses this; his trip to Sardinia suggests he hopes to regain his old self.You use the power his love gives you. Your position shows in your gestures and tone. You are the wanton your mother called you! Have you ever heard me contradict Louis? I respect his authority in public. If I want to advise him, I wait for private moments, but I never take the role of mentor publicly. If I didn’t remain a submissive wife outwardly, he would lose confidence. The good we do for others is spoiled unless we erase ourselves so they feel no inferiority. There’s sweetness in these hidden sacrifices, and it’s a triumph when you praise Louis.This is the sum of my observations. Right now, you love Felipe, not as a husband, but as a reflection of yourself. Your father’s words hold truth; beneath your passion lies a selfishness typical of a great lady. How I must love you to speak such bitter truths!If you promise not to tell the Baron, I’ll share the end of our talk. We praised you, and he soon realized I love you like a sister. I ventured, “Louise has never struggled with life. She has been fortune’s spoiled child, and she might pay for this if you weren’t there to act as father and lover.”“Ah! but is it possible?” he broke off, on the verge of a precipice. That exclamation sufficed for me. If you had stayed, he would have spoken more freely.Think of the day when your husband’s strength wanes, when pleasure turns to satiety, and he feels diminished before you. The stings of conscience will awaken remorse, painful for you, as you will feel responsible and end up despising the man you failed to respect. Remember, scorn can lead to hatred. You are too noble to forget Felipe’s sacrifices, but what more can he give when he has already served himself at the banquet? Woe to those who leave no desire unsatisfied! Women are insatiable in love.Oh, Louise, change while there’s still time. If you treated Macumer as I treat l’Estorade, you could awaken the hero within him. Wouldn’t you feel pride in using your power to elevate a gifted man, as I do with one of lesser stature?Had you remained, I would have written this anyway, for in conversation you might have interrupted me. But I know you will read this thoughtfully and weigh my warnings. You have everything to be happy; don’t spoil your chances. Return to Paris as soon as Macumer does. The demands of society, which I complain about, are necessary; otherwise you’ll be lost in self-absorption. A married woman shouldn’t be too generous with herself. A mother should reserve sacred hours for herself; even children shouldn’t take her presence for granted.Farewell, my dear jealous soul! Many women would be flattered by your pangs, but I can only mourn, for what isn’t mother in me is your dear friend. A thousand loves. Make whatever excuse you must for leaving; if you’re unsure of Macumer, I am sure of Louis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My beloved beauty,—I was eager to explore Italy and am thrilled to have brought Macumer, since his Sardinia plans are postponed.This country enchants; the churches, especially the chapels, are so alluring they could make any Protestant yearn for Catholicism. Macumer has been warmly welcomed—everyone pleased to have such a distinguished Italian. Felipe could secure the Sardinian embassy in Paris if I wished, since I am well regarded at court.If you write, send letters to Florence. I have no time for details now; I’ll tell our travel stories when you visit Paris, as soon as I can. We’ll stay here a week before heading to Florence, stopping at Leghorn; we plan a month in Tuscany and another in Naples, aiming to reach Rome in November. From there we’ll return home via Venice—where we’ll stay the first two weeks of December—and arrive in Paris through Milan and Turin in January.The journey feels like a perfect honeymoon; discovering new places revitalizes our passion. Macumer had not seen Italy, and we began with the stunning Cornice road, as if made by fairies.Goodbye, darling. Don’t be upset if I don’t write—there’s no time while traveling; I’m consumed with seeing and absorbing my experiences. I’ll share more once I can reflect properly.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear,—a full reply to your letter from Marseilles awaits you at Chantepleurs. This honeymoon has heightened my concerns; please have my letter sent from Nivernais.Rumor says the Government plans to dissolve Parliament—unfortunate for the Crown, since the last session would have enacted laws to strengthen its power. This alarms us, as Louis will not be forty until the end of 1827. Fortunately my father has agreed to stand and will resign his seat at the proper moment.Your godson walks without his godmother's help. He is delightful and already shows thought rather than mere infancy; his smiles are meaningful. I have succeeded in nursing and will wean Armand in December; a year at the breast seems sufficient—longer nursing, popular wisdom holds, brings dullness.You must be making an impression in Italy, my fair one with golden locks. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################XXXIX. THE BARONNE DE MACUMER TO THE VICOMTESSE DE L'ESTORADEYour dreadful letter reached me, forwarded by my steward as I requested. Oh, Renée... I will spare you my turmoil and say its effect.We had just returned from the ambassador’s reception, where I glittered and Macumer was utterly captivated. Then I read your harsh reply to my letter and sobbed. My dear Arab threw himself at my feet, calling you mad. He led me to our balcony; speaking Italian, his words were like poetry. He swore that even if your predictions were true, he would not trade one moment of our love for a lifetime of convention—he would choose our love and death over a dull existence with children.While he whispered, a bat’s cry startled us and I fainted. I am better this morning, though that sound haunts me. Upon waking, I knelt before Felipe, looked into his eyes, and confessed: “My love, I may be naive, and Renée might be right. Perhaps I love only your love, but this is the only feeling in my heart. If anything about me disturbs you, tell me. I want to follow your wishes.” Macumer was too choked with emotion to answer.I thank you, Renée; your letter revealed the depths of Macumer’s love. Rome is the city of love, where passion should flourish alongside art and faith.In Venice we shall meet the Duc and Duchesse de Soria. If you write, address Paris, as we leave Rome in three days after the ambassador’s farewell.P.S. Dear, naive child, your letter shows you know love only in theory. Love is a force that yields diverse fruits beyond any theory’s grasp.XL. THE COMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE TO THE BARONNE DE MACUMER January 1827.My father has been elected to the Chamber, my father-in-law has died, and I am near my second confinement; these mark the year’s end. I mention them first to spare you the fright of the black seal.Your letter from Rome chilled me. You and Felipe behave like children. Either he is a deceitful diplomat, or his love is like a man’s for a courtesan—lavishing affection while knowing she is unfaithful. Enough. You say I rave, so I will hold my tongue: from our differing fates I draw a harsh lesson—do not love if you wish to be loved.When Louis joined the provincial Council he received the Legion of Honor cross. Nearly three years on, my father asks the rank of Officer for his son-in-law. I would value your influence with the right person, but do not entangle yourself in my father’s affairs. The Comte de Maucombe seeks a marquisate for himself; reserve efforts for me. When Louis becomes a deputy next winter, we will be in Paris to secure him a government post so we may live on his salary. My father sits between center and right; a title will satisfy him. Our family was noble even under King René, and Charles X will likely grant a Maucombe title. My fear is my father using this to favor my younger brother—if a marquisate is dangled, he will focus on himself.January 15th.Ah, Louise, I have been through hell. I tell you because you are like a sister; still, I doubt I shall ever relive those five ghastly days. The word “convulsions” sickens me. Five days felt like five centuries. A mother who has not suffered this cannot comprehend it.The night before, the weather was close; I thought Armand affected by teething. Usually sweet, he grew peevish, crying and breaking his toys. I put him to bed beside me. Toward morning he cried “Mamma!” in a strange voice and twitched. I rose for a drink; as I handed him the cup he was motionless, repeating “Mamma!” in a voice hardly a voice. His hand was stiff. He gulped water convulsively, clung to me, eyes rolling back, limbs rigid. I screamed for Louis.“A doctor! Quick!... he is dying,” I cried.Louis vanished. Armand gasped “Mamma!” then lost consciousness. Veins on his forehead swelled, and convulsions began. For an hour before doctors arrived I held my lifeless baby—my pride and joy. His eyes! I cannot think of them without horror. My pretty Armand was a mere shell—black, shriveled.Two doctors from Marseilles hovered like birds of ill omen. One blamed brain fever, the other called them ordinary convulsions. Our country doctor offered no opinion. “Teething,” said one; “fever,” said the other. They agreed on leeches to the neck and ice to the head. To see a child like that—purple, silent—was horrible. They even cut his curls for the ice. The convulsions returned every ten minutes; the child writhed, white then violet, limbs clattering like wood. This unconscious flesh had smiled and said “Mamma!”—the thought pierced my soul.After four days and nights, the doctors recommended a dreadful ointment to create open sores. Sores on my Armand! Five days earlier he had been playing and laughing. I refused, trusting nature. Louis, who believes doctors, scolded me. But in those moments when the disease mimicked death, I felt that remedy might save him. His skin was dry and rough; the ointment would not work. I wept; the doctors were at dinner!Alone with him, I stripped off the medical appliances, pressed him to my bosom, and laid my forehead against his, begging God to spare him. For minutes I held him, longing to die with him. Then his limbs relaxed; the writhings ceased; the ghastly colors faded. I screamed; the doctors rushed in.“He is saved!” cried the oldest doctor.What music those words were! Two hours later Armand returned to life. I was crushed, saved only by the joy of his recovery. What tortures bind a mother to her child! Must the nails be driven to fasten him to my heart? Was I not mother enough? I who wept at his first words and steps—why these horrors?Now Armand plays and laughs. What causes this dreadful disease in children? I ponder it, especially as I am again with child. Is it teething?
