A brain disorder? A nervous susceptibility? Our country doctor blames nervous trouble from teething. I would give every tooth in my head to see Armand’s through. One white pearl peeking from swollen gums brings a cold sweat. The heroism with which my angel bore suffering proves he is his mother’s son. A look from him pierces me.Medical science offers no clear explanation for this tetanus that appears and vanishes swiftly, unpreventable and uncured. One thing is certain: it is hell for a mother to see her child in convulsions. How passionately I hold him now! I could walk forever with him in my arms!To have endured this six weeks before my confinement made it worse; I feared for the coming child. Farewell, my beloved. Do not wish for a child—this is the essence of my letter!XLI. THE BARONNE DE MACUMER TO THE VICOMTESSE DE L'ESTORADE Paris.Poor dear, Macumer and I forgave you upon hearing of your terrible trouble. I felt pain reading of your double agony; there seem compensations in being childless.I write to tell you Louis has been promoted; he may now wear the ribbon of an Officer of the Legion. You are fortunate, Renée—likely to have a little girl, as you wished!My brother’s marriage to Mlle. de Mortsauf was celebrated on our return. Our gracious King, who is extraordinarily kind, has granted my brother the reversion of the post of first gentleman of the chamber, presently held by his father-in-law, on condition the Mortsauf scutcheon be placed alongside the Lenoncourt’s.“The office ought to go with the title,” he told the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry.My father is justified a hundredfold. Without my fortune none of this would have happened. My parents came from Madrid for the wedding and will return after the reception I am hosting for the newlyweds.The carnival will be lively. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria are in Paris, which unnerves me. Marie Heredia is one of Europe’s most beautiful women, and I dislike the way Felipe looks at her. Therefore, I am extra sweet and affectionate. Every look and gesture says what my lips do not: “She could not love like this!” Heaven knows how lovely I am! Yesterday Mme. de Maufrigneuse said, “Dear child, who can compete with you?”I keep Felipe entertained, making his sister-in-law seem dull by comparison. I am less sorry that a little Abencerrage is not on the way, since the Duchess will likely remain in Paris for her confinement and lose her beauty. If the baby is a boy, he will be named Felipe in honor of the exile. Fate decrees I shall be godmother again.Goodbye, dear. I plan to go to Chantepleurs early this year: our Italian tour was shockingly expensive. I will leave around the end of March to economize in Nivenais. Besides, I am tired of Paris. Felipe and I long for the tranquility of the park, cool meadows, and the sparkling Loire. Chantepleurs will be delightful after Italy; splendor becomes tiresome, and a lover’s gaze is more beautiful than any opera or painting!We expect you there. Do not fear my jealousy. You are free to test Macumer’s heart and expose his doubts. I am utterly indifferent. Since that day in Rome Felipe’s love for me has deepened. He just told me (looking over my shoulder) that his sister-in-law, Princess Heredia, his childhood dream, has no brains. Oh, my dear, I am worse than a ballet dancer! If you knew the joy that remark brought me! I pointed out her faulty French—she says “esemple” for “exemple,” “sain” for “cinq,” “cheu” for “je.” She is beautiful but lacks charm and wit. When praised she seems puzzled. Felipe could not have lived two months with Marie after marriage. Don Fernand, the Duc de Soria, suits her perfectly: generous but a spoiled child. I am tempted to be mischievous and make you laugh, but I will refrain. Much love, darling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My little girl is two months old, Jeanne-Athenais, and my mother and Louis' grand-uncle are her godparents. I plan to visit Chantepleurs very soon, since you aren't worried about a nursing mother. Your godson can now say your name—calling Matoumer because he can't manage the letter c. You'll be delighted; he has all his teeth, eats meat like a big boy, and scurries about like a little mouse. I watch him anxiously, miserable that I can't keep him close while I recover. Doctors have prescribed precautions, so recovery feels especially long. The discomforts of childbearing remain the same. But don't tell Felipe—this little girl resembles me and may even outshine your Armand.My father noticed Felipe looking thin, and my pet doesn't seem as blooming either. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria have left, so there’s no reason for jealousy. Is something troubling you that you aren’t sharing? Your letter is shorter and less affectionate than usual—another whim of my whimsical friend?I’m writing too much; my nurse is upset and Mlle. Athenais de l’Estorade is hungry. Farewell; please write long letters.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For the first time, my dear Renee, I find myself alone and crying. I sat under a willow by the Chantepleurs marsh, a charming view that feels incomplete without children. Nearly three years into my marriage, I have no child. Your quiver full of little ones makes me probe my heart.I would endure any suffering—anything you endured at my godson’s birth—to have a cherub of my own, like your Athenais. I picture her beauty, yet you tell me nothing—just like you, Renee! I believe you sense my trouble.Each disappointment plunges me into melancholy, and I compose sad elegies. When will I embroider tiny caps and sew lace for a little head? Will I never hear “Mamma” from my child or have my dress tugged by a beloved little despot? Will there be no carriage wheelmarks on the gravel, no broken toys in the courtyard? I long to see a life unfold—another Felipe, dearer still. I would love a son to share the intimacy of a second self.My park and castle feel cold and desolate. A childless woman is, to me, a monstrosity of nature; we exist to be mothers. Oh, my wise friend, how well you understand life! Barrenness is dismal. If I cannot be a mother, I must indulge in some romantic sorrow.I told my Moor, and he wept. He cannot bear jokes about his love, so I let him off easily, calling him a paladin of folly.Sometimes I feel a desire to go on pilgrimage, to carry my longings to a shrine or a watering place. Next winter I will seek medical advice. I am too angry with myself to write more. Goodbye.---A whole year has passed without a letter! What does this mean? I am a little hurt. Does your Louis, who visits me almost every other day, make up for your absence? It is not enough to know you are well; I want to know your feelings and thoughts, as I share everything with you, risking scolding or misunderstanding. Your silence and seclusion in the country, while your husband enjoys Parliamentary honors, worry me. His eloquence will surely win him a fine post when the session ends. Are you writing him advice letters?Why did you not take the chance to see Paris? I could have enjoyed your company for four months. Louis told me you would fetch him and have your third confinement in Paris—what a terrible mother Gigogne! After pestering Louis with questions, I learned his grand-uncle, Athenais’s godfather, is very ill. I suppose you, as a careful mother, may be angling for a legacy from your husband’s last relative. Rest easy, Renee; we are all working for Louis. Martignac will likely place him in the audit department. But if you won’t tell me why you hide in the country, I shall be cross.Are you afraid the family’s political wisdom should seem to center in you? Or is it the uncle’s legacy? Perhaps you fear seeming less to your children in Paris? Or are you simply too vain to show yourself in the capital in your present condition? Vain thing! Farewell.---You complain of my silence; have you forgotten my two little brown heads, my subjects and tyrants? I didn’t want to drag a four-year-old and a soon-to-be three-year-old to Paris while expecting again. I didn’t want to trouble you or upset your husband with such a party. I also detest life in hotels.When I come to Paris for the session, it will be to my own house. Louis’ uncle, hearing of his grand-nephew’s rank, gifted me two hundred thousand francs to buy a house in Paris, and I have tasked Louis with finding one near you. My mother has given me thirty thousand francs for furnishing, and I shall do my best not to disgrace you.I appreciate all you have done for Louis at Court. However, I do not want him in a conspicuous position; it would commit him too much. I prefer the Audit Office for its permanence. Our affairs are in good hands; once the steward masters the details, I will support Louis.As for long letters, how can I? A single day would sample them all; they are all alike, characterized by whether the children are well or not. In this solitary grange, hours become minutes or minutes hours, depending on their health.My delightful hours come when they sleep and I am no longer needed to rock or soothe. When they sleep beside me, I think, “Nothing can go wrong now.” Every mother invents dangers for her children: razors, fires, ponds. A mother’s life is a series of tender and terrible dramas. Each hour contains joys and fears.At night I dream of their future, which seems bright under a guardian angel's watch. Sometimes Armand calls me in his sleep; I kiss his forehead and his sister’s feet, watching them. These are my joys. Just last night I was roused because Athenais’ cradle lay too low and Armand lay uncovered, his feet purple with cold.“Darling mother!” he cried, flinging his arms around me.How important it is for a mother to have her children by her side at night! A nurse, however careful, cannot comfort them after a nightmare. They have visions and half-awakened terrors that only a mother can soothe. I have learned to sleep lightly, sensing their slightest stir; the demon of convulsions always lurks by their beds.With the first light, my babies stir. Their chatter blends with the morning air; Nais struggles to reach me, and Armand climbs up with monkey-like agility. They turn my bed into a playground; the baby pulls my hair, and Armand stands guard over my breast, defending his property. Their antics and laughter banish sleep.Then comes dressing. I slip on my gown, turn up my sleeves, and don an apron. With Mary’s help, I wash my little blossoms. I control the bath temperature—so much crying comes from mistakes here. Paper fleets and glass ducks help; amusement is key to getting them clothed and washed. If you knew the diversions I invent to coax a soft sponge over them, you would be amazed. Prayers, scoldings, promises, juggling—all are used. Providence matches a child’s cunning with a mother’s ingenuity. Few triumphs are known outside the nursery; even their father knows nothing of it. Only God, His angels, and perhaps you can fathom the satisfaction Mary and I share when the children are finally clean amid the chaos.I admit English women excel in certain domestic arrangements; their material care is admirable. My children will always be bare-legged with woolen socks—no swaddling or bandages; they will never be left alone. The helplessness of some French infants gives the nurse freedom—that explains much. A true mother is never free.That is why I do not write. Besides managing the estate, I am raising two children.Motherhood involves silent self-denial and devotion to minute details. I must watch the soup warming before the fire. Am I the kind of woman to shirk such cares? A child’s laugh at a well-enjoyed meal repays a lifetime. Armand nods when pleased; how could I relinquish the privilege of blowing on the spoonful too hot for my Nais, who still remembers my breast? To mince a chop for Nais, newly teethed, requires patience; only a mother can cajole an impatient child to eat.No number of servants or English nurses can replace a mother in that daily contest, where gentleness must soothe the little griefs of childhood. Caring for these innocent darlings engages the whole soul. A crying child condemns mother or nurse alike, except when it stems from true pain. Now that I have two to care for (and a third on the way), they occupy all my thoughts. Even you, whom I love dearly, have become a memory.My own dressing is often incomplete by two o'clock. I have no faith in mothers whose rooms are immaculate and who look perfect. Yesterday was a lovely April day, and I wanted to take the children for a walk while I still could—time presses. Such an expedition is an epic for a mother! I dreamed of it the night before: Armand in a little black velvet jacket and Scotch cap, Nais in white and pink with a baby cap. All was laid out. The little brown heads had to be brushed. To see the children I dressed, their pure skin veined with blue, is a joy unmatched. I long for one more kiss on their white necks.Once outside, triumphant, Armand helped Nais along the path. A carriage approached; I tried to move them out of the way. They tumbled into a puddle, ruining my work! We had to return and change. I took the little one in my arms, forgetting my ruined dress, while Mary seized Armand; with a crying baby and a soaked child, what mind has a mother left for herself?Dinner arrives—and I have done nothing. Feeding two children, putting on bibs, turning up sleeves, persuading them to eat—these recurring cares, joys, and catastrophes force me to neglect myself. If the children are naughty, I may keep my curl papers all day. To write you, I have let them cut pictures from my novels, build book castles, and play with my silks.Yet I have nothing to complain of. My children are strong and independent; they amuse themselves more easily than you might expect. They find joy in small things: pebbles, shells, sand. Armand talks to flowers, flies, and chickens, imitating them; everything small interests him. He is beginning to ask “why” about everything. He even wonders what I am saying to his godmother, whom he sees as a fairy. Strange how children hit the mark!One day, a poor man followed us begging—beggars know a mother with a child cannot resist. Armand, unaware of hunger or money, held out a trumpet I had just bought him, saying, “Here, take this!”What joy could compare with that moment?“May I keep it?” the poor man asked. “I too, madame, have had children,” he added, scarcely noting the money I gave him.I shudder at the thought of Armand going to school; I have only three and a half years left to keep him with me. The flowers of his sunny childhood will fall before the scythe of a public school system; his gracious ways and candor will lose their spontaneity. They will cut the curls I have brushed and kissed so often! What will they do with Armand’s thinking, his spirit?And what of you? Are you still in love with Felipe? I have no worries about the Saracen. Goodbye; Nais has tumbled, and if I continue, my letter will become a volume.---My sweet, tender Renee, you will have learned from the papers of the terrible calamity that has overwhelmed me. For twenty days I never left his bedside; I received his last breath and closed his eyes, keeping vigil with the priests and repeating prayers for the dead. The cruel pangs I suffered felt like rightful punishment; yet, seeing the calm smile on his lips, his last farewell to me, how could I believe I caused his death?Be it so or not, he is gone, and I am left. To you, who have known us both, what more need I say? I would give my share of Heaven to hear that my prayers could bring him back! To see him again, even for a second, would let me breathe without agony. Will you not come soon and soothe me with such promises? Is your love strong enough to deceive me?You told me beforehand that he would suffer through me. Was it so? Yes, I had no right to his love. Like a thief, I took what was not mine, and my frenzied grasp crushed the life out of my bliss. The madness is over, but I feel alone. Merciful God! What torture can exceed the misery in that word?When they took him away, I lay on the same bed, hoping to die. There was but a door between us, and I thought I could force it! After forty days of cruel care and medical science, they slowly nursed me back to life. Now I find myself in the country, seated by my window, surrounded by the flowers he made bloom for me, gazing at the view he loved. Ah, Renee, no words can express how new surroundings hurt when the heart is dead. I shiver at the sight of the wet earth in my garden, as if I tread upon him. When I first went out, I trembled and could not move. It was so sad to see his flowers, and he not there!My parents are in Spain. You know my brothers, and you are detained in the country. But do not worry; two angels of mercy have come to my side. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria rushed to their brother in his illness and have been everything I could wish. The last nights before the end found the three of us gathered in calm, wordless grief around a great man, rare in any age, head and shoulders above the rest. Felipe’s patient resignation was angelic. His last words to me were simple: “I had almost gone from life without leaving Fernand the Barony of Macumer; I must make a new will. My brother will forgive me; he knows what it is to love!”I owe my life to my brother-in-law and his wife; they want to take me to Spain.Ah, Renee, to no one but you can I speak freely of my grief. A sense of my own faults weighs me down, and there is solace in pouring them out to you. My exactions, jealousy, and restless nagging wore him to death. My love was dangerous because we shared the same sensitive nature; nothing was lost on him, and often my careless words struck straight to his heart. You cannot imagine how submissive he was. I only had to tell him to leave me alone, and he obeyed without a murmur. His last breath blessed me, repeating that a single morning with me was more precious than a lifetime with another woman, even the Marie of his youth. My tears fall as I write.This is my life now. I rise at midday and go to bed at seven; I linger over meals; I stand motionless before a single plant; I gaze into the trees; I take a serious interest in trifles; I long for shade, silence, and night. I fight through each hour, taking a gloomy pleasure in adding it to the heap of the vanquished. My peaceful park gives me the company I care for; everything there is full of glorious images of my vanished joy, invisible to others but eloquent to me.“I cannot away with you Spaniards!” I exclaimed one morning as my sister-in-law embraced me. “You have some nobility that we lack.”Ah, Renee, if I still live, it is because Heaven tempers affliction to the strength of those who bear it. Only a woman can know what it is to lose a love that was genuine, a passion that satisfied spirit and flesh. How rare to find a man so gifted that worshipping him brings no sense of degradation! If such fortune befalls us once, we cannot hope for it again. Men of true greatness, veiled by poetic grace—beware of love! Love will ruin you and the woman of your heart. This is my cry as I pace my woodland walks.And he has left me no child! That love, rich in smiles and joy, has left no fruit. I am accursed. Can it be that the purity and vehemence of a single-hearted passion render it barren? Is it only a marriage of reason, like yours, that is blessed with a family? Can Heaven be jealous of our passions? Such wild thoughts.You are the one person whose company I could endure. Come to me; none but Renee should be with Louise in her sorrow. What a day when I first donned my widow’s bonnet! When I saw myself in black, I fell back and wept until night. I weep again recalling that moment.Goodbye. Writing tires me; thoughts crowd fast, but I have no heart to express them. Bring your children; you can nurse the baby here without making me jealous. All that is gone—he is not here—and I shall be glad to see my godson. Felipe wished for a child like little Armand. Come, then, come and help me bear my woe.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My darling—By the time you read this I'll be near; I leave shortly this morning. We'll be alone: Louis must remain in Provence seeking re-election against Liberal opposition.I come not merely to comfort but to bring my heart to resonate with yours and help you endure. Weep—through tears you'll find the joy of reuniting with him. Remember he is journeying toward Heaven, and each duty you perform loosens the chain between you.In my arms you'll lift your head and go toward him—pure, noble, freed from errors that don't define you, carrying the good you'll do in his name.I write hurried lines amid departure preparations, interrupted by children and Armand calling, "Godmother! I want to see her," making me truly jealous—he could be your child.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yes, Renée, it is true; I have sold my house, Chantepleurs, and the farms in Seine-et-Marne, but I assure you I am neither mad nor ruined.After settling everything I have about twelve hundred thousand francs from my late husband’s fortune. I invested a million in the Three per Cents at fifty, which yields sixty thousand francs instead of the thirty thousand the property gave. My former life was tedious—six months in the country managing leases, farmers who paid when they pleased, and selling produce at a loss. In Paris my house brought ten thousand francs in rent, but delays and legal troubles made life burdensome for a twenty-seven-year-old widow.Now my fortune is secured with the state. Instead of paying taxes, I receive thirty thousand francs every six months from the Treasury, handed to me by a pleasant clerk. If the nation were to fail, I would still be as well off as before, and I have time to save a little capital. Besides, the Comte de l’Estorade is a peer of this semi-republic and a friend at Court, so I have no cause for anxiety.What drives a woman to such financial calculations? Love, my dear!Now I must reveal the reasons behind my actions, which may have puzzled you. I am to be married in a village near Paris. I love and am loved. Forgive me for keeping this secret; I wished to spare you and de l’Estorade the inevitable concerns. You know how jealous I can be, and I wanted to avoid anxiety.The man I love had thirty thousand francs in debts, which I have paid. You might think him an adventurer, but I investigated him. He has been courting me for nearly two years. I am twenty-seven and he is twenty-three—a difference that may alarm you. He is a poet who has lived on very little and spent more time dreaming than working. Practical people often see artists as unreliable, and I understand that skepticism.Despite his debts and our age gap, I am ready to end a long period of restraint. This is not a naive surrender; my love is profound, and he has waited patiently. He is noble and tender, and his love is genuine. My beloved is Gaston, the illegitimate son of the beautiful Lady Brandon, who died tragically. He was raised by his brother Louis, who has not been heard from in years. Louis, a sailor, wrote encouraging letters, but his absence has left Gaston in despair.Gaston believed genius would bring fortune, but his life has been hardship. He has struggled to make a name in literature, and his debts have increased. I met him at Mme. d’Espard’s and felt an immediate connection. He has talent and pride, which can intimidate women.Gaston has no idea of the depth of my love. I am confident in our happiness, and I feel a passion I have never known. I adore him as Felipe once adored me, and I am full of fears of losing him or of becoming less attractive as he remains youthful. Yet I believe I can nurture our love and keep it alive.If his love should wane, I would blame myself, not him. I know my nature leans toward being a lover rather than a mother. Should disaster come, I beg you, Renée, to care for my children as my legacy. Your qualities would make my death less bitter.I wish to keep my marriage private, performed in secret, to avoid distressing you. I will retain control of my property, and Gaston will not know its full extent. I plan to assign him a stock yielding twelve thousand francs a year, which he will find on the eve of our wedding. If he refuses it, I would end our engagement.This long confession has exhausted me. I will continue later; I must spend tomorrow in the country.October 20th.I have taken steps to ensure our secrecy. I purchased a small property near Ville-d’Avray: twenty acres, a lakelet, and a charming garden. The area is secluded, surrounded by woods, and beautifully landscaped. My chalet, inspired by Swiss designs, is well-equipped and hidden from view.The property includes a separate house for staff and a concealed entrance, ensuring privacy. I have carefully selected servants who will be loyal and discreet. All my cherished belongings from Paris have been moved to the chalet, creating a simple but elegant home.The tranquility of this valley is unparalleled, filled with nature’s sounds. I worry such perfect happiness may attract misfortune, as you have often warned.When I visited recently, I was moved to tears and paid my architect without hesitation, insisting on confidentiality about my ownership.Now you understand my mysterious actions and the true purpose behind my investments. Love is serious, and I have removed financial burdens to focus solely on him.Gaston remains unaware of my plans. We will begin our life at the chalet soon, and while our living will be modest, I intend to invest in my appearance for him. I want to maintain my beauty throughout our marriage so that I am loved as much at the end as at the beginning.In two days I will be Mme. Gaston. Is it fitting for a Christian to love so deeply?My man of business remarked that he was losing a client. And you, my dear, may feel you are losing a sister.Please address future letters to Mme. Gaston, Poste Restante, Versailles. We will keep our lives secret from the locals and procure provisions from Paris. Our retreat has been prepared in silence; only the architect knows of our existence.Farewell. As I write I feel a mix of grief and joy, proving my love for Gaston is as deep as my sorrow in leaving you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My Dear Daniel,I need two witnesses for my marriage; please come tomorrow evening with our friend Joseph Bridau. My future wife wishes to live in complete retirement, which explains our rare meetings this past year. After the wedding we will be apart for a long time, but I know you will understand: friendship can endure absence. I shall miss you, yet I shall not see you in my own home. She has sacrificed the closeness of a childhood friend for me, and I must do the same.This proves our love is deep and genuine, founded on mutual understanding. It gives me immense joy, yet I carry a painful secret. You know how desperate my finances have been, and only through your support have I managed. My wife is wealthy; I am not. I once joked about wanting a rich heiress, but now that it is real I feel a bitter humiliation I cannot shake. This shame confirms my love for her, for I do not recoil from it, even if it overwhelms me.Otherwise, my dreams have come true. She is the most beautiful and noble woman, with a charming personality and a sharp mind. At twenty-seven she is a widow without children, full of life yet occasionally melancholic. Despite her aristocratic background, she loves me despite my humble origins. Our love has grown over time; we share a deep emotional connection and deliberately avoid the physical aspect of love out of mutual respect.One day she confessed that her marriage was her regret, revealing the depth of her feelings. You, Daniel, are a giant among men, and you love a remarkable woman, just as I love my Armande.---Louise, can it be that you intend a life of wedded solitude after sacrificing your first husband? You seem to court future misery. I see you are resolved, and the man who won your heart must possess rare charm. But have you forgotten your earlier criticisms of young men, when you claimed they had tarnished purity?You are lucky to believe in happiness, and while I cannot blame you, I must warn you: nature and society conspire against absolute happiness, and I foresee some disaster arising from your boundless joy. I have no objections to your fiancé, but I hope you will describe him to me one day.I have not told Louis about your marriage; it would only invite his ridicule. I will simply say you wish to keep it secret. You are brave to face winter first, and I will be your discreet friend. Your journey into this emotional wilderness is like an explorer’s perilous expedition, though fortunately only a short distance from Paris. I wish you a safe journey and a swift return.---What has happened to you, my dear? After two years of silence, René has a right to worry about Louise. Is this what love does, scattering friendships like ours? You must admit that, while I am devoted to my children, a mother’s love is expansive and does not diminish other affections. I miss your letters and long to see your face.Regarding our situation, I will be brief. Your last letter contained sharp comments about our political position; you mocked us for keeping our posts in the Audit Department, which Louis owes to Charles X’s favor. But how could we provide for Athénaïs and René on an income of forty thousand livres, of which thirty thousand is entailed? We must live on our salary and save the estate’s income for their future. In twenty years we will have saved enough to provide for both children.Louis has been raised to the Peerage and is now a grand officer of the Legion of Honor. He has made himself useful in the Chamber and was offered an embassy, which I declined for the sake of our children’s education. I will not leave Paris until René has finished his schooling.We could not remain loyal to the elder branch of the dynasty without jeopardizing our children’s prospects. A mother is not called to be a Decius, especially now. In fifteen years, Louis will retire on a good pension, and I hope to secure a post for Armand in the Audit Department.I am a happy mother; my children give me endless joy. Armand is now a day scholar at Henry IV’s school. I wanted him in public school but could not bear to part with him, so I arranged for him to attend by day and return home each evening. He is the same charming boy you loved, and his tutor praises his progress.Home education has drawbacks: children raised at home may be exposed too early to the world’s passions and may lack the social ease gained among peers. A mother must guard her children from society’s distractions, as I have tried to do.Now that I am thirty, I feel a duty fulfilled. My children seem to sense my thoughts and respond. Armand, once dull, has lately excelled, winning prizes for translation and essays. I felt immense pride when his name was called and longed to shout, “I am his mother!”René, inspired by Armand, wants to attend school too. The three children can be noisy, but I cherish every moment. I am the only one allowed to care for them; the joys of motherhood outweigh the challenges.I must study their characters closely, for a mother alone discerns her children’s natural aptitudes. Home-bred children often develop ease and tact that can compensate for lack of innate ability. I believe Armand will become an admirable public servant, while René will be a bold sailor.Nais is so dear to me I can hardly see her as separate from myself. I want her to be happy and will only give her to a man who truly loves her. Yet I worry about her future and the risk of a poor marriage. Your silence has reminded me of the anguish marriage can bring a mother. Farewell for now, dear Louise, and please write back.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################So, after two years of silence, you wonder why I have not written. My dear Renée, words cannot express my happiness. We bear everything with ease; we are in perfect harmony. There has been no discord in our feelings, and each day brings its own joy. We do not fear monotony; we regret that our time together may not suffice for all the expressions of our love. We take more pleasure in each other's company than on the first day, and every hour reveals new reasons to love one another.Every evening we talk about visiting Paris, the new boulevards, and the finished Madeleine, yet in the morning we linger over breakfast and take a siesta. Gaston gazes at me as if I were a painting, and we both weep with joy over our love. I still play the mistress, pretending to give less than I receive; I delight in that little play. There is something sweet about passion tempered by tenderness; Gaston, my master, remains a timid suitor at my command.You asked me to describe him, but it is impossible to portray the man we love without emotion. Society alters men; the public man can differ from the man of feeling. The charming suitor may lack deeper qualities a woman values, while an unrefined man may possess a profound capacity for love. Our ideal is a man whose outward appearance aligns with his true nature, a grace that cannot be acquired. This ideal haunts every woman's imagination, and in Gaston I have found it.I never knew love could be so intertwined with youth, talent, and beauty. Gaston is unpretentious, moving with effortless grace. When we walk together, our bodies fit perfectly, seeming a single figure gliding through the woods. Our thoughts, desires, and words harmonize. Sometimes we walk in silence, listening to the rain and admiring the sunset, feeling as if in silent prayer for our happiness. A timid kiss exchanged in nature feels like a divine act of worship.We return home each time more in love than before. Such passionate love between older married people would be scandalous in Paris; only in the woods can we express it freely.Gaston is of average height, well-proportioned, and agile. He has a fair complexion, dark hair, and a striking resemblance to Louis XIII. His honorable poverty has spared him the vices that ruin many young men. His teeth are magnificent, and he has a robust constitution. His blue eyes are filled with tenderness for me, though they can flash with intensity at inspiring thoughts.He has a remarkable temperament—surprisingly even. I have heard many tales of women’s troubles with men who are dissatisfied with themselves, but Gaston is free from such burdens. I have learned that marrying an older man can lead to unequal unions, and I urge you not to let Athénaïs marry one.His smile is a treasure—always fresh and full of unspoken love and gratitude. Our shared experiences have become part of our joy, and even the smallest details of nature remind us of our time together. We share a deep connection, as if we are two editions of the same book. Our ability to view subjects broadly brings constant pleasure; this unity of thought feels like a pledge of love.Despite these pleasures, life here is laborious. I do my own room, refusing to let a hired hand intrude on my personal space. My room is meticulously arranged so that Gaston finds it pleasing. I rise early, bathe in cold water to refresh myself before he wakes, and emerge like a newly opened flower. He appreciates this freshness without knowing the effort behind it.My daily grooming is handled by my maid, and I dress several times a day for Gaston’s delight. We take great interest in our flowers and horticulture, surrounding our chalet with beauty. Our garden is a source of pride; we tend our fruits and vegetables eagerly.To keep Gaston's mind engaged, I encourage him to finish plays he began during earlier struggles. This literary work requires reflection and inspiration, and I assist him, ensuring our winter evenings are never dull.Our servants have an easy time; they need no reprimand. They have cleverly presented us as a traveling couple, content with their roles. The gardeners sell surplus produce, and we enjoy an abundance hard to find in Paris.I strive to fill the role of society for Gaston, ensuring his solitude does not become tedious. I have learned true jealousy—the kind born of love. The thought of him ceasing to love me sends shivers through me, and I tremble before him.Alas, I am still without a child. I long for the day when our home will be filled with the laughter of children running through our garden. It pains me to see your lovely children, for my life feels narrower without them. Motherhood expands a woman's heart, while love can be selfish. I envy your happiness and the joy you find in your children.As I approach thirty, I feel the limits of my beauty closing in. Though still attractive, I worry about aging while Gaston remains young. I often ask him to promise he will tell me if his love ever fades, and he swears it will not, finding the idea absurd.Goodbye, dear friend. Shall we let years pass without writing again? Happiness can be monotonous, and perhaps that is why Dante seems greater in the Paradiso than in the Inferno. I am not Dante, only your friend, and I do not wish to bore you. You can write, as your children provide endless joy, while I... No more of this. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, I have read your letter repeatedly; it reads more like a child's than a woman's. You forget that pure love belongs to nature and is distorted by civilization. Society created the family as the foundation of organized life at the cost of individual desires. A married man dedicates himself to his wife, who benefits from his efforts.You have taken a misguided path: the dream of being adored suits the young, not a mature woman. Vanity may enjoy inspiring adoration, but true fulfillment lies in the life of wife and mother. Return to Paris; fear not misfortune but happiness.Life's simple pleasures—bread, air, silence—sustain us. Passionate love exhausts; motherhood’s affections endure and reward. I chose self-sacrifice and would choose it again. Your life, despite its romantic veneer, is rooted in selfishness; I plead with you as I watch your happiness tested.Life is balance; excess brings pain or only fleeting pleasure. You are unwell because you cling to a passion a marriage should steady. The glory of home is calmness and a shared life, which you overlook. You, more passionate than tender, would be both wife and mistress, undermining marriage's sanctity.If you could understand a mother's sweet self-discipline. My proud nature softened into gentle melancholy, rewarded by motherhood's joys. I fear your life may darken. I wish you could witness family delights, but my arguments seem futile against your illusions. I had hoped time would restore reason, but your appetite for love seems insatiable. Farewell; I despair my letter, meant to reconcile you with society, only revealed your selfishness.---Renee, calamity has struck—no, a thunderbolt for poor Louise. Doubt is my calamity; certainty would be death. The day before yesterday, after my toilette, I looked for Gaston to walk but found him gone. In the stable his mare Fedelta was in a lather, which suggested he'd been to Paris without telling me. Suspicion seized me; I struggled to compose myself.When Gaston found me I was pale and shaken. He carried me to the parlor; I would not speak and fled to my room to weep. He waited; I said I'd see him when ready. After composing myself he returned, and I asked about mud on Fedelta's legs. He playfully threatened to withhold explanations; yet I saw no disloyalty in his eyes, though I remained unconvinced.We spent the day together, and my heart clung to him. I hated that mare for my distress. I asked if I bored him; he reassured me. I suggested selling Fedelta; his response crushed me—he spoke as if my feelings were trivial. Alone, I was consumed by despair, surrounded by darkness.Next morning I told him to take Fedelta to Paris; my tone betrayed turmoil. He urged trust; I tried to suppress suspicion. He left, and I watched him go with a sense of doom. When he returned late with a whip as a gift I felt it only distracted from something deeper. I later learned he bought it from Verdier, confirming my unease. I resolved to keep watch while staying silent.Days passed; Gaston changed, working harder—seeming to need money. His drawer was empty; he received letters he quickly tore up. Jealousy consumed me; I followed him to the post office and saw one note from Nathan announcing a play's acceptance. Despite his success, I couldn't shake that he hid something. A growing certainty that he had a rival drove me to discover the truth.After investigation I learned Gaston visited a woman in Paris; I followed and found she was his brother's widow, legally married. He had been secretly providing for her and her children. The revelation shattered me: the idea that his attentions were for another woman tormented me, and jealousy and betrayal gnawed at my reason. I returned tormented by thoughts of humiliation and revenge.Despite the pain, my love for Gaston remained. I struggled with his past and wanted to confront him about his feelings for the widow, but feared the truth would destroy me. I even planned my own end, unable to bear the suffering.---My dear Louise, I write urgently; your last letter alarmed me. I confided in Louis to save you. Though our methods may seem extreme, the outcome is satisfactory. I even involved the police, kept secret among us.Gaston is not as you feared. His brother Louis died in Calcutta, leaving a widow and two children. Gaston has secretly supported them, using his earnings for his sister-in-law and her children. The woman you mistook for a rival is not a threat but a mother in need; Gaston acted from kindness and duty.You should love him more; he has shown sensitivity and care. Welcome the widow and her children into your life—perhaps adopt the boys. This revelation should give you peace, not despair.Ah, my dear friend, what can I say? It is too late for me. My actions have sealed my fate. Tormented by love for Gaston, I cannot bear losing him. I am filled with despair and long for relief. Come, dear friend, and witness my decline.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My love,—Take the children to Provence without me; I must stay with Louise, who has only a few days left. I cannot leave her or her husband, for whom I worry.You know the letter that sent me rushing to Ville d'Avray, gathering doctors along the way. Since then I have not left my dear friend, sitting up every night for two weeks.When I arrived, Louise was with Gaston, looking beautiful and happy, but it was a façade. She whispered, "He must not know; I am dying." A chill ran through me as I felt her burning hand and saw the red spots on her cheeks. I was glad I had kept the doctors in the woods until needed."Leave us for a little," she told Gaston. "Two women who have not met for five years have secrets to share." Once alone she collapsed into my arms, unable to hold back her tears. I urged her to tell me. "I have brought the best surgeon and physician from the hospital, along with Bianchon." "Ah! Have them in at once if they can save me! The passion that drove me to death now cries for life!""But what have you done to yourself?" I asked."I have brought myself to the last stage of consumption in just a few days.""How?""I got myself into a profuse perspiration at night, then lay by the lake in the dew. Gaston thinks I have a cold, but I am dying!""Send him to Paris; I will fetch the doctors myself," I said, rushing out. After the consultation, none of the physicians gave us hope; they all believed Louise would die with the fall of the leaves. Her constitution had aided her plan, but a few days of folly had made her case desperate.I cannot express my feelings. I have lived in Louise as much as in my own life. I was crushed and could not escort the doctors out. I was lost in bitter thought when Louise's clear voice upset me: "So there is no hope for me!"She made me get up and took me to her drawing-room. "Stay with me to the end; I won't have sad faces around me. I must keep the truth from him. I know I have the strength to do it. I am full of youth and spirit, and can die standing! I have no regrets. I am dying as I wished, still young and beautiful, in the perfection of my womanhood.""For him, I can see I would have made his life miserable. My jealousy has already wounded him. When my suspicions met indifference, that would have been my death. I have had my share of life. There are people who have lived sixty years without two years of real life, while my thirty years are doubled by the intensity of my love.""For him and me, the end is happy. But for you, dear Renée, it is different. You lose a loving sister, and that is a loss that cannot be repaired. You alone have the right to mourn my death."After a long pause she continued: "The moral of my death is cruel. My dear doctor was right; marriage cannot rest on passion or even love. Your life is noble, giving your husband growing affection, while my passionate eagerness diminished. Twice I have gone astray, and twice Death has reached for my happiness. The first time, he took the noblest of men; now he takes me from my poet husband, with all his beauty and grace.""I would not complain. I have known two men of nobility—one in mind, the other in form. I die adored—what more could I wish? Perhaps time to draw near to God, whom I may have thought too little of. My spirit will fly towards Him, full of love, praying to be reunited with the two who made my life a heaven. Without them, paradise would be a desert.""My example would be fatal to others; to meet a Felipe or a Gaston is more than mortals can expect. The doctrine of society regarding marriage aligns with natural law. Women are weak and should sacrifice their will to their husbands, who should lay their selfishness at their feet. The stir women have created by their whining is ridiculous, showing how well we deserve the epithet of children."She spoke in her gentle voice, expressing grave truths prettily, until Gaston entered with his sister-in-law, the children, and the English nurse he had fetched from Paris."Here are the pretty instruments of my torture," she said as her nephews approached. "What a wonderful likeness to their uncle!" She welcomed Mme. Gaston and insisted she consider the chalet her home, playing hostess in her best manner.I wrote to the Duc and Duchesse de Chaulieu, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, as well as to Madeleine. It was time. The next day Louise, exhausted from the exertion, could not go out; she only got up for dinner. That evening Madeleine de Lenoncourt, her brothers, and her mother arrived. The coolness caused by Louise's second marriage faded. Every day since, her father and brothers have visited, and the duchesses spend their evenings at the chalet. Death unites as well as separates; it silences petty feelings.Louise remains charming, graceful, sensible, witty, and tender. She has retained her perfect tact and lavishes her brilliant mind on us, which made her one of Paris's queens."I want to look well even in my coffin," she said with her matchless smile as she lay down on the bed where she would linger for a fortnight. Her room lacks the sick-chamber atmosphere; medicines and nursing supplies are concealed. "Is not my deathbed pretty?" she asked the priest who came to confess her.We watched her like misers. The anxiety and harsh truths have prepared Gaston for the worst. He is brave, but the blow has struck deep. I would not be surprised if he followed her soon. Yesterday, while walking by the lake, he said, "I must be a father to those two children," pointing to his sister-in-law with the boys. "But I want your promise that you will be a second mother to them and persuade your husband to accept the role of guardian."He spoke simply, like a man who knows his time is short. He wears smiles for Louise, but I see through his façade. He matches her courage.Louise wishes to see her godson, but I prefer he remains in Provence; she might want to remember him generously, putting me in a difficult position.Goodbye, my love.August 25th (her birthday).Yesterday evening Louise was delirious for a short time, but her babbling was beautiful—the madness of a gifted person, not of a fool. In a faint voice she sang Italian airs from I Puritani, La Sonnambula, Moïse, while we stood around her bed in silence. Not one of us, not even the Duc de Rhetore, had dry eyes, realizing her soul was departing. She could no longer see us, yet her charm lingered in the melody, sweet and otherworldly.During the night the death agony began. It is now seven in the morning, and I have just raised her from bed. A flicker of strength returned; she wished to sit by the window and asked for Gaston's hand. Then, my love, the sweetest spirit we shall ever see on this earth departed, leaving us with an empty shell.The last sacrament was administered the evening before, unbeknownst to Gaston, who was sleeping during the ceremony. After she was moved to the window, she asked me to read the De Profundis in French, facing the lovely scene she created. She repeated the words after me and pressed her husband's hands.August 26th.My heart is broken. I have just seen her in her winding-sheet; her face is pale with purple shadows. Oh! I want my children! Bring me my children!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY — ONGOING MIDPOINT.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Comtesse Bolognini, nee Vimercati.Madame, remembering the joy your conversation gave a traveler in Milan, I lay this work at your feet, hoping you will protect it with your name as you once shielded the tales of a beloved writer. You have a daughter, Eugenie, whose beauty and intelligence promise a joyful childhood, unlike the rigid upbringing of the Eugenie in these pages. While Frenchmen are often thought fickle, I remain Italian in my constancy and memory. Writing “Eugenie” recalls the laughter and warmth of our time in Milan; I now regard you as a distant, treasured masterpiece.Your devoted servant,De Balzac.A DAUGHTER OF EVECHAPTER I. THE TWO MARIESIn a luxurious boudoir in the rue Neuve-des-Mathurins, two young women wept before a fireplace. The blue-velvet room, belonging to Ferdinand du Tillet, a wealthy banker who had married into an aristocratic family for a dowry, set the scene. Despite his fortune, du Tillet lacked true social standing.Madame du Tillet held her sister Madame Félix de Vandenesse’s hand as they exchanged intimate confessions. Raised by a strict, pious mother, they had entered marriage without ever enjoying youthful freedoms. Their upbringing, so severe and cloistered that it left them naive about life and love, had forged an intense bond between them. Forbidden the wider world and taught only religion and needlework, they retained innocence at the cost of understanding life’s complexities. Though marriage had seemed an escape, both found themselves unprepared for what awaited them.CHAPTER II. A CONFIDENCE BETWEEN SISTERSMadame de Vandenesse sought solace in her sister’s company, overwhelmed by marital troubles. She confessed despair over a husband who seemed indifferent to her happiness. Madame du Tillet, feeling helpless, listened as her sister lamented; their sisterly love transcended their husbands’ indifference.Both revealed the emptiness of their marriages: du Tillet’s coldness and Félix’s inattentiveness. They voiced desires for passion and excitement, recognizing the limits society and marriage imposed. Each encouraged the other to seek contentment within those constraints while acknowledging their longing for more.CHAPTER III. THE HISTORY OF A FORTUNATE WOMANFélix de Vandenesse, of noble lineage, had married hoping to recreate an idealized love he had once known. Initially content, Madame de Vandenesse began to feel monotony and lack of passion. Though her husband was kind and attentive, he could not satisfy her craving for deeper emotional connection. She grew aware of the allure of romance outside marriage, and a yearning for adventure took hold as duty and social obligation stifled their relationship.CHAPTER IV. A CELEBRATED MANRaoul Nathan, an emerging literary figure, entered society with talent and audacity. Known for sharp wit and caustic humor, he charmed and provoked in equal measure. His unconventional appearance and manner set him apart and made him captivating to women.At a gathering hosted by Madame de Montcornet, Nathan drew Madame de Vandenesse’s notice. Their initial interactions held an unspoken attraction. As Nathan managed a complex career and personal life, he became increasingly drawn to her, seeing her as a possible muse. Yet society’s norms and both their circumstances threatened the budding connection.CHAPTER V. FLORINEFlorine, a talented actress and Nathan’s longtime companion, represented another side of his life. Their relationship mixed affection and practicality: she supported his spirits and career while relying on him for security and influence. The bond was complicated by public demands and private needs.As Nathan’s ambitions grew, he found himself torn between loyalty to Florine and growing feelings for Madame de Vandenesse. Florine, aware of his infatuation, felt threatened by the countess and the potential shift in Nathan’s affections.CHAPTER VI. ROMANTIC LOVEMadame de Vandenesse and Nathan’s relationship deepened, fueled by shared passion for art and literature. Their encounters became more intimate and exhilarating, but social expectations and their roles cast long shadows, creating tension and moral peril.Madame de Vandenesse wrestled with guilt and desire, torn between loyalty to her husband and attraction to Nathan. The emotional turmoil intensified her longing for a life of passion, even as she feared scandal and consequence.CHAPTER VII. SUICIDENathan, under mounting pressure in career and life, sank into despair. The weight of ambition and fear of failure drove him toward suicide. A chance encounter with Madame de Vandenesse, however, rekindled his will to live.Seeing his desperation, the countess resolved to help. She recognized the depth of his struggle and sought to encourage him, offering protection from his darkest impulses and from the destructive side of his ambitions.CHAPTER VIII. A LOVER SAVED AND LOSTAfter Nathan’s near-suicide, Madame de Vandenesse became more involved in his life, balancing protection and her own feelings. Their relationship became both refuge and torment as they faced moral and practical realities under social scrutiny.As Nathan’s work began to flourish, he remained torn between Florine’s pragmatic attachment and the countess’s inspiring influence. Florine, feeling endangered, grew increasingly possessive, heightening tensions among the three.CHAPTER IX. THE HUSBAND’S TRIUMPHFélix de Vandenesse, aware of his wife’s attachment to Nathan, acted to preserve his marriage. Determined to uncover the truth and shield her from scandal, he sought to protect her honor and well-being. His resolve to defend their union displayed his love and commitment.The story follows characters entangled by love, ambition, and societal expectation, leading to a dramatic conclusion that underscores the conflicts of desire and duty.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################I. EARLY MISTAKESOn a Sunday morning in April 1813 a luxurious cabriolet drew up before the Tuileries, carrying an anxious, aged father and his beautiful daughter, Julie. He prided himself on the attention she drew as they walked through the gardens; she was eager to see the military review commanded by Napoleon, the last before a disastrous campaign. They reached the gates to disappointment; the review had not begun, and he suggested leaving. Julie insisted on staying, afraid of missing the Emperor. An officer recognized her and admitted them to a prime vantage point. The spectacle was grand: the Old Guard prepared, the crowd buzzed, and anticipation mounted for Napoleon’s arrival.When Napoleon appeared, cheers arose. Julie was captivated, but her attention soon fixed on a young officer, Colonel Victor d’Aiglemont, on the Emperor’s staff. Seeing him among others, she felt a pang of jealousy and an unexpected attraction, while her father’s anxious manner reminded her of their precarious situation. As the review ended, her father warned her about future happiness, fearing youthful dreams could lead to sorrow. He advised against falling for a man like Victor, whom he deemed unworthy. Despite his cautions, Julie felt drawn to Victor and their attachment deepened.Nearly a year later, in March 1814, Julie and Victor were traveling together. The once vibrant girl had become a shadow; her health faltered as Victor, now a colonel, was absorbed in military duties, leaving her isolated. Their relationship strained; her yearning for love and connection grew. During their travels they met an Englishman, Arthur, detained in France. Julie felt a strange kinship with him; Arthur’s quiet admiration contrasted sharply with Victor’s indifference, and she found herself drawn to Arthur’s gentleness. Despite growing feelings, Julie remained bound by her marriage. Torn between duty and desire, she wrestled with emotions that deepened her inner conflict.As Napoleon fell and the political landscape shifted, Victor’s ambitions changed; he became more involved with the new regime. Julie continued to think of Arthur, who lingered in her thoughts as a steady presence. The relationship reached crisis when Victor’s infidelities surfaced. Julie’s heartache deepened as she grasped his selfishness. In desperation she sought solace in Arthur, though the weight of her choices pressed upon her.Over time Julie’s health declined further and she became increasingly isolated. The once vibrant woman was now a mere shadow trapped in a loveless marriage. Her longing for connection remained unfulfilled, and the hard reality of her situation closed in. At last, in a moment of clarity, Julie resolved to take control of her life. She refused to remain passive. With newfound determination she sought to reclaim her happiness, even at the cost of defying societal expectations.Julie’s journey becomes a poignant exploration of love, sacrifice, and the complexities of womanhood, forcing her to confront the choices that define her existence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between the Seine and the Loing lies a flat, dreary country bordered by the Forest of Fontainebleau, with towns like Moret, Montereau, and Nemours marking its southern limits. The landscape presents a monotonous horizon where melancholy seems to thrive. In the center of this plain stands the old château of Saint-Lange, surrounded by avenues and gardens, a remnant of diminished wealth.In late 1820 a young woman, known in Paris for her beauty and wit, arrived at Saint-Lange, startling villagers who had not seen gentry in years. The Marquise d’Aiglemont’s presence provoked curiosity, especially as she seemed frail and withdrawn; locals speculated that her husband’s gambling in Spain had caused financial ruin and that the estate might be sold, prompting them to consider their own interests.The Marquise remained isolated, rarely leaving her room and only dining briefly with her daughter, whom she could not emotionally reach. Her profound sorrow and silence prompted gossip, but interest waned, leaving her to solitude. At twenty-six she was grappling with an unacknowledged grief that had extinguished maternal instincts and left her feeling like a ghost in her own life.Her anguish sprang from a lost love, a man who had died to preserve her honor. Bound by social expectations, she felt unable to express her pain, producing a deep internal conflict. Her beauty and youth felt meaningless as she was haunted by the absence of true love and the joy it might have brought. She reflected on her past and recognized the emptiness of her marriage, which, while socially acceptable, lacked the emotional fulfillment she desired.Sitting in her room, the flat horizon mirrored her desolation. Seasons changed but her heart remained heavy with unfulfilled longing; she felt trapped in a life offering no hope or joy, her thoughts consumed by what might have been. Her internal struggle was compounded by guilt and the pressure to maintain a façade of happiness.One day the village priest, M. le Curé, visited, sensing her suffering. He spoke of the universal nature of grief and the importance of faith, but she initially dismissed his comfort, feeling her pain unique and insurmountable. The priest told of a father who lost all his sons in battle, emphasizing resilience in despair. This story resonated, and she found some solace in the idea that others had endured similar suffering.Gradually she opened up to the priest, revealing feelings of isolation and the emptiness of her motherhood. She felt disconnected from her daughter Hélène, whom she saw as a reminder of her lost love. The Marquise believed true motherhood required a deep emotional bond she lacked; her relationship with Hélène was marked by duty rather than affection, deepening her despair.The priest encouraged her to find strength in her maternal role, but she struggled to reconcile her inadequacy. She lamented the societal constraints dictating her life, feeling trapped in a marriage that resembled a prison. Her bitterness toward norms grew as she recognized the hypocrisy of a system that forced women into roles that stifled their true selves.With spring came some distraction: managing the estate offered a temporary reprieve from grief. Yet her emotional turmoil remained unresolved. By the time she left Saint-Lange in October she had regained some vitality, though the scars of sorrow lingered. She encountered the curé once more, but her avoidance of him signaled a desire to move on from the painful memories tied to the château.The Marquise d’Aiglemont’s journey through grief and isolation reflects the struggle of a woman caught between societal expectations and her desires: a story of profound loss, the search for identity, and the complexities of motherhood in a rigidly structured society.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Firmiani hosted a ball where M. Charles de Vandenesse, a promising young diplomat, came to take leave of Paris before his appointment to the Congress of Laybach. Having enjoyed Parisian pleasures yet accustomed to a transient life, he felt little regret. Near thirty, philosophical about existence, he favored ambition over romance and regarded the social scene as largely superficial and full of empty intrigues.Surveying the assembly, he noted the elite’s emptiness: men and women seemed devoid of genuine feeling, engaged in trivial pursuits. His reflections were interrupted when Madame Firmiani introduced him to the enigmatic Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman wrapped in mystery and known for past attachments. Vandenesse was intrigued by her beauty and the depth of her character, which contrasted sharply with the surrounding frivolity.The Marquise, also thirty, possessed a delicate beauty and an air of melancholy that drew him in. Her demeanor suggested unfulfilled dreams and resignation. He perceived a stark disparity between her sensibility and her husband, a dull man who had risen by her influence. Beneath her outward calm, Vandenesse sensed profound sadness, which deepened his interest.Their conversation shifted from light banter to themes of love and feeling, revealing a connection that excited and troubled him. He felt captivated, torn between admiration and longing, and before leaving he asked to visit again, drawn to her despite the complications of her past.In the days that followed, the Marquise occupied his thoughts. He oscillated between hope and doubt about her feelings and decided to see her. Their meetings mixed friendship and unspoken desire, with Vandenesse wrestling over the implications of affection for a married woman.She, too, was drawn to him but struggled with loyalty to the memory of a deceased lover. Aware of societal constraints, her heart was split between the past and the possibility of new love. Though reserved, she could not deny their bond.As their relationship deepened, obstacles became clearer. Vandenesse admired the Marquise’s strength yet felt the weight of her history. Their love blossomed amid social expectations and personal memory, producing moments of joy and anguish. One evening a tender kiss marked a turning point, but the intrusion of her husband shattered the moment and forced them to confront reality. Their connection attested to love’s power to transcend social bounds, yet remained entangled with unfulfilled desire and the haunting presence of the past, leaving both to navigate treacherous waters of love and convention.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between the Barrière d’Italie and the Barrière de la Santé, the boulevard toward the Jardin des Plantes opens a view of Paris able to stir the jaded. From an elevated point a tree-lined avenue descends into a valley of factories set among greenery and the brown streams of the Bièvre. Across the slope, the squalor of the Faubourg Saint-Marceau is contrasted by the majestic Panthéon and the somber dome of the Val-de-Grâce towering over dense rooftops and poplars. To the left the observatory’s windows throw odd shadows; beyond, the lantern tower of the Invalides rises between the Luxembourg and Saint-Sulpice. Architecture and foliage merge and shift with the light.To the right the canal Saint-Martin runs along reddish quays shaded by lime trees, with Romanesque granaries on its banks. Beyond, the smoke-streaked slopes of Belleville, dotted with houses and windmills, blend into the clouds. Between that horizon and the valley’s roofs lies a vast, veiled city alive with sounds that assert its presence. When sunlight pours over this portion of Paris it transforms windows and tiles and suffuses the scene with a magical atmosphere: an idyll of human and natural life. From the cypresses of Père-Lachaise the whole capital seems at peace.One morning, while admiring this view and thinking bitterly of those wealthy wanderers who scorn their homeland, my reverie was broken by the sound of a kiss. A young woman, simply elegant, stood with a handsome young man and a charming little boy; their joy was evident in movement and expression. A second child — a little girl — stood apart. Dark-haired and serious, perhaps seven or eight, she watched with a gravity that suggested premature maturity. The boy, fair and carefree, contrasted with her. Their clothes were alike, but the girl’s plainer collar hinted at some maternal preference.The boy offered his sister a small toy; she scowled, and an uneasy complexity showed on her face. Their interaction, indifferent at first, turned hostile. When the little boy advanced toward her she pushed him away; he stumbled, fell into the river and sank beneath the murky water. The scene happened with terrifying swiftness. I rushed to help but it was too late. The mother arrived frantic; nothing could be done. The loss imprinted itself on her — the grief she would carry home — and the darker implication of the daughter’s action hung in silence, haunting the family.Years later, the Marquis de Vandenesse, now mourning his father, entertained a notary at dinner. The notary, oblivious to social cues, monopolized the conversation. The Marquise, impatient to go to the theatre with her children, and her husband dropped hints to end the visit, but the notary persisted, blind to the couple’s desire to leave. As nine o’clock struck the Marquis interrupted, insisting the notary go so he might escort his wife. The notary still failed to comprehend, prolonging his talk and making misunderstandings worse.At that moment the Marquise’s husband returned unexpectedly with the children, who looked upset: the little girl’s eyes were red from crying; the boy was fretful. They had come back from a play that had distressed them. The Marquise’s face hardened as she realized the nature of their agitation. The notary, attempting to be jocular and unaware of the emotional weight in the room, only inflamed the situation with crude remarks about family dynamics and parental preference.Overwhelmed, the Marquise sent her daughter away. The notary, continuing in his tone-deaf way, still spoke until the Marquis, unable to bear it, demanded he leave. Confused and flustered, the notary withdrew. Later, in reflection, he failed to grasp the real cause of the evening’s strain, attributing the episode to aristocratic sensitivities. His wife, seeing the truth, warned him to restrict himself to business in future dealings. The notary’s inability to perceive social nuance promised further misunderstandings in his professional life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of Napoleon’s orderly staff-officers, known as the General or the Marquis, spent the spring at Versailles and, having amassed a fortune during the Restoration, took a country house near Montreuil for solitude close to Paris. The house, once a retreat for a nobleman’s fleeting romances, had expansive grounds; its entrance still faced the street, a reminder of a once-quiet lane.One winter evening the family was alone; the servants had gone to Versailles for a wedding. It was Christmas time, and as the revelers lingered, the General felt uneasy when the clock struck eleven with no sign of their return. Outside, silence lay over the countryside, broken only by the wind and distant footsteps.Inside, the family enjoyed a cozy evening. The General sat relaxed in a high-back armchair by the fire, watching his youngest son resist his mother’s attempts to undress him and prefer to play with his sister. Their laughter filled the room; the Marquise, beautiful and content, watched them with affection. The General, a rugged soldier, took joy in their innocence and the warmth of family life.At a nearby table their thirteen-year-old son read the Arabian Nights, absorbed in the tale. His sister, Helene, worked on a tapestry; striking in beauty and thoughtful in demeanor, she contrasted with the younger children’s play. The family’s dynamic mixed childlike play with older, more serious reflection.As the evening wore on, the General expressed concern over the absent servants, and the Marquise, sensing tension, urged their son to bed. Then a series of hurried knocks echoed from the street, signaling urgency. The General, armed and cautious, answered the door to find a breathless man seeking refuge, claiming he was being pursued.The stranger, desperate and disheveled with bloodied hands, insisted on secrecy and water. Despite hesitation, the General agreed to shelter him, feeling an inexplicable pull toward the man. He led the stranger to a hidden room, torn between unease and obligation.The Marquise, sensing something amiss, questioned her husband about the newcomer, but the General urged secrecy. Helene, curious and concerned, overheard their low conversation and felt compelled to investigate. Approaching the locked room, she was struck by the stranger’s presence: despite his grim state he exuded a magnetic power. Their eyes met and an unspoken connection formed, leaving Helene intrigued and unsettled. She returned to the family with her mind racing.As the night advanced, the General’s thoughts were consumed by the implications of harboring a fugitive. Tension in the household grew; the Marquise, noting her daughter’s distraction, pressed Helene to explain. Torn between loyalty to her family and fascination with the stranger, Helene struggled to articulate her feelings.The General, now fully aware of the danger the stranger’s presence invited, resolved to protect his family at all costs. He demanded answers about the servants’ delay and called for them to return, his anger reflecting the stakes. Gendarmes soon arrived seeking the fugitive, and the General prepared to confront them, his protectiveness surging. The stranger remained silent, contemplating his next move as the household braced for confrontation.In the ensuing chaos Helene’s feelings for the stranger deepened, forcing her to question her desires and the life she had known. The night culminated in a dramatic encounter that compelled the General to face the moral complexities of his hospitality and its consequences for his family.Ultimately, the General’s decision to shelter the fugitive had unforeseen repercussions: Helene’s connection to the stranger altered the course of their lives. What began as an idyllic evening became a night of turmoil, exposing the fragility of their happiness and the weight of the choices they had made.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It was a June day in 1844 when the Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman of about fifty, walked in the garden of her daughter’s Paris mansion. She lingered by a row of windows, sitting to gaze at the dome of the Invalides and the family grounds. The neighborhood was quiet; the aristocracy was only beginning to stir.Having lost her two sons, Gustave and Abel, the Marquise had given her fortune to her daughter, the Comtesse Moïna de Saint-Héreen. Her affection for Moïna was profound, a contrast to the faint memories she retained of her deceased children. Society regarded the Marquise’s devotion as a kind of superstition and largely overlooked both her past and her coolness toward her daughter-in-law.Despite her sacrifices, the Marquise was often alone; Moïna was absorbed in her social life. Her love for her daughter was evident but shaded by sadness and a sense of loss. Gossip suggested she might regret giving up her fortune, yet most praised her devotion. When she did appear in society she felt eclipsed by Moïna’s youthful charm.As she sat in the garden the Marquise reflected on her life; her face showed experience and beauty faded by sorrow and time. She was aware of Moïna’s flirtation with Alfred de Vandenesse, a man she regarded with disdain, and feared it could end in heartbreak.The Comte de Saint-Héreen had been away for months, and Moïna’s flirtation seemed perilous. Feeling powerless, the Marquise knew her warnings would likely be ignored; her heart ached at the thought of her daughter on a precipice she could not prevent.In a moment of despair she recalled the small incidents that had accumulated over the years, each marking Moïna’s growing distance. She went to Moïna’s room hoping to speak, only to meet indifference. The Countess’s dismissive attitude stung, and the Marquise felt the weight of contempt.When she finally confronted Moïna their conversation grew tense. The Marquise warned of the risks posed by Alfred; Moïna’s flippant replies deepened her sorrow. Leaving the room, the Marquise felt a physical pain in her heart, realizing her daughter had lost respect for her.Outside, overwhelmed by grief and the sense of irrevocable estrangement, she collapsed on a bench. Moïna, unaware of the gravity of the situation, believed she could repair things with a few kind gestures. The Marquise’s condition worsened; she was carried inside, lifeless.Servants rushed for a physician, but the Marquise’s fate was sealed. Struck by remorse as she watched her mother decline, Moïna suddenly grasped how she had taken that love for granted. It was too late for reconciliation. As she wept, the Marquise, in her final moments, managed a smile at her daughter — a testament to a mother’s enduring love and forgiveness. Moïna stood before her family hollow with regret, realizing the depth of her mother’s sacrifice.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######### ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In the spring of 1822 Parisian doctors sent M. le Baron Gaston de Nueil to Lower Normandy to recover from an inflammatory illness likely due to overwork or excess. Seeking rest, a light diet, and fresh air, he settled in Bayeux with welcoming relatives.After a few evenings with his cousin, Mme. de Sainte-Severe, Gaston met the local elite, who embodied provincial French types. One was an ancient but impoverished noble family: a patriarch, a determined sportsman contemptuous of modernity and the new political order; a loud, domineering wife convinced their daughters’ fortunes rested solely on their name. They clung to obsolete customs, representing nobility without wealth or influence.In contrast stood a wealthier, more recent family who embraced contemporary Parisian trends. The wife was a society woman—just behind the latest fashions—while the husband, a member of the Departmental Council, was shrewd with finances and versed in political gossip. Their Royalism was less zealous than the old nobles’, engaging with modern life while preserving ancestral ties.The local bishop, a former Vicar-General, mediated between these families: respected for his office but often reminded of his plebeian origins. Retired military officers and their families preserved a veneer of aristocracy while focusing on local affairs rather than national politics. The women dressed as if for court, yet their days were governed by routine and tradition.This exclusive social set included a few wealthy townspeople who had infiltrated the aristocracy and were regarded skeptically by the older generation. Ecclesiastics brought wit and a bourgeois element to otherwise staid gatherings.Collectively, the society was marked by antiquated beliefs and resistance to change.
