Conversations circled familiar themes, members issued immutable judgments, and intellectual life stagnated. New ideas were unwelcome; any outsider who criticized their way of life was quickly labeled a misfit.Before his arrival, Gaston had been appraised by the local elite: his cousin disclosed his fortune and family background. At twenty-three he was welcomed as a promising young man with an income of eighteen thousand livres from land in the Auge valley; his father was negotiating to buy more woodland, further securing their status. Yet his situation was complicated by an elder brother who was consumptive and likely dying—a fact Mme. de Sainte-Severe omitted.At first Gaston amused himself by observing and sketching his acquaintances’ quirks, enjoying their quaintness. But as weeks passed he grew weary of their monotony, feeling trapped in a cloistered existence that drained his spirits. This confinement produced a crisis of spirit: ennui and mounting discontent. Gradually he lost touch with the vibrant life of Paris; memories of intellectual engagement dimmed, and he risked becoming another fossil among the fossils—content to exist in a dull, complacent routine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening Gaston de Nueil sat between a dowager and a vicar-general in a gray-paneled drawing-room where family portraits looked down on four card tables and sixteen people playing whist. After a pleasant dinner he noted provincial customs—the old cards, threadbare tablecloths—and their philosophic indifference to luxury, which made him question the value of opulence. The vibrant life of Paris felt far away.His attention fell on a young lady with red hands and a shy demeanor, at first awkward and unattractive. A chance remark about Mme. de Beauseant, however, stirred him like a passage of good music in a tedious opera. An elderly woman mentioned the Vicomtesse had been unwell and had declined an invitation to dinner, which piqued Gaston’s curiosity. The conversation disclosed Mme. de Beauseant’s scandalous liaison with M. d’Ajuda-Pinto and her ensuing isolation at Courcelles. Despite noble lineage, she was shunned because of her separation from her husband—a circumstance that intrigued him.He became captivated by the idea of Mme. de Beauseant, seeing her as a figure of hope and desire amid provincial dullness. An obsession grew; he pictured her as a woman worth pursuing in a stagnant world. His thoughts turned to gaining access to her, aware of obstacles posed by her intelligence and social standing.The next morning he wandered near Courcelles, hoping for a chance encounter, his imagination suffused with romance. He considered writing but feared rejection. Instead he resolved to enlist M. de Champignelles to arrange an introduction. He told the Marquis he had a delicate matter to discuss with the Vicomtesse and asked him to inquire whether she would receive him. Gaston’s charm and apparent sincerity convinced the Marquis, who was glad to oblige a pretty man.M. de Champignelles hurried to Courcelles and, conveying Gaston’s request, aroused Mme. de Beauseant’s curiosity despite her initial reluctance. After deliberation she agreed to meet him, intrigued by the mystery surrounding this young suitor.Meanwhile Gaston brimmed with excitement and anxiety, hoping to impress her. He prepared meticulously, believing appearance would shape her impression—unaware that true attraction would hinge more on wit and character than looks.Approaching Courcelles he felt a mix of confidence and nervousness, conscious that entering a room with one woman could be more daunting than facing a crowd. When announced, he entered with a blend of ease and trepidation, ready to engage the enigmatic Vicomtesse.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A great fire burned on the hearth despite the mild weather, casting a soft light over a young woman seated in a high-backed bergère. The Vicomtesse, with graceful movements, stirred the coals, picked up a fallen glove, and pointed with a delicate hand to a chair for M. de Nueil. Her charm and poise—an elegant mix of fastidious carelessness and high breeding—set her apart from the automatons he had met during his two months’ exile in Normandy. In her presence he felt transported back to Paris, immersed again in the sophistication he had missed.Mme. de Beauseant had fair hair, dark eyes, and a pale complexion that gave her a regal air like a fallen angel; her hair, gathered in a crown of plaits, heightened that noble appearance. Yet beneath her beauty lay an underlying sadness, traces of past unhappiness and lost love. Alone in her silent salon, she seemed cut off from the world, haunted by memories of a youth now emptied. M. de Nueil felt humbled, captivated by the blend of beauty, nobility, and misfortune that surrounded her.When she acknowledged him, he felt the weight of his deception. He had come to deliver a message from M. de Champignelles, but the consciousness of his dishonesty made him flush. Summoning his courage, he confessed, “Madame, I do not deserve the happiness of seeing you. I have deceived you basely.” His apology met disdain; she rang for her servant to escort him out, her manner shifting from gracious to icy.As he left, he realized that if he went away now he would never return, and the Vicomtesse would think him a fool. He hesitated, considering she might repent. On a sudden impulse he turned back, claiming he had forgotten something; the servant, ignorant of the truth, followed him into the salon.“Jacques lighted me to the door,” he said with a half-sad smile, which softened Mme. de Beauseant’s demeanor. She invited him to sit; he did so eagerly, eyes shining with happiness. She felt a thrill at his delight, grateful for what she took as his comprehension of her complex emotions.“Madame,” he began, “my blunder you know, but you do not know how much I am to blame.” She interrupted with a playful gesture, warning him to be careful, but her expression darkened as she reflected on how her life had changed. “This is all very ridiculous! I cannot do as I like; I am obliged to think. What brings you here? Is it curiosity? If so, I am paying dearly for a fleeting pleasure.”She accused him of disrespect and asked whether he had come to pity her, asserting she would rather be scorned than pitied. The tension grew as she voiced her pain at his intrusion and urged him to see the impropriety of his conduct. Fearful gossip would follow any continued contact, she commanded him not to return.M. de Nueil acknowledged his fault, blaming youthful enthusiasm and a craving for happiness. He explained that his longing to see her was natural—the weariness of exile and inner fires that drove him. He painted himself as a young man yearning for love, who had made her the ideal of his dreams, and spoke of the feelings and wild thoughts that had led him to seek her out, hoping to awaken in her some indulgence for the folly he had committed in her name.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################An impassioned voice warmed the chill solitude with youthful charm. Mme. de Beauseant, moved by its genuine feeling, found herself captivated by M. de Nueil’s noble countenance—Gaston, in the bloom of youth, seeming the ideal man, untouched by ambition or vanity. Both were drawn to each other, reflecting on their feelings while concealing them. M. de Nueil saw in the Vicomtesse not only beauty but depth and goodness. Mme. de Beauseant, hearing Gaston’s youthful troubles, recognized the struggles of someone kept pure by hard work against society’s corruptions.As they explored their sentiments, Gaston’s approach was instinctive; Mme. de Beauseant’s was deliberate—she tested him by posing opposing opinions. Her wit and grace put him at ease, prompting his exclamation, “Oh! madame, how could any man have left you?” The remark struck her, reviving a sense of innocence, condemning the world for her past, and offering an absolution that helped her rise above former shame.She glanced at the clock and, with a gentle smile, acknowledged the fleeting nature of their encounter. Gaston, sensing its gravity, wished to prolong the moment. Mme. de Beauseant, however, insisted on preserving her solitary life, fearing further connection would tarnish her reputation. She spoke candidly of a forsaken love and the lessons solitude had taught her.Her eloquence and beauty captivated Gaston, who longed to declare his feelings but feared rejection. Torn between admiration and dread of appearing foolish, he eventually confessed his love and offered to heal her old wounds. Mme. de Beauseant, moved yet resolute, reminded him they must remain strangers and spoke of the pain of being unable to follow the man she loved through life’s changes.Afterward Gaston left feeling more distant, uncertain whether her charm was genuine or mere coquetry. He returned home consumed by thoughts of her, alternating between despair and hope. At twenty-three he was hampered by diffidence, feeling unworthy of her love and anxious about expressing himself.That night he was restless and began writing letters, none satisfactory. The next day he returned to Courcelles at night, cautious to avoid being seen. After much deliberation he penned a heartfelt letter, laying bare his deep feelings and the mark she had left on his life. He implored her not to dismiss his words, exposing his vulnerability and desperation.As he awaited her response, doubts gnawed at him; he feared she would laugh at his letter and at his love. He wished to retract his words, finding them inadequate and pretentious. Overwhelmed by emotion, he longed for friends to distract him from his turmoil, confronting the harshness of unrequited passion and the uncertainty of a future without Mme. de Beauseant.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Nueil received a letter from Mme. de Beauseant, sealed with the arms of Burgundy. He shut himself up to read it.She reproached him gently for misunderstanding her motives: she had tried to spare him a rebuff and had been drawn to his intellect, but his youthful generosity had disappointed her. She had spoken frankly to show she was not governed by passion. She had read his letter when another woman might have tossed it aside, and she now answered: she acknowledged his feelings but did not share them. Near thirty while he was just over twenty, she argued, he could not foresee what he would think at her age; vows made lightly might become intolerable. She believed he might give his life for a moment’s happiness, but later he would find sacrifices for her humiliating; there would come a day when everything would urge him to leave, and she had said she would prefer death to desertion. She could not yield to blind impulse. Her consolation lay in God, not in earthly attachments; she saw hearts too clearly to accept merely friendly intimacy. Forgiving his youthful folly, she urged him to remain in his country rather than ruin a fair life for an illusion. When he had attained his destiny he would understand and value the friendship of an older woman untainted by passion. Farewell; think of her as an absent friend.Gaston de Nueil answered at once: if he could cease to love her and accept the ordinary life she advised, he would deserve his fate. He refused to follow her counsel; his oath of fidelity could only be absolved by death. “Take my life,” he wrote, “unless you fear remorse.”When the messenger returned, he told the Baron he had given the reply to Mme. la Vicomtesse, who was in her carriage and had post-horses. Gaston inferred she was going away and at once prepared to follow her. She had indeed left for Geneva, unaware he pursued her. Convinced that if she cared for him she would choose Switzerland for its freedom from judgment, he followed.The vicomtesse rented a small house by the lake. One summer evening Gaston called; Jacques the servant announced him and Mme. de Beauseant, startled, dropped her book. He advanced, expressed his joy at following her, and they were reconciled.They lived for three years in the villa by Lake Geneva, alone and without visitors, enjoying a happiness that seemed like a dream. Their home was simple but suited lovers: fine views of lake and mountains, quiet days cherished together. Weighty matters called M. de Nueil back to France after the deaths of his father and brother. They sold nothing then; they bought the family house, regretting they could not take the villa.Mme. de Beauseant sold her property to buy an estate near Manerville, adjoining Gaston’s lands, and followed him. They lived together; Gaston let his mother occupy Manerville, and again they enjoyed secluded happiness.For nine years their bliss continued. But Mme. de Beauseant’s husband, the Marquis, remained in good health, and that lawful union made a public marriage impossible. The situation grew precarious. Mme. la Comtesse de Nueil, Gaston’s mother, would never accept Mme. de Beauseant, and the Marquise feared the dowager might try to draw Gaston away. The idea of selling her estate and returning to Geneva occurred to her, but she could not bring herself to do so for fear of showing distrust in Gaston.Complication came when a young heiress, Mlle. de la Rodiere, moved nearby and Gaston met her at Manerville. This provoked a letter from Mme. de Beauseant revealing her inner torment.She began awkwardly—absurd to write when they were together, yet unable to speak face to face—then admitted anguish. He had erased her past pain; she did not know love until him. In nine years she had never felt jealousy and had known infinite happiness, but now she feared he might feel burdened by their relation. He was thirty, she forty; the age gap preyed on her. He might think of his future: a marriage bringing wealth and children. The thought that he might owe his love to pity was painful. She sensed his hesitation and had wept privately; too proud to show him her tears, she had not reproached him. She believed she should not bind his future to her fading life. Yet if she were wrong, if he still loved her, she begged him to come after reading the letter—she would love him more than ever. She asked him to speak openly and not deceive her; if he wished freedom, she would not let him see her suffering. She loved him enough to put his happiness above her own.When Mme. de Beauseant learned her own letter had come into M. de Nueil’s hands, she was overwhelmed; her thoughts grew numb. Reading her words, M. de Nueil found himself in the painful crisis that confronts many young men.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By this time he had nearly yielded to his mother’s pleadings and the attraction of Mlle. de la Rodiere, a plain young woman with a forty-thousand-livre dowry. Mme. de Nueil, with maternal concern, urged him toward this respectable match, stressing the honor of being chosen by a woman who had refused many distinguished suitors. She reminded him to consider his future: he might one day secure eighty thousand livres from land and thus an easier life. If Mme. de Beauseant truly loved him, she should encourage his marriage. Using every argument, his mother left him torn.Just as Gaston’s attachment to Mme. de Beauseant resisted convention, a letter from her changed everything. He resolved to end their liaison and marry. “One must live a man’s life,” he told himself, though he knew the choice would wound Mme. de Beauseant. Vanity and conscience deepened his sorrow; he felt genuine pity for her. He decided to prepare her gently for their separation, at first sacrificing Mlle. de la Rodiere to ease the transition. He planned to marry later, to honor Mme. de Beauseant’s wishes, and meant to write her a letter to quiet her suspicions. But he knew a woman of such perception might be devastated by a mere note.When Jacques delivered the letter, Mme. de Beauseant trembled, dread of impending loss closing in. If he did not come to her in person, she feared all was lost; yet a woman in love can endure much pain. As she read, an inner voice cried, “He lies!” She saw the words were not a true declaration but a calculated message. In despair she tore the letter and cast it into the fire, feeling betrayed and abandoned.M. de Nueil went to fetch Jacques, who soon returned with a note. “Madame la Marquise has left the chateau,” he said. M. de Nueil opened the note and found it was his own letter from nine years earlier, in which he had sworn eternal fidelity to Mme. de Beauseant; at the bottom Claire de Bourgogne had written, “Monsieur, you are free.” Stunned, he went to his mother and, within three weeks, married Mlle. Stephanie de la Rodiere.If the tale ended there it would seem trivial. But the notoriety of this true tragedy resonates with those who have known love’s profound joys and losses. After their parting Mme. de Beauseant never left Valleroy. She lived in seclusion, rarely seen except by servants, and attended mass daily.M. de Nueil, soon after his marriage, fell into a state of emotional indifference—interpreted variously as happiness or unhappiness. His mother insisted he was content. Mme. Gaston de Nueil, like many young brides, was sweet but shallow. A month after their wedding she expected a child, fitting social expectations. Two months later, however, M. de Nueil grew increasingly pensive.After seven months of this lukewarm contentment a seemingly trivial incident hinted at deeper turmoil. One day after hunting, M. de Nueil crossed Mme. de Beauseant’s park and asked Jacques if she still enjoyed game. Jacques confirmed, and M. de Nueil paid him to set aside whatever he shot for her. Jacques saw no harm in this, and for days M. de Nueil returned with an empty bag while Jacques discreetly delivered the game to the Marquise.Eventually M. de Nueil dared to write Mme. de Beauseant, but his letter was returned unopened. One evening, as his wife played the piano, he suddenly rushed to the Marquise’s room. There he found her in despair, her posture emptied of hope. When she saw him, she threatened to throw herself from the window if he approached. Terrified, M. de Nueil fled.Back home he wrote another letter, insisting it was a matter of life and death, and sent it with his servant. An hour later the servant returned with the unopened note. In despair M. de Nueil retired to another room and shot himself.The tragic end, though at odds with young France’s conventions, was perhaps inevitable. Those who have experienced a profound soul-connection will understand Gaston de Nueil’s suicide. Love requires time and nurturing; when a man abandons deep love for a loveless marriage, he faces either despair or a cold, material life.Mme. de Beauseant likely did not foresee that her friend’s anguish might lead to such an extreme act. She may have believed herself the only sufferer. Still, she rightly refused to accept humiliation; a wife may compromise for social reasons, but a mistress cannot submit to such degradation, for the purity of her passion demands respect.ANGOULEME, September 1832.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Marquis and Comte de: Father Goriot, An Episode under the Terror- Beauseant, Marquise de: Letters of Two Brides- Beauseant, Vicomte de: Father Goriot- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot, Albert Savarus- Champignelles, De: The Seamy Side of History- Jacques (M. de Beauseant’s butler): Father Goriot- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman, Albert Savarus############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##### #################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #################### #################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################La Grenadiere is a quaint house on the right bank of the Loire, about a mile downstream from the bridge of Tours. The broad river, dotted with green islands, flows between cliffs crowned with white-stone country houses set among gardens and vineyards. With a favorable southern exposure, the region yields some of the finest fruit, thanks to generations who terraced the cliffs to grow warm-climate produce outdoors.A church spire marks the nearby village of Saint-Cyr; further on the Choisille joins the Loire through a fertile valley. La Grenadiere stands halfway up the hillside, roughly a hundred paces from the church. The house, two to three hundred years old, is typical of picturesque Touraine. A fissure in the rock provides steps down to the “dike,” the embankment that keeps the Loire in its bed and serves as the causeway from Paris to Nantes. At the top of the steps a gate opens onto a narrow footpath between terraces with retaining walls; trees partly conceal the path, and the river view widens as one ascends.At the path’s end a crumbling Gothic archway leads into a small garden of flowers and rose bushes hewn from the rock on the highest terrace. A wooden summer-house, draped in jessamine and honeysuckle, stands nearby. The house sits centrally in this garden above a vine-covered flight of steps, with an arched doorway giving access to extensive cellars carved into the rock. Trellised vines and pomegranate trees—whose fruit gives the property its name—surround the house. The front has two large windows flanking a rustic door and three dormer windows in a steep slate roof; the walls are yellowwashed with green-painted doors and shutters.Inside, a crooked staircase rises to a lobby. To the right is a large dining room with white tile floors; to the left, a sitting room of similar size with saffron-coloured walls and exposed walnut rafters. The first floor holds two whitewashed bedrooms with simple stone chimneypieces. Most doors and windows face south, except one that opens toward the vineyard. A small timber-framed kitchen, a later addition, contains a rustic pump and well, suggesting La Grenadiere was originally a vintner’s retreat for townsfolk of Tours who came for picnics or the grape harvest.The property includes two acres of vineyard, with a steep slope at the back. A vinedresser’s cottage leans against the western gable; stone walls and espaliers enclose and maximize the land. Neglected corners fill with fig trees, wildflowers, or strawberries.La Grenadiere is modest yet dignified, rich in fruit and fragrance, and embodies Touraine’s beauty. Its vines yield grapes along with figs, peaches, and melons, while Spanish broom and Italian oleanders add an exotic touch. The Loire flows below; evening breezes carry scents of the sea and distant flowers, and clouds alter the light to reveal views of the south bank, Tours, and hills dotted with châteaux and parks.Owned by a prosperous burgess of Tours, this vintage house appeals to the imagination as a peaceful retreat for lovers and dreamers. Its atmosphere is suffused with happiness; the river’s sounds invite contemplation, and the owner can sit among perennial flowers and fruit, feeling the vibrant world around him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################If an Englishman rents La Grenadiere for the summer, he pays a thousand francs for six months, excluding vineyard produce; orchard fruit doubles the rent, and the vintage doubles it again. What could La Grenadiere be worth? With its stone staircase, triple terrace, two acres of vineyard, flowering roses, and well-head, it seems priceless. Unsold since its 1690 sale for forty thousand francs, it has remained a family possession. The view embraces three valleys of Touraine and cathedral towers, making a monetary valuation feel trivial compared with the health and beauty it offers.In a bright spring during the Restoration, Mme. Willemsens arrived in Tours with her housekeeper and two children, aged thirteen and eight. She chose La Grenadiere, perhaps for its distance from town. She turned the drawing-room into a bedroom, gave the children the two rooms above, and allotted the housekeeper a closet. The dining-room served as a combined sitting and drawing room, simply furnished in plain walnut; the house’s charm lay in neatness and harmony rather than luxury.Mme. Willemsens was hard to place socially: her plain dress invited assumptions, but her manners suggested higher rank. Her reserve provoked local curiosity. Tall and slender with delicate features and a melancholy expression, she had dark eyes that sometimes revealed secret anguish and premature wrinkles. She wore a long black gown with a silk ribbon at the waist and an English-style bonnet, signaling mourning. Her health seemed fragile; she walked with her children to the bridge of Tours for fresh air and sunsets.She went into Tours only twice—once to inquire about the best teachers for her children and once to arrange their lessons. Her occasional appearances on the bridge interested townspeople, but little was known of her status. The owner of La Grenadiere told friends she signed the lease as Augusta Willemsens, Countess of Brandon, hinting at her husband’s name; this tidbit circulated within the business community but did not fully lift the mystery.To the local elite she remained enigmatic. Her natural manners and soft voice charmed those around her, yet her sadness and beauty made her seem almost untouchable. Several young men fell in love, but timidity kept them away. She burned all letters she received, signaling a desire for solitude and a life free from outside entanglements.The three tutors at La Grenadiere admired the close bond between Mme. Willemsens and her children. The boys resembled their mother and drew envy from local mothers. Louis-Gaston, the elder, had dark hair and a robust demeanor; Marie-Gaston, the younger, had almost golden hair and a delicate look. Their contrasting appearances reflected differing temperaments—Louis protective of his brother.They were well-behaved and attentive, the product of careful upbringing: early rising, prayer, and a scrupulous morning routine. Their interactions with their mother were affectionate and intuitive; playful mornings, mutual laughter, and an instinctive understanding. Louis noticed small signs of his mother’s declining strength—worries she tried to hide—and, though she reassured him, he remained vigilant. Their bond was profound, shaped by a shared life of love largely untouched by the outside world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mme. Willemsens dressed during her children's early breakfast and play to be a pleasing sight for them. She was ready to hear their lessons from ten in the morning until three in the afternoon, with a midday lunch in the summer-house where they all ate together. After the meal the children played while she rested on a sofa, enjoying the Touraine landscape.The children explored the orchard and garden, chasing lizards and studying plants and insects, and often ran to their mother with eager questions. Mme. Willemsens embroidered during their lessons, following attentively without interrupting. She concentrated on her eldest son, Louis, encouraging his ambition and asking little about Marie. When lessons ended she would walk the tutor to the gate and inquire about Louis’ progress; the tutors answered frankly about his strengths and weaknesses.Their days struck a careful balance of work and play, leaving little room for discouragement or quarrels. Her steady nurturing taught her sons moderation and submission; she often shed tears of joy as she watched them play, reflecting on their happiness and her own contentment.The hours in the garden-house were precious, full of laughter and the children’s voices. They spoke both English and French, thanks to their English nurse, and their mother guided their learning so they could understand the world. She supplied interesting books about great figures in history and used those stories to teach them about ambition and success.While the children listened to these readings, little Marie would often fall asleep on her mother’s knee. Afterwards Mme. Willemsens sometimes grew pensive and tearful. One evening Louis noticed her tears and asked why she was crying. She confessed that she feared for their future, that she might leave them alone and without resources, and she spoke with the deep love that made her sorrow so poignant.Puzzled, Louis promised he would take care of Marie. She reassured him that love kept her alive and hoped the sunny air of Touraine might help her recover. From that day, as she spoke more of her impending death, Louis became more focused on his studies and adjusted the family’s routine to her melancholy. The once vibrant household grew quieter; the old servant Annette watched with growing concern as Mme. Willemsens’ health declined.By August their life had changed markedly. Mme. Willemsens remained beautiful but increasingly frail, making an effort to preserve her appearance. The house felt somber and they rarely ventured far; Louis noticed her exhaustion and often suggested shorter walks.With autumn’s sunny days and ripe fruits, Mme. Willemsens spent almost all her time with the children, cherishing each moment. Meanwhile Louis worked late into the night, making steady progress toward the École Polytechnique entrance examination. He frequented the bridge of Tours, where a retired naval officer told him tales of adventure; Louis asked many questions and learned of the hardships of military life. Encouraged by his mother, he took long walks to build endurance, climbed trees and learned to swim, slowly changing from a boy into a young man whose sunburned face reflected a new purpose.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When October arrived, Mme. Willemsens could only rise at noon. The Loire’s warmth made the air almost like the Bay of Naples, and the faculty had recommended the area. At midday she sat in the shade with her two boys, who never left her side. With lessons over they sought a life of closeness, free from outside worries: the elder, Louis, lay beside her, basking in her affection, while Marie, the more restless child, gathered flowers. Mme. Willemsens, with tired eyes and languid movements, complained little, smiling at her vibrant children—a poignant scene made keener by autumn’s melancholy.Eventually the doctor ordered her to stay in her room, brightened daily by her favorite flowers and by the constant presence of her children. One early November day she sat at the piano for the last time, gazing at a Swiss landscape above it and at her children playing outside. Her fingers moved over the keys with passionate feeling; when the doctor arrived he insisted she stay in bed, and she was stunned.After he left she asked Louis to take her to the terrace for one last view of her homeland. Stepping outside she lifted her gaze to the sky, captivated by the clouds. Anguish crossed her face as she clutched her children’s hands, fearing their uncertain future and dreading what they might think of her life when they grew older. Then she saw Louis and Marie kneeling beside her, their love obvious. Wishing to carry their smiles with her, she returned to bed, never to rise again.Days passed in a blur with Annette and Louis keeping vigil; the atmosphere grew heavy with dread. On the fifth day the doctor banned flowers from her room, a clear sign of decline. As her condition worsened Louis refused to leave her. One quiet Sunday night he noticed her hand move the curtain; she spoke solemnly, telling him tomorrow would be her last day. She instructed him to fetch important papers from her table—birth certificates among them—and a letter she wished to send to Lord Brandon, her estranged husband.As she dictated the letter she hesitated over how to sign it, at last choosing “Louis-Gaston.” She then revealed that she had twelve thousand francs saved—their remaining funds—and urged him to keep the money close, since everything would be sealed after her death. Louis vowed to care for Marie, planning to send her to school while he apprenticed at sea, determined to return successful.Her eyes brightened at his resolve and she felt a surge of hope, but her strength soon waned and she requested a priest. Louis fetched Annette, who hurried to find one. The next morning, surrounded by loved ones, Mme. Willemsens received the sacrament, her children kneeling beside her. As her breathing slowed the room fell silent; she eventually passed away, leaving them in shock.Afterward Annette and the vinedresser’s wife prepared the body for burial according to local custom. Only a few mourners attended—far fewer than the grand send-off she might have had under different circumstances. Marie, still too young to grasp death’s finality, cried as they lowered the coffin. A simple cross marked the grave, inscribed with a plea for prayers.The children returned to La Grenadiere for a last look before leaving with Annette, who took them to her cousin’s house in Tours. Louis entrusted Annette with Marie’s birth certificate and the money for her care. The next day he took Marie to school, offered her heartfelt advice, and then parted from her.A month later Louis-Gaston, now an apprentice on a warship, watched the French coast recede. Alone on the vast ocean he felt lost but was comforted by an old sailor’s words. With newfound resolve he embraced life at sea, determined to honor his mother’s memory.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########### ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################I have always wanted to tell a simple, true story that would terrify two young lovers and drive them to seek refuge in each other’s hearts, like children clinging together at the sight of a snake. Announcing this intention may spoil the tale and make me seem vain.I played a part in this almost ordinary tragedy, so if it fails to engage you, the fault is partly mine and partly the mundane nature of reality. Life often lacks interest; the artist’s task is to select from reality those elements with poetic potential.In 1819 I was traveling from Paris to Moulins and, for want of money, took an outside seat on the coach. An Englishman, who seemed better off, joined me, and we quickly bonded over our love of fresh air and the landscape. Conversation turned to women and love, and we soon admired “the woman of a certain age,” specifically those between thirty-five and forty. We agreed a birth certificate was meaningless and that some women were younger at forty than girls at twenty. This freed a boundless exploration of love.As we described our mistresses—young, charming, devoted—my companion revealed his lady was thirty-eight; I confessed to adoring a woman of forty. Young again in our voices, we traded romantic follies: one had traveled six hundred miles for a brief meeting; the other had risked danger to meet his lady in secret. We relived joys and perils: my friend’s countess had lighted a cigar for him; mine made chocolate and wrote daily when we were apart. Our countesses were adored by their husbands, whose simplicity provided just enough danger to heighten our pleasure. But soon, our laughter was swept away by the wind.At Pouilly I observed my new friend closely. He was well-proportioned, with an expressive face, dark hair, blue eyes, and a pallor suggesting recent illness. His shapely hands, intelligence, and bearing suited a countess. He was a Vicomte with a respectable income; many a girl would have desired him.About a league out of Pouilly the coach overturned. My companion jumped to escape but miscalculated and was crushed beneath the vehicle. We carried him to a peasant’s cottage, where, in agony, he entrusted me with a sacred task—delivering the news of his death to his mistress. He feared the shock she would feel if she learned of his death from a newspaper. He asked me to retrieve a key he wore around his neck, which I found embedded in his flesh. After giving me directions to his home in La Charite-sur-Loire for his love letters, he grew speechless and died, leaving me with the burden of his last wish.The coachman said it was partly my friend’s fault, but I was consumed by the task ahead. At La Charite I fulfilled his wishes, but his mother was away, leaving me to comfort an old servant who fainted at the news. I took the sealed letters, burdened by the sorrow of a woman who had lost her last love.The Countess’s chateau lay eight leagues beyond Moulins, and I had little money for the journey. Nevertheless, youthful enthusiasm propelled me to walk as fast as possible, eager to deliver the bad news before anyone else. As I neared the Chateau de Montpersan, unease about my role as the bearer of such tragic news increased.When I reached the chateau, the open doors unsettled me. I entered and encountered barking dogs and a servant-maid who directed me to the park where Madame was. I felt lost and unsure how to find her. A little girl overheard my inquiry and, with a playful air, led me through the winding paths of the park, calling for her mother.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I paused behind the last shrub on the avenue, adjusting my shabby clothes so the lady would not mistake me for a local rate collector. Thinking back now amuses me.Just then I spotted Juliette and her husband among the flowers; the lady's pace quickened at her child's ambiguous remark. Surprised by my presence, she regarded me coolly, disappointment visible. I fumbled for the elegant phrases I had prepared.That hesitation let her husband approach. I awkwardly asked if they were indeed M. le Comte and Mme. la Comtesse de Montpersan, using the questions to size them up. The husband was a provincial nobleman: large shoes, worn coat, self-importance and bad manners, led by his wife while fancying himself master.The Countess was the opposite: petite, graceful, a delicate figure that inspired desire. Dressed in white muslin with rose-colored accents, she seemed youthful and lively, recalling types familiar to any young man who has read Louvet's romance.Choosing diplomacy, I took the Count aside with an air of mystery and requested a private word. He obliged, confident his wife could learn his secrets at will, and left her to wander off.I told the Count of my traveling companion's death, noting his genuine affection. He expressed concern for his wife and insisted on caution in delivering the news. I explained I could not approach the Countess without first informing him, emphasizing the sacredness of the message entrusted to me. Flattered, he allowed me to proceed.As we neared the house, Juliette glanced at us with a mix of curiosity and disdain, likely due to my appearance and the unexpected guest. I recalled her beauty in the sunlit alley and sighed."Alas, madame, I have made a difficult journey solely for you," I began. Her face paled."Is he ill?" she asked, voice dropping."Yes, but control yourself. He entrusted me with secrets concerning you; I am a discreet messenger.""What is wrong with him?" she pressed."What if he no longer loves you?" I replied, and she reacted immediately."That is impossible!" she exclaimed, a faint smile flickering. Panic followed. "Is he alive?"Her question hit me; I only nodded. Distress overwhelmed her."Give me an answer!" she cried."Yes, madame.""Is it true? Tell me the truth! Any pain would be better than this suspense."I could only respond with tears; she leaned against a tree, crying out in despair."Your husband is coming!" I warned."Do I have a husband?" she fled from sight.The Count called me to dinner; I followed him into a dining room set with a lavish meal, though the Countess was notably absent. The canon of Saint-Denis inquired after her, but the Count shrugged and hastily served dinner.After a while, a maid reported the Countess missing. Alarmed, I rushed out with the canon and the maid to search the garden and park. As we called, I recounted the tragic accident; the maid's concern for her mistress was evident.At last we heard a muffled cry from a granary. Inside we found Juliette buried in hay, sobbing. The maid and I helped her to her room; I instructed that she not be disturbed, claiming she had a headache.Returning to the dining room, I was surprised the Count still ate, seemingly unfazed. His indifference was explained: a serious medical condition had left him ravenous, overshadowing his concern for Juliette.The evening was somber. I was exhausted; the canon pondered the cause of Juliette's tears while the Count, preoccupied with his meal, accepted the maid's vague explanation. We retired early.Passing the Countess's room, I asked the servant for news. Juliette called me in, tried to speak but could not. I left, feeling the weight of her agitation.Later that night I was awakened by the sound of curtain rings. The Countess sat at the foot of my bed, pale and drawn."Is it true?" she asked, voice trembling. "I don't know how I can live after this blow, but I want to know everything."She had lost her youthful glow; her eyes were red and swollen. I briefly recounted the accident, avoiding distressing details. She listened intently, leaning forward to catch every word.When I related the dying man's fears and the message he had entrusted to me, her tears dried into a deep pallor. I handed her letters from him; she took them mechanically, lamenting that she had burned all his other letters and now had nothing."I cut this lock of his hair," she said, revealing a precious token. I gave it to her; her tears fell on my hands, a moment of profound gratitude and connection."Ah! You love! May you always be happy," she said, clutching the lock and fleeing with her treasure.The next morning felt surreal. I searched under my pillow for the letters but found nothing. I spent the day with Juliette, witnessing her noble spirit and selflessness.That evening the Comte de Montpersan accompanied me to Moulins and asked if I could deliver a sum of money to a contact in Paris. I agreed, taking a rouleau of twenty-five louis d'or, which covered my journey back. On arrival I appreciated the delicate way Juliette had arranged the loan, revealing the depth of her love and thoughtfulness. What a joy to share this adventure with a woman who cherished me, saying, "Oh, my dear, not you! You must not die!"############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo M. le Baron Barchou de Penhoen.Among all the pupils of the Oratorian school at Vendome, I believe we are the only two who afterward met in mid-career in a life of letters. When we met, you were engaged on your noble works on German philosophy, and I on this study. So neither of us has missed his vocation; and you, when you see your name here, will feel as much pleasure as I do in inscribing my work to you. Your old schoolfellow, 1840 De Balzac.GOBSECKIt was one o’clock in the morning in the winter of 1829–30, and in the Vicomtesse de Grandlieu’s salon two guests not of her family remained. A young man took his leave; as his carriage drove off, the Vicomtesse turned to her daughter Camille, who stood by the chimney-piece ostensibly looking at a fire-screen while listening to the courtyard, which stirred maternal anxiety.“Camille,” the Vicomtesse said, “if you continue to treat young Comte de Restaud as you have this evening, I shall have to prevent his visiting. Trust my love and let me guide you. At seventeen one cannot judge the past or the future or social standings. M. de Restaud has a mother, Mlle. Goriot, who squandered her father’s fortune and is of no birth. Though he behaves admirably toward her and his siblings, any family would hesitate to entrust their daughter’s future to him while she lives.”“I overheard part of your conversation with Mlle. de Grandlieu,” interposed Derville, a family friend. “I have won, M. le Comte,” he added to the departing man. “I shall assist your niece.”“See what it is to have an attorney’s ears!” exclaimed the Vicomtesse. “How could you know what I was saying to Camille?”“I knew it from your looks,” Derville replied, settling by the fire.Camille’s uncle approached, and the Vicomtesse sat on a hearth-stool between them.“It's time for a story that may change your view of Ernest de Restaud’s prospects,” Derville said.“A story?” Camille cried. “Please begin!”Derville’s look at the Vicomtesse made clear the tale was meant for her. The Vicomtesse de Grandlieu was a leading figure in the Faubourg Saint-Germain by birth and fortune; a Paris attorney’s intimacy with her would seem odd until one knew the facts.After returning to France with the Royal family, the Vicomtesse had lived on a pension from Louis XVIII, a state she found intolerable. The Hotel de Grandlieu had been sold during the Revolution. Derville discovered defects in the title, pursued legal action, and reclaimed it. He also secured judgments restoring significant property, raising her income to about sixty thousand francs plus indemnities. Derville, a man of integrity and modesty, became a close family confidant.His management of the Vicomtesse’s affairs gained him esteem in the Faubourg Saint-Germain and brought many aristocratic clients. Yet he did not exploit this popularity for personal advancement. The Vicomtesse urged him to accept a magistracy; he declined, preferring to keep his practice and social ties. His steadfast service revealed his talents, and since Ernest de Restaud’s arrival—especially as Camille showed interest—Derville had been visiting the Hotel de Grandlieu more often.At a recent ball Derville had taunted Camille: “It’s a pity that young man doesn’t have two or three million francs, isn’t it?”“Is it a pity? I don’t think so,” she answered. “M. de Restaud is capable and well educated. He will have as much money as he wishes when he comes into power.”“But what if he were already rich?” Derville persisted.“Rich already?” Camille blushed. “All the girls would be vying for him,” she said, watching the dancers.“Then Mlle. de Grandlieu might not be the one he favors,” Derville teased. “That blush says it all! You like him, don’t you? Speak up.”Camille suddenly rose to leave.“She loves him,” Derville thought.Since that evening Camille had been unusually attentive to Derville, who approved of her feelings for Ernest. Though she felt deep gratitude for his services to her family, she had previously shown respect rather than warmth, keeping a polite distance. Gratitude often weighs heavily on the next generation, which may seek to escape it.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“This adventure,” Derville began, “reminds me of the only romantic event in my life. You may find it odd for an attorney to speak of romance, but when I was twenty-five I knew some strange people. I must tell you about one you couldn’t possibly know—a usurer.“Picture a sallow, wan face, hair iron-gray and sleek, features impassive like Talleyrand’s, little yellow eyes beneath a shabby cap, thin lips, a gimlet nose, a low suave voice, and perfect temper. He seemed either prematurely aged or unnaturally preserved. His room matched him: clean, threadbare, like an elderly spinster’s chamber. In winter the fire smoldered without a flame. He moved with pendulum regularity, economizing every movement and emotion, saving himself for inward pleasures.“His life ran soundlessly, like sand through an hourglass. His victims sometimes erupted in rage and then fell silent. Toward evening, when business pleased him, his metals became a human heart; he would rub his hands and let his inward glee escape through the wrinkles of his face, a soundless laugh. Even in joy his words were monosyllables and his expression noncommittal.“He was my neighbor in the Rue de Gres while I lived as a second clerk finishing my studies. The house was damp, dark, and convent-like, so gloomy that gaiety faded before reaching his door. We both lived sparingly. He and his house resembled one another; he communicated only with me after four years of acquaintance, borrowing a light or a book, and letting me visit in the evenings. Whether he had relatives or friends, whether rich or poor, no one could say. I never saw money in his room; his capital was likely in the Bank. He collected debts himself, darting across Paris on thin legs. Once gold fell from his pocket and another lodger returned it; he feigned surprise, saying if he were rich he wouldn’t live as he did. He made coffee on a cast-iron chafing-dish, bought dinner from a cookshop, and had our porter’s wife tidy his room. A whimsical chance named him Gobseck. Later I learned he was about seventy-six then—Jean-Esther Van Gobseck, born around 1740 near Antwerp to a Dutch father and Jewish mother. He once commented, without much feeling, that La belle Hollandaise, a murdered relative, was his grandniece. He shrugged at family ties—“the women never marry in our family”—and the idea of heirs repelled him; the thought of his wealth passing on was inconceivable.“He had been ten when sent as a cabin boy to the Dutch Straits, spending twenty years at sea. The inscrutable lines on his forehead hid stories of horrible adventures, panic, luck, and lost fortunes. He had known notable men and pirates, searched for treasure in Buenos Ayres, and had lived through the American War of Independence. Yet when he spoke of those lands he seemed to regret it, as if some things were indiscreet. If humanity and sociability are religion, Gobseck was an infidel; his nature remained impenetrable. I never learned his religious views—he seemed indifferent.“One evening I visited Daddy Gobseck. He sat motionless in his armchair, staring at the mantel, a lamp burning while his face looked paler. He barely spoke but motioned me to a chair. I wondered what such a being thought of God, feeling, women, happiness. I pitied him, like a diseased creature—he had millions and the world in idea, having explored, ransacked, weighed, appraised, and exploited it.“‘Good day, Daddy Gobseck,’ I said.“He turned, contracting his bushy brows in a look as communicative as a Southern smile. He mocked my gloom, reminding me of a bookseller’s bankruptcy that I had profited from. When I asked if he had bills to protest, he replied he was amusing himself. I asked if that amused him, and he retorted that not all poets print verses—poetry can exist in the head. He then spoke of life in his own terms. He bolted the door and drew a curtain, then began a monologue on his worldview.“His creed was blunt: happiness lay in applying faculties to realities; belief and illusions were for the young. Moral rules change with latitude; conventions adapt to climate; the only constant is self-interest. After a life of traveling, he claimed, the only tangible reality worth caring for is GOLD—representing all human power. Gold realizes dreams and contains everything in embryo. Pleasure, power, vanity—these find their satisfaction in gold. Science, art, passion, calm—these might be higher ends, but for him insight into human motives replaced scientific curiosity. He wanted to show me his daily pleasures.“He told of that morning: he had two amounts to collect—the rest he had let go—saving the small expense of collection. The first bill, a thousand francs, came from a dandy who presented a note bearing the signature of one of Paris’s prettiest women, a Countess. Why had a Countess signed a bill? Innocence, imprudence, love, charity—he wanted to discover which. The second bill, signed “Fanny Malvaut,” came from a linen-draper near bankruptcy; no one with credit comes to him—coming to Gobseck meant desperation. The Countess lived in Rue du Helder, Fanny in Rue Montmartre. Gobseck relished his hypotheses about what each woman’s bill meant.“At the Countess’s: a maid said she had returned at three in the morning from a ball and could see him at noon. When the Countess appeared, she was magnificent yet disordered—an Indian shawl over bare shoulders, a loose ruffled gown, dark curls escaping a bright handkerchief, a bed and room strewn with signs of revelry: crushed pillows, satin slippers, open drawers, scattered jewels, a vague perfume. Beneath the luxury he perceived Misery: tired, coarsened beauty, circles around her eyes—a life of extravagance and remorse. He was struck; the scene awakened desire and memory in him.“She asked him to wait. He answered that he could not protest until the next day. She pleaded, and as we spoke a man—her husband, the Count—entered. The Countess trembled; Gobseck, unmoved, revealed his inexorability by half-drawing the bill. She came and placed a diamond worth twelve hundred francs in his hands; he accepted and left. Outside, he noted flunkeys polishing boots and sumptuous carriages—this was what brought such people to him: they preserved appearances while their splendor depended on exploiting others.“The young man who had brought the bill returned; Gobseck gave him two hundred francs to return to the Countess and told him he would keep the pledge for a week. The man smiled with cynical ease; Gobseck read the Countess’s future—ruin by a gay gambler, a portrait of Parisian salons wrecked by reckless appetites.“He then visited Mlle. Fanny in Rue Montmartre, climbing steep stairs to a neat two-room apartment on the fifth floor. She was a young, quietly dressed seamstress with a fresh, delicate face, clear blue eyes, hair brushed in a modest style, surrounded by shaped linen. A crucifix and a sprig of box on her painted bedstead suggested faith and humble roots. She said the money had been left with the porter’s wife; she seldom left the room but worked hard and kept herself clean after long nights. Gobseck saw in her a life of honest toil, simplicity, and goodness—an innocent with faith. For a moment his generosity stirred; he considered offering lenient terms, even twelve percent instead of more. But suspicion tempered his impulse—perhaps she had kin who would exploit her signature. He restrained himself, aware that benevolence can ruin those it aims to help.“Gobseck contrasted Fanny’s pure, obscure life with the Countess’s extravagance. He saw in his power a terrible insight into human hearts: every drama, every secret, every degradation and grandeur passed before him. He could read people as God reads them; the holder of purse-strings can bind or loose consciences and control ministers through their dependents, from clerks to mistresses. Power meant he could possess beauty and pleasure; gold was the spiritual basis of society. He described ten of them in Paris—silent, unknown kings—arbiters of destinies, meeting at the Cafe Themis, keeping a Black Book of important bills and secrets. They were casuists of the Bourse, analyzing the actions of the great, confident in their forecasts. One oversaw the judicial world, another the financial, another administrative affairs; Gobseck watched eldest sons, artists, society, and gamblers—the sensational side of Paris. Thwarted passion and mortified vanity were great babblers, feeding their knowledge. Thus they loved power and money for themselves.“‘Here,’ he said, indicating his bare room, ‘the proudest man kneels with clasped hands. No merchant is so haughty elsewhere; no woman so vain; no soldier so bold—here they entreat me, with tears of rage or anguish. Famous artists, men of letters—here, inheritances and concerns of Paris are weighed. Behind this blank, impassive mask are delights of power.’“I went back to my room stupefied. The little wizened man had become great in my eyes: the personification of gold’s power. I shrank from life. I asked myself if everything must be resolved into gold.“That night I could not sleep, visions of gold surrounding me and the Countess’s beauty filling my thoughts. I confess, to my shame, that the Countess eclipsed the image of the quiet, innocent Fanny; yet in the morning Fanny’s sweet face rose before me, and I thought of nothing else.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Will you take a glass of eau sucrée?” asked the Vicomtesse, interrupting Derville.“I should be glad of it.”“But I see nothing here that concerns us,” said Mme. de Grandlieu, ringing the bell.“Sardanapalus!” cried Derville, invoking his favorite phrase. “Mademoiselle Camille’s happiness once depended on Daddy Gobseck; but with his death at ninety, M. de Restaud will soon inherit a fortune. This needs explanation. As for poor Fanny Malvaut, you know her; she is my wife.”“Poor fellow, he would admit that, with his usual frankness, in front of a crowd!” said the Vicomtesse.“I would proclaim it to the universe,” said the attorney.“Go on, drink your glass, my poor Derville. You will always be the happiest and best of men.”“I left you in the Rue du Helder,” remarked the uncle, waking from a gentle doze. “You had gone to see a Countess; what became of her?”“A few days after my conversation with the old Dutchman,” Derville continued, “I submitted my thesis, became a licentiate in law, and then an advocate. The old miser’s opinion of me improved. He consulted me (for free) on delicate matters which would have seemed risky to any ordinary practitioner. This man, who seemed immune to influence, listened to my advice with respect, and it often turned out well.“Eventually, I became head-clerk in the office where I had worked for three years and then moved into my employer’s house. I had board and lodging and a salary of one hundred and fifty francs per month. It was a great day for me!“When I went to bid the usurer goodbye, he showed no emotion, neither cordial nor regretful, and did not ask me to visit him again, only giving me a glance that seemed to reveal a second sight.“A week later, my old neighbor came to see me with a thorny business matter, an expropriation, and continued to seek my advice as if I were still his clerk.“My principal was a man of pleasure and expensive tastes; by the end of the second year (1818–1819), he found himself in difficulties and had to sell his practice. Back then, a professional connection didn’t fetch the exorbitant prices of today, and my principal asked for one hundred and fifty thousand francs. An active, competent man might hope to pay off the capital in ten years, living respectably in the meantime—if he could command confidence. But as the seventh child of a small tradesman from Noyon, I had no money and no connections except Daddy Gobseck. An ambitious idea and a glimmer of hope encouraged me. I went to Gobseck, my heart pounding as I knocked on his door in the gloomy house, recalling his warnings about the anguish awaiting those who crossed his threshold. Now, I was about to beg like so many others.“‘Well, no, not that,’ I told myself; ‘an honest man must keep his self-respect. Success is not worth cringing for; let’s show him a front as determined as his own.’“Daddy Gobseck had taken my room since I left, so as to have no neighbor; he had even added a grated window to his door and didn’t open until he had looked at me.“‘So your principal is selling his practice?’ he said.“How did you know that?” I asked, surprised.“‘Nothing else would have brought you here,’ he replied dryly.“‘Listen to me, M. Gobseck,’ I began, trying to maintain my composure. ‘I am a penniless clerk, and only you can understand my future. Let’s leave feelings aside. My principal’s practice is worth about twenty thousand francs per annum; in my hands, I believe it could bring in forty thousand. He is willing to sell it for one hundred and fifty thousand francs. If you would lend me the purchase money, I could pay it off in ten years.’“‘Come, that is plain speaking,’ said Daddy Gobseck, shaking my hand. ‘Nobody has stated their motives more clearly. Guarantees?’ he asked, scanning me. ‘None to give,’ he added after a pause. ‘How old are you?’“‘Twenty-five in ten days,’ I replied.“‘Precisely. Well?’“‘It is possible.’“‘We must be quick about it, or I shall have someone buying over my head.’“‘Bring your birth certificate tomorrow morning, and we will talk. I will think it over.’“Next morning, at eight o’clock, I stood in the old man’s room. He took the document, put on his spectacles, coughed, and read it through. Then he said, ‘We will try to arrange this business.’“I trembled.“‘I make fifty percent on my capital,’ he continued, ‘sometimes I make a hundred, two hundred, five hundred percent.’“I turned pale.“‘But as we are acquaintances, I shall be satisfied to take twelve and a half percent per—well, yes, from you I would be content to take thirteen percent per annum. Will that suit you?’“‘Yes,’ I answered.“‘But if it is too much, stand up for yourself, Grotius!’ (a name he jokingly gave me). ‘When I ask for thirteen percent, it is all in the way of business; look into it, see if you can pay it; I don’t like a man to agree too easily. Is it too much?’“‘No,’ I said, ‘I will make up for it by working a little harder.’“‘Gad! your clients will pay for it!’ he said, looking at me wickedly.“‘No, by all the devils in hell!’ I cried, ‘it shall be I who pays. I would sooner cut my hand off than flay people.’“‘Good-night,’ said Daddy Gobseck.“‘Fees are all according to scale,’ I added.“‘Not for compromises and settlements out of court, and cases where litigants come to terms,’ he replied. ‘You can send in a bill for thousands of francs, six thousand even at a swoop, depending on the case’s importance. A man must learn to look out for business of this kind. I will recommend you as a competent attorney. I will send you so much work that your colleagues will be fit to burst with envy. Werbrust, Palma, and Gigonnet, my cronies, shall hand over their expropriations to you; they have plenty of them! So you will have two practices—the one you are buying and the other I will build up for you. You ought almost to pay me fifteen percent on my loan.’“‘So be it, but no more,’ I said firmly.“Daddy Gobseck’s face relaxed; he looked pleased.“‘I shall pay the money over to your principal myself, to establish a lien on the purchase and caution-money.’“‘Oh, anything you like in the way of guarantees.’“‘And you will give me bills for the amount made payable to a third party (name left blank), fifteen bills of ten thousand francs each.’“‘Well, so long as it is acknowledged in writing that this is a double—’“‘No!’ Gobseck interrupted. ‘Why should I trust you any more than you trust me?’“I kept silent.“‘Furthermore,’ he continued, ‘you will give me your advice without charging fees as long as I live, will you not?’“‘So be it; as long as there is no outlay.’“‘Precisely,’ he said. ‘Ah, by the by, you will allow me to visit you?’ (The old man found it hard to assume an air of good humor.)“‘I shall always be glad.’“‘Ah! yes, but it would be difficult to arrange in the morning. You will have your affairs to attend to, and I have mine.’“‘Then come in the evening.’“‘Oh, no!’ he replied briskly, ‘you ought to go into society and see your clients, and I have my friends at my café.’“‘His friends!’ I thought. ‘Very well,’ I said, ‘why not come at dinner-time?’“‘That is the time,’ said Gobseck, ‘after “Change,” at five o’clock. Good, you will see me Wednesdays and Saturdays. We will talk over business like friends. Aha! I am gay sometimes. Just give me the wing of a partridge and a glass of champagne, and we will chat. I know many things that can be told now at this distance of time; I will teach you to know men, and what is more—women!’“‘Oh! a partridge and a glass of champagne if you like.’“‘Don’t do anything foolish, or I shall lose my faith in you. And don’t set up housekeeping grandly. Just one old general servant. I will come and see that you keep your health. I have capital invested in your head, he! he! so I must look after you. There, come round in the evening and bring your principal with you!’“‘Would you mind telling me, if there is no harm in asking, what was the good of my birth certificate in this business?’ I asked as we stood on the doorstep.“Jean-Esther Van Gobseck shrugged his shoulders, smiled maliciously, and said, ‘What blockheads youngsters are! Learn, master attorney (for learn you must if you don’t want to be taken in), that integrity and brains in a man under thirty are commodities that can be mortgaged. After that age, there is no counting on a man.’“And with that, he shut the door.“Three months later, I was an attorney. Soon after, madame, I had the good fortune to undertake the suit for the recovery of your estates. I won, and my name became known. Despite the exorbitant interest, I paid off Gobseck in less than five years. I married Fanny Malvaut, whom I loved deeply. Our parallel lives, our hard work and luck, strengthened our bond. One of her uncles, a wealthy farmer, died and left her seventy thousand francs, which helped clear the loan. Since then, my life has been nothing but happiness and prosperity. Nothing is more utterly uninteresting than a happy man, so let’s return to the other characters.“About a year after purchasing the practice, I was dragged into a bachelor breakfast-party given by one of our number who had lost a bet to a young man greatly in vogue in fashionable circles. M. de Trailles, the epitome of dandyism, enjoyed a prodigious reputation.”“But he is still enjoying it,” interjected the Comte de Born. “No one wears clothes with a finer air, nor drives a tandem with better grace. Maxime can gamble, eat, and drink more gracefully than anyone. He is a judge of horses, hats, and pictures. All the women lose their heads over him. He spends about a hundred thousand francs a year, and no one can discover that he has an acre of land or a single dividend warrant. The typical knight-errant of our salons, our boudoirs, our boulevards—Maxime de Trailles is a singular being, fit for anything, and good for nothing, capable of both noble and base acts, more often bespattered with mire than blood, knowing more anxiety than remorse, more concerned with his digestion than any mental process, shamming passion, feeling nothing. Maxime de Trailles is a brilliant link between the hulks and the best society; he belongs to the eminently intelligent class from which a Mirabeau, or a Pitt, or a Richelieu springs at times, though it more often produces Counts of Horn, Fouquier-Tinvilles, and Coignards.”“Well,” Derville continued, “I had heard much about this individual from poor old Goriot, a client of mine, and I had already taken pains to avoid the dangerous honor of his acquaintance, as I sometimes encountered him in society. Still, my friend was so insistent about this breakfast-party that I could hardly refuse without earning a reputation for squeamishness. Madame, you could hardly imagine what a bachelor’s breakfast-party is like. It means superb display and a studied refinement seldom seen; the luxury of a miser when vanity leads him to be sumptuous for a day.“You are surprised as you enter the room at the neatness of the table, dazzling with silver, crystal, and damask linen. Life is in full bloom; the young men are graceful, smiling, and speaking in low, demure voices like brides; everything about them looks girlish. Two hours later, you might mistake the room for a battlefield after a fight. Broken glasses, crumpled serviettes lie strewn among the nauseous remnants of food. There is an uproar of jesting toasts, a fire of witticisms and bad jokes; faces are flushed, eyes inflamed and expressionless, unintentional confidences reveal the truth. Bottles are smashed, songs are sung amidst a diabolical racket; men call each other out, hang on each other’s necks, or fall to fisticuffs; the room is filled with a horrid scent made up of a hundred odors, and noise enough for a hundred voices. No one knows what he is eating, drinking, or saying. Some are depressed, others babble, one turns monomaniac, repeating the same word like a bell; another tries to keep the tumult in check; the steadiest proposes an orgy. If a sober person were to enter, he would think he had interrupted a Bacchanalian rite.“It was in the midst of such chaos that M. de Trailles tried to ingratiate himself with me. My head was clear, and I was on guard. He pretended to be drunk, but was perfectly cool and knew what he was about. Somehow, by the end of the evening, I had promised to introduce him to our Papa Gobseck. The words ‘honor,’ ‘virtue,’ ‘countess,’ ‘honest woman,’ and ‘ill-luck’ were mingled in his discourse with a magical potency, thanks to his golden tongue.“When I awoke the next morning, trying to recollect the previous day, I struggled to piece together my impressions. Eventually, it seemed that the daughter of one of my clients was in danger of losing her reputation and her husband’s love unless she could gather fifty thousand francs that morning. There had been gaming debts and other accounts. My charming companion had impressed upon me that she was rich enough to cover these reverses with a few years of economy. Only then did I begin to grasp the urgency of his request. I confess, to my shame, that I had no doubt it was crucial for Daddy Gobseck to reconcile with this dandy. I was dressing when the young gentleman appeared.“‘M. le Comte,’ I said after the usual greetings, ‘I fail to see why you need me to introduce you to Van Gobseck, the most civil and smooth-spoken of capitalists. Money will be forthcoming if he has any, or rather, if you can provide adequate security.’“‘Monsieur,’ he replied, ‘I do not wish to force you to do me a service, even though you have given your word.’“‘Sardanapalus!’ I thought, ‘am I going to let him think I won’t keep my word?’“‘I told you yesterday,’ he said, ‘that I had fallen out with Daddy Gobseck most inopportunely; and as there is scarcely another man in Paris who can provide a hundred thousand francs at the end of the month, I begged you to make my peace with him. But let us say no more about it—’“M. de Trailles looked at me with a civil insult, making as if to leave.“‘I am ready to go with you,’ I said.“When we reached the Rue de Gres, my dandy looked around with a circumspection and unease that puzzled me. His face changed color, and by the time we reached Gobseck’s door, sweat stood in drops on his forehead. Just as we were getting out of the cabriolet, a hackney cab turned into the street. His hawk eye detected a woman inside. His face lit up with a gleam of almost savage joy; he called to a little boy passing by and gave him his horse to hold. Then we approached the old bill discounter.“‘M. Gobseck,’ I said, ‘I have brought one of my closest friends to see you (whom I trust as I would trust the Devil,’ I added for the old man’s private ear). ‘To oblige me, you will do your best for him (at the ordinary rate), and pull him out of his difficulty (if it suits your convenience).’“M. de Trailles made his bow to Gobseck, took a seat, and listened with a courtier-like attitude; its charming humility would have touched your heart. But Gobseck sat impassively by the fireside, resembling the statue of Voltaire under the peristyle of the Théâtre-Français. He had partly risen as if to bow, and the skull cap covering his head, along with the narrow strip of sallow forehead, completed his likeness to the man of marble.“‘I have no money to spare except for my own clients,’ he said.“‘So you are cross because I may have tried in other quarters to ruin myself?’ laughed the Count.“‘Ruin yourself!’ Gobseck repeated ironically.“‘Were you about to say that it is impossible to ruin a man who has nothing?’ the dandy asked. ‘I defy you to find a better stock in Paris!’ he cried, turning on his heels.“This half-earnest buffoonery produced no effect on Gobseck.“‘Am I not on intimate terms with the Ronquerolles, the Marsays, the Franchessinis, the two Vandenesses, the Ajuda-Pintos—all the most fashionable young men in Paris? A prince and an ambassador (you know them both) are my partners at play. I draw my revenues from London and Carlsbad and Baden and Bath. Is not this the most brilliant of all industries!’“‘True.’“‘You make a sponge of me, begad! You encourage me to swell myself in society, so you can squeeze me when I am hard up; but you yourselves are sponges, just as I am, and death will squeeze you someday.’“‘That is possible.’“‘If there were no spendthrifts, what would become of you? The pair of us are like soul and body.’“‘Precisely so.’“‘Come, now, give us your hand, Grandaddy Gobseck, and be magnanimous if this is “true” and “possible” and “precisely so.”’“‘You come to me,’ the usurer answered coldly, ‘because Girard, Palma, Werbrust, and Gigonnet are full up of your paper; they are offering it at a loss of fifty percent; and as they likely only gave you half the figure on the face of the bills, they are not worth twenty-five percent of their supposed value. I am your most obedient! Can I in common decency lend a stiver to a man who owes thirty thousand francs and has not one farthing?’ Gobseck continued. ‘The day before yesterday, you lost ten thousand francs at a ball at the Baron de Nucingen’s.’“‘Sir,’ said the Count, with rare impudence, ‘my affairs are no concern of yours,’ and he scrutinized the old man. ‘A man has no debts until payment is due.’“‘True.’“‘My bills will be duly met.’“‘That is possible.’“‘And at this moment, the question between you and me is simply whether the security I am going to offer is sufficient for the sum I have come to borrow.’“‘Precisely.’“A cab stopped at the door, and the sound of wheels filled the room.“‘I will bring something directly which perhaps will satisfy you,’ cried the young man, and he left the room.“‘Oh! my son,’ exclaimed Gobseck, rising and stretching out his arms to me, ‘if he has good security, you have saved my life. It would be the death of me. Werbrust and Gigonnet thought they were going to trick me; and now, thanks to you, I shall have a good laugh at their expense tonight.’There was something frightful about the old man’s ecstasy. It was the one occasion he opened his heart to me; that flash of joy, swift though it was, will never be effaced from my memory.“‘Favor me by staying here,’ he added. ‘I am armed and a sure shot. I have gone tiger-hunting and fought on deck when there was nothing for it but to win or die; but I don’t care to trust yonder elegant scoundrel.’He sat down again in his armchair before his bureau, and his face grew pale and impassive as before.“‘Ah!’ he continued, turning to me, ‘you will see that lovely creature I once told you about; I can hear a fine lady’s step in the corridor; it is she, no doubt;’ and indeed, the young man returned with a woman on his arm. I recognized the Countess, one of old Goriot’s two daughters.The Countess did not see me at first; I stayed in the window bay, my face against the pane; but I saw her give Maxime a suspicious glance as she entered the money-lender’s damp, dark room. She was so beautiful that, despite her faults, I felt sorry for her. There was a storm of anguish in her heart; her proud features were drawn with pain she strove to disguise. The young man had become her evil genius. I admired Gobseck, whose perspicacity had foreseen their future four years ago at the first bill she endorsed.“‘Probably,’ I thought, ‘this monster with the angel face controls every possible spring of action in her: rules her through vanity, jealousy, pleasure, and the current of life in the world.’”The Vicomtesse de Grandlieu interrupted the story.“Why, the woman’s very virtues have been turned against her,” she exclaimed. “He has made her shed tears of devotion, then abused her kindness and made her pay dearly for unhallowed bliss.”Derville did not understand the signs Mme. de Grandlieu made to him.“I confess,” he said, “that I had no inclination to shed tears over the lot of this unhappy creature, so brilliant in society, so repulsive to eyes that could read her heart; I shuddered rather at the sight of her murderer, a young angel with a clear brow, red lips, white teeth, and a winning smile. There they stood before their judge, he scrutinizing them much as a fifteenth-century Dominican inquisitor might have peered into the dungeons of the Holy Office while the torture was administered to two Moors.“The Countess spoke tremulously. ‘Sir,’ she said, ‘is there any way of obtaining the value of these diamonds, and of keeping the right of repurchase?’ She held out a jewel-case.“‘Yes, madame,’ I interjected, stepping forward.“She looked at me, and a shudder ran through her as she recognized me, giving me the glance that means, ‘Say nothing of this,’ all over the world.“‘This,’ I said, ‘constitutes a sale with faculty of redemption, a formal agreement to transfer and deliver a piece of property, either real estate or personalty, for a given time, after which the previous owner recovers their title upon payment of a stipulated sum.’“She breathed more freely. The Count looked black; he doubted whether Gobseck would lend much on the diamonds after such a fall in their value. Gobseck, impassive as ever, took up his magnifying glass and scrutinized the jewels. If I were to live for a hundred years, I would never forget the sight of his face at that moment. There was a flush in his pale cheeks; his eyes seemed to catch the sparkle of the stones, for there was an unnatural glitter in them. He rose and went to the light, holding the diamonds close to his toothless mouth, as if he meant to devour them; mumbling vague words over them, holding up bracelets, sprays, necklaces, and tiaras one after another, to judge their quality; taking them out of the jewel-case and putting them back, letting the light bring out all their fires. He was more like a child than an old man; or rather, childhood and dotage seemed to meet in him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Fine stones! This set would have fetched three hundred thousand francs before the Revolution. What water! Genuine Asiatic diamonds from Golconda or Visapur...
