No one in Paris but Gobseck can appreciate them.” Gobseck examined the jewels with greedy delight, alternating rapture at their perfection and a merciless, calculating austerity. His gaunt face, animated only by the reflected light of the gems, revealed nothing human beneath the glitter.The Count had come to Gobseck seeking a loan of one hundred thousand francs for three years, offering the Countess’s diamond set as security. Gobseck weighed and judged the stones, then, after a glance at the Countess and counsel from the narrator—an attorney who intervened to protect legal formality—rejected making out a deed in the Countess’s name because married women’s agreements could be voidable by their husbands. He insisted instead on immediate possession. Negotiations hardened: Gobseck initially offered eighty thousand francs, then fifty thousand in cash and thirty thousand in bills—paper likely protested and worthless as coin. Maxime, the Countess’s dissolute lover, bristled; tempers flared and Gobseck, unshaken, even produced pistols to underscore the point. The Countess accepted; the jewels were Gobseck’s. His small brutal triumph at possessing the stones made the narrator uneasy.Shortly after, the Count confronted Gobseck in fury, insisting the diamonds were family property illegally taken by his wife. Gobseck answered coolly that if the jewels had been sold in separate pieces there would have been no way for him to know whose they were, and that a married woman able to put her name to a bill could appear to buy and sell in her own right. The narrator, acting as mediator, urged compromise: a legal dispute would force the Count to expose his wife and would be precarious because the goods were not easily identifiable. He advised an instrument allowing the husband to redeem the stones later. Gobseck, shrewd and merciless, negotiated the Count’s acceptance of a settlement—an instrument acknowledging receipt of eighty-five thousand francs in consideration of Gobseck’s promise to return the diamonds—while extracting terms to their advantage.After the Count left, the narrator learned of a more elaborate plan the Count had proposed to him confidentially: to “transfer” his estate to Gobseck by simulated sale so that Gobseck would hold the property in trust and deliver it to the Count’s eldest son when he came of age. The Count feared the boy’s affection for his mother and would not entrust him with the papers. The narrator, reluctant to harm the other children, demanded that any counter-deed provide for them too; the Count, moved and unwell, agreed and signed. The narrator left thinking the deed would be safely held in trust, intending to protect the heirs if necessary.Illness and mutual mistrust between the Count and Countess grew. As the Count weakened, he became aversive to his wife and forbade her and the two youngest children from his room. The Countess, terrified at the prospect of destitution for her children, watched the dying man closely, feigning devotion and keeping near his bedside to overhear every word. She suspected the papers that would secure their future were hidden somewhere and guarded the house with a mother’s vigilance and cunning. The Count’s peculiar, ascetic disorderliness in his sickroom—the damp, broken furniture and a general state of neglect—was part of his medical state and, perhaps, of his scorn.The narrator feared for the counter-deed signed but not delivered to him: if the Countess found or destroyed it, a legal tangle between her and Gobseck would ensue—one the usurer would not surrender. To forestall such a catastrophe, the narrator sued the Count in a contrived action nominally to secure a claim for Gobseck, procured a judgment, and bribed a servant to inform him when the Count was at death’s door so he could take legal steps to seize any papers or documents the Count might leave. Money and the fear of losing it drove the Countess’s vigilance; she studied the law with the dying man’s moans in her ears, seeking ways to keep the family’s patrimony intact.When the Count lay dying, he summoned his young son Ernest and, with solemnity, entrusted him with a sealed packet belonging to the narrator, charging the child to post it so that the narrator would have notice of what the Count intended. The Count pressed Ernest to secrecy, promising that in years his son would understand and be rewarded. Ernest obeyed; on leaving the room he encountered his mother, who questioned him sharply. The Count, hearing the exchange, accused her of trying to pervert their son and corrupt him, and their confrontation was violent and passionate. The Countess fell at his feet, imploring mercy and offering penance to secure her children’s future; the dying man reproached her and called her unfaithful, then lapsed into unconsciousness. Priests administered the sacraments. The Count died at midnight.When the narrator and Gobseck arrived at the house after the death, the scene was chaotic: the room was in disarray, drawers flung open, papers and boxes scattered, evidence that the Countess had rifled the place in the frantic hope of finding the documents that would restore her children’s rights. The pillow lay on the floor; a paper with the Count’s seal had been ripped and was burning in the grate. The narrator snatched an unburned fragment addressed to him and realized with horror that the Countess had been destroying the very papers that might have secured her children’s property—mistaking them, perhaps in ignorance and frantic guilt, for a will that disinherited them. Gobseck, who had come to claim the estate, coldly revealed his intention to assert rightful ownership of the property sold to him by the late Count. He showed neither pity nor moral qualms; the usurer was adamant about enforcing the terms of the sale—possession, he insisted, being tantamount to title.When confronted, Gobseck asked, with suave malevolence, whether the narrator intended to judge him. The narrator left the Countess alone by her husband’s bed, weeping and paralyzed by the discovery. Gobseck took immediate control: he let out the house, assumed the role of landlord and manager, and, over time, settled the estate into practical order—repairing mills, planting timber, reaping income. He transformed himself, paradoxically, into a feudal steward, severe and efficient.The narrator’s later attempts to move Gobseck to act generously toward Ernest—who, it appeared, might yet claim a sufficient fortune to marry and establish himself—met the usurer’s hard moral: adversity would teach the boy the value of money; let him learn his lesson. Gobseck refused to be a patron or a sentimental trustee; he believed in a harsh natural selection by poverty.Years passed. The narrator visited Gobseck, now bedridden, kept for the most part as he always had been: letting rooms be taken as they became vacant, preserving one private room untouched, receiving clients even from bed, and closely managing affairs. His old portress and a pensioner cared for him. Despite failing health he remained alert to deals, discounts, and profits. During the liquidation of claims after France recognized the Haitian Republic, Gobseck’s knowledge of old colonial fortunes and claims had gained him a place on a commission; he devised agencies and systems to profit from buying or speculating on debts and claims, and those who sought his help rewarded him in kind—presents of game, silver plate, snuffboxes. He accepted gifts and bargains but meted them out in stingy, petty barter; nothing positive flowed from his hoard.Called by Gobseck’s urgency as he neared death, the narrator found the usurer in the posture of a man reconciled to fate: naming bequests, talking of relatives, half-delirious, then stretched out and died in full consciousness. His body looked like a bronze portrait of old Rome—brittle and lean. Stirring the cinders of Gobseck’s grate, the narrator found metal and coins among the ashes—signs of secret hoards. The rooms disclosed the habits of a miser: putrid pies and decaying provisions in one room, the stench of neglect among stacked chests and crates, while other rooms were crammed with unrecorded treasures—silver-plate, furniture, jewelry, weapons, pictures—many items pledged and taken in but not sold; none catalogued. A thousand-franc note slipped from a misplaced book. The squalor and the petty disputes recorded in his letters showed the miser’s paradox: infinite appetite for possession but niggardly to the point of losing the value of what he hoarded through his parsimony.The narrator wondered who would inherit such scattered riches. Gobseck had left, among other instructions, an address pointing to a reputed heiress, a girl known as “La Torpille” in the Rue Vivienne—a hint that the usurer’s fortune might pass to someone morally unworthy. Yet before any such fate could be settled, the narrator reflected on Ernest de Restaud: though the Countess’s frantic search and destruction of papers may have cost the children, Ernest still stood to inherit an estate large enough to allow him to marry Mlle. Camille, so long as proper provision was made for his mother and siblings.Thus the narrative closes on the uneasy mixture of justice and irony: the usurer’s triumph at legalities and possession, the Countess’s ruinous panic and maternal devotion distorted into theft and destruction, the narrator’s legal maneuvers to protect heirs becoming entangled with moral compromise, and the final image of Gobseck dying amid the very hoard and petty quarrels that defined his life. The tale leaves the question of the family’s future and the distribution of Gobseck’s wealth unsettled, foreshadowing further complications in the lives of the Restaud family and those around them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Monsieur de Manerville, a respected Norman gentleman, married a wealthy Bordeaux heiress and became a Gascon, drawn to her estate, the château de Lanstrac. He served as major of the Gate Guards until his death in 1813, having avoided the Revolution by going to Martinique in 1790. On his return he found Lanstrac well managed by Mathias, a notary’s clerk who embraced new ideas. After Madame de Manerville's death in 1810, the old man grew miserly and deprived his only son, Paul, of resources.Paul returned from college in 1810 and endured three years of strict paternal control that stifled his character. Physically courageous, he lacked the will to oppose his father and thus lost moral courage; repressed feelings made him timid in society, trapped by the old mansion and unable to engage with peers. His father took him to royalist gatherings where Paul felt out of place among the remnants of nobility, bored and unaware of potential social connections.To escape monotony, Paul practiced activities his father deemed essential for a gentleman—fencing, horseback riding—and read novels in his free time. His father’s death liberated him, leaving him a substantial inheritance and well-maintained estates. Yet he harbored a deep aversion to Bordeaux and Lanstrac, where he had spent summers hunting.Eager for a new life, Paul invested in consols and traveled across Europe, serving at various embassies. Years of indulgence and poor management eroded much of his fortune; he then decided to return to Bordeaux to manage his affairs and settle down as a country gentleman. Conscious of his rank, he felt compelled to marry well, believing his foreign experience had given him the knowledge and elegance for a successful match.Despite extravagance, Paul had harmed no woman and kept a reputation for integrity. He maintained a refined household with well-dressed servants and a fashionable bachelor establishment. One day he confided in his friend de Marsay about his desire to marry and settle. De Marsay cautioned him, arguing marriage would invite ridicule and responsibilities that would diminish his happiness.De Marsay dismissed marriage as chiefly for securing money or heirs, warning it would not guarantee affection and that children often resent parental authority. He believed marriage mortifies individuality, confines people to mediocrity, and provokes the hatred it seeks to prevent. He urged Paul to wait until older and more established, doubting a young wife would share Paul’s ideals or disdain for social expectations.Paul, however, sincerely desired an affectionate companion—emotional support and domestic happiness rather than social advantage. He rejected the notion that marriage must stifle him and insisted he was ready to embrace family responsibilities. The conversation highlighted de Marsay’s cynicism versus Paul’s earnest longing; despite warnings, Paul remained resolute, accepting the risks in pursuit of fulfillment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite earlier conversations, the Comte de Manerville returned to Bordeaux in the winter of 1821 resolved to implement his plans. Restoring and furnishing the family mansion reinforced his reputation for elegance. Reintroduced to Bordeaux’s royalist society, he quickly rose to fashionable prominence, bringing Parisian manners and savoir-faire. An old marquise dubbed him “the pink of fashion,” a phrase used satirically by liberals and in earnest by royalists.Paul de Manerville welcomed the title and prospered on attention. Combining charm and wit, he was respectful yet engaging with women, attentive to appearance without mere foppery. Medium height, healthy complexion, and graceful movements added to his appeal. More inclined to receive happiness than bestow it, he wanted understanding and encouragement—qualities that made him well suited for marriage. Conscious that provincial life required a household, Paul—still young and averse to mundane pursuits—saw marriage as a source of variety. His inclination intensified by a secret love for the beauty of Bordeaux, Mademoiselle Evangelista.Around the turn of the century a wealthy Spaniard named Evangelista had settled in Bordeaux and, by fortune and connections, entered noble salons. His wife, a Creole from an illustrious Spanish family, maintained their status with extravagant tastes, indulging whims without heed to expense, backed by her devoted husband. After Evangelista’s death in 1813, Madame Evangelista, a widow with a fortune and a beautiful daughter, continued to live lavishly even as her wealth diminished.By Paul’s return, Natalie was striking, yet no proposals had been made—largely because her mother’s extravagance discouraged suitors. Rumor held that only a prince could afford to marry her, a notion fomented by jealous mothers and rivals. Natalie, accustomed to luxury and adoration, remained largely unaware of life beyond privilege.Six months after Paul’s arrival they met at a high-society event and immediately recognized each other’s charm. Madame Evangelista perceived his interest and hoped he would become her son-in-law; Paul imagined Natalie as his future wife. He had known of the Evangelista fortune since childhood, which eased his approach. Warnings about the family’s extravagance were dismissed by Paul, whose Parisian experience let him navigate provincial gossip. Madame began inviting him to formal dinners, and he found himself drawn into the prospect of marriage.As he spent time with them he felt pride in their company. Society began to view him as Natalie’s suitor, and he enjoyed the attendant attention. Royalist circles flattered him, suggesting a match would be prudent given Natalie’s beauty and her mother’s intelligence and connections. Paul frequently visited their home and was captivated by Madame Evangelista’s beauty and charm; at forty she retained a striking presence, and her reputation for virtue enhanced her allure. Their relationship carried a mutual understanding: Madame saw Paul as a means to raise her social standing in Paris.Paul was enchanted by the mother-daughter dynamic, which blended friendship with familial affection, and he flattered himself on the intimacy. Yet Madame Evangelista harbored ambitions of her own and hoped to use Paul to further them. Flattered, he increased his visits.A year after his return, Paul’s attentions to Natalie led society to conclude he was courting her, though neither mother nor daughter seemed actively to pursue marriage. Natalie’s charming reserve intrigued Paul; he valued her ability to engage without familiarity, a restraint that allowed him to imagine a future together and to see marriage as desirable. He rationalized that marriage would bring him happiness, especially given his financial means and the compatibility of her upbringing in luxury with his lifestyle. He focused on these benefits and brushed aside concerns about potential difficulties.Contemplating their future, Paul saw Natalie’s beauty and standing as enhancements to his own—an alliance that would secure his position and elevate his social status. He remained unaware of the complexities ahead, idealizing the relationship without grasping the realities of marriage. Natalie, sheltered and naive, was attracted to Paul’s charm but lacked experience of married life. Paul, though enamored, was cautious about possible pitfalls and whether he had the strength to manage a relationship with a woman who might not fully appreciate him.The narrative then widens to the broader theme of marriage in French society, notably the negotiations over marriage contracts. Often ignored in literature, these contracts are crucial in shaping couples’ lives by binding personal desires to societal expectations. The impending discussions about the marriage contract will ultimately influence Paul and Natalie’s future, foreshadowing the complexities to come.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the beginning of winter 1822, Paul de Manerville formally asked for Mademoiselle Natalie Evangelista’s hand through his great-aunt, the Baronne de Maulincour, who stayed in Médoc to assist him. After discussing the dowry with Madame Evangelista, the baroness reported the marriage was likely: Natalie’s dowry was her patrimony, and her mother would contribute nothing. The baroness advised Paul to consult their notary, Maitre Mathias, to ensure the marriage contract protected him.Anxious, Paul visited Madame Evangelista to discuss the contract. She proposed that both families’ notaries meet to negotiate terms, but privately confessed she owed her daughter a large sum from her late husband’s estate and could not pay. Trapped by this debt, she feared that if Paul withdrew, Natalie’s prospects would be ruined.The next day Madame Evangelista summoned her notary, Maitre Solonet, and revealed her troubles. Solonet, confident and pragmatic, assured her they could craft a contract that concealed the financial difficulties while favoring their side, and believed they could negotiate without exposing her debt.That evening both notaries met. Mathias, representing Paul, was cautious and traditional, intent on shielding his client from risk. Solonet was more modern and opportunistic. During dinner Paul and Natalie shared tender moments while the lawyers considered the marriage’s financial implications. Mathias warned of the imbalance: Natalie’s family had squandered wealth, and marrying into such a household could imperil Paul.Madame Evangelista and Solonet proposed she sell her house and use the proceeds to pay debts, thus improving Natalie’s dowry. Mathias doubted this would suffice and feared it still exposed Paul to vulnerability. Tension rose as the notaries debated contract terms; Mathias insisted on safeguards, Solonet pushed for arrangements benefiting Madame Evangelista, and their discussion grew heated as both underscored the marriage’s financial stakes.Ultimately they compromised. Madame Evangelista agreed to create an entail on her property to secure her daughter’s future while protecting Paul’s interests. This arrangement allowed the marriage to proceed without immediate financial threat to either party.As the evening ended, both families felt relieved—Paul optimistic about his future with Natalie, Madame Evangelista satisfied with the negotiation—yet underlying tensions and potential conflict lingered. The first day of negotiations revealed the complexities of love, ambition, and money, setting the stage for challenges ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The following day Elie Magus, then in Bordeaux, answered Madame Evangelista’s summons expecting to discuss jewels for the bride, Comte Paul’s fiancée Mademoiselle Natalie. Instead he was asked to appraise the mother-in-law’s property, including her diamonds, for the marriage contract. Aware of differences between Asian and Brazilian stones, Magus valued Madame Evangelista’s necklace and earrings at 250,000 francs and the “Discreto” at 100,000 francs. When she asked about selling them he warned of the heavy loss she would incur.That conversation sparked staff rumors and speculation about the marriage’s stability. Society seized on the drama: gossip claimed Madame Evangelista was selling her house and that the marriage was in jeopardy. Notaries denied the claims, but public opinion remained skeptical, fueled by envy of the Evangelistas’ wealth and rank.Paul organizing a hunting party only intensified the talk. Madame de Gyas, eager to confirm the story, visited Natalie and her mother under a pretense of concern and relayed the circulating tales. The two women dismissed the gossip, recognizing its motives—especially the scheming of mothers pushing daughters into advantageous matches.Preparing for the wedding, Madame Evangelista planned a grand fête of the camellias to display her wealth and counter rumors. The fête required lavish decorations and the finalization of the marriage contract. As the day approached she had a moment of doubt about the contract’s implications, suspecting her daughter’s fortune might be at risk.On the day of signing the atmosphere was festive; guests arrived for the celebration. Natalie, radiant in bridal attire, and Paul, equally enamored, paid little heed to the legal discussions. Madame Evangelista concentrated on the contract, which spelled out financial arrangements and an entailment of property.When the notaries explained the entail, Madame Evangelista understood that her daughter’s financial security was compromised: if Paul died first, Natalie would inherit only a fraction of the expected fortune. The revelation struck her deeply; she felt her daughter was being ruined.Although she had once intended to manipulate the arrangement, Madame Evangelista found herself constrained by the public nature of the wedding preparations and the expectations they created. She resolved to ensure her daughter’s happiness even if it meant sacrificing some of her own interests.As the contract was finalized, Madame Evangelista’s emotions surged. She vowed to protect her daughter’s future while nursing resentment toward Paul. The signing marked a turning point, cementing the marriage and the financial ties that bound the families.After the festivities, Madame Evangelista resolved to reclaim her daughter’s fortune. Determined to invest wisely and regain control, she transformed from a careless spender into a shrewd strategist, driven by a desire for vengeance against Paul and setting the stage for future conflicts.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The fête briefly distracted Paul from his anxieties, but they returned that night. He feared Madame Evangelista might have deceived him without Mathias's intervention, though he comforted himself that in two days Natalie would be his and their finances seemed settled. Still, he resolved to stay vigilant, uncertain whether Mathias’s worries were justified.Unbeknownst to Paul, his future had shifted. The more astute woman in his life had become an adversary, considering withdrawing her interests from their shared future. He failed to see the cunning of his Creole mother-in-law—her blend of European intellect, tropical passion, and Eastern reserve—dormant during a happy marriage but now inflamed by the prospect of losing her daughter. With only a day until the wedding, Madame Evangelista realized she could shape the couple’s life: Natalie trusted her implicitly, and a single conversation could bend social expectations.The next morning she sat on Natalie’s bed, recalling their harmonious life and mourning the idea of Natalie bound to a man she might have to obey. Natalie dismissed obedience, expecting love to prevail, while Madame Evangelista warned that conjugal love can fade without careful cultivation and urged Natalie to establish authority from the outset.She proposed that her own presence could complicate Natalie’s new role and suggested leaving to avoid conflict, counseling Natalie to appear obedient while subtly asserting dominance. Their talk exposed marital complexities: Madame Evangelista advocated maintaining a social life to keep Paul interested, warning that constant proximity breeds indifference. She advised Natalie to preserve her allure, balance affection with independence, and keep Paul entertained so their relationship stayed vibrant.Overwhelmed, Natalie sought guidance on navigating her new life. Madame Evangelista reassured her they would remain in contact and that she would visit, but insisted separation was necessary for Natalie’s happiness and future.When Paul arrived he attempted to hide his unease; Madame Evangelista noticed and offered to sell her jewelry to support the couple, framing it as a sacrifice for Natalie’s happiness and vanity. Paul, touched, accepted.Soon Solonet, the notary, arrived with news of potential buyers for Madame Evangelista’s property, which could provide a substantial financial boost. Natalie consented to the sale, acknowledging the need for prudent management of their affairs.Determined to secure her daughter’s prospects, Madame Evangelista decided to step back from their Parisian life, believing her presence might impede the marriage. She would support them from a distance, allowing Natalie and Paul to establish their own dynamic.As the wedding neared, preparations for the midnight ceremony carried an air of foreboding; guests speculated about Madame Evangelista’s absence and its implications. After the ceremony she adopted the pose of a grieving mother, extracting promises from Paul that ensured her continued influence and sending the newlyweds to Paris satisfied that her plans were unfolding. Alone with Paul, Natalie began to assert her authority, setting the stage for the complex dynamics of their marriage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years later, in November, Comte Paul de Manerville, cloaked and somber, arrived at the home of his old friend Monsieur Mathias in Bordeaux. The retired notary, too old for business and living quietly after selling his practice, was absent; his housekeeper led Paul to the room of the late Madame Mathias, who had died a year before. Exhausted, Paul slept until evening. When Mathias returned, he found Paul asleep and, noting it was the anniversary of his wife's death, expressed concern.Mathias lamented the public notices of Paul's financial troubles, feeling personal shame at seeing the seizure announcements. He reflected that since Paul's marriage to Natalie Evangelista, things had gone badly, blaming that union for Paul's ruin. Despite his own difficulties, Mathias was relieved to have saved the titles to Paul's estates, though the usufruct had been lost.When Paul awoke, he seemed calm despite his altered appearance. They shared a meal, and Mathias voiced sorrow over the seizure of Paul's properties. Paul revealed he had left Natalie without her knowledge and was sailing to Calcutta to rebuild his fortune. Mathias was shocked that a count would enter commerce, but Paul insisted he was no longer a count and was determined to succeed under another name.Paul outlined his financial situation, admitting massive debts incurred through lavish spending and gifts to Natalie. Mathias was incredulous at the scale of liabilities, including large sums owed to usurers. Paul acknowledged he had been forced to sell assets and take loans to maintain their lifestyle, culminating in the current catastrophe.Despite Mathias's concerns, Paul was resolute about seeking fortune abroad. He believed he could return wealthy by forty. Mathias doubted the plan's feasibility and the reliability of de Marsay, who was supposed to send money.The next day Mathias received a bill of exchange from de Marsay, confirming funds would arrive. Paul, however, remained determined to leave for India, convinced it was his only chance to recover. As he prepared to depart, Mathias lamented losing his friend, and Paul boarded the ship, waving goodbye.Onboard, Paul was consumed by thoughts of his past and his love for Natalie. He felt a mix of regret and determination facing an uncertain future. Overcome by seasickness, he spent days in his cabin, reflecting on his choices and the affection binding him to his wife.Once better, Paul opened letters from Natalie and de Marsay. Natalie's letter revealed anguish at his departure and a resolve to support him financially—she pledged her jewels to pay his debts and vowed to live at Lanstrac with her mother and economize to help him. She expressed love and commitment, promising practical sacrifices.Paul was moved by her words, torn between love and despair. He recognized the depth of their bond yet was haunted by doubts about their future. De Marsay's letter laid out the extent of Paul's ruin and the machinations of his mother-in-law, who had conspired against him. It painted a grim picture: Paul's wife and her mother profited from his downfall.As Paul processed betrayal and reality, he felt anger and helplessness. He realized he had been naive in trust and love; the weight of his decisions pressed on him. In the end, Paul was left to grapple with the consequences of his choices, the love he had for Natalie, and the uncertain path ahead as he sailed away from France, contemplating what the future might bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Laure.A START IN LIFECHAPTER I. THAT WHICH WAS LACKING TO PIERROTIN’S HAPPINESSRailroads will soon make certain trades and travel habits obsolete; the Scene’s elements will become relics, and future generations will be curious about the old social fabric. The once-popular “coucous” of 1830 were already nearly extinct by 1842, surviving only at occasional suburban spectacles. Even in 1820 many celebrated districts around Paris lacked regular stage-coach services.The Touchards, father and son, held a near-monopoly on travel within a forty-five-mile radius from their base in the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis. Despite resources and legal rights, they faced fierce competition from local coucous on short routes. Parisians’ fondness for the countryside allowed small local enterprises—often run by innkeepers intimately familiar with their districts—to thrive. These proprietors offered rapid, convenient service and lower prices, bending or ignoring permits and passenger limits; travelers preferred familiar coachmen and would delay journeys to ride with them. Eventually omnibuses, carrying more passengers more efficiently, would supplant many coucous.One contested line ran from Paris to Beaumont-sur-Oise, lucrative because of stops at Saint-Denis and Saint-Brice; in 1822 it supported three competing services. The Touchards lowered fares and improved coaches, but rivals persisted, extending to places like Chambly and Beauvais. By contrast, the road to Isle-Adam—famous for quarries and history—was less speculative in 1822 and served by only two coaches. Its terminus discouraged competition, granting Pierrotin and his partner a virtual monopoly. Even travelers on the main road to the Cave preferred their familiar vehicles to the often-overcrowded Beaumont coaches.Pierrotin, in his forties, had taken over his father’s coucou service after being discharged from the cavalry in 1815. He married an innkeeper’s daughter, expanded the business, and combined intelligence with a military precision that shaped his conduct. His appearance suited his trade: sturdy boots, bottle-green trousers, and a blue blouse with bright embroidery. Habit made him respect social order, but his work caused him to regard passengers chiefly as cargo.Aware that the industry was changing, Pierrotin sought to modernize. He ordered a larger coach from a respected maker to meet rising demand. His existing fleet included an inherited yellow-and-blue coucou that officially seated six but often carried more, and a four-wheeled coach that could hold seventeen though legally limited to fourteen. The four-wheeler was noisy and instantly recognizable, announced by locals long before it arrived. Pierrotin paid taxes only on the coucou, skirting regulation via a special permit for the four-wheeler.On a Saturday morning in autumn 1822, Pierrotin stood outside the Lion d’Argent watching other coaches depart and fretting over the lack of passengers and parcels. He lamented his financial strain: he still owed a thousand francs to finish paying for the new coach. Though hopeful about its promise, he considered asking Monsieur Moreau, the steward at Presles, for a loan.A footman arrived seeking a ride for his master, the Comte de Serizy, who wished to travel incognito. Pierrotin seized the chance, hoping the fare would ease his debts. The footman explained the count had countermanded a carriage for reasons not given, implying secrecy. Pierrotin sensed the importance of the visit, felt both excitement and concern for Moreau—whom he respected—and, urged by the footman’s admonitions to conceal the count’s identity, resolved to ensure a smooth, discreet journey despite his uneasy finances and the uncertain fate of his new coach.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Monsieur Huguet de Serisy descended from the notable President Huguet, ennobled under François I, and bore a coat of arms split gold and black with a motto that played on modesty and the estate’s name. His father had been a president of parliament before the Revolution. The count himself became a councillor of State at twenty-two, noted for diplomatic memoranda. He did not emigrate during the Revolution but remained at Serizy, protected by his father’s reputation. After his father’s death in 1794 he joined the Council of Five Hundred to distract himself from grief. Following 18th Brumaire he gained the First Consul’s favor, rejoined the Council of State, and reorganized a disordered department, helping shape the administrative framework under Napoleon.In 1806, at forty, he married the widow of a prominent Republican general, significantly increasing his wealth. By 1814, worn by relentless labor and ill health, he resigned; though Napoleon viewed him with some suspicion, he retained status and later won Louis XVIII’s trust, who made him a cabinet minister. During the Hundred Days he remained loyal to the Bourbons and stayed at Serizy. After Napoleon’s final fall he resumed duties as privy councillor and vice-president of the Council of State, influencing public affairs without seeking praise. He rose early, worked tirelessly, and received many honors, including the grand cross of the Legion of Honor. Despite public recognition, he led a life of quiet dedication, marked by a deeper, unspoken sorrow.The count had long been passionately in love with his wife; the feeling persisted despite marital strains. His constant work distracted him from heartache, and he gave her considerable freedom, treating her with respect and admiration. She appreciated some of his qualities but was sometimes ungrateful, which wounded him; only occasionally did she soothe his pain.A pressing matter then confronted him: a farm encroaching on the Serizy estate, Presles. The farm, leased by a tenant named Léger from a local burgher, Margueron, was coming up for sale as the lease expired. The count wanted to buy it to end ongoing disputes and instructed his notary and lawyer to prepare for quick negotiations, stressing prompt action to avoid losing the opportunity.His steward, Moreau, had served faithfully for seventeen years. Moreau’s father had saved the Serizys during the Revolution, and in gratitude the count had helped Moreau avoid a death sentence. Moreau had since married and asked to manage the Presles estate, where he could live comfortably. Initially diligent, Moreau’s loyalty faded after securing his post; he began accepting bribes and engaging in questionable dealings.As the count prepared to visit Presles, Moreau plotted to profit from the sale, having already arranged a lucrative deal with Léger to secure funds for his family. Moreau’s conduct, however, provoked the ire of a neighboring retired officer, Monsieur de Reybert, who sought revenge for perceived slights. Madame de Reybert approached the count, alleging Moreau’s dishonesty and an attempt to defraud him in the purchase, and provided particulars. Though the count at first suspected Madame de Reybert’s motives, his doubts about Moreau mounted. He decided to investigate, instructing his notary to accompany Madame de Reybert back to Presles and to meet him there. Thus the count’s incognito journey, meant as a straightforward purchase, became charged with intrigue and the threat of betrayal by those he trusted.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Pierrotin left the Café de l’Echiquier, he saw a woman and her son at the Lion d’Argent looking for him. The woman, in a faded black gown and an old shawl, was anxious—a devoted mother sending her son Oscar out alone for the first time. Her attire suggested sacrifice; she was eager to entrust him to Pierrotin’s care.Oscar, in a short-sleeved coat and patched trousers, was eighteen and still under his mother’s sway. Madame Clapart fretted that he might soil his gloves and worried aloud about his trip to Monsieur Moreau. Pierrotin knew the family; he often carried goods for Moreau, who maintained a discreet, intimate connection with Madame Clapart, though Pierrotin did not know all the particulars.They lived modestly on the third floor amid old furniture and signs of poverty, but Madame Clapart retained a regal air. Her husband, Clapart, a government clerk, earned little; the household subsisted partly on Moreau’s occasional help. Oscar was the center of her world, and she remained blind to his faults, which his stepfather’s neglect only worsened.Moreau had once been close to Madame Husson—Madame Clapart in her better days. After misfortunes, including her first husband’s suicide, she married Clapart, who failed to fulfill her hopes. When Moreau returned to France he secured a scholarship for Oscar, yet the family’s condition stayed precarious.Reared on memories of his mother’s past glories, Oscar developed vanity and entitlement. Unaware of his own limits, he worried Moreau, who suggested the young man spend time with the steward as a test of his potential. Madame Clapart’s constant reminders about propriety intensified Oscar’s discomfort as he prepared to leave.At departure she fussed over him, packing food and offering last-minute counsel. He felt humiliated by her attentiveness, especially before two young men who mocked him. Their laughter stung; he longed to free himself from his mother’s affection.Those two, better dressed and more confident, deepened his insecurity. Boarding the coach, Oscar tried to ignore their jests while grappling with feelings of inferiority.A shabby young artist and his lively apprentice, Mistigris, arrived and enlivened the scene; their banter and ease contrasted with Oscar’s self-consciousness and deepened his sense of isolation.The Comte de Serizy’s entrance caused a stir. Disguised as a bourgeois, the count sought anonymity while navigating the coach’s social order. Pierrotin, eager to please him, rearranged seating and drew complaints from other passengers. Tension charged the compartment as they waited.When the coach set off, the passengers’ interactions revealed varied origins and motives. Beneath the surface, Oscar’s struggle with identity and self-worth continued to simmer, setting the stage for the journey ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Those who travel in public conveyances often remain silent at first, using the time to observe and settle in; conversation usually breaks only when the most talkative person speaks, especially in France where social interaction is encouraged. The text sketches national types: Englishmen reserved, Germans somber, Italians cautious, Spaniards rarely using public transport, Russians hampered by poor roads. In France the atmosphere is livelier, and talk flows freely among the lower classes and bourgeois.Georges, a clever, lively young man, quickly sized up his fellow passengers, judged himself superior, and set out to amuse himself at their expense. As the coach descended from La Chapelle to Saint-Denis, he experimented with identities and chose to present himself as a disguised Russian prince or a soldier returned from the East. When Mistigris complained about dust, Georges joked and steered the conversation toward supposed travels in the Levant, claiming to have served under Ali, pacha of Janina, and inventing a scare about a plague that alarmed the count, who asked the driver to stop. He added that he had fought in the 1813 campaign and at Waterloo, where he said he was promoted to captain.The count asked about decorations; Georges answered he did not wear them in travel. When the count produced his own honors, Georges professed admiration for the Emperor and continued with tales of Egypt and an alleged harem presented to him by Ali Tebelen, though he dismissed harem life as uninteresting. Arriving at Saint-Denis, the group disembarked at a tavern famed for its cheese-cakes, where the count, suspicious, discovered in a portfolio that Georges was actually Crottat’s second clerk. This revelation made the count doubt Georges’s narratives and motives.Meanwhile Georges impressed others—especially Pere Léger and Oscar—by paying for their treats and ordering drinks, cultivating the pose of someone important. Mistigris, a modest painter, introduced himself. The count observed how easily Georges bought admiration and tried to elevate his status.As the journey resumed, Georges continued spinning accounts of Eastern oddities: lack of police, peculiar farming practices, heavy taxation under the pacha that left little for peasants, and farmers bent on subsistence rather than cultivation. The count and Mistigris discussed the implications and drew parallels to French conditions, while the count remained skeptical of Georges’s authenticity.Georges’s stories grew more elaborate, culminating in a dramatic love affair with a beautiful Greek woman named Zena in Dalmatia. He recounted danger from her jealous husband, a pirate, and a narrow escape from execution after the husband was poisoned. The tale—full of peril and romance—captivated the group; Oscar was especially envious of Georges’s purported adventures. Mistigris added his own anecdotes, lending humor and variety.Throughout, the coach’s atmosphere became lively, with laughter, banter, and storytelling warmed by drink. Georges’s confidence and charisma won admiration from many travelers, even as the count remained wary and alert to contradictions. The journey continued as a weave of tales, camaraderie, and intermittent skepticism, the passengers drawn together by shared narrative and the entertaining performance of identity.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pierrotin’s coach came down into Saint-Brice around half past one, where he usually stopped an hour to rest his horses. The innkeeper greeted them. Père Léger, the fat farmer, said he would break a crust, while Georges, ever theatrical, twirled his cane and ordered a good breakfast. Georges then produced a straw case and lit a pale cigar, asking if anyone smoked. Oscar, puffing to appear at ease, accepted, though he coughed and showed he did not know how to smoke. Schinner sniffed at the cheap cigars—“ten-sous cigars”—and Georges explained they were remnants from Spain. Schinner, who had already eaten at the Lion d’Argent, declined breakfast.Oscar, inwardly calculating how little his clothes and carriage matched his ambitions, felt uneasy as Georges showed off his extravagances—Alicante cheese, expensive cigars, likely a lavish breakfast. Mistigris joked that Georges would spend ten francs at least, and that “sharp stomachs make short purses.” Georges proposed a bottle of Bordeaux with Père Léger; Oscar, noting the expense, muttered about the cost and sat on a stone post, unaware that his trousers exposed mismatched stockings. Mistigris noticed and teased; Schinner displayed a similar mismatch, and the count laughed, envying their casual air.Conversation turned to Les Moulineaux and finances. The innkeeper asked Père Léger if he intended to buy it after going to Paris for the money; Léger answered quietly, “I’ve done for him.” The innkeeper offered to lend twenty thousand francs if needed and reported that Monsieur Margueron had been invited to dine with the Comte de Serizy at Presles. Père Léger laughed that they had ways to thwart such plans. He explained that if the count secured the ministry he might appoint Monsieur Margueron’s son, but Léger had the King—he would give Moreau forty thousand of his pictures on coins of the realm, which would enable him to buy Les Moulineaux outright for two hundred sixty thousand francs in cash before the count could act. The innkeeper and Pierrotin argued the farm’s value: Les Moulineaux brought in six thousand francs and at a lease of seven thousand five hundred for eighteen years it was a solid investment. If Moreau received ten thousand francs from the count, he could make fifty thousand; the count, they reasoned, would not mind since he was rich and perhaps intended to live at Presles if he was spending two hundred thousand restoring it.Pierrotin came out to water the horses, and the count, fearing gossip, drew him aside. He reminded Pierrotin of the ten louis he had promised to secure his silence and added an inducement: a thousand francs the next day for a new coach if he continued to conceal the count’s name and avoided going into the inn. Pierrotin promised compliance, assuring the count it concerned Père Léger. Pierrotin urged the farmer to hurry so they would not be late; he had a fish to deliver by three to Stors for a great dinner and could not delay customers. Père Léger suggested harnessing the horse Pierrotin wanted to sell into the cabriolet so they could breakfast and then overtake Pierrotin. To everyone’s surprise, Pierrotin rebridled his horses and drove off; Schinner and Mistigris had already walked on.As the coach rolled toward Moisselles, Georges praised the landscape with theatrical enthusiasm. Schinner, who had traveled more widely, dismissed such provincial charm. Georges offered his remaining cigars to be finished by Schinner; Père Léger and the count stayed silent but consenting. Oscar, offended at being referred to as a “little young man,” declared he was not an aide-de-camp and that he would likely travel to the East in time, though the career his family intended for him would spare him certain annoyances before then. The others mocked him; Mistigris crowed and made his indignation seem absurd.When Georges asked Oscar what career his family had destined for him, Oscar replied “diplomacy.” This provoked laughter from Schinner and Mistigris, and even the count smiled at the conceit. Georges observed that Oscar’s mother apparently did not fit the figure of an ambassadress; Oscar corrected him: his mother ran their household. The group capitalized on Oscar’s pretension to amuse themselves. Schinner teased that well-bred people spoke of “households,” and the count praised the company ironically: a famed painter, a future general, and a young diplomat destined to recover Belgium for France. Oscar tried to assert dignity with a proverb but was counseled that proverbs alone would not make a diplomat.The conversation turned to Oscar’s upbringing. He named his tutor, the Abbé Loraux, now vicar of Saint-Sulpice, and boasted that his family would further the fortunes of clerical acquaintances like the Abbé Frayssinous. The count asked about the abbé and then about Oscar’s destination. Oscar said he was going to the Château de Presles to see the Comte de Serizy, claiming to be a comrade of the count’s son and frequently riding with him. Surprise rippled among the others: Père Léger wondered whether the count was at Presles, Schinner asked how often Oscar saw him, and Mistigris muttered witticisms. The count, hearing this, said he wanted information about Monsieur de Serizy for a matter that would cost little; he hoped Oscar could provide it.Oscar, eager to display insider knowledge, advised the count not to approach the Comte de Serizy for help but his wife, who, he claimed, was the count’s real power because the count was disfigured by a stubborn skin disease. He described treatments the count took—sulphur and steam baths, an iron box fashioned by a valet—mocking the count’s brittle, parchment-like condition and suggesting that no one could blame the countess for finding diversions elsewhere. He proposed that the Marquis d’Aiglemont, a former admirer of Madame de Serizy, would be the man to approach to secure both her and her husband’s favor. The young man’s talk grew increasingly intimate and indiscreet. Schinner and Mistigris chided him for speaking so publicly of someone’s private life.The count, hearing his name and the countess’s, interrupted sharply and forbade any word against the Comtesse de Serizy. He declared himself a friend of her brother, the Marquis de Ronquerolles, and warned that anyone who disparaged the countess must answer to him. Then, with a certain hauteur, he insisted that he knew people—ministers, architects, and court officials—and that he prevented unworthy men from receiving honors. He added, with a glance toward Georges, that he did not wear decorations but ensured they went properly. He then asked Pierrotin to stop at the next inn so he could step out.Pierrotin hurried through Moisselles. The short distance to the next stop passed in silence. Drawing Pierrotin aside in the inn-yard, the count asked about the lad who claimed intimacy with Presles. Pierrotin disclosed that the young man was the son of a poor woman in the rue de la Cerisaie, Madame Husson, for whom Pierrotin often delivered the game and fruit from Presles. The count learned that the youth was not a noble at all but a steward’s or concierge’s son. He was going to be left on the road near Maffliers, telling Pierrotin he did not wish to go on to Isle-Adam.The others, overhearing this, felt exposed as though caught in a lie; they dared not meet each other’s eyes. Mistigris mocked the predicament as “suffering for licence’s sake.” Oscar, mortified, insisted he did know the count; Georges dryly replied that even if he had, Oscar would never make an ambassador because people must learn to talk without saying anything when in public conveyances—words meant to impress but not to reveal realities.The coach continued in silence, then passed through the Carreau woods. Mistigris muttered proverbs in mock consolation. Oscar asked about the Château de Franconville and was scolded for ignorance; the count teased that Oscar knew men, not castles. Oscar, eager for recognition, demanded his name be remembered: “I am Oscar Husson, and ten years hence I shall be famous,” he declared, then flopped back, embarrassed. The count, amused, explained with a touch of irony the family’s stamped name—Husson de la Cerisaie—suggesting noble pretensions. The hill of La Cave descended toward the great château of Presles.Before they reached the chateau, the count gave his friends parting comments. He wished the colonel reconciliation with the restored Bourbon monarchy, praised Schinner’s rising fame while warning of his domestic perils, and congratulated Père Léger on his enterprise in taking the Comte de Serizy’s property, urging him to be resolute. He paid a civil, slightly condescending compliment to Oscar: he had secrets of statesmen and could make them tremble. Then the count stepped down at an iron gate and told Pierrotin to set him out and to pick him up at the same place the next day. He left the coach amid the company’s confusion.Père Léger and the others speculated that the count had hired Franconville, noting the path to it. Schinner swore never again to blague in a public coach, blaming Mistigris, who shrugged that fortune mocked him as usual. Georges warned Oscar privately that he would not envy him if the passenger had indeed been the Comte de Serizy, for Oscar’s indiscreet talk could come back to haunt him. Oscar, remembering his mother’s cautions, turned pale.Pierrotin announced their arrival at a fine iron gate. The others feigned either knowledge or surprise; Georges quickly claimed he was actually going on to Les Moulineaux to avoid looking foolish. Père Léger revealed he was the farmer at Les Moulineaux, welcoming the colonel to his house. Oscar told Pierrotin to leave his things at the steward’s; he intended to go straight to the chateau, plunging down a narrow path until Père Léger called out that he must go through the little gate to reach the forest entrance properly. Reluctantly directed, Oscar entered the grand courtyard.Père Léger watched as Georges slipped away when he realized Père Léger occupied Les Moulineaux, in effect revealing that Georges had been heading there after all. At Pierrotin’s request the iron gates opened, and he proudly drove in to deliver Schinner’s parcels. Oscar was surprised to learn that Mistigris and his master would actually be staying in the chateau. In ten minutes Pierrotin had unloaded the painter’s packages, Oscar’s bundles, and a small leather portmanteau, which he handed to the concierge’s wife. He then drove off, cracking his whip, beaming with the sly satisfaction of a peasant who has calculated his profit: the next day he expected a thousand francs from the count and, with that, a magnificent new coach.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Oscar, uneasy, hid behind trees in the courtyard and watched Monsieur Moreau emerge from the guard-room in a blue overcoat with a riding whip. Moreau greeted him warmly and introduced himself to Mistigris and his master, the two painters sent by architect Monsieur Grindot. He told the concierge to show them to their rooms, invited them to dinner at five, and mentioned hunting on the estate.As Moreau led Oscar through the park, he sent his son Jacques ahead to tell Madame Moreau of their arrival. Stern in appearance but kindly, Moreau impressed Oscar, who felt small beside him. Moreau urged Oscar to learn to ride and hunt despite his inexperience. Oscar’s mother had permitted only a two-week stay because Madame Moreau was present, yet Moreau seemed intent on extending it.The steward’s house, built before the Revolution, stood in a large garden and adjoined the chateau. It contained a salon, dining room, and a modern boudoir furnished to Madame Moreau’s taste. The Moreaus lived comfortably on estate provisions with little expense. Madame Moreau, who had once been a maid, kept an air of elegance and worked to be seen as mistress of the chateau, often patronizing villagers.Tension with the Reyberts stemmed from a past slight when Madame de Reybert had reminded Madame Moreau of her former status. The Moreaus had advance notice of the painters’ arrival, and Madame Moreau was eager to impress them, hoping the artist she hosted would raise her social standing.On the day the painters were due, Madame Moreau dressed elegantly, but the Paris coach brought only Mistigris’s packages, leaving her disappointed. The two artists—Mistigris and Bridau—eventually arrived; Madame Moreau welcomed them and professed a love of art. The painters quickly recognized her pretentiousness and decided to humor her, engaging in conversation about art.Their talk was interrupted when a maid announced the count’s arrival, and Madame Moreau grew flustered. Oscar, feeling out of place, was summoned by his younger brother and chastised for his appearance. Mistigris made a joke about Oscar’s future, heightening the awkwardness. The maid returned saying the count wanted dinner promptly, adding to the household’s bustle.The count had arrived unexpectedly and was distressed to learn Moreau was absent. He had overheard remarks suggesting Moreau’s disloyalty, prompting a confrontation. The count expressed disappointment that Moreau had discussed private matters, especially in front of Oscar. Moreau tried to explain but ultimately faced the consequences of his indiscretions.As the count prepared for dinner, guests including notaries and the Reyberts arrived, sensing tension and the count’s displeasure. Mistigris’s light remark about the count’s appearance briefly eased the mood.Moreau returned home distraught over the loss of his position and the implications of Oscar’s careless words. Estelle, his wife, was furious and grasped the gravity of their predicament. Moreau confronted Oscar, demanding an explanation; Oscar remained silent, overwhelmed by fear and shame. In anger, Moreau dragged him back to the count to apologize. The count dismissed Oscar’s behavior and doubted his worth. Humiliated, Moreau took Oscar home to contemplate the consequences and the uncertain future of their family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While the horses were being harnessed, Moreau wrote to Madame Clapart in distress about Oscar’s conduct. During a journey Oscar had disclosed sensitive information about Madame de Serizy to a count traveling incognito, which led to Moreau’s dismissal as steward of Presles. The count ordered Oscar sent home immediately, a decision that terrified Moreau and his wife because it threatened the family’s future and Moreau’s hopes of improving their fortunes.That evening Madame Clapart was knitting when Monsieur Clapart mentioned a friend’s forthcoming visit and voiced doubts about Oscar’s prospects, criticizing the boy’s character. Madame Clapart defended Oscar, insisting on his good nature and potential. Their conversation was interrupted by Brochon’s arrival, who brought Oscar back and delivered a letter from Moreau explaining the situation. Overwhelmed, Madame Clapart fainted on reading the letter, stricken with panic about her son’s prospects.The next day Madame Clapart sought Oscar alone, her appearance revealing her distress. She reminded him of Moreau’s support and how precarious their situation had become now that he had lost the stewardship. She outlined the few realistic career options remaining and stressed the need for discretion, hard work, and prudence. Oscar remained largely indifferent to the gravity of their circumstances, which frustrated her entreaties.Madame Clapart pressed Oscar to promise greater discretion and to heed her advice, and proposed visiting Uncle Cardot as a last hope. Uncle Cardot, a self-made businessman who had amassed a fortune and was known for generosity to his children, lived modestly and was unaware of their struggles.At Uncle Cardot’s he greeted them warmly and, while pleased to hear of Oscar’s academic achievements, remarked that the boy still had much to learn. Moved by Madame Clapart’s account of their hardship, Cardot offered to support Oscar’s legal education and to pay the costs. He urged Oscar to apply himself, maintain integrity, and cultivate discipline, convinced that with effort he could succeed.Afterward Madame Clapart felt renewed hope, though the family still faced financial strain. Moreau later visited to say he had begun land speculation and could help place Oscar as a pupil in a law office, again stressing that discipline and hard work were essential to repair his reputation and secure his future. Madame Clapart was grateful and urged Oscar to express his gratitude; Moreau pressed him to focus on studies and behavior as the path to improvement.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Ten days later Monsieur Moreau took Oscar to Maître Desroches, a young solicitor in the rue de Béthisy who had recently set up in a spacious but inexpensive office. Desroches, twenty-six, himself raised in poverty, recognized Oscar’s situation; his gaunt appearance and curt manner intimidated the youth. Seated amid a mountain of papers, he told them, “We work here day and night,” and instructed his head clerk, Monsieur Godeschal, to supervise Oscar’s training so he would not lose time on the way to law school and would master the Code. Oscar was to live in a small attic next to Godeschal’s room; Desroches declared his ambition to make him a capable clerk.For two years Oscar lived in the office, surrounded by petty but diligent work. Under Godeschal’s strict eye he kept a monk-like routine: rising at five, running errands, studying the Code under rigorous examination, and returning after classes to more tasks. Meals were plain. His social life was meager — monthly breakfasts with his uncle Cardot and Sundays with his mother — and although Moreau sometimes took him out, Oscar’s attempts at elegance met the disapproval of his superiors.Godeschal counseled modest dress and frugality, insisting a good clerk should not spend more than seven hundred francs a year. He practiced these principles himself, combining honesty and integrity with no pretense. When another clerk failed in his duties, Godeschal discreetly urged him to leave rather than endure dismissal. He sought to shield Oscar from temptation, even occasionally covering the costs of outings to give him some enjoyment, since Oscar’s home life lacked interest. Moreau was supportive but less able than Godeschal to connect with the young man. Both hoped to guide him safely through his formative years.By twenty, Oscar had become the third clerk, performing the second clerk’s duties without salary but receiving room and board. His knowledge surpassed that of many licensed graduates, and he began to argue cases in chambers. Yet he felt trapped in a life at odds with his desires: he envied the well-dressed young men in the streets and yearned for freedom and pleasure, feelings he often suppressed. Godeschal noticed these tendencies and worried they might lead Oscar astray; he thought Oscar’s talents might suit a barrister better than a solicitor’s clerkship. “Too much vanity,” he commented when Moreau asked how Oscar was getting on.In November 1825, shortly after Oscar’s promotion, a new clerk arrived: Frédéric Marest, a well-off young man with ambitions for the magistracy who wished to learn practical legal work. Godeschal announced him, hoping Marest would join the office camaraderie. The clerks enjoyed practical jokes; Godeschal maintained a humorous tradition — a fictitious register of receptions for new clerks filled with absurd entries — as a source of amusement and bonding. Desroches’ recently established office lacked such a welcome, but Godeschal was determined to uphold it.Oscar, settling into his role, became part of the jest and called for the register when Marest arrived. Marest read it and laughed privately, then said he would consult his cousin, a head clerk, about the welcome he should extend — a remark that aroused Godeschal’s suspicions about Marest’s novice status. Despite the lighthearted atmosphere and clerkly camaraderie, Oscar’s internal conflict persisted as he navigated professional pressures and the expectations placed upon him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day at two o’clock Oscar recognized Georges Marest, now head-clerk of notary Hannequin, who entered the office. Georges greeted Oscar, recalling past mischief involving a peer of France that had led to Georges’ transfer to Hannequin. He then invited Oscar and his colleagues to a gathering at the Rocher de Cancale and later to spend the evening with Madame la Marquise de las Florentinas y Cabirolos, promising a night of high society.Desroches, the office head, interrupted to instruct Oscar to obtain a crucial legal document from the Palais and warned him to be careful of Derville, who might obstruct their efforts. Oscar was keen to prove himself in this first assignment as second clerk.After Georges and Oscar left, Godeschal tried to learn more about the Marquise, but Frederic, keeping a serious demeanor, sustained the ruse, suggesting she was a wealthy widow from Mexico. The clerks, excited by the promise of a grand breakfast and fashionable festivities, eagerly anticipated meeting the Marquise.In truth, the “Marquise” was Mademoiselle Agathe-Florentine Cabirolle, a successful danseuse at the Gaîté, supported by Père Cardot, a merchant who had taken her and her mother under his wing. Cardot had invested in Florentine’s career; she became a celebrated star, living comfortably and preparing for a possible engagement at the Opera.Georges, with new prosperity, actively courted Florentine, and the clerks, keen to impress, dressed in their best. Oscar’s mother, Madame Clapart, voiced hopes for her son’s advancement and warned him about Georges, whom she blamed for Oscar’s earlier misfortune.On the day of the gathering, Oscar felt both excitement and apprehension, resolved to enjoy the day while mindful of his mother’s and Godeschal’s advice. After a lavish, boisterous breakfast the clerks, now intoxicated, went on to Florentine’s salon, greeted by dazzling guests and opulence.As the evening progressed, Oscar was introduced to Florentine, who charmed him with grace and beauty. He was drawn into a game of vingt-et-un with her and the actress Fanny Beaupré, quickly losing the money he had been given for the legal document. Despite reluctance, he gambled more and sank into desperation.Florentine, noticing his distress, offered him a thousand francs to recover his losses, which he accepted, mistaking her aid for noble kindness. His luck failed; he lost everything and plunged into despair and drunken stupor.The party continued into the early morning, Florentine and her friends reveling while Oscar remained oblivious to the wider chaos, asleep and unaware of the consequences his actions would bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning a loud noise woke clerk Oscar Husson. Hearing his uncle Cardot's voice, he pretended to sleep as Cardot scolded him for late-night revelry and the company he kept. Florentine, a danseuse, laughed and revealed Oscar as Cardot's nephew and urged the uncle to help him. Oscar had lost gambling money that belonged to his employer, Monsieur Desroches. Desperate and ashamed, he begged Cardot for aid, fearing disgrace and the loss of his job.Cardot reluctantly lent him five hundred francs, warning it would be the last help. Oscar left uncertain. Mariette, worried, sent a note and money; Godeschal, another clerk, rushed to prepare the papers for Desroches. Angered, Desroches resolved to dismiss Oscar despite Godeschal's defense. Godeschal recounted Oscar's past errors and blamed the influence of Georges Marest, a bad companion. Half in jest, Desroches suggested Oscar should try the law, thinking his vanity might fit the bar.At home, Clapart, Oscar's father, tormented his wife with reminders of their son's failures. Madame Clapart defended Oscar while Clapart remained cynical. When Oscar returned, he confronted his father, asserted his independence, and protested his treatment, but his plea fell on deaf ears.Determined to take responsibility, Oscar faced the prospect of military service, which Clapart believed would instill discipline. He drew a number for conscription and, despite his fears, hoped for a favorable outcome. Three days later, through the Comte de Serizy's influence, Oscar was assigned to the cavalry. Overwhelmed by their son's misfortunes, Madame Clapart turned to religion, believing past sins had brought trouble and seeking redemption through piety. Oscar applied himself in the army and rose steadily, eventually becoming a lieutenant-colonel.Years later, a decorated Oscar stood with his mother outside the Lion d’Argent, waiting for a coach. Both were unrecognizable from their former selves. Though haunted by past mistakes, Oscar had achieved success. As they prepared to board, they encountered Georges Marest, now a shadow of his former self, who failed to recognize Oscar. Poorly dressed, Georges tried to preserve dignity despite his decline. Oscar reflected on how far they had diverged since youth.On the journey, Georges' attempts to impress others with tales of past exploits fell flat. Oscar, confident and composed, defended his achievements and credited his military career for his stability. He was invited to a wedding, signaling his integration into respectable society. He married Georgette Pierrotin, the coach proprietor’s daughter, cementing his social standing. His earlier errors became instructive lessons, guiding him to discretion, honesty, and steady work. Ultimately, discipline, connections, and changed character led to a secure, respected career in a world that once seemed precarious.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ THE MIDDLE OF THE STORY ###### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo a Polish lady.Daughter of an enslaved land, angel through love, witch through fancy, child by faith, aged by experience, man in brain, woman in heart, giant by hope, mother through sorrows, poet in thy dreams—this book belongs to thee; thy love, fancy, experience, sorrow, hope, dreams form the warp through which a less brilliant woof is shot, and thy countenance, when it shines, is to those who love thee like the characters of a lost language to scholars.De Balzac.MODESTE MIGNONCHAPTER I. THE CHALETIn October 1829 Monsieur Simon Babylas Latournelle, a notary, walked from Havre to Ingouville with his son Exupère, his wife, and their head clerk Jean Butscha. At a bend Latournelle lowered his voice to instruct Exupère in a little stratagem: Exupère was to pay his respects to the Mignons and, after a short promenade, return to whisper to Monsieur Dumay, “the young man has come,” signaling romantic interest. Exupère, bound for Paris to study law, was enlisted in the scheme.Butscha, a hunchbacked clerk who adored Modeste, asked if she had a lover; Madame Latournelle, vain and pretentious about social standing, hushed him. Latournelle himself was odd but trustworthy—memorable for his green goggles. Ingouville, a picturesque Havre suburb, housed merchants’ country homes. The Chalet, a charming cottage with a garden and a beloved hot-house, belonged to Dumay, cashier to the Mignons; his lease annoyed the new owner Monsieur Vilquin, who offered him money to leave, but Dumay refused, determined to protect Modeste from suitors.CHAPTER II. A PORTRAIT FROM LIFEThe Latournelles found Gobenheim, a young banker, already at the Chalet. Pale, serious, and visiting more from self-interest than affection, Gobenheim added to the household’s unease. Dumay prepared secretly to confront any suitor who might compromise Modeste. Blind and dignified, Madame Mignon was the family’s center; Modeste embroidered beside her while unspoken anxiety filled the room. That evening was to test Modeste’s affections under Dumay’s watchful eye.CHAPTER III. PRELIMINARIESJean François Bernard Dumay, once a soldier, became devoted cashier to the Mignon family after misfortunes. His loyalty to Colonel Mignon—exiled, humiliated, afterward sent to Siberia—was unshaken. Dumay’s wife, bereft of her own children, lavished maternal care on Modeste. After a financial disaster, Dumay and Latournelle labored to settle debts while Modeste and her blind mother retreated to the Chalet. Madame Mignon, though sightless, sensed a change in Modeste and suspected love; Dumay, protective and suspicious, vowed to guard her honor.CHAPTER IV. A SIMPLE STORYThe Mignons’ decline began with business failure and the colonel’s forced departure. Dumay and Latournelle worked to discharge obligations; the women lived quietly at the Chalet. Madame Mignon’s health waned under grief; Modeste’s innocence and quiet dreams troubled Dumay, who took his guardian role seriously. Their life fell into routine, deprived of male companionship and marked by anxious prudence about Modeste’s future.CHAPTER V. THE PROBLEM STILL UNSOLVEDAs Modeste’s beauty and charm drew speculation, her mother’s fears grew. The household women became vigilant, suspecting Modeste might secretly entertain romantic feelings. Dumay resolved that no man should approach her without his knowledge. Despite their scrutiny, Modeste’s heart brimmed with dreams shaped by reading and by her sister’s tragic fate; the women conspired to shelter her from passion’s dangers while unease mounted.CHAPTER VI. A MAIDEN’S FIRST ROMANCEModeste’s imagination thrived on literature; she longed to be the muse of a great poet and to escape provincial obscurity. Influenced by her sister’s tragedy and hungry for significance, she dared to write to the poet Canalis, pouring out her heart in a letter that sought connection and uplift. The anticipation of his reply filled her with hope, though she knew the risk of addressing a man of fame.CHAPTER VII. A POET OF THE ANGELIC SCHOOLCanalis received Modeste’s letter with amusement and mild condescension. He saw her innocence and feared misunderstanding. His reply combined flattering civility with caution, warning her against romanticizing a poet’s life. Reading it, Modeste felt disappointment but also renewed resolve—the reality of Canalis’s temperament clashed with her idealized vision, and she determined to press the correspondence further, intent to assert her identity and desires. Thus begins her navigation of love, ambition, and social expectation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################CHAPTER VIII. BLADE TO BLADETo Monsieur de Canalis:Monsieur, you are a great poet and an honest man. After your frankness to a young girl on the brink of despair, can you answer me truthfully? Would your response have been the same if you had known that Mademoiselle O. d’Este M. has six million francs and seeks a fool for a husband?Consider this frankly. I am wiser than my years; nothing candid can harm you. If you share your thoughts, I will answer your first letter.Having admired your talent, I must also pay tribute to your integrity, and remain,Your humble servant, O. d’Este M.Ernest de La Brière read this and paced the boulevards, conscience and curiosity at war. Parisians might call the note flirtatious; a man of honor felt judged by Truth, Justice, and Honor. Truth accused him of hypocrisy; Justice defended the girl’s rebellion against materialism; Honor demanded sincerity. He delayed, then replied:Mademoiselle, you are not a D’Este. The name is a disguise. Do I owe truth to someone who lies about herself? Are you noble or of the people? Ethics are constant but obligations vary with rank. A daughter of Este worth six million can frankly confess a fondness for poetry; a girl of the people would be mocked. What class are you? Answer me plainly, and I will reply to your question.Modeste answered:To Monsieur de Canalis, you grow sensible, my dear poet. My father is a count; our family includes a cardinal, and I am the last of our name. We are equal to the Canalis. You will excuse my refusing to send our arms. Please answer me as truthfully as I have answered you. I await your response, to know if I may sign myself,Your servant, O. d’Este M.La Brière smiled at her mischief and admired her frankness. Four years in Paris had taught him the art of intrigue. He booked a seat to Havre, wrote that duty delayed him, and obtained a note from the post-office director to ensure secrecy. He watched Francoise Cochet collect letters and followed her discreetly to Ingouville, catching a glimpse of Modeste at the Chalet window. He learned the estate belonged to Monsieur Vilquin, the richest merchant in Havre. The Vilquins were immensely wealthy; one daughter was married to M. Althor and rumors spoke of high-society visitors and a possible match with a duke.Having seen enough to dream—and to regret the reconnaissance—La Brière resumed the correspondence. He wrote a letter shaped by conscience and passion; Modeste read it at her window on a fine summer day.Mademoiselle, without hypocrisy: had I known your wealth I would have acted differently. Men confuse happiness with means; money becomes a religion. Faced with a prize, a young man may pursue it for social gain, flatter, lose esteem for you, and earn yours for himself. Poets complain where strong men forgive. Honesty compels me to say this.Your letter forced deep reflection—on you and on myself. You stirred many base thoughts, from which something generous emerged, and I salute you. I went to Havre, followed Francoise to Ingouville; you are as beautiful as a poet’s dream. I blush at such spying and stopped. You have altered my opinion: you are not merely a woman but a poetic Ideal. Exceptional souls may transcend the crowd’s laws. If your letter sprang from lofty reveries and the wish for friendship beyond ordinary relations, you are an exception; yet you may have done too much or not enough.Accept my thanks for forcing me to sound my heart.
