Contact with men who abandoned domestic virtues for cigars, whist, and clubs gradually undermined the young Breton’s domestic habits. A wife’s indulgence often supports such dissipation; she is proud to see him return after granting freedom.One October evening, to escape their newly weaned child’s cries, Calyste, urged by Sabine, went to the Variétés for a new play. During the first interlude he saw Madame de Rochefide in a proscenium box. Beatrix in Paris! The shock after nearly three years struck him like an arrow. She had not been forgotten; sometimes he had even substituted Beatrix for his wife, as Sabine had noticed. Beatrix represented light, life, the Unknown; Sabine embodied duty and the expected. It was a thunderbolt.Out of loyalty, Calyste first wanted to leave. He noticed the proscenium box door ajar and approached. He found Beatrix between Canalis and Raoul Nathan. In three years she had changed—gaunt, faded, eyes hollow, but heightened by Parisian artifice; more poetic, even more attractive to him.“Why isn’t Conti here?” Calyste asked Canalis softly.The former poet, now a minister, signaled silence.“I’m happy to see you,” Beatrix said demurely. “When I recognized you I thought you would not disown me. Ah! my Calyste, why did you marry—and with such a little fool!”A woman whispering to an old acquaintance and inviting him to sit often sends men away.“Come, Nathan,” Canalis said; “Madame la marquise will allow me to speak to d’Arthez.”Left alone, Beatrix poured her heart out: she had lost love and friendship, was “broken,” yet could still feel fidelity to a past. Calyste, unable to speak, shed tears; she took his hand and pressed it, making him pale.“Leave me now; people are watching; it might trouble your wife if someone tells her we were seen together—innocently,” she murmured, then pretended strength. She laughed and affected comic resignation at aging.“But where can I see you?” he asked.“In rue de Chartres opposite the Parc Monceaux, a little house; I cram my head with literature to distract myself,” she replied. “Now go—if you stay I shall burst into tears!”Calyste withdrew with the strange sensation of renewed temptation. He thought, “Sabine never knew how to stir my soul so.” The rest of the evening Beatrix sent him sidelong looks that tore his soul.At home the splendor of his apartments reminded him of the mediocrity Beatrix had mentioned; he hated his wealth because it could not belong to her. Learning Sabine was in bed, he rejoiced at a night to relive emotion. A wife who adores reads her husband like a book; she knows every quiver and questions every sadness. Calyste feared Sabine’s scrutiny.The next morning, Calyste found comfort in the child’s improved condition and left the house, fleeing breakfast as if escaping prison. What is love? Why does Nature rebel against social constraints? Calyste, raised sacredly, was drawn to a woman unworthy of him. Beatrix’s poetic airs masked courtesan instincts, yet she appealed to something dormant in him. Perhaps some buried vanity sought superiority over a fallen woman; perhaps he needed a nature unlike his own. He found nothing to challenge him in Sabine; his dormant powers vibrated for Beatrix.He lived on the thought, “I shall see her again!” and went to rue de Chartres. Though never there, he recognized the house. He waited on the stairs, feeling he should leave her freedom to receive him. The old footman’s question, “Monsieur’s name?” stopped him. He admired the little garden’s walls streaked by rain.Beatrix had left her fortune to her husband when she fled and could not beg from him. Conti and Mademoiselle des Touches had helped her; her mother sent sums. She now lived in a “little house” of a formerly great lord—eighteen hundred francs rent—served by an old footman, maid, and cook from Alençon: penury compared to her past, but luxury to many.Calyste ascended a well-pumiced staircase; silk and velvet furnished the rooms. A dim salon with garnet velvet walls and conservatory-like windows held celadon, a silver cup said to be by Benvenuto Cellini, gilded furniture, and ten watercolors of places where Beatrix had lived. On a table lay a manuscript half-written, a dagger handle used as a paper-cutter—symbolic!Her rustling dress announced her. Her gown, arranged to display her bosom, her cap, her elongated face: she smiled, “Already! A lover could not have shown more eagerness. You must have secrets to tell me.”She posed, inviting him to sit. By chance—or choice—Beatrix wore the scent from Les Touches. The perfume, the dress, her eyes in the dimness turned his head. He was again seized by an impulse that once nearly cost her life; but she rose to ring the bell and placed a finger on his lips to restore order.“Antoine, I am not at home—for anyone,” she said. “I treat you as a friend—do not treat me as a mistress. Two things: I would not deny myself foolishly to a man I truly loved; and I will belong to no other man on earth. You are under sacred duties: a young, amiable wife, a father. We would be two fools.”“My dear Beatrix, all these reasons vanish before a single word—I have never loved anyone but you, and I was married against my will.”“Ah! a trick by Mademoiselle des Touches,” she said with a smile.Three hours passed. Beatrix counselled conjugal faith and disparaged Sabine’s merits: “Your wife will never go wrong; she is a Grandlieu, but a brown, dry woman—Eve was fair; brown women descend from Adam, blondes from God,”—one of her petty jibes.About six, Calyste took his hat to leave.“Yes, go, my poor friend,” she said; “don’t give her the annoyance of dining without you.”He stayed. At his age, it was easy to ensnare him.“What! You dare to dine with me?” she feigned shock. “My poor food—have you enough independence to crown me by dining here?”“Let me write a note to Sabine; otherwise she will wait for dinner until nine.”“Here,” she said, “this is the table I write at.”She looked over what he wrote.“My dear Sabine—”“‘My dear’? Can you still call your wife ‘dear’?” she asked coldly. “Go—better dine with her.”“_I dine at a restaurant with some friends._”“A lie. Oh, fy! You are not worthy to be loved by her or me. Go, monsieur, go.”Calyste recoiled, then sat up magnificent and resolute. He glowed with Breton obstinacy; a Northern or Southern woman might have fallen at his feet, but Beatrix, hard and vindictive, belonged to a race of rancor. She wanted notoriety as revenge.“Dictate what I ought to write,” said Calyste.“You shall love me as at Guerande. Write: I dine out; do not expect me.”“Very well,” he answered.She seized the note, calling Antoine to send it. Calyste returned home about two a.m. Sabine, having waited until half past twelve, had gone to bed, exhausted and distressed by the terse note. She reasoned: true love explains everything in the man’s favor.Next morning the child was better; Sabine, smiling, brought him to his father before breakfast. Calyste behaved like a model husband; he overdid the tenderness. Yet subtle distinctions eluded Sabine.At breakfast she suddenly asked, “What did you do yesterday?”“Portenduere kept me to dinner,” he lied, “and after that we went to the club.”“That’s foolish, my Calyste,” she said, launching into a lecture on nobility’s duty and the loss of national ground—ideas that had expanded while nursing. “Admit the first note you wrote me was stiff.”“I didn’t think of writing until I got to the club.”“But you wrote on a woman’s note-paper; it had feminine perfume.”“The club directors are dandies.”Portenduere and his wife were intimate with the du Guenics; the vicomtesse and Sabine had become friends through their infants. While Calyste, novice in falsehood, thought, “I must warn Savinien,” Sabine resolved to find the paper she had thrown into her letter-box the night before.After breakfast, Calyste left, went to the vicomte’s to arrange Savinien’s fib, then urged his coachman to rue de Chartres to learn of Beatrix. He found her after her bath, fresh and merry, breakfasting heartily. He admired the grace with which she ate boiled eggs from gold service—gifts from a fool who had appropriated Conti’s ballads.He listened entranced until she wept as he left. He thought he had been there half an hour; it was past three when he returned. His English horse was drenched in sweat. By chance Sabine watched from a window; the horse’s condition alarmed her.“Where can he have come from?” she whispered, that premonition neither angel nor devil, but the mind’s intuition.On the landing she met him. “Abd-el-Kader is nearly foundered. You told me you would be gone but a moment; I have waited three hours.”“Dear little mother,” he soothed, taking her waist with more cajolery than before, “they are finishing a dressing-table for you, true artists have worked, and my mother and aunt have contributed.”Sabine clung to him, faint with joy—not for the furniture but for dispelling doubt. It was a sublime moment. Then, as if bitten by a viper, she snatched away, threw herself on a sofa, and fainted. The perfume on the paper had betrayed another woman’s presence; she had kissed where the rival’s kisses had been. She cried that her milk had turned and insisted he fetch the doctor and nurse.Calyste rushed out. Sabine, beside herself, rang for her maid and sent her to Madame de Portenduere. She felt her ideas whirl; she wrote a letter to you, dear mother, to thank you for the dressing-table—in a frenzy—and gave it to the footman.When the vicomtesse arrived, the chill of reaction had set in.“Where did Savinien and Calyste go after they dined with you yesterday?” Sabine asked.“Savinien and I dined alone and went to the Opera without Calyste,” Ursula replied.“Ursula, in the name of your love for Savinien, keep silence about what I shall tell you. You alone shall know why I die—I am betrayed at the end of three years, at twenty-two!”She shuddered, accusing fate. She held out the fatal letter. “See,” she said, “the coronet of a marquise! Find if Madame de Rochefide has returned to Paris. Am I to weep? My faith, my poetry, my idol, my virtue, my happiness—lost! No God! no love! no life!” At that thought tears flowed—tears she had not shed earlier.Ursula, first stunned, then struck by the truth of her friend's agony, resolved to save her. She went to the duchess, swore secrecy, and explained Sabine’s situation.“Madame,” she said, “do you not think we should inform the doctor and invent a tale to clear Calyste for the moment, to avoid sickness or madness?”“My dear child,” the duchess replied, chilled but prudent, “friendship gives you experience for now. I know how Sabine loves him; you are right—she might become insane.”“Or lose her beauty,” Ursula added.“Let us go to her!” cried the duchess.Fortunate for Sabine, they arrived before the great accoucheur Dommanget and before Calyste returned. The duchess had the servants ready; she caught Calyste and Dommanget in the salon.“Sabine’s life is at stake,” she told Calyste; “you have betrayed her to Madame de Rochefide.”Calyste blushed like a girl caught. The duchess explained that Sabine had guessed the truth. “You must save my daughter and forget Madame de Rochefide. Learn to lie; have the courage of a criminal. I have told a lie myself and will do penance for this mortal sin.”She then invented a lie: Calyste had lost a great deal of money at cards. Dommanget, sitting at Sabine’s bedside, used that lie to treat her symptoms. Calyste tried to show tenderness.“So it was play that put those black circles under your eyes?” Sabine asked weakly.The doctor, mother, and vicomtesse trembled and exchanged glances. Calyste turned red as a cherry. “That’s what comes of nursing a child,” Dommanget said, brusquely but cleverly. “Husbands are lonely and go to the club. But you needn’t worry about the thirty thousand francs monsieur lost last night.”“Thirty thousand francs!” Ursula gasped.“Yes,” Dommanget said. “They told me Monsieur de Trailles won that money.”Seeing the three respected people lie like shopkeepers to save Sabine, Calyste understood the danger; tears rolled down his face, deceiving her. She sat up, fierce. “Monsieur du Guenic can lose thirty, fifty, a hundred thousand francs if he pleases; no one has the right to lecture him.” Calyste embraced her and whispered, “Sabine, you are an angel!”Two days later she was thought out of danger. The next day Calyste was at Madame de Rochefide’s, making a merit of his infamy.“Beatrix,” he said, “you owe me happiness. I sacrificed my poor little wife to you; she discovered everything—your coronet on the note, the perfume, the lies. Sabine nearly died; her milk went to her head; erysipelas set in; she may bear the marks for life.”Beatrix listened, cold as the north wind. “So much the better,” she said; “perhaps it will whiten her for you.” Her voice was harsh; she answered with atrocious sarcasm.Calyste’s mistake was to speak of his virtuous wife to his mistress; he neither knew how to lie to his wife nor how to tell his mistress the truth—two arts the world teaches. He begged Beatrix for pardon for two hours; she refused, raised her eyes to the ceiling to avoid seeing him, and in a trembling voice recited reasons sacred to marquises, wiping furtive tears.“To speak to me of your wife on the day after my fall!” she cried. “Why not tell me she is a pearl of virtue? I know she thinks you handsome; pure depravity! I love your soul! You are ugly compared to many shepherds on the Campagna.” Such speeches fit into Beatrix’s system: at each passion she became another being, advancing into profligacy. Clever women know themselves; they count wrinkles, preserve themselves, and use every artifice to triumph. Fallen by Conti’s desertion, Beatrix resolved at least to win fame that unprincipled conduct can bring. She would make the misfortune of the rich and beautiful Sabine her pedestal.To conquer Calyste she adopted contempt as a moral lever, constantly comparing her poetic, comfortable home to the hotel du Guenic and attacking the Saint-Germain luxury as stupid. She sought to make him feel he had conquered a repugnance—flattery in the guise of dislike—which appeals to conquering natures. In the perfect bower of her rare plants and ravishing flowers she made him swear hatred of his wife, persuaded him to renounce Sabine, and displayed the trivial sciences of luxury to captivate him. Thus, having been mortified, she aimed at a new triumph: to be the celebrated victor over a noble and charming young wife.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When a woman returns to ordinary life after nursing her first child, she often seems rejuvenated and charming, bringing freshness to other young mothers. But when Sabine comes back to her husband Calyste, she discovers he is not the man she married. Instead of security she feels anxiety and craves certainty about their relationship. That certainty arrives in a letter from her mother hinting at Calyste's discontent and the presence of another woman, Béatrix.Sabine's heart sinks reading the vague note, which reveals nothing of Calyste's feelings. In despair she writes a comment on the letter to signal her awareness and leaves it where Calyste will find it. He sees the note but ignores it, throwing the paper into the fire. Sabine is left agonizing, convinced she has displeased and humiliated her idol.As days pass, her torment deepens. Béatrix, emboldened, writes to Calyste; Sabine, recognizing the perfume, realizes how extensive her rival's influence is. In a moment of madness she dances with her child, seeking solace in motherhood's innocence, yet her heart remains heavy and she weeps alone when Calyste departs to read Béatrix's message.Her suffering grows with each attempt to win Calyste's affection. She decorates their home with flowers to please him, but his indifference increases her despair. Obsessed with understanding his feelings and comparing herself to Béatrix, she is trapped by jealousy and self-doubt. Efforts to charm him with cooking and appearance prove futile as Calyste stays unresponsive.Despite her struggles, Sabine's love persists. She tries to embody devoted-wifely virtues, but these efforts bring disappointment. One evening, after another failure to move him, she succumbs to despair and tears off her beautiful dress in frustration.Over time Sabine's health declines under the weight of grief. The duchess, her mother, notices the deterioration and attempts to draw her away from the source of suffering. Sabine remains silent, fearing interference in her marriage. Eventually she confides in her sister, asking that their child be cared for should she die, and warns Athenais about love's dangers and the need for caution in marriage. She reflects that her virtues have become obstacles to happiness: her straightforwardness and passion have not won Calyste, and she feels pushed toward the duplicity she believes necessary to retain him.Concerned, the duchess seeks counsel from Abbé Brossette, a respected priest, revealing a plan to intervene in Calyste's attachment to Béatrix and restore her daughter's happiness. The abbé cautions against mixing spiritual authority with worldly intrigue but recognizes Sabine's plight. The duchess proposes a scheme to persuade Béatrix to return to her husband, thereby freeing Calyste. Contemplating his advice, the duchess becomes determined to act for her daughter's sake, even if it means engaging in morally questionable measures, resolved to restore peace to the family and spare Sabine further suffering.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A storm was brewing for Monsieur de Rochefide, who enjoyed Parisian life as fully as he had with Beatrix and thought such a satisfactory existence nearly impossible to disrupt. To see how society treats men and women differently in similar situations, we follow Rochefide after Beatrix left—a tale that might encourage women to stay home and defend their dignity, as Sabine du Guenic did.After Beatrix's departure, Arthur de Rochefide, now an only child after his sister’s death, became sole owner of the hôtel de Rochefide and inherited a large fortune. His income reached a thousand francs a day, ample for his happiness. While Beatrix fulfilled maternal duties, Rochefide enjoyed his wealth without extravagance. His vanity, aided by good looks, let him dismiss women and appropriate others' wit. His military background lent a jaunty tone to his conversation, making him seem witty to acquaintances.As a husband he was pitied; society blamed Beatrix for leaving such a “good” man. He joined clubs and championed fashionable causes to distinguish himself. His main indulgence was racehorses, run by an English groom, costing him a sizable monthly sum. Despite wealth, he lacked taste and intellect, relying largely on inherited fortune. After the downsides of marriage he welcomed bachelorhood, claiming he was "born with a caul," destined for luck.Rochefide's indifference to domestic life was clear: he rarely dined or slept at home. After several affairs he married Madame Schontz, a woman of dubious reputation known in circles around Fanny Beaupré and others—an environment where many women ended in poverty or early death and where subtle social skill was essential. Madame Schontz, originally La Petite-Aurélie, emerged from a difficult upbringing, orphaned and educated at Saint-Denis, a school that schooled girls but offered few prospects.Aurelie, daughter of Colonel Schiltz, led a tumultuous life of various relationships, which brought her by chance to Rochefide at Valentino’s. Captivated by her charm, Rochefide married her two years after Beatrix left, and society did not condemn him for the choice.Their relationship unfolded in four phases. First, courtship: he provided a modest allowance and a furnished apartment. He discovered her noble charm and admired talents, though ambition soon surfaced and she sought a more comfortable life. Second, she moved to a better apartment; Rochefide lavished gifts and support while she ran the household efficiently, impressing him with budgeting and superior dinners—convincing him she loved him for himself by rebuffing wealthier suitors.Third, she became mother figure to Rochefide's son, overseeing his education and finances and advising Rochefide on investments that yielded significant gains she did not claim. Taking on responsibilities, she managed affairs and ensured his comfort. Fourth, she established herself socially, curating guests and preserving reputation, accumulating a small fortune through savings, investments, and selling jewelry while downplaying her wealth.Through these phases Madame Schontz demonstrated tactical superiority, transforming the relationship into mutual benefit while skillfully navigating their social world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous of Madame du Bruel for two years, she was determined to marry in both church and civil ceremonies and thus needed a second Arthur. Rochefide’s secret rival remained unknown; Bixiou suspected Leon de Lora, the painter thought Bixiou himself was favored, and Victor de Vernisset and Stidmann were also considered. None truly understood Madame Schontz, whose sole aim was securing a good marriage.The only man of questionable reputation in her salon was Couture, a stock-gambler who had fallen from grace but remained loyal. When he introduced her to a provincial, Fabien du Ronceret, Madame Schontz began to take him seriously. Fabien, son of a chief justice, had left a judgeship in Alençon for Paris with a modest income and the ambition to exploit changing social mores.The modern era encouraged pride, vanity, and the desire to be talked about; Fabien, not particularly clever, seized on this and aimed to make a name by ostentation. His Paris debut was theatrical: a lavish apartment and a greenhouse meant to dazzle. He sought association with influential figures and entry into Madame Schontz’s circle. After failed attempts through established acquaintances, he turned to Couture, who recognized Fabien’s potential and recommended him.Madame Schontz quickly assessed Fabien as suitable for her ambitions. She flattered him until he supposed he was the object of her affection. Eager to impress, he presented himself in his finest attire, captivated by her charm. A month before a significant meeting, Madame Schontz disclosed her identity and aims to Fabien, who misunderstood her intentions. Realizing his mistake, she took him for a carriage ride and spoke plainly of wanting a man of ambition who could appreciate her character. Despite her wealth and encouragement to rise, Fabien hesitated at marriage, and she then turned to Couture, who promptly proposed upon learning of her fortune.Meanwhile the Duchesse de Grandlieu sought the Comte Maxime de Trailles’s help with a delicate family matter. Maxime, a seasoned political operator, read the duchess’s motives and agreed to assist only on terms, including her acceptance of his future wife into her social circle. His plan required removing a rival, and he demanded the duchess follow his instructions closely. He acknowledged the emotional entanglements—especially Calyste’s deep infatuation with Béatrix—and the difficulty of manipulating such feelings, revealing the moral ambiguity of his methods.The duchess agreed to support Maxime’s wife, and he assured her he would handle the intricacies, hinting at the lengths people would go to secure desires at others’ expense. The next day Rochefide informed Madame Schontz of Maxime’s impending visit, prompting her to ready her house and appearance to impress him. Maxime’s reputation as a powerful figure in Parisian society preceded him, and his presence posed both a privilege and a challenge for Madame Schontz as she navigated her ambitions and relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot to arrange a breakfast at the Café Anglais with Couture and Lousteau, where they discussed Madame Schontz. Maxime then loitered on Tortoni’s portico under the pretense of transacting with du Tillet while waiting for the Comte de la Palferine. When La Palferine arrived, Maxime questioned him about his debts; the Comte admitted his straits, and Maxime, admiring his spirit, offered to fund a courtship with a loan of twenty thousand francs.Maxime’s scheme targeted a well-bred marquise; he urged La Palferine to woo her sincerely, and they agreed to meet later at Madame Schontz’s. Meanwhile Maxime visited the duchess, who fretted over her daughter Sabine’s distress at Calyste’s absence. He asked the duchess to keep his plans secret and requested twenty thousand francs for his maneuver, assuring her it would not be costly.Madame Schontz prepared a dinner, inviting notable guests including Claude Vignon and La Palferine; she dressed carefully to impress. Maxime praised her success with Rochefide, noting she had cost him less than other conquests. Madame Schontz insisted she was an artist rather than a courtesan and expressed a desire for a respectable marriage.At dinner Maxime gave La Palferine the promised funds and detailed instructions for making a striking impression on the marquise, emphasizing the need for an unforgettable display. As the evening continued, Maxime and Madame Schontz plotted to manipulate Rochefide’s affections; Maxime vowed to secure a position for Couture in return for his cooperation.The next day Calyste, feeling neglected, confronted Beatrix, who was torn between genuine feeling for him and a growing attraction to La Palferine. Their relationship strained as Beatrix wrestled with guilt and desire, fearful of consequences. La Palferine’s charm increasingly drew her, making her question loyalty to Calyste.Maxime intensified his scheming, arranging encounters between Beatrix and La Palferine that further complicated matters. Calyste, increasingly desperate, tried to reclaim her affection, but she remained entangled by La Palferine’s allure. The tension peaked with a public humiliation for Beatrix at the opera, where she was left exposed and alone.Afterward Beatrix’s attachment to Calyste waned as she sank deeper into La Palferine’s orbit. Maxime, observing the drama, perceived shifting loyalties and emotional turmoil and counseled Calyste to reconsider his tactics, suggesting the pain might not be worth persisting.The narrative sketches conflicting desires and ambitions, portraying love, betrayal, and the complexity of human relations, where ambition and affection yield disillusionment and force characters to question past choices and future paths.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################If the French have a strong aversion to traveling, the English have a propensity for it; both nations have reasons. Other countries may offer pleasant scenery and comfort, but the unique charms of France—its wit, conversation, and unspoken understanding—are hard to replicate. Many Frenchmen therefore take pleasure in encountering familiar ways abroad, comforts reminiscent of Paris.In 1836, during the Sardinian Court’s stay in Genoa, two notable Parisians found themselves in a palazzo, a remnant of Genoese nobility. Genoa’s evenings, especially after rain, create a serene atmosphere suited to friends and Italian cuisine. At a dinner hosted by the French Consul-General were the celebrated Mademoiselle des Touches, known as Camille Maupin, and her companions: the painter Léon de Lora and the critic Claude Vignon.Camille had gone to Florence on business and brought Léon to see Italy. Their stop in Genoa was a courtesy to the Consul-General, who wished to honor her presence. Despite her fame, Camille avoided formal engagement with the ambassador and preferred to enjoy the evening without pressure. The dinner was lively, conversation flowing, though literature was seldom mentioned until the Consul-General shared a personal story to change the mood.The Consul-General, thirty-four, bore a striking resemblance to Lord Byron, with a dreamy expression that charmed many. He had married Onorina Pedrotti, the only child of a wealthy banker, after a period of reluctance; their union had been influenced by the ambassador’s intervention and by the Consul’s own hidden struggles. Onorina, a beautiful Genoese woman, embodied her heritage’s elegance. Their family life seemed harmonious, though Camille perceived an underlying melancholy in the Consul.The evening’s debate turned to women’s morality and accountability in cases of infidelity. The three women present judged the woman harshly, while the men argued for a more nuanced view. To shift the mood, the Consul-General recounted a pivotal moment from his youth.At twenty-two, his uncle, the Abbé Loraux, arranged for him to become secretary to Comte Octave, a prominent statesman. The uncle’s influence secured a position promising a bright future, despite the young man’s initial innocence and naivety, sheltered from society’s complexities.As he settled into his role, he observed the Count’s character and burdens. Outwardly composed and pious, the Count harbored a profound sadness the Consul gradually understood. Their relationship deepened: the Count treated him with kindness and respect, often blurring lines between master and mentor. The Consul was struck by the Count’s intellect, devotion to duty, and the contrast between public success and private sorrow.The Count’s meticulous nature and love for flowers revealed a tenderness that mirrored his own faded beauty. His life was marked by relentless duty and a search for a happiness he could not attain; outward devotion to work and religion masked an ongoing yearning for inner peace. This tension between public expectation and private despair shaped their relationship and the Consul’s understanding of human complexity.The Consul-General’s story, rich in feeling, captivated his audience, leaving the guests to ponder the depths of the Count’s character and the struggles beneath seemingly perfect lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“On a certain Monday,” he resumed, “M. le Président de Grandville and M. de Serizy (then Vice‑President of the Council of State) met at Comte Octave’s house. They formed a committee of three with me as secretary. The Count had secured my appointment as Auditor to the Council of State. All the necessary documents for their inquiry lay in the library; MM. de Grandville and de Serizy relied on the Count for the preliminary examination. To avoid carrying all the papers to M. de Serizy, they agreed to meet first in the Rue Payenne. The Tuileries valued this work, and it led to my appointment as Master of Appeals that year.“Though the Comtes de Grandville and de Serizy, who rarely dined out, were still discussing late into the evening, a servant interrupted me: ‘MM. the Cures of Saint‑Paul and of the White Friars have been waiting in the drawing‑room for two hours.’“It was nine o’clock.“‘Well, gentlemen, you find yourselves compelled to dine with priests,’ said Comte Octave. ‘I do not know whether Grandville can overcome his horror of a priest’s gown—’“‘It depends on the priest.’“‘One of them is my uncle, and the other is the Abbé Gaudron,’ I said. ‘Do not be alarmed; the Abbé Fontanon is no longer second priest at Saint‑Paul.’“‘Well, let us dine,’ replied the Président de Grandville. ‘A bigot frightens me, but there is no one so cheerful as a truly pious man.’“We entered the drawing‑room. The dinner was delightful. Politicians with real knowledge make excellent storytellers; my uncle’s wit was refined and elegant, characteristic of those who hide thought under a black robe. There was nothing vulgar in this light talk; I compared it to Rossini’s music.“The Abbé Gaudron, as M. de Grandville observed, resembled more a Saint Peter than a Saint Paul: a peasant full of faith, whose ignorance of worldly matters enlivened the talk with guileless amazement. They began discussing a social issue—adultery. My uncle pointed out the contradiction between civil and religious law left after the Revolution.“‘In the eyes of the Church,’ he said, ‘adultery is a crime; in your tribunals, it is a misdemeanor. Adultery drives to the police court in a carriage instead of standing at the bar. Napoleon’s Council of State, touched with tenderness towards erring women, was quite inefficient. Should they not have harmonized civil and religious law and sent the guilty wife to a convent, as of old?’“‘To a convent!’ said M. de Serizy. ‘They must first have created convents; monasteries were being turned into barracks. Besides, think of what you say, M. l’Abbé—give to God what society would have none of?’“‘Oh!’ said Comte de Grandville, ‘you do not know France. They had to leave the husband free to take proceedings; well, there are not ten cases of adultery brought up in a year.’“‘M. l’Abbé preaches for his own saint,’ said Comte Octave; ‘in the East, woman was merely a luxury, regarded as a chattel; no virtues were demanded of her but obedience and beauty. By exalting the soul above the body, modern Europe—daughter of Christ—invented indissoluble marriage and made it a sacrament.’“‘Ah! the Church saw the difficulties,’ exclaimed M. de Grandville.“‘This institution has given rise to a new world,’ the Count continued with a smile. ‘But its practices will never match a climate where women marry at seven and are old at twenty‑five. The Catholic Church overlooked half the globe. Let us discuss Europe only.“‘Is woman our superior or inferior? That is the real question. If woman is inferior, placing her on such a high level as the Church does required severe punishments for adultery. Formerly, cloister or death summed up legislation. But practice has since modified the law. The throne served as a hotbed for adultery, and the growth of this inviting crime marks the decline of Catholic dogmas. Nowadays, where the Church demands only sincere repentance from the erring wife, society is satisfied with a brand instead of execution. The law still condemns the guilty, but it no longer terrifies. In short, there are two moral standards: that of the world and that of the Code. Where the Code is weak, the world is audacious and satirical. Few judges would not gladly have committed the faults they condemn. The world punishes a blunder after encouraging hypocrisy. The entire marriage law seems to require reconstruction. The French law would be perfect if it excluded daughters from inheriting.’“‘We three know the question thoroughly,’ said Comte de Grandville with a laugh. ‘I have a wife I cannot live with. Serizy has a wife who will not live with him. As for you, Octave, yours ran away from you. So we represent every case of the conjugal conscience, and if divorce is ever brought in again, we shall form the committee.’“Octave’s fork dropped, breaking his glass and plate. He turned pale and shot a thunderous glare at M. de Grandville, hinting at my presence.“‘Forgive me, my dear fellow. I did not see Maurice,’ the President continued. ‘Serizy and I, after witnessing your marriage, became your accomplices; I did not think I was committing an indiscretion in front of these venerable priests.’“M. de Serizy changed the subject, recounting his efforts to please his wife without success. The old man concluded that human sympathies and antipathies cannot be regulated; social law is most perfect when closest to natural law. Nature does not consider the affinities of souls; her aim is the propagation of the species. Thus, the Code, in its current form, wisely leaves latitude to chance. The incapacity of daughters to inherit while male heirs exist is an excellent provision, whether to prevent degeneration or to make households happier by abolishing scandalous unions.“‘But then,’ he exclaimed, lifting his hand in disgust, ‘how are we to perfect legislation in a country that insists on bringing together seven or eight hundred legislators! After all, if I am sacrificed,’ he added, ‘I have a child to succeed me.’“‘Setting aside the religious question,’ my uncle remarked, ‘Nature only owes us life; society owes us happiness. Are you a father?’ he asked.“‘And I—do I have any children?’ said Comte Octave in a hollow voice, making such an impression that the topic of wives and marriage was dropped.“When coffee was served, the two Counts and the two priests quietly left, noticing that poor Octave had fallen into a melancholy fit, oblivious to their departure. My patron sat in an armchair by the fire, crushed.“‘You now know the secret of my life,’ he said to me when we were alone. ‘After three years of marriage, I came home one evening to find a letter from the Countess announcing her flight. The letter was dignified; women often retain some virtues even when committing such a sin. The story now is that my wife went abroad on a ship that was wrecked; she is presumed dead. I have lived alone for seven years! Enough for tonight, Maurice. We will discuss my situation when I am ready to speak of it. When suffering from a chronic disease, it takes time to adjust to the idea of improvement. That improvement often seems merely another aspect of the complaint.’“I went to bed greatly agitated; the mystery seemed more obscure than ever. I anticipated a strange drama, realizing that the events leading the Countess to leave such a noble and loving man must have been extraordinary. M. de Grandville’s remark had illuminated the caverns I had been navigating, and though the light was dim, I could perceive their vastness. I could imagine the Count’s suffering without grasping its depths. His sallow face, parched temples, overwhelming studies, and moments of absentmindedness stood out vividly during the twilight of mental questioning before sleep.“Oh! how I loved my poor master! He seemed sublime. I read a poem of melancholy, seeing perpetual activity in the heart I had accused of being torpid. Must not supreme grief eventually lead to stagnation? Had this judge, with so much power, ever sought revenge? Was he feeding on her long agony? Is it not remarkable to harbor anger for ten years in Paris? What had Octave done since this great misfortune—when the separation of husband and wife has become a social question?“We allowed a few days to pass, for great sorrows have their own diffidence; but eventually, one evening, the Count said gravely:“‘Stay.’############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“This, as nearly as may be, is his story.“My father had a ward, rich and lovely, who was sixteen when I returned from college to live in this old house. Honorine, raised by my mother, was just awakening to life. Full of grace and childish ways, she dreamed of happiness as one dreams of jewels; even religion was poetry to her innocent heart. She viewed the future as a perpetual fête, awaiting marriage without desiring it, her imagination untainted by corruption. We lived together like siblings.“After a year, I asked her one day in the garden, by the basin where we fed the fish, if she would like to marry me. She told my mother, who insisted we wait eighteen months to see if our feelings remained. When I was twenty-six and Honorine nineteen, we married. Out of respect for my parents we did not modernize the house; I introduced my wife to society as a duty to guide her.“I later realized marriages like ours often harbor hidden dangers. The husband becomes a teacher, and love can wither under such authority. I trusted her innocence too much. Who can say if empires and happiness are destroyed by too much confidence or too much severity? I recall the self-reproaches I made, like an anatomist seeking a disease’s cause. His merciful indulgence reminded me of Christ’s compassion for the woman taken in adultery.“It was eighteen months after my father’s death, shortly after my mother passed, that I received Honorine’s farewell letter. What delusion had led her to leave? I was stunned for a month, then chose ignorance, learning too much about such matters through her misfortunes. So far, Maurice, the story is commonplace, but one word changes everything: I love Honorine, and I have never ceased to worship her. Since her departure, I have lived on memories, recalling pleasures she may not have appreciated.“Do not make a hero of me; I sought no diversion. I was either too young or too in love; I have not met another woman like her. After struggling to forget, I stood on the brink of infidelity, but Honorine’s memory stopped me. I fled temptation, haunted by thoughts of her. My life is a constant paroxysm of fears, joy, and despair.“I felt no anger towards her, like a father seeing a beloved child in danger. I understood I had idealized my wife, believing she shared my intoxication. An uncritical passion in a husband can lead to a wife’s infidelity. I may have exhausted her with my love before she was ready. Too young to understand that a wife’s constancy is the seed of maternal devotion, she mistook our early marriage for life itself. In her cruel position, she was defenseless against any man who stirred her deeply. I, who should have been wise, failed to grasp the unwritten laws of a woman’s heart until it was too late. I absolved her and condemned myself. Yet love held me like a desperate passion.“Society laughs at such a predicament. Where it pities a lover, it mocks a husband who cannot keep his wife. I had to remain silent. Serizy is happy; he can see and protect his wife, enjoying the joys of a benefactor. But I have nothing; I live solely on a love that starves. I loathe prostitution and remain faithful under a spell. Without religious faith, I might have killed myself. I threw myself into work, fevered and sleepless.“You can imagine my feelings when he concluded with this revelation: the interior drama interests nobody; one can only know another’s sorrow vaguely. I calm despair by contemplating a miniature of her, kissing her brow, breathing her fragrance. I have fits of collapse, wild hilarity, and terrors like a murderer meeting a sergeant of police.“The real drama is this: you think I am occupied with the Council of State, the Chamber, the Courts, Politics. Seven hours at night suffice for that. Honorine is my true concern. To recover my wife is my only study; to guard her without her suspecting my presence, to satisfy the little pleasures she allows herself, to be always near her like a sylph without her knowing it—that is my life. For seven years, I have never gone to bed without checking on her night-lamp or her shadow on the curtains.“She left my house, taking only the dress she wore that day. Eighteen months later she was deserted by her lover, who was appalled by poverty. Honorine has sixty thousand francs a year of her own. In November 1820, I persuaded the best accoucheur to act as a humble suburban apothecary and induced the parish priest to assist her as an act of charity. Then, to hide my wife, secure her against discovery, and find a devoted housekeeper who would be my confidante—it was a task worthy of Figaro! Finding where my wife had taken refuge was not difficult for me.“After three months of desperation, I devoted myself to Honorine, with God as my only witness. Love must have its daily food. Should I not protect this child, whose guilt sprang from my imprudence, against further disaster? At seven months, her infant died, happily for her and for me. For nine months she lay between life and death, deserted when she needed a man’s support. But this arm was extended over her head. Honorine was cared for as she would have been in her home. When she recovered and asked who had assisted her, she was told it was the Sisters of Charity, the Maternity Society, and the parish priest.“This woman, whose pride is almost a vice, has shown resistance in misfortune that I sometimes call obstinacy. Honorine was determined to earn her living. For five years, I have lodged her in the Rue Saint-Maur, in a charming little house where she makes artificial flowers. She believes she sells her elegant creations to a shop, earning twenty francs a day, living as if she had fifteen thousand. She is devoted to flowers, paying a gardener a hundred crowns, which costs me twelve hundred in wages. I promised the gardener a market-garden with a house close to the porter’s lodge in the Rue Saint-Maur, held in the name of a clerk of the law courts. The smallest indiscretion would ruin the gardener’s prospects. Honorine has her little house, a garden, and a splendid hothouse for a rent of five hundred francs a year. She lives under the name of her housekeeper, Madame Gobain, an old woman of impeccable discretion whose affection Honorine has won. For three years, Honorine has been happy, believing she owes her comfort to her own toil.“Oh! I know what you are about to say,” cried the Count, seeing a question in my eyes. “Yes, I have made the attempt. My wife was living in the Faubourg Saint-Antoine. One day, believing in a chance for reconciliation, I wrote her a letter, which I rewrote twenty times. I will not describe my agonies. I went from the Rue Payenne to the Rue de Reuilly like a condemned wretch going to execution. It was dark and foggy; I went to meet Madame Gobain. Honorine, recognizing my handwriting, threw the letter into the fire without reading it, exclaiming, ‘I leave this tomorrow.’“What a dagger-stroke to a man who takes pleasure in trickery! I had just succeeded in deceiving my wife—sending her an Indian shawl, presented as the property of an actress, which I had wrapped myself in for a night! My life may be summed up in two words: I love, and I wait! I have in Madame Gobain a faithful spy on the heart I worship. I visit the old woman every evening to hear about Honorine’s day; for a single exclamation might reveal the secrets of that soul which is willfully deaf and dumb. Honorine is pious; she attends church and prays, but she has never confessed or taken Communion, foreseeing what a priest would say. She will not heed advice to return to me. This horror of me overwhelms me, for I have never harmed her. I have always been kind. Even if I was hasty in teaching her, is that a reason for her determination, which only the most implacable hatred could inspire? Honorine has never told Madame Gobain who she is; she keeps absolute silence about her marriage, so the housekeeper cannot speak in my favor. The others know nothing; they live in awe of the Prefect of Police and respect the power of a Minister. Hence, it is impossible for me to penetrate her heart; the citadel is mine, but I cannot enter it. I have no means of action. An act of violence would ruin me forever.“How can I argue against reasons of which I know nothing? Should I write a letter, have it copied by a public writer, and laid before Honorine? But that risks a third removal, which cost me fifty thousand francs. The last purchase was made in the name of the secretary who succeeded you. The unfortunate man, unaware of my vigilance, was caught opening a box containing a private agreement. I coughed, and he panicked; the next day, I forced him to sell the house to the man in whose name it now stands, and I turned him out.“If I did not feel my noblest faculties satisfied, happy, and expansive; if I were not playing the role of divine fatherhood; if I did not drink in delight by every pore, I might believe I was a monomaniac. Sometimes at night, I hear the jingling bells of madness. I dread the violent transitions from feeble hope to complete despair. Recently, I considered the tragic end of Lovelace and Clarissa Harlowe, thinking that if Honorine were the mother of my child, she must return under her husband’s roof.“I have such complete faith in a happy future that ten months ago, I bought one of the finest houses in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré. If I win back Honorine, I will not allow her to see this house again, nor the room from which she fled. I intend to place my idol in a new temple, where she may feel that life is altogether new. That house is being transformed into a marvel of elegance and taste. I was inspired by a poet who, mad with love for an actress, bought the finest bed in Paris without knowing how she would reward his passion. Well, one of the coldest lawyers, thought to be the gravest adviser of the Crown, was stirred to the depths of his heart by that anecdote. The orator of the Legislative Chamber can understand the poet who fed his ideal on material possibilities. Three days before Maria Louisa’s arrival, Napoleon threw himself on his wedding bed at Compiègne. All great passions share the same impulses. I love as a poet—like an emperor!”As I heard the last words, I believed Count Octave’s fears were realized; he rose, walking and gesticulating, but stopped, shocked by the vehemence of his own words.“I am very ridiculous,” he added after a long pause, looking at me, as if craving pity.“No, monsieur, you are very unhappy.”“Ah yes!” he said, resuming his confidences. “From the violence of my speech, you must believe in the intensity of a physical passion that has absorbed me for nine years; but that is nothing compared to the worship I feel for her soul, mind, and heart—all the divinities of Love. By retrospection, I now see the graces of Honorine’s mind and heart I overlooked in happiness—like all who are happy. Day by day, I appreciate my loss, discovering the exquisite gifts of that capricious and proud young creature who has grown strong under poverty and the cowardly shock of desertion. And that heavenly blossom is fading in solitude and hiding! The law we discussed is a squad of gendarmes—my wife seized and dragged away by force! Would that not be to triumph over a corpse? Religion has no hold on her; she craves its poetry, prays, but does not heed the Church’s commandments. I have exhausted all mercy, kindness, and love; I am at my wits’ end. Only one chance of victory remains: the cunning and patience of bird-catchers who finally trap the wariest birds. Hence, Maurice, when M. de Grandville’s indiscretion revealed my life’s secret to you, I regarded it as fate’s decree, one of the utterances gamblers listen for in their most impassioned play. Have you enough affection for me to show me romantic devotion?”“I see what you are coming to, Monsieur le Comte,” I interrupted. “Your first secretary tried to open your deed box. I know your second’s heart—he might fall in love with your wife. Can you send him into the fire? Can anyone put their hand into a brazier without burning it?”“You are a foolish boy,” replied the Count. “I will send you well-gloved. It is not my secretary who will be lodged in the Rue Saint-Maur in the little garden-house I have at his disposal. It is my distant cousin, Baron de l’Hostal, a lawyer high in office…”After a moment of silent surprise, the gate bell rang and a carriage entered. The footman announced Madame de Courteville and her daughter. The Count had family connections on his mother’s side. Madame de Courteville, a widow, had a daughter and no fortune. What could a woman of nine-and-twenty be compared to a lovely young girl of twenty, ideal for a mistress?“Baron, and Master of Appeals, until you get something better, and this old house settled on her—would you not have enough reasons not to fall in love with the Countess?” he whispered as he introduced us.I was dazzled, not so much by advantages I had never dreamed of, but by Amelie de Courteville, whose beauty was adorned by a mother’s artful clothing.“But I will not talk of myself,” said the Consul after a pause.Three weeks later, I moved into the gardener’s cottage, cleaned, repaired, and furnished with the celerity explained by three words: Paris; French workmen; money! I was as much in love as the Count could desire as security. Would the prudence of a young man of five-and-twenty be equal to the part I was undertaking, involving a friend’s happiness? I confess I counted on my uncle’s advice; for I had been authorized by the Count to confide in him if necessary. I engaged a garden, devoted myself to horticulture, and worked frantically, turning up the soil and appropriating it to flower cultivation. Like the maniacs of England or Holland, I claimed devotion to one kind of flower, especially dahlias. My conduct, even in small details, was dictated by the Count, whose intellect focused on the tragi-comedy enacted in the Rue Saint-Maur. As soon as the Countess went to bed, around eleven, Octave, Madame Gobain, and I held council. I heard the old woman’s report on Honorine’s daily activities, meals, frame of mind, and plans. Octave lived only for that hour.For two months, while I worked in the garden, I never saw the little house where my fair neighbor lived. I had not even inquired whether I had a neighbor, though the Countess’ garden was divided from mine by a paling, along which she had planted cypress trees already four feet high. One morning, Madame Gobain announced that an eccentric neighbor intended to build a wall between the two gardens at the year’s end. I will say nothing of the curiosity that consumed me to see the Countess! My wish almost extinguished my budding love for Amelie de Courteville. My scheme for building a wall was indeed a dangerous threat: no fresh air for Honorine, whose garden would then be a narrow alley. This dwelling, once a summer villa, was like a house of cards; the garden front imitated a trellis with flowers up to the second floor, a charming Pompadour style. My wall would cut away three-quarters of the plot.“The Countess was in despair.“‘My good Gobain,’ she said, ‘what sort of man is this florist?’“‘He seems to dislike women,’ said the housekeeper. ‘He is the nephew of a Paris cure. It is quite possible that this priest encourages his nephew in his love of flowers, to prevent anything worse…’“‘What do you mean?’“‘Well,’ said Gobain, ‘he is a little cracked!’“Now a harmless lunatic is the only man no woman ever distrusts in matters of sentiment. You will see how wise the Count was in choosing this disguise for me.“‘What ails him then?’ asked the Countess.“‘He has studied too hard,’ replied Gobain; ‘he is misanthropic and has his reasons for disliking women…’“‘Madmen frighten me less than sane folks; I will speak to him myself! Tell him that I beg him to come here. If I do not succeed, I will send for the cure.’“The day after this conversation, as I walked along my graveled path, I caught sight of the half-opened curtains and a woman’s face peering out. Madame Gobain called me. I hastily glanced at the Countess’ house, shrugging rudely to express indifference.“‘Madame,’ said Gobain, ‘the madman bid me leave him in peace, saying that even a charcoal seller is master in his own premises, especially when he has no wife.’“‘He is perfectly right,’ said the Countess.“‘Yes, but he ended by saying, “I will go,” when I told him that he would greatly distress a lady living in retirement, who found her greatest solace in growing flowers.’“Next day, a signal from Gobain informed me that I was expected. After the Countess’ breakfast, when she was walking in front of her house, I broke some palings and approached her. I had dressed like a countryman, in old gray flannel trousers, heavy wooden shoes, a shabby shooting coat, a peaked cap, a ragged bandana, and hands soiled with mold.“‘Madame,’ said the housekeeper, ‘this good man is your neighbor.’“The Countess was not alarmed. At last, I saw the woman whose conduct and my husband’s confidences had made me so curious to meet. It was early May. The air was pure, the weather serene; the fragrance of spring formed a setting for this creature of sorrow. As I saw Honorine, I understood Octave’s passion: ‘A heavenly flower!’“Her pallor struck me with its unique tone of white—there are as many tones of white as of red or blue. At the slightest emotion, blood mounted under the surface in rosy flushes. The sunshine through the acacia cast a pale gold glow around Honorine, like the ambient glory Raphael and Titian gave the Virgin. Her brown eyes expressed tenderness and vivacity, their brightness reflected through long downcast lashes. Merely lifting her eyelids, Honorine could cast a spell; she could freeze or give life with a look. Her light-brown hair framed a high, dreamy brow. The mouth was voluptuous. Her head bore a stamp of nobility that would defy the outrages of time.“Though slight, Honorine was not thin; her figure could revive love when it believed itself exhausted. She embodied the idea of mignonne, one of those pliant women who allow themselves to be petted. Her small feet made a light sound on the gravel, harmonizing with the rustle of her dress, producing a feminine music distinct from others. Her gait bore the pride of her race; even a working man would make way. Gay and tender, haughty and imposing, she combined incompatible qualities yet remained a child. But this child could be as strong as an angel; once hurt, she would be implacable.“Coldness on that face must be death to those on whom her eyes had smiled, for whom her lips had parted, whose souls had drunk the melody of her voice. Inhaling the perfume of violets that accompanied her, I understood how the memory of this wife had arrested the Count on the threshold of debauchery. Honorine inspired devotion, chivalrous devotion, regardless of reward. A man seeing her must think of offering his life to procure one day’s happiness for her.“Many women study their expressions; in her it was the outcome of a delightful nature that went straight to the heart. If I tell you all this, it is because her soul, thoughts, and exquisite heart are what we are concerned with, and you would have blamed me had I not sketched them for you.“I was near forgetting my part as a half-crazy lout, clumsy and by no means chivalrous.“‘I am told, madame, that you are fond of flowers?’“‘I am an artificial flower-maker,’ she said. ‘After growing flowers, I imitate them, like a mother painting her children… That shows I am poor and unable to pay for the concession I long to obtain from you.’“‘But how,’ said I, grave as a judge, ‘can a lady of such rank ply so humble a calling? Have you, like me, good reasons for employing your fingers to keep your brains from working?’“‘Let us stick to the question of the wall,’ she said with a smile.“‘Why, we have begun at the foundations,’ said I. ‘Must I not know which of us ought to yield to the other in behalf of our suffering, or, if you choose, our mania?—Oh! what a charming clump of narcissus!’“She had created a perfect museum of flowers and shrubs, arranged as only the sun might see, prompted by artistic genius; even the most heartless landlord must have treated it with respect. The garden breathed comforting scents, suggesting only sweet thoughts. I looked alternately at the mass of narcissus and at the Countess, pretending to be far more in love with the flowers than with her, to maintain my part.“‘So you are very fond of flowers?’ she said.“‘They are,’ I replied, ‘the only beings that never disappoint our care and affection.’ I spoke at length comparing botany and the world, and we ended miles away from the dividing wall, which the Countess naturally brought us back to; for women, when not in love, have all the cold blood of an experienced attorney.“‘If you insist on my leaving the paling,’ said I, ‘you will learn all the secrets of gardening I want to hide; I am seeking a blue dahlia, a blue rose; I am crazy for blue flowers. Is not blue the favorite color of superior souls? We are neither of us really at home; we might as well make a little door of open railings to unite our gardens… You, too, are fond of flowers; you will see mine, I shall see yours. If you receive no visitors at all, I have none but my uncle, the Cure of the White Friars.’“‘No,’ said she, ‘I will give you the right to come into my garden at any hour. Come and welcome; you will be admitted as a neighbor with whom I hope to keep good terms. But I like my solitude too well to burden it with any loss of independence.’“‘As you please,’ said I, and with one leap I was over the paling.“For a fortnight I seemed to take no heed of my neighbor. Towards the end of May, one lovely evening, we were out on opposite sides of the paling, both walking slowly. Having reached the end, we exchanged a few civil words; she found me in deep dejection and spoke of hopefulness in brief sentences like lullabies. I then leaped the fence and found myself at her side again. The Countess led me into the house, wishing to soothe my sadness. At last, I had penetrated the sanctuary where everything harmonized with the woman I have tried to describe.“Exquisite simplicity reigned there. The little house was a dainty box of eighteenth-century art. The dining-room was painted in fresco with flower garlands; the staircase was charmingly decorated in monochrome. The little drawing-room was faded but hung with panels of tapestry; upstairs was one bedroom, a dressing-room, and a library used as her workroom. The kitchen was in the basement, raised by several steps outside. Only the lead cornices were visible. In this retreat, one was a hundred leagues from Paris.“But for the bitter smile that occasionally played on her lips, one might have believed the violet buried in her thicket of flowers was happy. In a few days we reached a degree of intimacy born of proximity and the Countess’ conviction that I was indifferent to women. A look would have spoiled everything, and I never allowed a thought of her to be seen in my eyes. Honorine regarded me as an old friend. Her manner stemmed from a kind of pity. Her looks, voice, and words showed she was a hundred miles away from coquetry. She soon granted me the right to enter her pretty workshop, full of books and curiosities, as smart as a boudoir where elegance emphasized the vulgarity of her tools. The Countess had poetized a thing far from poetry—a manufacture.“Perhaps of all the work a woman can do, making artificial flowers allows her the most grace. For coloring prints one must bend over a table; embroidery entails consumption; engraving requires minute care. But making flowers and light articles permits a pretty woman to remain herself—chatting, laughing, singing, thinking.“There was certainly an artistic feeling in how the Countess arranged petals on a long table. The saucers of color were white china, arranged so the eye could see each shade. A little cabinet with a hundred drawers held the steel molds; a Japanese bowl held the paste. Under her eyes, in a Venetian glass shaped like a flower-cup, was the living model she strove to imitate. She had a passion for achievement; she attempted the most difficult things—tiny corollas, wildflowers—in movements like an accomplished pianist. Her fingers seemed fairies, infinite in the actions of twisting, fitting, and pressure.“I could not tire of admiring her shape a flower. She displayed the genius of a painter in bold attempts; she copied faded flowers and yellowing leaves. ‘This art,’ she said, ‘is in its infancy. If the women of Paris had a little of the genius of the harem, they would lend a complete language of flowers to the wreaths they wear. To please my taste as an artist, I have made drooping flowers with leaves the hue of Florentine bronze, such as are found before or after winter. Would not such a crown on the head of a young woman whose life is a failure have a certain poetical fitness? How many things a woman might express by her head-dress! Are there not flowers for drunken Bacchantes, flowers for gloomy and stern bigots, pensive flowers for bored women? Botany can express every sensation and thought of the soul.’“She employed me to stamp out leaves, cut material, and prepare wires. My affected desire for occupation made me skilled. We talked as we worked. When idle I read new books to her, to keep up my part as a man weary of life, gloomy and soured. My person led to adorable banter about my resemblance—except for his club foot—to Lord Byron. It was tacitly acknowledged that her troubles far outweighed mine, though the causes I assigned for my misanthropy might have satisfied Young or Job.“I will say nothing of the shame that tormented me as I inflicted false wounds on my heart to excite this enchanting woman’s compassion. Her renunciation of love, her dread of what is called happiness, she proclaimed with vehemence and candor. These happy days proved to me that a woman’s friendship is far superior to her love.“I suffered the revelations of my sorrows to be dragged from me with as many grimaces as a young lady allows before the piano, so conscious of the annoyance to follow. The Countess found in me such an exact counterpart of her antipathy to love that I fancied she was content with the chance that had brought a sort of Man Friday to her desert island. Solitude weighed on her. At the same time there was nothing of the coquette in her; nothing remained of a woman; she did not feel she had a heart, except in the ideal world where she found refuge. I compared the two lives—his, all activity and emotion; hers, all inaction and quiescence. My misanthropy allowed cynical sallies against both sexes, hoping to bring Honorine to confidence; but she was not to be caught, and I began to understand that mulish obstinacy is common among women.“‘The Orientals are right,’ I said one evening, ‘when they shut you up and regard you merely as playthings. Europe has been punished for admitting you to society on an equal footing. Woman is the most dishonorable and cowardly being to be found. Nay, and that is where her charm lies. Once a woman has inspired a man’s passion, she is sacred to him forever; but to you women, the man you loved is as nothing; more, you feel the popular calumny that, when you have had your fill, nothing should survive but the remembrance of pleasure.’“‘God has, no doubt, reserved such perfect bliss for Paradise,’ she said. ‘But if your argument seems witty to you, it has the disadvantage of being false. What can those women be who give themselves up to a succession of loves?’ she asked, looking at me as the Virgin in Ingres’ picture looks at Louis XIII offering his kingdom.“‘You are an actress in good faith,’ said I. ‘Still, lovely as you are, you have loved; ergo, you forget.’“‘I!’ she exclaimed, evading my question, ‘I am not a woman. I am a nun, and seventy-two years old!’“‘Then, how can you so positively assert that you feel more keenly than I? Sorrow has but one form for women. The only misfortunes they regard are disappointments of the heart.’“She looked at me sweetly and persisted in her willfulness.“‘I am a nun,’ she said, ‘and you talk to me of the world where I shall never again set foot.’“‘Not even in thought?’ I said.“‘Is the world so much to be desired?’ she replied. ‘Oh! when my mind wanders, it goes higher. The angel of perfection, the beautiful angel Gabriel, often sings in my heart. If I were rich, I should work all the same, to keep me from soaring on the many-tinted wings of the angel and wandering in fancy. There are meditations that ruin us women! I owe much peace to my flowers, though sometimes they fail to occupy me. I feel some great event is impending; my life is about to change; I listen vaguely; I have no liking for my work, and after fatigues I find everyday life once more. Is this a warning from heaven? I ask myself…’“After three months of this struggle between two diplomats—costumed melancholy and a woman whose disgust for life made her invulnerable—I told the Count it was impossible to draw this tortoise from her shell; it must be broken. The evening before, Honorine had exclaimed: ‘Lucretia’s dagger wrote in letters of blood the watchword of woman’s charter: Liberty!’ From that moment, the Count left me free to act.“‘I have been paid a hundred francs for the flowers and caps I made this week!’ Honorine exclaimed gleefully one Saturday evening when I visited her in the ground-floor sitting-room, which the unavowed proprietor had had regilt.“It was ten o’clock. The twilight of July and a glorious moon lent us misty light. Gusts of mingled perfumes soothed the soul; the Countess jingled the five gold pieces given to her by a supposititious dealer in fashionable frippery, another of Octave’s accomplices.“‘I earn my living by amusing myself,’ she said; ‘I am free, when men, armed with their laws, have tried to make us slaves. I have transports of pride every Saturday! I like M. Gaudissart’s gold pieces as much as your double liked Mr. Murray’s.’“‘This is not becoming in a woman,’ said I.“‘Pooh! Am I a woman? I am a boy with a soft soul, that is all; a boy whom no woman can torture…’“‘Your life is the negation of your whole being,’ I replied. ‘What? You, on whom God lavished His choicest treasures of love and beauty, do you never wish—’“‘For what?’ said she, disturbed by a speech that contradicted my assumed role.“‘For a pretty little child, with curling hair, running among the flowers, calling you mother!’“A prolonged silence followed. Leaning on her sofa, the Countess did not faint, but froze under a nervous attack, feeling like the influence of an insidious poison. I called Madame Gobain, who led her mistress away, laid her on her bed, unlaced and undressed her, restoring not life, but consciousness of dreadful suffering. I walked the path behind the house, weeping and doubting my success. I wished to abandon the part of the bird-catcher I had assumed. Madame Gobain found me with my face wet with tears and hastily went up to say to the Countess: ‘What has happened, madame? Monsieur Maurice is crying like a child.’“Roused to action by the evil interpretation that might be put on our behavior, she summoned strength to put on a wrapper and come down to me.“‘You are not the cause of this attack,’ she said. ‘I am subject to these spasms, a sort of cramp of the heart…’“‘And will you not tell me of your troubles?’ I asked, wiping my tears. ‘Have you not just now told me that you have been a mother and so unhappy as to lose your child?’“‘Marie!’ she called as she rang the bell. Gobain came in.“‘Bring lights and some tea,’ said she, with calm decision.“When the housekeeper had lit the tapers and closed the shutters, the Countess showed a mute countenance; her indomitable pride and gravity had reasserted mastery. She said:“‘Do you know why I like Lord Byron so much? It is because he suffered as animals do. Of what use are complaints when they are not an elegy like Manfred’s, nor bitter mockery like Don Juan’s, nor a reverie like Childe Harold’s? Nothing shall be known of me. My heart is a poem that I lay before God.’“‘If I chose—’ said I.“‘If?’ she repeated.“‘I have no interest in anything,’ I replied, ‘so I cannot be inquisitive; but if I chose, I could know all your secrets by tomorrow.’“‘I defy you!’ she exclaimed, uneasy.“‘Seriously?’“‘Certainly,’ she said, tossing her head. ‘If such a crime is possible, I ought to know it.’“‘In the first place, madame,’ I went on, pointing to her hands, ‘were those pretty fingers made for toil? Then you call yourself Madame Gobain, you who, on receiving a letter, said to Marie: “Here, this is for you?” Marie is the real Madame Gobain; so you conceal your name behind that of your housekeeper. Fear nothing, madame, from me. You have in me the most devoted friend you will ever have: friend, do you understand me? I give this word its sacred and pathetic meaning, so profaned in France. Your friend, who will defend you against everything, only wishes your happiness. Who can tell whether the pain I have involuntarily caused you was not a voluntary act?’“‘Yes,’ she replied with threatening audacity, ‘I insist on it. Be curious, and tell me all that you can find out about me; but,’ and she held up her finger, ‘you must also tell me by what means you obtain your information. The preservation of the small happiness I enjoy here depends on the steps you take.’“‘That means you will fly—’“‘On wings!’ she cried, ‘to the New World—’“‘Where you will be at the mercy of the brutal passions you inspire,’ I interrupted. ‘Is it not the essence of genius and beauty to shine and attract men’s gaze? Paris is a desert with Bedouins; it is the only place where those who must work can hide. What have you to complain of? Who am I? An additional servant—M. Gobain, that is all. If you have to fight a duel, you may need a second.’“‘Never mind; find out who I am. I have already said that I insist. Now, I beg that you will,’ she said with the grace you ladies command.“‘Well, then, tomorrow, at the same hour, I will tell you what I may have discovered,’ I replied. ‘But do not therefore hate me! Will you behave like other women?’“‘What do other women do?’“‘They lay upon us immense sacrifices, and when we have made them, they reproach us for it later as if it were an injury.’“‘They are right if the thing required appears to be a sacrifice!’ she replied pointedly.“‘Instead of sacrifices, say efforts and—’“‘It would be an impertinence,’ said she.“‘Forgive me,’ said I. ‘I forget that woman and the Pope are infallible.’“‘Good heavens!’ she said after a pause, ‘only two words would be enough to destroy the peace so dearly bought, and which I enjoy like a fraud—’“She rose and paid no further heed to me.“‘Where can I go?’ she said. ‘What is to become of me? Must I leave this quiet retreat, which I arranged with such care to end my days in?’“‘To end your days!’ I exclaimed with alarm. ‘Has it never struck you that a time would come when you could no longer work, when competition will lower the price of flowers and articles of fashion—?’“‘I have already saved a thousand crowns,’ she said.“‘Heavens! what privations such a sum must represent!’ I exclaimed.“‘Leave me till tomorrow. This evening I am not myself; I must be alone. Must I not save my strength in case of disaster? If you should learn anything, others besides you would be informed, and then—Good-night,’ she added shortly, dismissing me with an imperious gesture.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“The battle is to-morrow, then,” I said with a smile, trying to seem indifferent. As I walked down the avenue the phrase kept ringing in my ears.Octave shared Honorine’s agitation. The Count and I paced together until two in the morning, near the Bastille’s trenches like generals awaiting a decisive hour. We lay awake in opposite moods—one hopeful, the other dreading reunion—aware that real drama lay less in events than in feeling, in that spiritual world where Octave and Honorine existed as lofty beings.The next evening I arrived at ten and entered Honorine’s pale blue-and-white bedroom—her sanctuary. She seemed about to speak but fell silent at my respectful manner.“Madame la Comtesse,” I said gravely.She sank back, wrapped in grief with a pose that might have pleased a painter.“You are the wife of a noble, respected man,” I went on. “You and he are lofty natures. Do you know where you live?”“In my own house,” she answered, astonished.“In Count Octave’s,” I corrected. “You have been deceived. M. Lenormand is only a front. The seclusion, the income, the protections around you—all come from him. He has explained your disappearance plausibly; he even hopes you escaped the Cécile’s wreck. He hides you; his devotion has lasted seven years.”She trembled, defiant; her dry eyes eventually yielded helpless tears. She had believed herself free—marriage weighed on her like a prison.“I will go!” she cried. “He forces me to it; I will go where no one will come after me.”“Kill yourself?” I asked. “You must have powerful reasons not to return.”“Certainly I have!” she said.“Then tell me, tell my uncle. We will find a solution. Your soul is pure; if you have sinned it is expiated. If you wish to evade the Count’s tyranny I will help; he shall never find you.”“Oh! there is always a convent!” she said.“Yes, but the Count, as Minister, could bar you from many. Yet I will save you—if you prove you cannot and ought not to return. Do not fear fall from his power into mine. You shall have peace and solitude.”“And how? By what means?” she asked.“That is my secret. Prove this life is impossible for you, and I will decide in your favor.”She insisted that no man could understand the scruples of her conscience; only God. Another man had once had all her love—there could never be two loves for her. “I esteem, respect, venerate him,” she said of Octave, “but I can never love him. I had but one heart. My husband had the girl; a worthless lover had the woman—there is nothing left! I will not be a prostitute.”“You love him,” I observed.“I esteem him. I respect him. I will not return but to a convent. I could not bear to live with the perpetual memory of my sin. Marriage would make me guilty before him; his generosity would make my shame evident. I would kill him by coldness and comparisons; I might die the victim of his impatience. No position in that life but hides a snare. For five years I have wandered in a sandy desert of the future; my soul is possessed by repentance. Religion tells me to accept suffering as punishment; I lack the energy to be comforted by that. If I were a girl now I should choose him; that is why I refuse—because I cannot be better treated because of my sin. One must be either loved cleanly or not at all.”I tried to show reason: at thirty, attractive, with hidden powers, she might live elsewhere. Her devotion of nine years to the Count served as a guarantee for her tranquillity. I urged her to confide with my uncle, a priest of weight equal to a minister, gentle as Fenelon, who would understand.She was suffocated by the choice. I left at midnight and found the Count nearby; he had been drawn to the spot by irresistible attraction.“What a night my poor child will go through!” he exclaimed when I told him. “Suppose I went to her—what if she saw me suddenly?”“At this moment she might throw herself from the window,” I answered. “She is one of those Lucretias.”“You are young,” he said; “you don’t know that a soul tossed by such choices has a will like a storm-lashed lake; the wind may drive it to either shore.”“And you would accept the equal chances?” I asked.“Well, come,” he said; “I have an opium draught Desplein prepared to send me to sleep without risk.”Next day at noon Gobain brought a letter: Honorine had gone to bed at six, exhausted, and after taking a chemist’s draught had fallen asleep. He gave me a copy of the letter she had written in anguish. Its main points were these: she knew conscience and civil and religious law told her to reconcile with Octave; society and marriage would ratify forgiveness, and she ought to sacrifice her egoism for family.
