But she said she could not live with the memory of her sin. She would rather die than be the wife who cannot love. A widow might remarry and forget, but she could not love Octave; every meeting of eyes would revive her guilt. Marriage would force a hypocritical life of feigned joy; she could not accept the degradation. The child she had borne and nursed for seven months—the memory—would haunt any future children. Her suffering was her expiation; she saw only two hells—the solitude she chose, and the hell under Count Octave’s roof. God might give strength to others; she had not that energy. She asked me to tell where to find solitude, peace, and silence.After delivering the letter I went to Rue Payenne. Anxiety had conquered the opium. Octave paced his garden like a madman. I gave him the letter.“She is mine!” he cried, joy lighting his face. He waved me away; I understood how extreme happiness and extreme pain obey the same law. Later I saw Madame de Courteville and Amelie dining with the Count. Seeing Mademoiselle de Courteville beside him made me compare kinds of love: Honorine’s tragic, demanding love seemed more sublime than Amelie’s peaceful constancy; the former called for special generosity, the latter settled into comfortable motherhood. At twenty-five I did not trust myself; I resolved to do my part.I returned to the Count to announce his relatives’ arrival and saw him grow young at hope. He asked me why I looked changed.“Monsieur le Comte—” I began.“No longer Octave?” he said; “you, to whom I shall owe my life, my happiness—”“My dear Octave, if you bring the Countess back I have studied her—she must never see me again; she must never know that Maurice was your secretary. Never mention my name to her, or all will be undone... You got me an appointment as Maître des Requêtes—get me instead a diplomatic post abroad, and do not think of my marrying Amelie—Oh! do not be uneasy,” I added quickly. “I will play my part to the end.”“Poor boy!” he said, holding my hand.We agreed on the evening’s role, and I returned to Honorine’s. It was August; a hot storm had left the air heavy. She sat beneath an arbor in a loose white muslin dress. She did not rise but motioned me to sit.“Now, is not life deadlocked for me?” she asked.“Life as you have made it,” I answered. “But not the life I propose; if you choose you may be very happy...”“How?” she asked.“Your letter is in the Count’s hands.”She started, then came inside. “You—a friend? Say rather a traitor! A spy sent by my husband.”Women’s instinct is as sharp as men’s perspicacity. I told her there was but one man who could answer such a letter; she must read his reply. I had forwarded her letter through my uncle and asked him what he would reply if his wife wrote thus. In presence of that priest and me she must read the Count’s answer out of respect for dignity, law, and God.My uncle’s entrance into that little Pompadour drawing-room at ten was formidable. His silver hair and calm face soothed Honorine. He handed her the Count’s letter. It said, essentially: if she had trusted him and read a letter he’d written five years before, she could have avoided long privations; he blamed himself for failing to sense danger and begged mercy. He proposed a new life: not a forced conjugal passion but a relation of brotherly tenderness, a home in Faubourg Saint-Honoré where she would be unassailed, occupied as she pleased, with legitimate protection and fortune for good works. He did not want to force her by Church or law; he wanted her consent. He asked only to watch over her and to be permitted the tender care of a father or brother, making life equable and genial. If that compact did not suit her, he would accept her refusal and free her completely.Honorine folded the letter to her bosom and thanked my uncle, saying she would take the Count’s permission to remain where she was. My involuntary cry of “Ah!” betrayed me; she had been testing whether I was actor or spy. She read my emotion as love and answered with a mischievous glance: “Ah, Maurice, you know how to love.”My uncle signaled me to leave. On the threshold Honorine took my hand, pressed it, said “We shall meet again...”“No,” I replied, wringing her hand so she cried out. “You love your husband. I leave tomorrow.”I rushed away. My uncle explained my agitation as a touch of madness; I did not contest the explanation.Six days later I set out as vice-consul to a Spanish commercial town. After I had established myself Octave sent a letter:If he were happy he would not write, but he was in a new life of suffering. When I left, he had not yet been received at the pavilion, though a letter promised. After waiting a month, he called; Honorine had had to dress. At last, overcome with trembling, he told her he loved her; she replied that there was no crime in loving one’s wife. He urged her to give up toil; she had sixty thousand francs a year of her own and should not squander it. He offered certificates worth twelve thousand francs each—symbolic gifts of freedom. She accepted, and with a look he understood he had offered not money but emancipation. “I am conquered,” she said, holding out her hand, which he kissed.For two months a springtime of love gave him bliss; she was no longer afraid; she studied him. When he proposed going to England to resume rank and life in the new house she was alarmed. “Why not live always as we are?” she asked. He feared he might force a consent that was not full. She finally consented in a manner he could accept: she would return as his wife when he wished, but she warned that resignation was not the love he sought. “I do love you,” she told him, “but not as you wish. I love your soul... I love you enough to die in your service without regret. It will be my expiation.” She knelt and added, “And perhaps I shall not die!” For two months he struggled within.I did not reply to that letter. Two months later the papers announced the Comtesse Octave’s return on an English vessel, restored after contrived adventures. When I moved to Genoa I received formal notice of the birth of a son to the Count and Countess. I sat two hours holding that news on a bench. Two months later, urged by Octave, by M. de Grandville, by M. de Serizy, and broken by my uncle’s death, I agreed to marry.Six months after the Revolution of July a final letter concluded the story. Honorine wrote that she was dying—though she was a mother—and declared she had played her part as wife, deceiving her husband, and had had a happiness as genuine as an actress’ tears. She confessed to the “malady of memory”: she could never twice feel that identity of love which unites a woman with a man. To the last she cried in the confessional and to her husband, “Have mercy!” but there was none. She nursed the saintly uncle in his last illness; he showed her the way to heaven and bade her persevere in duty. Yet memory consumed her. Although the doctors ascribed death to a plausible complaint, she knew it was the strain of acting a life without love that was killing her. She asked me to be her little Count’s guardian and left a codicil with her wish. “Do not mourn for me,” she said; “I have been dead this many a day if Saint Bernard was right that where there is no love there is no life.”“And the Countess died,” said the Consul, closing the pocket-book.“Is the Count still living?” asked the Ambassador; since July he had vanished from politics.“Do you remember seeing me go to the steamboat with—” the Consul-General asked.“A white-haired man? an old man?” the painter interrupted.“An old man of forty-five, going to Southern Italy for health and amusement. That man was my poor friend and patron, passing through Genoa to take leave of me and to place his will in my hands. He appointed me his son’s guardian. I had no need to tell him of Honorine’s wishes.”“Does he suspect himself of murder?” Mademoiselle des Touches asked the Baron de l’Hostal.“He suspects the truth,” replied the Consul, “and that suspicion is killing him. I stayed on board the steam packet until it departed; a small boat brought me back. We sat a long time taking leave—for ever, I fear. God only knows how much we love those in whose hands we place the confidant of our love when she who inspired it is no more.“The Count went to the prow and looked down at the Mediterranean. ‘Ought we not, in the interests of human nature, to inquire what irresistible power leads us to sacrifice an exquisite creature to the most fugitive of pleasures, and in spite of reason? In my conscience I heard cries. Honorine was not alone in her anguish. And yet I would have it!... I am consumed by remorse. In the Rue Payenne I was dying of joys not had; now I shall die in Italy of joys I have had... Wherein lay the discord between two equally noble natures, I dare assert?’”A profound silence fell on the terrace. The Consul turned to the two women and asked, “Was she virtuous?”Mademoiselle des Touches rose, took his arm, and said, “Are not men wrong too when they marry a young girl while cherishing an angelic image. They compare us to unknown rivals and find us wanting?”“Mademoiselle, you would be right if marriage were based on passion; and that was the mistake of those two, who will soon be no more. Marriage with mutual, heart-deep love would be Paradise.”Mademoiselle des Touches turned and Claude Vignon whispered: “A bit of a coxcomb is M. de l’Hostal.”“No,” she replied; “he has not yet guessed that Honorine would have loved him.—Oh!” she cried, seeing the Consul’s wife approaching, “his wife was listening! Unhappy man!”At eleven the guests walked home along the seashore. Mademoiselle des Touches reflected that Honorine was rare, perhaps the most extraordinary exception in intellect—a pearl—and that for such souls solitude and prayer might suffice. Leon de Lora admired the idea of a woman who could live by a studio and withdraw from the world. Claude Vignon scoffed; the Ambassador recalled other similar tragedies. Camille Maupin, leaning on the quay’s balustrade, stood silent, pensively admiring a woman who had lived and died sustained by a memory no law could touch.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ####################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ####################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“HULLO! There is that old Box-coat again!” cried the lawyer’s clerk Simonnin, leaning from a window and munching dry bread as he shot a crumb at a stranger crossing Maître Derville’s courtyard in the Rue Vivienne.“Come, Simonnin, don’t play tricks on people, or I will turn you out of doors. However poor a client may be, he is still a man!” admonished Boucard, the head clerk, busy with a bill of costs.Simonnin, a typical thirteen- or fourteen-year-old messenger, was known for impudence and mischief. He and the other clerks, including Godeschal, joked about the old man, dubbing him “old Box-coat.” Godeschal proposed a prank, claiming M. Derville received clients only at odd hours.The office buzzed with laughter and camaraderie. It was cluttered and dimly lit, the smell of food mingling with the musty odor of papers; the clerks accepted the disarray as part of their chaotic work.When the old man entered, looking weary and out of place, he politely asked if M. Derville was available. Preoccupied, the clerks dismissed him. Boucard brusquely suggested he return at one in the morning, leaving the visitor bewildered.The clerks resumed their mockery, speculating about the old man’s past—nobleman, porter, or worse—trading extravagant guesses to explain his shabby appearance. When the old man insisted he was Colonel Chabert, supposedly dead at Eylau, they erupted in laughter and treated the claim as a joke.Amid the jeering, they debated evening plans and which theatre to attend; their playful disputes underscored the youthful spirit that pervaded the office despite monotonous tasks. Finally Boucard quelled the chatter and refocused them on finishing work efficiently. The scene captured their rowdy, irreverent camaraderie in stark contrast to the old man’s somber, unsettling presence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At about one in the morning, Colonel Chabert knocked at Maître Derville’s door. The porter said Derville had not returned, but the head clerk, preparing papers for the next day, explained that Derville preferred to work at night. The Colonel was only the third person to seek him so late.When Derville arrived he was struck by Chabert’s appearance: thin, pale, his head scarred as if by a harsh past. The Colonel introduced himself as the man presumed killed at Eylau, which led Derville and the clerk to suspect madness. Chabert, however, insisted on telling his story.Derville, used to varied clients, urged brevity. Chabert recounted serving at Eylau, where he was gravely wounded and buried among the dead. Miraculously he survived because his horse’s body had shielded him; he described awakening in a grave, struggling free, and being rescued by a woman who took him to a hospital.After months of recovery he discovered he had lost everything, including proof of his identity. With no documents, attempts to reclaim his life were met with disbelief and ridicule. He wandered as a beggar and was confined in a mental institution for two years. Released but haunted by his past, he found his wife, the Countess Ferraud, had remarried and borne children.Derville, sensing the seriousness of the claims, expressed skepticism but a willingness to help. Chabert said he sought to reclaim his rights and fortune, which he believed had been unjustly taken, lamenting that no lawyer had taken him seriously enough to prove his identity and recover his estate.Moved by the Colonel’s plight, Derville offered to obtain necessary documents from Germany and gave him a small sum to subsist on. Overwhelmed with gratitude, Chabert felt hope for the first time in years. He also voiced a longing for vengeance against his wife and revealed the depth of his emotional turmoil.Derville acknowledged the case’s complexity and likely legal obstacles. Chabert declared that if he failed he would rather die than continue living in obscurity. Derville promised to treat the matter seriously and provided a letter to a notary for further assistance.As Chabert left, he experienced a renewed sense of purpose and determination: immediate aid and the promise of legal support gave him, after years of despair, a glimmer of hope.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About three months after the interview, in Derville’s room, the notary Crottat — a young man who had recently taken over an office after his chief fled in bankruptcy — raised a serious matter and asked Derville to refund the six hundred francs the old soldier had received.“My philanthropy will not extend beyond twenty-five louis; I fear I have already been duped by my patriotism,” Derville replied. He noticed papers on his desk, including a letter with stamps from various post offices. The letter was in German; he ordered his head clerk Boucard to translate it.The notary in Berlin informed Derville that the requested documents would arrive soon and were legally stamped. Most witnesses to the recorded facts were still in Eylau, and the woman who had saved Comte Chabert was alive in Heilsberg. After Boucard translated, Derville treated the matter as serious business and asked Crottat about Chabert’s estate, calling to mind the rogue Roguin.“Let’s call him the unfortunate Roguin,” Crottat mocked. He explained that, when he was third clerk, he had copied papers showing Rose Chapotel, wife of Hyacinthe, had married Chabert without settlement, so their property was common. The personalty had been about six hundred thousand francs. Before marrying, Colonel Chabert had made a will leaving one-quarter to hospitals and another quarter to the State; the will was contested, triggering a forced sale, and the widow received the portion meant for the treasury. Roguin had absconded with clients’ funds — some said eight hundred thousand francs — and had handled Chabert’s estate.“So Comte Chabert’s personal fortune was no more than three hundred thousand francs?” Derville asked.“Exactly,” Crottat replied.Derville then went to find Colonel Chabert, lodging in the Faubourg Saint-Marceau with an old quartermaster named Vergniaud. His coachman refused to drive in the unpaved street, so Derville walked. He found dilapidated gate-posts and a sign, “Vergniaud, dairyman,” and entered a dismal yard and a falling house. Inside, mismatched windows, a dirt floor, and a trodden atmosphere greeted him. Chabert emerged calmly, smoking a pipe, still bearing the soldier’s coolness.“Why did you not write to me?” Chabert asked, guiding Derville away from the manure. His room contained only a chair and a straw bed; the walls were damp. Despite the squalor, the Colonel seemed content.“Here, we are not distinguished for luxury,” he said. “But I have done no one wrong, and I sleep in peace.”Derville hesitated over the six hundred francs he had advanced and questioned why Chabert hadn’t moved to Paris. “The good folks here have taken me in and fed me for a year,” Chabert explained. “I cannot leave them now that I have a little money.”Derville told him the papers from Heilsberg were expected and that the woman who saved him was alive. Chabert, frustrated at still lacking money, threw down his pipe. Derville then explained the legal complexities: proving Chabert’s identity would require lengthy, costly proceedings in which opponents could use the bigamy issue — Comtesse Ferraud’s remarriage — to obstruct him.“Things are not done so in the legal world,” Derville warned. “You will face many challenges, and your opponents will demand inquiries that could take years.” Overwhelmed, Chabert despaired at the prospect of a drawn-out lawsuit. Derville reassured him the outcome could be favorable and proposed a friendly suit to annul both the death certificate and the later marriage, offering to send a power of attorney and financial help.Chabert, feeling hopeless, agreed to trust Derville completely. As Derville left, he encountered Louis Vergniaud, who expressed concern for Chabert’s well-being and asked for a loan to improve the Colonel’s living conditions. Derville agreed to provide funds but insisted that Chabert should be the one to help his friends.Derville then planned to visit Comtesse Ferraud, considering her position and the leverage potential if Chabert’s identity were proved. Monsieur le Comte Ferraud, the son of a former Councillor, had returned to France after the Revolution and married Madame Chabert, who had turned her share of her husband’s fortune into a substantial income. Their marriage was socially accepted; Ferraud was politically ambitious but had begun to regret his union, leaving the Countess in a precarious, though affluent, position.At the Hotel Ferraud, Derville found the Countess surrounded by luxury — a stark contrast to Chabert’s squalor. She greeted him lightly, but his seriousness silenced her. He bluntly revealed that Colonel Chabert was alive and warned of the danger she faced if his identity were proven, hinting at legal consequences. She laughed initially, confident that her second marriage was secure because of her children, but Derville countered that the law might not favor her; Ferraud might not support her if his name and fortune were at stake.Derville’s words unnerved her; she began to consider the implications. Sensing her vulnerability, he proposed a compromise. The Countess, now calculating, asked, “Does he still love me?” — a question that revealed her intent to use Chabert’s affection to her advantage. Derville left her to ponder a settlement, confident he had gained the upper hand.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A week after Derville's visits, Colonel Chabert and his wife, Madame la Comtesse Ferraud, set out from opposite ends of Paris to meet at their lawyer's office. Thanks to Derville's support, Chabert arrived in a dignified blue suit with the red ribbon of the Legion of Honor, recovering the soldierly bearing that had been lost and transforming from a shabby figure into one reflecting his former rank.When Chabert entered Derville's office, the Countess arrived shortly after, elegantly dressed to appear youthful. The office was unchanged; clerks made jesting remarks about the transformation. Sensing the couple's tension, Derville separated them to discuss terms. He presented a memorandum: Chabert's formal acknowledgment of his restored identity in exchange for financial arrangements from his wife.The Countess was taken aback by the terms, especially the financial obligations, and insisted she did not want the matter dragged into court. When Chabert returned he confronted her about their past, exposing the pain of their separation. She feigned surprise and attempted to leave, but Derville urged her to stay. Chabert, bitter and wounded, pressed for justice even as Derville warned against rash actions.After a heated exchange the Countess put Chabert into her carriage and they went to her country house in Groslay. During the ride her tone shifted, revealing emotional turmoil: she admitted attachment to another man, Comte Ferraud, and wanted to preserve her dignity without public scandal. Chabert, though hurt, offered a measure of understanding, aware of the social consequences she feared.At the country house she treated him kindly, trying to erase past pain. They reminisced, and Chabert found temporary solace in her presence even as resentment and the sense of betrayal lingered. The Countess, meanwhile, worked discreetly to secure her position and undermine Chabert's claim. As days passed her unease grew; she wrote to Delbecq to manipulate the situation further. When Chabert discovered this, he felt deeply betrayed and resolved to confront her. Their relationship became a battleground of emotion, with Chabert torn between lingering affection and mounting anger.Ultimately he decided to sacrifice his legal identity for the sake of the Countess and their children, accepting anonymity to avoid scandal. Her continued manipulations revealed the depth of her betrayal; he confronted her, expressed his disdain, and chose to live without reclaiming his name. Chabert then vanished from society. Months later Derville found him in a courtroom, reduced to a vagabond. Despite his circumstances he retained a measure of dignity and reflected on the world's indifference. Derville, moved, tried to help, but Chabert rejected material concerns. His story remains one of loss and resilience, illustrating the complexities of love, betrayal, and the struggle for identity in an indifferent world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In June 1840 Godeschal, an attorney, accompanied Derville to Ris. Near Bicêtre they noticed an old pauper beneath an elm drying his handkerchief. In a shabby wrapper-coat with a striking look, the man prompted Godeschal to compare him to grotesque German carvings; Derville recognized him as Comte Chabert, the husband of the pious Comtesse Ferraud, who had likely sent him to the workhouse because he reminded her of their past.Intrigued, Godeschal urged Derville to tell Chabert’s story. Two days later they returned and found the old man on a tree trunk, drawing in the sand. When addressed, he insisted he was no longer Chabert but “Hyacinthe,” a mere number of the institution, and envied a condemned man, thinking him fortunate for not being married.Godeschal offered money for snuff; Chabert eagerly accepted, thanking them as “brave troopers,” playfully brandishing his stick and crying “Vive Napoleon!” Another pauper observed that Chabert, though sometimes childish, could be philosophical and imaginative, recalling how he once impressed a Prussian officer with his military reminiscences.Derville reflected on Chabert’s tragic arc: from foundling to soldier under Napoleon, only to end in a workhouse. He lamented the disillusionment of three professions—the priest, the doctor, and especially the lawyer—each bearing witness to humanity’s darker side. Derville sketched the lawyer’s grim view, full of betrayal, greed, and moral decay, citing cases of familial cruelty: fathers abandoned by daughters and mothers who exploit children for gain.Godeschal conceded he had seen similar horrors and expressed a desire to flee Paris’s corruption for a quiet country life with his wife. The narrative closes with an addendum listing characters from Balzac’s “Human Comedy” and their appearances elsewhere, including figures like Napoleon and Comtesse Ferraud.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Bianchon, a physician known for his theoretical physiology, first trained in surgery under the celebrated French surgeon Desplein. Desplein’s diagnostic and operative skill was extraordinary, yet his genius remained personal rather than institutional. His atheism—strengthened by dissecting bodies and perceiving no soul—was a central part of his outlook.Critics used his contradictions to diminish him: eccentric habits of lavish then indifferent dress, a temperament swinging from kindness to harshness, and a contemptuous attitude born of close observation of human folly. Among his students, Horace Bianchon stood out for enduring poverty to study medicine; noted for integrity, loyalty, and cheerfulness, he won Desplein’s recognition and was introduced to medical practice and Parisian society by his mentor.When Bianchon told Desplein about a destitute water-carrier with a crippling illness, Desplein rushed to help, revealing a compassionate side beneath his stern exterior and a recurring sympathy for the less fortunate, especially fellow Auvergnats.Bianchon later saw Desplein enter a church—a surprising sight given the surgeon’s outspoken unbelief. Curious, he followed and observed Desplein at mass; at a subsequent dinner Desplein dismissed the service as a farce, leaving Bianchon puzzled. Months later, after another physician questioned Desplein, Bianchon resolved to investigate. Returning a year later, he again found Desplein at mass; the sacristan disclosed that Desplein had attended that service regularly for twenty years and had even founded it.The discovery intensified the mystery of Desplein’s beliefs and behavior: a man who publicly professed atheism yet privately participated in religious ritual. These episodes reveal a multifaceted character—skillful, skeptical, compassionate—whose public declarations and private practices resist simple labels and highlight the complex relationship between belief, practice, and human contradiction.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Some time passed before Doctor Bianchon, despite their friendship, found an opportunity to discuss a significant incident in Desplein's life. Their meetings, whether professional or social, rarely allowed privacy. Finally, seven years later, after the Revolution of 1830, Bianchon saw Desplein entering Saint-Sulpice and knelt beside him without surprise. They attended mass together."Can you explain your sudden piety?" Bianchon asked as they left the church. "You, who do not believe in God, attending mass? You owe me an explanation.""I am like many devout people—outwardly religious but inwardly skeptical," Desplein replied, then launched into a critique of certain political figures."That doesn't answer my question," Bianchon insisted. "Why did you found this mass?""Well, my dear boy," Desplein said, "I am nearing the end of my life; I can tell you its beginnings."They stood in the Rue des Quatre-Vents, a grim Paris street. Desplein pointed to a building where he had lived for two years. "I lived up there," he said, recalling the hardships of his early life. He had endured hunger, poverty, and isolation, with no money for books or medical training, and an irritable nature that made it hard to connect with others. He was alone, struggling to rise from the depths of poverty.He recounted meager meals and the sacrifices he made for study. He worked tirelessly, often without sympathy or support, with no friends to share his burdens and feeling the full weight of his circumstances.He expressed frustration at those born into wealth who could not grasp his struggles. "They ask why I got into debt, as if it were a choice. They have no idea what it means to be truly destitute."Desplein explained how early experiences shaped his view of humanity. "If I seem hard or bitter, it's because I've witnessed the selfishness and envy around me. In Paris, mediocrity often seeks to undermine those who strive for excellence."He recalled a pivotal moment when he faced utter poverty and considered enlisting. He had hoped for a box of linen from a relative, but when it arrived the porter demanded an exorbitant fee to release it. Despairing, he met his neighbor, Bourgeat, a water-carrier who offered help.Bourgeat, despite his own hardships, paid Desplein's debts and helped him find new lodgings. "He devoted his savings to my future," Desplein said, visibly moved. "He understood my mission and supported me selflessly."Desplein described Bourgeat as a nurturing figure who cared for him like a mother. "He took pride in my success and never sought recognition for his sacrifices. When I became a house surgeon, I suffered leaving him, but he was proud of me."Desplein later repaid Bourgeat by buying him a horse and barrel, fulfilling a long-held dream. "He was furious I had spent my money on him, yet he was delighted. He never claimed credit for my success, though I owed him everything."When Bourgeat fell ill, Desplein dedicated himself to his care, but Bourgeat died in his arms. "He left me everything he had. He had a strong faith, and I honored his wishes by having masses said for him after his death."Desplein concluded, "I can only offer this as a tribute to Bourgeat's faith. I hope that if there is a paradise, he finds peace there. I would give anything for the faith he had."Bianchon, who had witnessed Desplein's last days, could not definitively say whether the great surgeon died an atheist. He pondered whether Bourgeat might have opened the gates of Heaven for Desplein, just as he had opened the doors to the earthly temple they had shared.PARIS, January 1836.ADDENDUMCharacters appearing in other stories of the Human Comedy:Bianchon, Horace; Father Goriot; Cesar Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Bachelor's Establishment; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan's Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Muse of the Department; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty; The Country Parson.In addition, M. Bianchon narrated:Another Study of Woman; La Grande Breteche.Desplein appears in:Cousin Pons; Lost Illusions; The Thirteen; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Bachelor's Establishment; The Seamy Side of History; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan's Life; Honorine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Hashes mark the MIDDLE OF THE STORY, centered, framed by rows of hashes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In 1828, at one in the morning, Dr. Horace Bianchon and Baron de Rastignac left a house in the Rue du Faubourg Saint-Honoré and walked to the boulevard because neither had a carriage. Their conversation turned to the Marquise d’Espard. Bianchon was skeptical of her character; Rastignac, dazzled by her beauty and rank, believed she could advance his social standing and help pay his debts.Bianchon warned that pursuing the Marquise would be a mistake, likening her to a “one-eyed horse” and suggesting she was older than she pretended. As a physician, he insisted physical signs betray truth despite cosmetics, and he expressed contempt for fashionable women, whom he portrayed as polished façades lacking genuine feeling and driven by selfishness. Rastignac replied that his motive was social power and opportunity, dismissing moral scruples: a woman of fashion, he argued, could be a useful ally for an ambitious man, offering connections and influence.Despite misgivings, Bianchon agreed to assist by introducing Rastignac to his uncle Jean-Jules Popinot, a judge who might intervene in the Marquise’s affairs. Still, Bianchon doubted Popinot’s willingness given the judge’s reputation for inflexibility.The next morning Bianchon went to Popinot’s residence in a poor, dilapidated quarter of Paris; the building’s decay suggested past grandeur. Popinot himself was unkempt and unambitious; his career had stalled despite ability—he had been passed over and remained a supernumerary judge. His life was defined by a rigorous commitment to justice, often to his own detriment. Known for kindness and a talent for eliciting confessions without coercion, he combined compassion with firmness and was frustrated by legal injustices.As Bianchon approached Popinot’s door, he reflected on how difficult it would be to persuade his uncle to help Rastignac, aware of the complexities and obstacles ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Twelve years before this narrative, in 1816, during a severe food shortage in France, Popinot was named President of the Commission Extraordinary to distribute food to the needy in his neighborhood. Though he had intended to leave the Rue du Fouarre, his work as a prominent lawyer and criminal judge—after five years watching legal outcomes without grasping their causes—led him to deep compassion for the destitute, turning him into a protector of the poor, almost a modern Saint Vincent de Paul.The change was gradual. Popinot moved from one charitable act to another, becoming central in his community and active in various benevolent organizations. He strove to prevent crime, provide employment, and aid the vulnerable, while keeping a private life little known in Paris. The gratitude of those he helped was often unspoken: they were too burdened to acknowledge him.By the end of his second year he had converted a storeroom in his home into a parlor for the needy. Simply furnished with wooden benches and basic chairs, the room became a hub where the poor sought assistance. Popinot recorded donations and requests with care to manage resources, while his aide Lavienne handled pawn tickets and visited areas hit hardest by famine.From early morning into the day the parlor was crowded with women, children, and the destitute. The atmosphere was often stifling, but the crowd’s warmth made heating unnecessary. The poor revered Popinot and often waited outside before dawn; even criminals respected his house. He devoted mornings to the needy, afternoons to legal work, and evenings to his judicial duties.Popinot’s keen observation let him discern hidden virtues in the impoverished and detect the faintest signs of wrongdoing in others. His modest inheritance, supplemented by his late wife’s wealth, was stretched by frugality and his charitable outlays; his judge’s salary was limited, and despite his service he remained one of the few judges in the Court of the Seine without the Legion of Honor.At the request of the President of the Second Chamber of the Court, Popinot was charged with examining the Marquis d’Espard after the marquise, the Marquis’s wife, sought a Commission in Lunacy. The Rue du Fouarre, bustling with the needy in the early hours, would be empty by nine. Bianchon, Popinot’s nephew, rode to find his uncle amid these duties, expecting a contrast between Popinot’s unassuming presence and the marquise’s expectations.Bianchon found the parlor full—a tableau of hardship, faces marked by despair and resilience. Popinot, in a worn nightcap and dressing gown, was engrossed in his work, a man wholly committed to easing suffering. Lavienne kept the process moving, issuing assistance efficiently.Bianchon interrupted with urgency while Popinot continued consultations. After a brief exchange, Popinot attended a woman who needed funds to sustain her grocery business while her husband was imprisoned for debt; he gave assistance and recorded the transaction in his ledger.As the morning wore on, Bianchon learned Popinot had seen over two hundred people that day, with more waiting. Sent away on urgent medical errands, Bianchon returned to find the parlor empty—the last of the needy helped.Popinot’s home reflected his chaotic life: dust, disarray, papers everywhere, and remnants of his late wife’s possessions. Yet tokens of gratitude from those he aided adorned the room, poignant reminders of his impact.Preparing for breakfast, Bianchon urged Popinot to dress properly for the meeting with the marquise. Popinot resisted, citing legal rules against magistrates accepting hospitality from interested parties. Bianchon argued it would help Popinot understand her concerns in her own environment. Conceding the point, Popinot agreed to visit the marquise, noting the appointment and preparing to examine the Marquis d’Espard’s mental state and its implications for the family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next evening at nine Bianchon climbed his uncle’s dusty stairs to find him bent over a complex judgment. Since the tailor had not sent the coat Lavienne ordered, Popinot wore his old stained coat, amusing those unaware of his private life. Bianchon straightened his uncle's cravat and buttoned his coat to hide the stains, but the judge’s habitual movements soon revealed a gap between waistcoat and trousers. Bianchon noted the absurdity just as his uncle entered the Marquise’s room.A brief account of Madame d’Espard follows. For seven years she had been a prominent Parisian figure, keeping vogue despite the city’s fickleness. After separating from her husband in 1815, the Marquise—about thirty-three and mother of two boys, fifteen and thirteen—managed to preserve an appearance and social standing that usually favored youth and beauty.She appeared far younger than her years, using elaborate coiffures, cold baths, and austere eating to maintain her looks—an effort likened to a famous Polish woman famed for retaining youthful vigor. Her complexion and figure remained attractive; emotionally she stayed detached, unfamiliar with either love or hatred. She parted from M. d’Espard without remorse, since he took the children she regarded as hindrances to her ambitions.With an income of twenty-six thousand francs, the Marquise initially hesitated over her post-separation life. Though capable of luxurious living, she adopted a quieter existence for some years. By 1820 she re-entered society, hosting gatherings and supplanting other notable women in Paris’s elite circles.Madame d’Espard cultivated ties with influential figures—a duchess, a countess—amplifying her political influence. Her salon became a center for political conversation; she was known for discretion and skill in navigating Parisian complexities. Ambitious young men admired her; she maintained independence while exercising power. Yet she was also indiscreet and vain, treating relationships as instruments to extend her influence rather than as genuine connections. She managed interactions to avoid personal compromise while keeping her drawing-room full of notable figures.Rastignac, seeking to align with her, recognized her as an ally and a cunning adversary. When Popinot arrived at her household he noted its opulence. The Marquise received him with feigned frailty; the Chevalier d’Espard stood silently observing.Though outwardly simple, Popinot was perceptive and quickly assessed the situation. He questioned the Marquise about her husband’s mental state and the separation. She offered a crafted story: a change in her husband’s temperament led to estrangement. Popinot probed the children’s welfare and financial claims, finding inconsistencies between her lavish life and assertions of hardship. He suggested her motives for seeking control over her husband’s affairs might be self-serving.Tension rose as Popinot outlined potential consequences, emphasizing transparency and warning that the court would scrutinize her motives. The Marquise, feeling cornered, tried to deflect, but Popinot’s persistence forced her to face the risk that her carefully constructed facade could collapse.Afterward Bianchon and Rastignac discussed Popinot’s character and the case’s implications, recognizing its complexity and scandal potential; Bianchon worried about the Marquise’s influence. Popinot resolved to investigate further, planning to summon Madame Jeanrenaud, the woman linked to M. d’Espard, to clarify matters.The next day Madame Jeanrenaud arrived at Popinot’s office, a stout, unrefined contrast to the Marquise. She denied wrongdoing and expressed sincere concern for M. d’Espard. Intrigued by her straightforwardness, Popinot began to untangle M. d’Espard’s financial dealings, realizing the matter was more complicated than it first appeared and would require careful examination of all involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################People who have matured know how trivial incidents can shape major events: Popinot's minor illness delayed a crucial meeting. The judge, feeling unwell, stayed home instead of visiting the Marquis d’Espard, costing a day whose loss echoed historic delays with wide consequences.Before following the lawyer and his clerk to the Marquis’s residence, consider this father's home life, which his wife’s petition branded as mad. In old Paris, some buildings still show a taste for decoration and permanence. The house on Rue de la Montagne-Sainte-Genevieve where M. d’Espard lived was such a stone structure: once grand, now weathered and damaged by time and revolutions. Formerly home to dignitaries, it had become multi-tenant, its interior degraded by a printing establishment.M. d’Espard took the ground floor, likely for his children’s sake, since the garden was unusually large for the area. He secured the rooms at a reasonable rent, given the building’s disrepair and the depressed market. The house, dating from the reigns of Henry III–Louis XIII, still bore traces of former glory, including a perron and the garden.The Marquis furnished his rooms in rich browns, creating a peaceful atmosphere with carefully chosen pieces and a harmonious scheme. Few visitors were admitted to the family’s private life, which remained largely secluded. The household consisted of the Marquis, his two sons, and a small staff, including a long-serving cook and a man-servant, both contributing to the orderly quiet of the house.M. d’Espard avoided socializing with other tenants to shield his children from outside influences. That choice, however, bred gossip and suspicion: neighbors labeled him mad and his servants untrustworthy. Tenants believed the Marquis was financially ruined, a view reinforced by his extreme frugality and habit of paying cash, avoiding credit.The Marquis and his family were noble by birth, embodying ideals becoming rare in France. They retained a deep sense of dignity and pride in their lineage, which clashed with egalitarian sentiments among their neighbors. This disconnect fostered animosity, as tenants resented the d’Espards for perceived superiority despite apparent financial strain.M. d’Espard, about fifty, exemplified the aristocracy of his time. He had a distinguished, somewhat cold appearance that commanded respect. A slightly crooked nose and a prominent forehead lent an eccentric air that fueled rumors of madness. Despite elegant manners, he was frugal, wearing the same coat for years.His sons, Comte Clément de Négrepelisse and Vicomte Camille d’Espard, were handsome and displayed aristocratic grace. They inherited their father’s features and their mother’s dignified manner. The elder, nearly sixteen, had moved to more fashionable attire, while the younger still wore a round jacket. The Marquis concentrated on giving them a rounded education, emphasizing mathematics, Eastern languages, and history, while avoiding what he considered unproductive studies.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Popinot planned to question M. d’Espard fell on a Thursday holiday. About nine, before their father was up, the two sons were in the garden. The younger begged to visit the shooting-gallery for the first time and pressed Clément to support the request to the Marquis. The Viscount often teased Clément and delighted in wrestling with him. Their noisy quarrel roused their father, who watched amused from the window. Two neighbors, observing, commented that the Marquis seemed mad to make his children fight. Noticing them, the Marquis called the boys; they leapt into his room and, after a moment’s hesitation, Clement obtained permission to go.All through the house people were talking about the Marquis’ supposed new madness. When Popinot arrived near noon with his clerk, the portress led him to the third floor and repeated the rumor that M. d’Espard had made his children fight that morning and had laughed when the younger bit the elder until he bled; she added that the Marquis rarely showed himself. The landing’s muddy floor and grimy banisters lent some credibility to her gossip and made Popinot, though impartial, receptive to the tale.“This is the man who swallows enough to feed the whole neighborhood,” the porter’s wife declared. The clerk smiled; Popinot controlled his expression. They entered an outer office where an elderly man, probably the office clerk, sat amid shelves of published numbers; a grating separated a private room and a cash-desk indicated a commercial enterprise.“M. d’Espard?” Popinot asked the man in a gray blouse. The shopman opened the next room to reveal the Marquis: a venerable, simply dressed old man wearing the Cross of Saint-Louis, seated at a desk. Popinot explained they wished to speak on private business. After formalities, the Marquis asked their purpose. Popinot introduced himself: “Monsieur le Marquis, my duties and the inquiry that brings me here require us to be alone, although such inquiries are usually public. I am a judge on the Inferior Court of Appeal for the Department of the Seine, charged with examining you regarding a petition for a Commission in Lunacy filed by the Marquise d’Espard.” The old man withdrew; the clerk sat to take notes. The Marquis reddened and trembled, then steadied himself.“How is it,” he asked, “that I have had no notice of such a petition?” Popinot explained that notice is unnecessary because those thought subject to such a commission are presumed to lack reason; the Court must verify the petitioner’s claims. The Marquis accepted this and asked what he must do. Popinot urged him to answer fully and confidentially, especially concerning delicate accusations Madame d’Espard might make. The Marquis feared that his explanations could bring blame on his wife. Popinot promised discretion and the customary courtesy should the inquiry reveal a misunderstanding.Popinot confronted the major allegation: that the Marquis had dissipated his fortune for the benefit of Madame Jeanrenaud, widow of a bargemaster, or her son, Colonel Jeanrenaud, for whom he allegedly procured an appointment and exhausted influence with the King. The Marquis flushed; tears came, but his pride returned quickly.“You place me in a strange dilemma,” he said. Revealing his motives would mean disclosing secret wounds and trusting the family’s honor to strangers. Popinot assured him of confidentiality. Freed from reserve, the Marquis then spoke with clarity and passion.After their marriage his wife had incurred heavy expenses that forced him to borrow. He could not afford a steward; most of his title-deeds had been taken to Paris by his father, who feared scrutiny. Their family name was Negrepelisse; the title d’Espard had been acquired through marriage in the time of Henri IV, when they lost the town of Negrepelisse in the religious wars and an ancestor was ruined by the destruction of his property. Though a later ancestor secured favor with Louis XIV and fortune, a stain of blood and disgrace remained, and the Marquis was determined to erase it.He had discovered that his grandfather had obtained the lands of Negrepelisse through means tied to the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes: a Protestant merchant was arrested and hanged, and the land passed to his ancestor by iniquitous means. The merchant’s family had the rightful claim; the victim’s name was Jeanrenaud. The Marquis could not bear the disgrace and resolved to find the heirs. Eventually he located M. Jeanrenaud, reduced to poverty and serving as a lieutenant under Napoleon. Believing the heir’s rights incontestable and their lack of means to sue, he offered to pay them the lands’ value at the time they were taken—eleven hundred thousand francs—payable at his convenience and without interest.To effect restitution he lived on little for a long time. When he proposed to Madame d’Espard that they move to the country to live on half her income, she treated him as mad; her coldness convinced him she would have condoned his ancestors’ crimes. Terrified by her attitude, he resolved to leave her in control of her fortune after settling their debts, and to take charge of educating their children himself, determined to raise them as gentlemen. He invested prudently, repaid debt sooner than expected, and had recently completed the payment of the eleven hundred thousand francs, making restitution without harming his children's prospects. These were the reasons for payments to Madame Jeanrenaud and her son.Popinot asked whether Madame d’Espard knew his motives for retiring from public life. “Yes, monsieur,” the Marquis replied. Popinot, visibly moved, excused his clerk. He then inquired about other allegations, such as that the Marquis was engaged in a business unbecoming his rank. The Marquis declined to discuss it there and summoned Comte de Nouvion, an old family friend, explaining that he used the rooms as offices for the publication of the Picturesque History of China by a reputable firm, not as his private residence.He showed Popinot his ground-floor apartment—a place of simple, touching harmony that struck the judge. The pleasant weather and garden convinced Popinot that a madman would hardly produce such beauty. The Marquis said he intended to remain until his younger son finished his studies, then travel with the children to European capitals to further their education and languages. He explained his China project: Comte de Nouvion, returned to France without fortune, was his partner; the Abbé Grozier, Keeper of the Books at the Arsenal, had been his tutor and taught him about China. Though enamored of China’s history, he remained a French gentleman. Financially, they had two thousand five hundred subscribers—twelve hundred in France—the book would cost about three hundred francs, and the Comte would earn six to seven thousand francs a year from it; the hundred thousand francs he hoped to make would cover his children’s education and pleasures. Twelve years of retirement to educate his children had cost him his memory at Court and political prospects, but he believed he had purified his family’s past and secured a glorious future for his children—a noble, if secret, task.At that moment horses clattered in the courtyard. The two boys arrived, robust and sunburnt, dressed for riding. They greeted their father and the lawyer. Popinot needed no further evidence of the Marquis’ bond with his sons. The boys reported shooting success and that they had ridden in the Bois, where they had seen their mother. “Why didn’t you speak to her?” the Marquis asked. “I think I noticed, father, that she doesn’t want us to speak to her in public,” Clément whispered. A cloud passed over the Marquis’ brow; Popinot felt deep sympathy at the scene of fatherly devotion. “Justice may look in here at any time,” the Marquis said. “If anyone is mad, it can only be the children, who are a little crazy about their father, and the father who is very crazy about his children—but that kind of madness rings true.”Madame Jeanrenaud then entered, disregarding protests from the man-servant. “I take no roundabout ways!” she exclaimed. Bowing to those present, she announced she had come too late because the criminal judge was there. She declared that she and her son were of one mind to return everything, for their honor was at stake; they preferred to give back everything rather than cause trouble. “They must be as stupid as pans without handles to call you a lunatic,” she added. The boys clung to their father, alarmed.“Silence, madame,” Popinot interposed. The Marquis sent his children to the garden. Popinot addressed Madame Jeanrenaud: the payments made by the Marquis were legally due, though prompted by an expansive sense of honesty. If every possessor of confiscated goods were compelled to make restitution every century and a half, few legitimate owners would remain in France. The law allowed M. d’Espard to dispose of his income without accounting for it. A Commission in Lunacy is appropriate only when actions lack reason; here, the remittances were driven by the highest motives. She could keep the money without remorse and let the world misinterpret a noble action. Popinot declared he would rather see the Marquis crowned than subjected to a Commission. Madame Jeanrenaud applauded his defense, calling it judge-like and warmly praising his words.The Marquis extended his hand to Popinot, who pressed it gently; a private understanding passed between the humane judge and the noble, sublime man. “Monsieur le Marquis,” Popinot added, “from the first words of this inquiry, I regarded my clerk as unnecessary.” Leading M. d’Espard to the window, he said it was time to return home and that Madame la Marquise had likely acted under an influence the Marquis should counteract. Popinot left, the scene lingering with him. “Those rooms would suit me perfectly,” he thought. “If M. d’Espard leaves them, I will take up his lease.”The next morning, having written his report the night before, Popinot went to the Palais de Justice to pursue prompt justice. As he prepared to put on his gown, the usher said the President wished to see him privately. “Good morning, my dear Popinot,” the President greeted. “I’ve been waiting.” Popinot asked if something was wrong. The President called it a silly trifle: the Keeper of the Seals, with whom he had dined, had suggested it would be best if Popinot did not sit on the case. Despite Popinot’s protest that he had left Madame d’Espard’s house before tea, the President cited propriety—“Caesar’s wife must not be suspected”—and the need to avoid any appearance of conflict that might expose the Bench to journalistic malice. It was a matter of propriety, not discipline.Popinot was to signal his retirement from the case as a personal favor; in recompense, the President would secure the Cross of the Legion of Honor for him, long deserved. M. Camusot, a newly appointed judge from a provincial court, had been named in Popinot’s place. Popinot noted with ironic amusement Camusot’s pale ambition. He bowed and withdrew, choosing not to contest the implication.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############ ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyPIERRE GRASSOUBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDedicationTo The Lieutenant-Colonel of Artillery, Periollas, As a Testimony of the Affectionate Esteem of the Author,De BalzacPIERRE GRASSOUHave you felt unease and sadness in the crowded galleries since the revolution of 1830? The true Salon has vanished, overwhelmed by a multitude of artists. Once the Salon displayed only the finest works, honoring a chosen few and provoking passionate public debate; criticism even helped make names like Delacroix and Ingres. Now the excitement has faded: the public is numbed by sheer volume, exhibitions close quickly, and individual works lose impact. Before 1817 only a select number of paintings were shown; now galleries overflow, diluting quality and attention.The Salon’s essence is lost in this chaos. Instead of refined selection we face a tumultuous bazaar where great artists are overshadowed. Decamps’ notable works, for example, would have stood out more among fewer high-quality pieces than they do amid mediocrity. Ironically, despite more artists, talk of unrecognized genius has increased. When the Academy’s selection was strict, true talent was acknowledged; without that judgment, art suffers and the Salon risks perishing.Among the swelling catalogue of names stands Pierre Grassou, known as “Fougeres” to his acquaintances. In 1832 he lived in a cramped rue de Navarin apartment with a studio overlooking Montmartre. Modest and tidy, his studio reflected his careful nature and limited means. As a genre painter he avoided the burdens of historical subjects, concentrating on easel painting.One December morning Grassou, preparing to work, was visited by Elie Magus, a picture dealer. Magus bought Grassou’s paintings cheaply and proposed a business arrangement: Grassou would paint portraits of the bourgeois Vervelle family, eager to enter the art world. Their daughter had a substantial dowry, and Magus hinted that marriage could benefit Grassou financially.Grassou, living simply and solitary, was startled by talk of marriage. The idea of painting three family portraits, however, intrigued him as a chance to establish himself as a portraitist. Magus left to find the Vervelles, leaving Grassou to consider the opportunity’s potential.To understand Grassou’s situation we must look back. He studied under various masters—Servin and Schinner among them—but never fully grasped their techniques. His education was marked by a lack of recognition; he was often mocked for mediocrity. Gentle and diligent, he nevertheless failed to stand out among flashier or more eccentric peers.His first Salon attempt, a village-wedding scene in 1819, was rejected. Rather than despair, he sought Schinner’s critique, who bluntly advised him to give up painting. Still determined to improve, Grassou labored to refine the rejected piece, only to sell it to Magus for a meager sum. That sale began a long, difficult career characterized by perseverance against discouragement.As Grassou navigated his art life he remained committed to his craft, embodying the struggles and hopes of many contemporaries. His story reflects broader themes of ambition, recognition, and the harsh realities of the art world, where circumstance often overshadows talent.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For several days Pierre Fougeres lingered near Elie Magus's shop hoping his painting would attract notice, but it went unseen. After a week it vanished; when he asked, Elie said he had sold it and would buy more if Fougeres produced Flemish interiors, anatomy lessons, and landscapes. Overjoyed, Fougeres felt validated, missing the irony in Elie's manner that a more cynical artist might have detected.He met his friend Joseph Bridau but, consumed with painting, could not enjoy the evening. Back home he sketched furiously, inspired by newfound confidence, bought supplies, hired models, and devoted himself to his craft. After two months he produced four paintings. Schinner and Bridau dismissed them as derivative and suggested he try literature; undeterred, Fougeres retouched the canvases and sold them to Elie for twenty-five francs apiece, barely breaking even. He noticed a strange mist over his paintings in the shop but dismissed it as imagination.Over seven years he honed his skills, producing competent but not exceptional work. Elie sold the pieces, letting Fougeres earn a modest living. At the 1829 Exhibition, with friends' help, he entered a powerful piece—a young man in prison inspired by Gerard Douw—which drew significant attention, even from Charles X. Madame purchased the painting and commissioned more.Fougeres’s success rested on imitation rather than innovation, a lesson he learned well. Despite artistic criticism, his modesty and perseverance won him supporters; he gained a reputation for dedication. With his earnings he furnished a studio and completed commissions punctually, a rarity among artists.By thirty-seven he had produced around two hundred paintings, achieving a style that appealed to the bourgeoisie. Friends often preferred his character to his art and regretted his career choice, yet he still gave sound advice and maintained a keen appreciation for beauty.After the July Revolution he continued submitting work to the Salon, living frugally and managing finances with his notary Cardot. He dreamed of a comfortable life as an artist, aspiring to join the Institute and gain recognition.One day the wealthy Vervelle family visited his studio for portraits. The father, once a bottle-dealer, was impressed by Fougeres's Legion of Honor cross. The family admired his work, and Pierre was charmed by their daughter Virginie. On subsequent sittings they grew familiar, discussing their art collection and financial stability.During sessions Pierre flattered the family, endearing himself to them. The father boasted of their collection, while the notary praised Fougeres's character and prudence, stressing his ability to provide for a family. This reputation strengthened the Vervelles' interest. As sittings progressed, Pierre's feelings for Virginie deepened and he began envisioning a future with her, his personal hopes increasingly entwined with professional ambitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Fougeres began painting Mademoiselle Virginie, he was already accepted into the Vervelle household; their neat studio had become a second home. Near the end of the sitting Joseph Bridau burst in, disheveled and startling the family; he scanned the room, approached Grassou and, struggling with his coat, remarked, "Wood is dear," then asked for five hundred francs to placate creditors. Grassou agreed to write a note while Bridau criticized his painting style and urged him to embrace nature's imperfections.When Bridau left, Madame Vervelle fretted about the portrait, but Grassou reassured her he had only improved it. Intrigued by the artist's talent, the Vervelles invited him to their country house the next Sunday, hoping to dazzle him with hospitality and their paintings. Flattered, Grassou accepted and was warmly received at Ville d'Avray: shown their well-kept grounds, treated to a lavish dinner, and feted like a celebrated artist. Afterwards they led him to their picture gallery, eager for his praise of a collection they fancied rivaled King Louis-Philippe's.As Grassou examined the paintings, he was shocked to recognize many as his own works long ago sold to the dealer Elie Magus. Vervelle boasted the collection was worth three hundred thousand francs; Grassou, conscious of the irony, whispered that he had sold those pictures for far less. Impressed, Vervelle promised to double his daughter's dowry if Grassou could substantiate the claim. The revelation raised Grassou's standing with the family, who continued to regard him as a great artist; when he completed their portraits he gifted them, further cementing their bond.Today Pierre Grassou is acknowledged as a respectable portrait painter, earning about twenty thousand francs a year. He lives comfortably with his wife, who brought a six thousand franc dowry, and remains securely bourgeois, painting family portraits for five hundred francs each. His financial success bolstered his reputation; he even received the Legion of Honor for his conduct during civil unrest. Yet he harbors insecurities, convinced other artists mock his work and critics overlook him. He still strives for Academy recognition, while secretly replacing his poorer works with those of famed artists in the Ville d'Avray collection. His mediocrity, tempered by kindness and generosity, makes him a complex, sympathetic figure in the artistic community.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########### ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyEUGENIE GRANDETBy Honoré de BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Maria.May your name, that of one whose portrait is the noblest ornament of this work, lie on its opening pages like a branch of sacred box, taken from an unknown tree, but sanctified by religion, and kept ever fresh and green by pious hands to bless the house.De Balzac.EUGENIE GRANDETIIn some provincial towns certain houses give off a monastic melancholy; silence reigns, life seems frozen, and the routine is punctuated only by the solitary figure at a window. Such an air hung over a house in Saumur at the end of a steep, seldom-used street that climbed toward the château. The pebble pavement and three-century-old buildings, with great carved oaken beams, slate-covered façades, and worn window-sills, recalled antiquarians’ delight. Doors studded with large nails bore traces of history—Protestant marks or the insignia of forgotten bourgeois families—so that the street itself seemed to record France’s past.Beside a dilapidated house stood a country gentleman’s mansion, its stone arch faintly showing armorial bearings rubbed by revolutions since 1789. The merchants’ ground floors were not modern shops but dark rooms where goods were exposed plainly. A girl knitting might call to her parents to serve customers, who were attended with varying courtesy.In this region the weather shaped commerce. Merchants, wine-growers, and coopers watched the sky anxiously, fearing frost or rain that could ruin them. On Saturdays vineyards summoned their owners and the streets fell silent, where life unfolded plainly and neighbors observed one another closely.The old town of Saumur, scene of this tale, contained a particularly melancholy mansion owned by Monsieur Grandet. To grasp the house’s meaning one must know the man.Monsieur Grandet began life as a master cooper in 1789 and married the daughter of a rich wood merchant. With his savings and his wife’s dowry he bought prime vineyard land during the sale of church property, displaying shrewdness that would mark his rise. He grew in reputation as protector of local nobility and supplier to Republican armies, accumulating wealth and influence through timely purchases and cautious sales. By 1806 Grandet was mayor; that year he also inherited three fortunes, cementing his standing in Saumur. His estate came to include vast vineyards, farms, and an abbey, making him among the region’s wealthiest men.The townspeople speculated about the extent of his riches. He had a knack for timing sales to secure maximum profit, and his financial acumen made him both admired and feared. By 1816 estimates put his fortune at nearly four million francs, and many believed he kept an equal sum in cash. His reputation suppressed earlier mockery; his decisions came to be treated almost as community law. Frugality governed his household; expenditures were limited to necessities.Grandet’s manner was plain and unpretending. He spoke little and stuttered when pressed into long conversation—a seeming lack of eloquence that concealed calculation in business. He consulted his wife before committing to affairs, keeping her in a subservient state. Physically he was stout, with a round, crafty visage and simple, practical dress signaling thrift. Only a few had access to his house, among them a young magistrate, C. de Bonfons, who hoped to marry Grandet’s daughter, Eugénie.Social life in Saumur centred on competition for Eugénie’s hand, chiefly between the Cruchots and the des Grassins. The Cruchots, a powerful family, sought her for their nephew; the des Grassins pushed their son Adolphe. This rivalry buzzed through the town, with many guessing Grandet’s plans for his daughter. In early 1811 the Cruchots gained an edge when the estate of Froidfond was bought through negotiations arranged for Grandet; he paid in gold—an ostentatious act that further displayed his wealth and confirmed his position as a formidable figure in Saumur.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It is now clear what is meant by “the house of Monsieur Grandet,” a cold, silent dwelling above the town, sheltered by ruined ramparts. The entrance has two pillars and a tufa arch, a soft white stone worn to irregular holes that give the arch and walls a jail-like look. Above the arch is a crumbling bas-relief of the four seasons and a plinth where plants, even a small cherry tree, have taken root.The door is solid oak, brown and split, secured by iron bolts. A small rusted grating in the middle panel lets curious visitors glimpse a dark, slimy vault leading to a garden enclosed by thick, damp walls, remnants of the ramparts that border neighboring gardens.The principal ground-floor room is a large hall serving as antechamber, salon, office, boudoir, and dining room. It is where the barber cuts Monsieur Grandet’s hair and where farmers and officials conduct business. The hall, with two street-facing windows, is entirely wooden, with gray panels and exposed beams. An old brass clock and a greenish mirror adorn the mantel above a white stone chimney. Faded tapestry chairs furnish the room; in each corner are closets or buffets with dirty shelves.An old card table stands between the windows, topped with a barometer and two pastel portraits of Madame Grandet’s ancestors. Red gros de Tours curtains, a rare luxury, contrast with Monsieur Grandet’s frugality. A straw chair by the window lets Madame Grandet watch passersby; a work table and Eugenie’s armchair occupy the rest. For fifteen years, Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet’s lives have revolved around constant work; fires are lit only on specific dates, and they rely on a foot-warmer for warmth in chilly months.La Grande Nanon, the family servant, has been with Monsieur Grandet thirty-five years, earning only sixty francs a year but saving enough to invest in an annuity. Despite an unattractive appearance, she was welcomed into the household and worked tirelessly—cooking, washing, and managing domestic affairs with unwavering loyalty. Grandet’s rare acts of kindness, such as giving her an old watch, were regarded as monumental gestures.In 1811, during a difficult grape harvest, Grandet’s treatment of Nanon combined affection and control: he depended on her labor while she remained devoted. Nanon’s gratitude for employment and the meager kindnesses she received formed a bond both simple and complex, reflecting the dynamics of power and dependence in their relationship.The household displayed a stark contrast between Nanon’s careworn but efficient domain and the expectations of other servants in Saumur. Nanon’s kitchen was a model of cleanliness and order; she often worked late into the night and slept in a small closet-like space. The upper floors, in contrast to the hall’s modest comforts, were bare and unadorned.On a November evening in 1819, Nanon lit the fire for the first time that season, coinciding with Eugenie’s birthday. The family prepared for a gathering, with local notables arriving to pay their respects. Grandet presented Eugenie with a gold coin, a tradition he had maintained for years, while Madame Grandet gave her a new dress. The family’s finances and habits were steeped in thrift and preparation: Grandet urged Eugenie to save for her future marriage.As the evening progressed, the arrival of guests, including the des Grassins family, introduced a competitive tone. Madame des Grassins, a lively woman, and her son Adolphe presented gifts to Eugenie, sparking jealousy among other visitors. Interactions were laced with underlying tensions: compliments and attentions masked rivalry and calculation. Grandet observed the dynamics with a mix of pride and possessiveness over his daughter.The gathering, full of laughter and light games, concealed attendees’ deeper motivations as they subtly vied for a share of Grandet’s wealth. The scene blended familial warmth with cold calculations of social status, Grandet at the center, manipulating relationships for his benefit. The simplicity of Eugenie and her mother’s lives contrasted sharply with the materialism of their visitors, revealing the complexities of human relations and the hidden motives behind polite society.As the evening unfolded, a loud knock at the door signaled an unexpected interruption that would change the course of the night.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nanon took a candle and opened the door, followed by Monsieur Grandet. His wife, alarmed, called to him, and the players exchanged worried glances. Monsieur des Grassins suggested they all go, sensing something ominous. A young man arrived with a porter carrying trunks; Grandet brusquely told his wife to return to the game and left to speak with the newcomer.The players sat but did not resume. Madame des Grassins asked if the traveler was from Saumur; her husband said he was from Paris. The notary noted the time, confirming the diligence was on schedule. Abbé Cruchot asked if the gentleman was young; des Grassins confirmed he was, with heavy luggage.Eugénie remarked on Nanon's absence, and the president suggested the newcomer might be a relative. Madame Grandet gently urged them to continue, sensing her husband’s annoyance. Adolphe, trying to be discreet, hinted the young man might be a cousin, but his mother silenced him.Grandet returned alone; the traveler entered and received polite bows from the men and curtsies from the women. Madame Grandet offered him warmth, but Grandet, absorbed in a letter, dismissed her concern. The stranger, Charles Grandet, thanked Eugénie for her hospitality but insisted he was not tired.Madame des Grassins asked if he had come from the capital. Charles, adjusting his eyeglass, surveyed the room and its occupants. He noted the loto game that had been underway while Eugénie observed him with admiration and curiosity. At twenty-two, handsome and confident, he aimed to impress his provincial relatives with Parisian sophistication.Sent by his father to stay with his uncle in Saumur, Charles intended to display his fashionable attire and lifestyle. He brought luxurious clothing and accessories—a hunting costume, various waistcoats, a gold toilet set—and, despite his father's instructions to travel modestly, had taken a private coupé to flaunt his means.His elegance contrasted sharply with the provincial guests. The Cruchots, shabby and unkempt, were taken aback; locals reacted with both admiration and disdain at his nonchalance and fashion. Eugénie, captivated by her cousin's charm, longed for the world he represented; enchanted by his appearance and the delicate items he carried, she felt a keen desire to connect with him.The loto ended when Nanon returned to ask for sheets for Charles’s bed. Madame Grandet followed her; the guests, sensing the evening's end, began to leave. Eugénie, seized by a new impulse, excused herself to help her mother and Nanon prepare Charles’s room, eager to make it comfortable and elegant. She suggested improvements—a warming pan, a wax candle instead of tallow—using her birthday as an excuse to persuade Nanon.While Eugénie fussed over the room, Madame des Grassins engaged Charles, subtly vying for his attention and hinting at social opportunities in Saumur. He responded graciously, while the others speculated about his intentions and possible interest in Eugénie. Abbé Cruchot made sly comments that revealed the underlying tensions and ambitions among the guests.Grandet read a letter from his brother revealing a dire financial situation and pleading that his son be cared for. The letter described impending bankruptcy and hoped Grandet would shield Charles from the fallout. Grandet masked his emotions as he processed the implications; his demeanor shifted, showing the complex interplay of familial duty and personal greed.When the guests departed, Grandet offered his nephew a modest room, downplaying his wealth. The evening closed with speculation: Charles's presence could unsettle Saumur. The Cruchots and des Grassins recognized a common rival and resolved to unite to prevent Eugénie from falling for her cousin and to manipulate the situation to their advantage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the four relatives were left alone, Monsieur Grandet told his nephew Charles it was too late to discuss the matters that had brought him and set the household schedule: breakfast at eight, a light lunch at noon, dinner at five.
