In the end Joseph inherited Philippe’s estate and found success in his art, becoming Comte de Brambourg, yet remained humble, often reflecting on his family’s tragic fate and the lessons of their tumultuous lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ######################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Duchesse de Castries.CHAPTER IThe commercial traveller, a modern type, signals the shift from material enterprise to intellectual force: he links isolated local power with universal influence, equalizing products and spreading ideas from the capital to the provinces. A self-taught scholar and mimic, he knows the world superficially, reads habits and solvency at a glance, and must judge quickly for his trade. Abroad such agents sometimes show truer diplomacy than ambassadors, negotiating commercial treaties in taverns rather than salons. By persuasive language they import Parisian innovations into provincial life.One such traveller is Gaudissart. His olive-green overcoat and striped shirt announce his profession. An acrobat in commerce, he extracts wealth from the provinces with a flood of phrases. Persuasive, disarming, and eager for attention, he made his fortune in the hat trade, enchanting vendors and winning social favor. Jovial and festive, yet businesslike when needed, he masters diplomacy by adapting to his audiences.Until 1830 Gaudissart stuck to hats; the upheaval that year turned ideas into commodities. Writers began to sell thought and speculators followed. Gaudissart seized the change, taking a life-insurance commission and quickly learning the business. His employers praised him; newspapers sought him to sell subscriptions too. Confident in his persuasive talents, he agreed to sell both life insurance and periodicals.CHAPTER IIAfter a week steeped in Saint-Simonism and financial talk, Gaudissart prepared to travel in mid-April. Two commercial houses, however, tempted him back into hats with good commissions. Riding in a hackney with Jenny, a florist, he joked about refurnishing her room from his travels; she doubted such promises, prompting playful banter about his prospects.Gaudissart assured her he would earn more than before, aiming for a substantial income from insurance and subscriptions and measuring himself against prominent figures in politics and commerce. The next morning he left for the towns assigned him, stopped in Blois, and wrote to Jenny boasting of selling life insurance and, especially, the profitable "Children’s Journal."CHAPTER IIIArriving in Vouvray, Gaudissart expected his persuasive skills to prevail but met a provincial spirit resistant to new ideas. The Tourangians were witty, satirical, and hostile to Saint-Simonism. At the Soleil d’Or inn he encountered Monsieur Vernier, a local skeptical of commercial travellers and aware of Gaudissart’s reputation. Amused at the idea of humbling a top salesman, Vernier arranged a prank: he sent Gaudissart to Margaritis, a local reputed lunatic.Margaritis, obsessed with his wine and newspapers, began a conversation that quickly turned absurd. Despite Gaudissart’s efforts to explain life insurance, the madman’s eccentricities produced nonsensical replies. Gaudissart tried to make sense of Margaritis’s chatter and to sell his product, but met only distraction and mockery.CHAPTER IVSeated at home and drinking, Margaritis spoke delusionally while two women listened amused and Vernier watched, intent on humiliating Gaudissart. His erratic behavior—fixations on wine, newspapers, and peculiar views—thwarted any rational exchange. The talk devolved into misunderstandings and incoherent replies. Gaudissart patiently outlined the benefits of life insurance; Margaritis answered with unrelated absurdities, entertaining the onlookers and frustrating the salesman.Despite the absurdity, Gaudissart persisted, but the lunatic’s unpredictability made success impossible. The scene exposed the limits of persuasive rhetoric when confronted with deliberate mockery and provincial antipathy.CHAPTER VAfter prolonged banter, the innkeeper disclosed that Margaritis was indeed a lunatic and that Vernier had engineered the encounter to trick Gaudissart. Enraged at the insult, Gaudissart demanded satisfaction. The townspeople enjoyed the spectacle; Vernier refused to apologize, and tension escalated toward a duel. The innkeeper, fearing trouble, proposed mediation over breakfast.Ultimately the dispute was patched up: Gaudissart and Vernier reconciled, recognizing the quarrel’s absurdity. Gaudissart left Vouvray resolved to return to Paris and his usual work, with a renewed appreciation for provincial peculiarities and the necessity of reading local mindsets. On the journey back he reflected that commerce and human interaction require not only eloquence but discernment about whom one bargains with and when persuasion becomes folly.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyTHE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENTBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by James WaringDEDICATIONTo Monsieur le Comte Ferdinand de Gramont.MY DEAR FERDINAND,--If these lines endure, they will be a small token of gratitude for your efforts. You, the Hozier, the Cherin, the King-at-Arms of these Studies of Life, have enriched the “Human Comedy” with your heraldic designs. Your manual of French Heraldry, showing deep knowledge of the feudal spirit and learned symbolism, has greatly influenced me.Your old friend,  DE BALZAC.THE MUSE OF THE DEPARTMENTOn the skirts of Le Berry lies Sancerre, a hilltop town that charms travelers with its views. It overlooks the Loire flats, which at times flood, leaving rich alluvium though some stretches are ruined by sand. Set back from the river, Sancerre benefits from the nearby port of Saint-Thibault for shipping wine and goods.By the time of this story, suspension bridges at Cosne and Saint-Thibault have replaced ferries, a sign of progress since the upheaval of 1830 and the improvements favored by the House of Orleans.Sancerre’s steep, narrow streets within the Great Ramparts form the town’s arteries. Its chief industry is wine, producing vintages rivaling Burgundy and making Sancerre an important supplier to Parisian wine merchants. Below the town, villages such as Fontenoy and Saint-Satur recall the vineyards of Neuchâtel.Though picturesque, Sancerre’s military importance has declined and its prospects dim as trade shifts to the Loire’s right bank. With about 3,500 inhabitants it has fallen behind Cosne’s 6,000, showing a reversal of fortune over fifty years. Yet the town retains historic charm, magnificent views, pure air, and a friendly—if watchful—community, largely Calvinist but not Puritanical.Sancerre prides itself on notable natives like Horace Bianchon, a leading modern physician, and Etienne Lousteau, a successful journalist. Politically, the municipality is dominated by a few large landowners who manipulate elections. Locals once attempted to break this hold, but their conspiracy—born of vanity—collapsed through jealousy among its members.The proposed cure was unity behind a single champion for the next election, specifically one of Sancerre’s two prominent Parisian representatives. This was a progressive provincial idea at a time when parochial dignitaries had multiplied since 1830, making genuine statesmen scarce in the lower chamber.Though the plan’s origins were dubious, it sprang from the Superior Woman of the borough, driven by personal interests. To understand her ambitions fully, one must learn her past and her prospects.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Sancerre, around 1836, was home to a notable figure called the Superior Woman, long overlooked but now attracting local notice. At the same time two Sancerrois prospered in Paris: Etienne Lousteau, a writer, and Bianchon, a physician and professor. George Sand's influence produced "Sandism," a charming but excessive sentimentality that revealed many superior women in France who had previously remained out of public view.The Superior Woman of Sancerre lived at La Baudraye, a town-and-country house near Saint-Satur. The Baudrayes were a diminished branch of an illustrious family that had once joined the Crusades. Their story begins with a sheriff named Milaud, a former Calvinist who converted at the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes. King Louis XIV rewarded him with a title and fief, but later measures against heretics brought misfortune. Under Louis XV a Milaud de la Baudraye advanced, securing a military commission for his son, who died in battle leaving a child later favored by Louis XVI.In 1800, an ex-farmer-general returned from exile and bought La Baudraye out of vanity. His only son, Jean Athanase Polydore Milaud de la Baudraye, was frail—a product of his father’s excesses. His noble mother devoted herself to raising him until her death, which brought his father back to France. On his father's death Polydore inherited a fief, some money, and a collection of autographs. He survived by strict habits and Sancerre's healthy air. From 1802 to 1815 he expanded his vineyards but hesitated to press Parisian debts during the Restoration. After Napoleon's fall he tried to monetize his father's collection but failed and returned to Sancerre.In 1823, at forty-three, Polydore sought marriage to secure an heir. He chose Dinah Piedefer, a beautiful, educated, intelligent woman from a once-Calvinist family now fallen into poverty. Educated in a prestigious school, Dinah hoped to rise socially by converting to Catholicism to attract a suitable husband. The Archbishop of Bourges, seeing Dinah's promise, attempted to arrange a match, but families hesitated because of her beauty and intellect. When Polydore learned of her he hurried to Bourges just as Dinah's mother considered a wealthy suitor. Polydore sought the Cardinal's support to recover debts, but his blunt approach offended the Minister.Polydore’s marriage to Dinah stirred Sancerre, provoking speculation about his motives. Local elites, including President Boirouge, observed that Polydore was partly driven by jealousy of a rival, the handsome Milaud. Polydore’s financial dealings in Paris were fraught. He pressed debtors, including the Duc de Navarreins. A government agent proposed a bargain: Polydore could obtain a tax-collector post if he forgave the Duc’s debts. Tempted, he accepted and returned to Sancerre as a tax collector.After a brief tenure he went back to Paris, secured a series of lucrative appointments, and ultimately became a Royal Commissioner. By 1827 he realized a long-held ambition, buying the estate of Anzy and raising his regional standing.Dinah transformed La Baudraye into a pleasant residence. She laid out gardens, improved interiors, and maintained a refined, cultured atmosphere while keeping her husband’s financial maneuverings private, which added an air of mystery to him. As Dinah emerged socially, her beauty and intellect drew both admiration and envy. Her refined tastes and correspondence with a Paris friend kept her informed on fashion and culture, distancing her from provincial women. Her desire for meaningful conversation alienated local women, who found her superiority intimidating. Local men admired her, but the women resented and isolated her. Dinah's mother, Madame Piedefer, basked in her daughter's success, while Polydore reveled in having a Superior Woman as a wife.This dynamic shows how societal perceptions shape reputations: Dinah’s superiority became both a source of admiration and resentment, ultimately isolating her in a community unable to accept exceptionalism.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1825 Dinah de la Baudraye was accused of socializing only with men and became the target of criticism and misrepresentation. In a moment of anger she told a false friend, “I would rather have my bowl empty than with anything in it!”—a rash remark that backfired. Childless after five years of marriage, she suffered ridicule.Monsieur de la Baudraye was a frail man with thin legs who sometimes wore false calves and favored peculiar dress. Gentle and rarely contradicting Dinah, he allowed her to dominate conversation. Dinah, admired for both beauty and intelligence, drew gentlemen from nearby towns; she enjoyed the attention and grew adept at delivering prepared speeches informed by wide reading across varied subjects.Her charm created a protective aura that shielded her from much censure. Monsieur often excused himself from her long expositions, preferring walks or business. As she established herself, she championed the romantic arts, collected antiques, and decorated their home with Renaissance treasures; her husband tolerated and supported these interests while focusing chiefly on his land.She filled their house with a collection that impressed visitors and provided material for her talks on art and history. Hoping to elevate local culture, she tried to form a literary circle in Sancerre, but it degenerated into social gatherings—games and casual chat—rather than serious discussion, a disappointment to her. Admirers, including magistrates and a priest, were often diverted from intellectual pursuits, producing both admiration and frustration. One young man who yawned during a lecture fell from her favor, illustrating the strict expectations she set for companions.When Dinah turned twenty in 1826 suitors still courted her. She remained devoted to her role as wife, sheltered by her mother and the visible presence of admirers, which kept slander at bay. The jealousy among suitors amused onlookers and signaled that none dared leave her unguarded.Monsieur de la Baudraye recognized the leverage his marriage afforded and used it to obtain favors and legal victories through his connections. Local women speculated about the marriage’s nature, imagining hidden complexities. In reality, a slow, monotonous domestic drama played out at La Baudraye—one that might have remained private if not for the era’s appetite for exposing human relationships—and that tension helped shape Dinah’s seemingly virtuous early demeanor.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A young woman whose academic successes sprang from pride was unlikely to halt her promising trajectory. Monsieur de la Baudraye, though seemingly frail, proved an unexpected match for Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer. She wondered why he married a seventeen-year-old and what she might gain.At first he surprised her: he let her use five acres for pleasure and financed house improvements so she could buy furniture and redecorate in varied styles. Dinah struggled to reconcile his reputation for stinginess with this apparent generosity, but the illusion dissolved after his second trip to Paris. When she asked for supplies she learned he strictly allotted a monthly allowance and paid her mother a yearly sum, stressing caution because their winegrower income was uncertain.Dinah’s attempts to provoke jealousy produced only indifference; he stayed steady and unyielding. Frustration grew as she felt trapped in La Baudraye and Sancerre, longing for a more ambitious life. She briefly believed he might share such ambitions when he bought the estate of Anzy and applied for a patent of entail, and for a time she hoped for a brighter future. But their short reconciliation only revealed deeper contrasts: Dinah’s energy and potential clashed with Monsieur de la Baudraye’s lack of ambition and rigid routine.Over six years Dinah became increasingly provincial, losing individuality and spirit. The difference between Parisian and provincial life became clear: Paris offered a variety of social classes and opportunities, while the provinces confined women to a single, often uninspiring identity. Despite initial resolve, Dinah succumbed to mediocrity as the absence of stimulating company and the monotony of rural life dulled her aspirations.Her beauty and charm, once celebrated, faded as she adapted to local expectations. Her wardrobe shrank; she began making her own clothes and lost the elegance she once maintained. The provincial atmosphere stifled her creativity and intellect, and she adopted the mannerisms and attitudes around her, further diminishing her distinctiveness.A pivotal moment came when Dinah met her former school friend Anna, now a sophisticated Parisian. The comparison left Dinah feeling inadequate and despondent: Anna had learned to thrive while Dinah had only endured. In her anguish Dinah turned to poetry. Her piece "Paquita la Sevillane" drew attention in the provinces, revealing talent and offering a temporary escape, yet it also brought fear of exposure and backlash. Despite literary efforts, Dinah remained trapped, oscillating between dreams of fame and the suffocating routine that constrained her, her writing fueling a perpetual dissatisfaction.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After the Revolution of 1830, George Sand’s fame inspired French towns to celebrate local female talent, prompting many aspiring women writers to voice previously unacknowledged ideas. Monsieur de Clagny capitalized on this trend by publishing a posthumous volume by Jan Diaz, a young poet virtually unknown outside her home region. Two hundred copies were printed; many sold across departments, though some sent to Paris were lost in editorial offices. It later emerged that Jan Diaz was a pseudonym for Madame de la Baudraye—Dinah—who quickly became celebrated and even cast as a rival to George Sand. The provincial public, often polarized, lavishly praised work that might otherwise have been dismissed.Dinah had long felt unrecognized and sought fame to fill unmet ambitions. The late Abbé Duret had warned her about her husband’s character, but she resisted his counsel. Gradually she discovered her husband incapable of love and, at first slowly then clearly, felt the depth of his resentment. The Abbé had cautioned that offending him would invite vengeance rather than forgiveness.As her reputation grew, her domestic life felt increasingly constraining. Public sympathy for her plight deepened her despair. Her husband, outwardly civil, harbored a hidden rancor she eventually perceived. After a confrontation she vowed to stop writing and withdrew into desolation at Sancerre, where her absence from the literary scene was keenly felt.Despite fame, Dinah faced jealousy and opposition among local rivals. She had hoped to bolster Monsieur de Clagny’s political ambitions, but his electoral defeat left her disillusioned. The household’s troubles masked a deeper dissatisfaction that intensified her longing for freedom from marriage.At the Château d’Anzy Dinah contemplated her future and the possibility of meeting influential figures. She admired Lousteau, a journalist noted for his charm with women, and sought to draw him to Sancerre. Her efforts to engage another prominent man, Bianchon, initially met indifference.When Lousteau and Bianchon finally arrived—at first unrecognized—Dinah, eager to impress, invited them to her château and played the gracious hostess. In conversation she aired frustrations with provincial life: its monotony, narrow experiences, and the despair it bred, especially for women. She criticized societal constraints that stifled creativity and individuality and lamented culture’s centralization in Paris. Her frankness intrigued her guests, who appreciated her intelligence and wit.As the evening progressed, Dinah’s charm and candor captivated them, particularly Lousteau, who found her both fascinating and exasperating. Despite efforts to project superiority, she disclosed vulnerabilities that heightened his interest. The dynamic suggested a potential deeper connection as Dinah navigated attraction alongside ambition.In a lighter moment she asked for autographs, revealing her desire for validation and a tangible link to the literary world. Bianchon and Lousteau complied, leaving their marks in her album—a small symbol of the recognition she craved. The exchange highlighted the complexities of her character as she tried to reconcile aspiration with provincial realities.Ultimately, Dinah’s journey exemplifies the struggle of a woman caught between societal expectations and the pursuit of personal fulfillment. Her interactions with Lousteau and Bianchon catalyze self-discovery as she grapples with her identity as a writer and a woman in a restrictive environment. The narrative captures the tension between ambition and domesticity and the challenges women face when carving out their own paths in a world that limits their possibilities.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At five the next morning the Chateau d’Anzy was astir; young La Baudraye had organized a day of sport for the Parisians largely to display the twelve hundred acres he planned to reclaim at great cost and profit.“Why hasn’t the Public Prosecutor joined us?” Gatien Boirouge asked Monsieur Gravier.“He said he had to sit today; minor cases are before the Court,” Gravier answered.“Did you believe that?” Gatien exclaimed. “My father says Monsieur Lebas won’t come early because Monsieur de Clagny asked him to sit in his place!”“Really?” Gravier replied, troubled. “And Monsieur de la Baudraye has gone to La Charité!”“Why do you meddle?” Bianchon asked Gatien.“Horace is right,” Lousteau added. “You worry like provincials; it’s a waste of time.”They went on through the woods. At a copse Gravier left the men with a keeper to take a ravine, while the others reached a clearing.“We should wait for Monsieur Gravier,” Bianchon proposed.“You may be a great physician,” Gatien answered, “but you don’t know provincial life. By now he’s probably at Anzy for breakfast.” He checked his watch. “He’ll be just in time.”“At the chateau with Madame de la Baudraye and Monsieur de Clagny. I can’t think of them alone. Two of them will watch each other. Dinah will be guarded.”“Ah, so Madame de la Baudraye hasn’t decided?” Lousteau remarked.“Mother thinks so,” Gatien said. “But I fear Monsieur de Clagny has bewitched her. If he gets a chance at the Keeper of the Seals, he’ll hide his faults in eloquence. Madame de la Baudraye is ambitious; she dreams of Paris.”“But what interest have you?” Lousteau asked. “If she loves the Public Prosecutor—”“You in Paris meet different women every day,” Gatien replied. “In Sancerre, with hardly six women—and five pretending to be virtuous—a young man of twenty-two can guess her secrets when she treats him with consideration.”“Consideration? Is that what you call it here?” Lousteau smiled.“I’d think Madame de la Baudraye has too much taste to bother with that ugly ape,” Bianchon said.“Let’s set a trap for the Public Prosecutor,” Lousteau proposed. “After dinner we’ll tell stories of betrayed wives and avenged husbands—burnings, hewn or cut to pieces. We’ll watch Madame de la Baudraye and de Clagny for any sign.”“Good idea,” Bianchon agreed. “One of them must betray something by expression.”“I know an editor,” Lousteau continued to Gatien, “who shows his wife tales of lovers boiled or baked to frighten her—‘See what the smallest error can lead to!’”“Madame de la Baudraye is innocent; I acquit her,” Bianchon said. “She’d have to deceive too many people—mother, husband, maid, housekeeper. Plus her husband never ‘quits her.’”“We can remember a few stories to make Dinah quake,” Lousteau said. “Be stern in the hunt; be casual but observant. This morning we’ll hunt the hare; this evening we’ll hunt the Public Prosecutor.”That evening Lousteau triumphed in a smaller, more winning way: he returned an album to the lady with an elegy—melancholy verses signed Etienne Lousteau, September 1836, Chateau d’Anzy. Clagny suspected the poem was recent.“You wrote those since yesterday?” he asked.“Indeed I did, as I followed the game,” Etienne feigned, though the reader suspects he had known the lines for years. Madame de la Baudraye looked upon him with the pity genius evokes; Clagny, seeing her expression, was inwardly enraged at the sham "Jeune Malade." He sat down to play backgammon with the cure of Sancerre while the Presiding Judge’s son placed a lamp that illumined Madame de la Baudraye as she took up her embroidery. The three conspirators—Lousteau, Bianchon, and Gravier—sat close by.“For whom are you decorating that basket?” Lousteau asked.“For Monsieur de la Baudraye,” she replied.The Public Prosecutor glanced slyly, as if to say, “I have lost my paper basket!”“Why may we not think him happy in having a lovely wife, happy in her decorating his baskets?” Lousteau persisted.“If ever I marry, I hope twelve years after my wife’s embroidered baskets may still be for me,” he added.“And why should they not be for you?” she said, fixing her fine gray eyes on Etienne with invitation.“Parisians believe in nothing,” Clagny said bitterly. “You Paris authors turn on adultery—farces, dramas—”“Come, Monsieur the Public Prosecutor,” Etienne laughed, “I left you to play in peace; now you bring an indictment against me. If you condemn love in literature, you must condemn Homer, Milton—Eve and her serpent are symbolical adultery.”“You’ll be burning many works,” said Clagny.Etienne, nettled by Clagny’s superiority, provoked him with paradox. “Suppose we are Public Prosecutors of all ages: Christianity could be seen as infected at its fountain if Joseph’s wife were judged adulterous by earthly eyes. Pilate and Anytus represented social orders defending themselves—Socrates and Jesus were criminals to aristocracies. If such judges triumphed, there’d have been a ‘ninety-three’ in Rome or Attica.”“Your argument is blasphemy,” Clagny said.“I grant it,” Etienne replied. “History can’t be suppressed. Pilate and Anytus defended social interests. Today’s Public Prosecutors are as logical as those judges.”“If there were many men like you in France—all government would be impossible.”“And there would be no religion at all,” Madame Piedefer interjected, making faces as the talk grew heated.“You are paining them,” Bianchon warned Lousteau. “Do not talk of religion.”“If I were a writer,” Monsieur Gravier said, “I should side with the luckless husbands. Among deceived husbands there are dramatic, energetic men.”“You are right,” Lousteau said. “I never thought deceived husbands ridiculous. Do you not think a husband’s confidence sublime?” Bianchon asked. “He trusts. But if he trusts, you mock him; if jealous, you hate him. What’s the happy medium?”“If Monsieur de Clagny had not just condemned immoral stories, I could tell you of a husband’s revenge,” Lousteau said.Clagny threw the dice and dared not look up.“A story from you!” cried Madame de la Baudraye. “I should hardly have hoped for such a treat.”“It was told me—delightfully—by Charles Nodier,” Etienne began.He narrated a tale after the 18th Brumaire about a young Chevalier de Beauvoir, captured and sent to the Castle of l’Escarpe. The Corsican commandant treated him well; his wife took a fancy to Beauvoir. Jealousy led the commandant to confine the prisoner in fetters. Secretly aided by a turnkey who provided a file and rope, Beauvoir feigned escape when it was a trap: the rope would’ve dropped him onto rocks. When the jailer came to inspect, Beauvoir struck him, donned his clothes, mimicked his walk, and walked out the gate, escaping.The lawyer and Madame de la Baudraye felt no application of this narrative to themselves; the conspirators exchanged surprised looks at the perfect composure of the two supposed lovers.“Oh! I can tell a better story,” said Bianchon.At Lousteau’s cue, Bianchon told La Grande Bretèche—the Spanish tale of a grandee walled up and starved to death in a cupboard by a jealous husband—which stunned the company. Madame de la Baudraye mused on love’s force. Monsieur Gravier then related a Spanish adventure: at a Madrid ball a surgeon-major was abducted by a waiting-woman and made to help deliver a secret birth so a husband would not know. The operation failed to save the infant; the surgeon, after peril, was given diamonds by the lover—later stolen by his servant. The waiting-woman, poisoned, warned him before dying. Later, the surgeon awoke to find the husband holding an amputated arm with a mole; in fury the husband stabbed him to the heart. Clagny interjected that half of Paris’s startling crimes would outdo these tales.“Do you suppose such adventures could occur now, in France?” Madame de la Baudraye asked.“Of the ten or twelve startling crimes committed annually here,” Clagny said, “many rival such romantic details. The Gazette des Tribunaux is a bad Press abuse, but true stories abound. In Saint-Pierre-des-Corps at Tours, a widow, zealous in piety, confessed to murdering her husband, salting and cutting his body into pieces and throwing them into the Loire; the head remained in a cask. She was sentenced and executed.”“And do you suppose,” Bianchon asked, “we know all tragedies behind private life’s curtain? Human justice is ill adapted to judge crimes between husband and wife. It can intervene as police but knows little of the heart.”“The victim has often been the tormentor so long,” Madame de la Baudraye observed, “that the crime might seem almost excusable if the accused could tell all.”The Parisians remained puzzled about Dinah’s position. After the guests withdrew the bachelors lingered in the passages for a council. Monsieur Gravier was briefed: their aim had been to probe Madame de la Baudraye. Lousteau warned her serenity could indicate cunning; Gravier proposed a simple test—seal the bedroom doors of Madame de la Baudraye and of the Public Prosecutor with hairs affixed to wax, country spycraft, to see if anyone entered or left at night. That night the doctor, the journalist, the receiver of taxes, and Gatien, barefoot, applied seals and agreed to return at five.At dawn they found the hairs unbroken on both doors.“Is it the same wax?” asked Gravier.“Are they the same hairs?” Lousteau asked.“Yes,” Gatien replied.“This alters the matter,” cried Lousteau. “You’ve been chasing a will-o’-the-wisp.”Gravier and Gatien exchanged glances of puzzlement. Lousteau whispered to Bianchon that if Dinah was virtuous, she deserved the effort—he pictured storming a fortress that had resisted Sancerre’s besiegers for nine years.With this in mind, Lousteau sought out his hostess in the garden. She wished to converse.“You were out shooting yesterday,” Dinah said. “This morning I’m puzzled how to amuse you; unless you’d like to come to La Baudraye to study provincial life—”“That simpleton Gatien told you I said I adored you,” Etienne said. “Your silence at dinner betrayed a kind of innocence we never commit in Paris. I plotted those stories to see if I could rouse you and Monsieur de Clagny. Be at ease; your innocence is proved. If you fancied that magistrate, you’d lose value in my eyes—I love perfection. You cannot love that cold, taciturn usurer who’d leave any man for twenty-five centimes. At twenty-eight, handsome, well-conducted, and childless—Monsieur de la Baudraye expresses virtue admirably.”By confronting her bluntly, Lousteau cut through sentimental subtleties and solicited the kind of reply women sometimes offer to flattering audacity. Madame de la Baudraye, true to the reputation of a Superior Woman, consoled him: “You have sought pleasure but never loved. True love often comes late.”“I can still believe in love,” he said, “but I have ceased to believe in woman. Perhaps I have defects that hinder me from being loved; I’ve often been thrown over. I may have too strong a feeling for the ideal.”She was pleased; he represented Paris’s daring axioms—the city’s artless depravity and advanced convictions. Etienne, performing, scored a success. She spent one of her most delightful days with Lousteau and Bianchon, who told anecdotes about great men—sayings likely to be the Ana of the century—novelties to provincial ears. Lousteau flattered her by disparaging a great Berry celebrity, suggesting she could rival that writer. Flattered, she warmed to Etienne; Clagny, Gravier, and Gatien mistook her amiability for affection.That evening the exaggerated reports drew sixteen persons to Anzy—coaches, wagonettes, and bachelors on hired horses. By seven the drawing-room glittered; Dinah had prepared the furniture like a hostess triumphant. Lousteau, Bianchon, and Dinah exchanged looks as the town’s notables arrived.What invalided ribbons, ancestral laces, and imaginative caps the assembly displayed! The Presidente Boirouge extracted medical advice from Bianchon, who gravely prescribed a daily cup of tea for her “English malady.” Rumors circulated that Dinah had summoned him not about elections but to ask why she had no children.A footman brought in letters and a printer’s packet; Lousteau left proof sheets for Bianchon. Dinah, seeing the parcel, exclaimed at literature’s pursuit of him.“What is it?” asked Etienne.“A fascinating romance on a spoiled proof used to wrap yours,” Bianchon said, offering “Olympia, or Roman Revenge.”Lousteau read aloud fragments: brigands in a cavern, Rinaldo, Lamberti, a Duchess’s glove, Cardinal Borborigano—then the page broke. He mocked local color blunders and speculated on plot and authorship with comic erudition, improvising commentary: Rinaldo as a man of spirit, the Duchess possibly guilty, the Cavaliere Paluzzi a figure. Dinah, delighted, said she could picture the Roman scene: a murdered husband and a wicked wife haunted by discovery.The assembly was captivated. Lousteau continued, teasing them with missing pages and the mechanics of printing—a waste sheet printed on one side only, the vexations of making up a volume. He and Bianchon joked about style, vignettes, and the probable literary status of the author. From aging grandees to curious youths, the guests drank in his reading and mock-critique, amused and bewildered by the fragments of romance and by the city wit that reframed them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For three days Dinah’s imagination was stirred by the talk of her two Parisian visitors. Lousteau watched her growing distraction, waiting for the moment she would be wholly captivated. Dinah wanted to show La Baudraye to her guests, and the charade began when Bianchon’s papers were left behind in Anzy. Gatien rode off to fetch them while Madame Piedefer went shopping, leaving Dinah alone with Lousteau and Bianchon. They sat close and rode together, mocking the local men and debating the unhappy circumstances of her life, leaving Dinah defensive.Crossing the Loire, Bianchon told her plainly she had shut herself against love but now needed it. Dinah, taken aback, asked if he prescribed love; he warned that her present way of living would make her unattractive. Lousteau half-jokingly defended Bianchon, saying his counsel was for her good.At Cosne a crowd gathered near the old carriage bearing Louis XIV’s arms. Dinah insisted they disembark; she took Bianchon’s arm and walked briskly along the Loire. Bianchon suggested Lousteau loved her but was frivolous and hard to hold; better to befriend him, he said, since Lousteau could bring her beauty and happiness. Under Bianchon’s scrutiny Dinah felt vulnerable and uncertain about competing with Lousteau’s past lovers.Their coachman announced Gatien’s return. Bianchon blessed them as they entered town. Lousteau then took Dinah’s hand and declared his love, but she resisted, asking if she should give herself to a man who regarded her as a passing pleasure. Gatien’s arrival complicated things: Lousteau whispered that she should pretend resistance to preserve his honor and, in a reckless moment, crushed her muslin dress, leaving her disheveled.Gatien, oblivious to the tension, rode up, and Lousteau tried to distract him. Dinah urged Gatien to fetch Bianchon’s papers from the inn; he complied reluctantly. Lousteau urged them to hurry back to La Baudraye, claiming Dinah was unwell. Humiliated, Dinah retreated inside, overwhelmed, and Lousteau followed, begging forgiveness.Madame Piedefer, anxious at Dinah’s distress, appeared; hearing Gatien’s horse Dinah fled to her room. Outside, Lousteau and Gatien confronted one another; Gatien accused Lousteau of trickery, which Lousteau shrugged off, suggesting Dinah had mocked Gatien. Dinah changed into a velvet gown; when she rejoined the men, Gatien awkwardly commented on her dress. Lousteau explained the change as a response to the cool air; Dinah blushed and her mother glared. Conversation turned to Bianchon, whom Lousteau praised, and Dinah, weighed down by events, stayed silent.Determined to win her, Lousteau declared he wished to stay in Sancerre to be near her and swore he had left Parisian vices behind. On the road back to Anzy his charm began to pierce Dinah’s reserve; she laughed, a sign her feelings were shifting. During dinner he was buoyant, sometimes lost in thought imagining happiness with Dinah. After coffee the women left the men to stroll in the gardens; Lousteau seized the moment to speak to Dinah, who was now more receptive.The next day Lousteau learned he must soon return to Paris. He and Dinah visited the elderly artist restoring the château’s coat of arms; Lousteau found the old man’s plans amusing, while Dinah begged for one more day with him. They exchanged pledges of affection, and Dinah accompanied him to Cosne, disregarding social proprieties. Back at La Baudraye she praised Lousteau, saying he had shown her what true love felt like. Her three admirers, including Monsieur de Clagny, were left to reckon with their feelings; Dinah made Clagny a confidant, lavishing affection on him while he remained devoted despite her new happiness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Back in Paris Lousteau quickly forgot the happy days at the Chateau d’Anzy. He lived by his pen, and in this new bourgeois era writing felt like servitude: to live by the pen required constant creation, or at least the appearance of it. He wrote daily for a newspaper and several literary magazines but lacked artistic integrity, placing him among Paris’s literary hacks. Deprived of literary pretensions and advancement, he had settled into journalism.Still, his life was not unpleasant. He moved in circles of blue-stockings, aspiring actresses, publishers, and authors, and could sustain a lifestyle that demanded little beyond paying the rent. He kept theatre boxes and the sale of books he reviewed covered minor expenses. His days were social, and his writing the price of that easy existence. Yet after ten years of struggle he remained as financially constrained as when he arrived, still nursing the belief that a monthly income of five hundred francs would make him rich.Lousteau lived in well-furnished ground-floor rooms in the Rue des Martyrs, rented for twelve hundred francs. His cash flow predictably ran thin in January, April, July, and October, when rent and other outlays drained his resources. He spent freely on cabs, meals, and gifts for his lovers, which kept him perpetually broke despite earning seven to eight hundred francs a month.Occasionally weary of this literary life, Lousteau would reflect with friends such as Nathan and Bixiou about their futures, joking about marriage and romantic entanglements. He kept a mistress, Madame Schontz, proud to have a writer as her lover; she lived nearby and delighted in teasing her acquaintances about their relationships. These bohemian details mattered because they would later affect Dinah: he could laugh about his affairs, including the one with Dinah, whom he had nearly forgotten.When Bixiou asked about his time in Sancerre, Lousteau recounted encounters with three provincial men too timid to pursue a local beauty. He had received a long, affectionate letter from Dinah, which he read and then burned, dismissing her feelings. A week later another letter arrived and he discarded it unread. Madame Schontz noticed the scented paper, examined hampers Dinah had sent—game and flowers—and suggested they host a dinner where the gifts might be used. Lousteau wrote back a clever but insincere letter he believed would charm Dinah, though by December he had stopped reading her correspondence; letters simply piled in a drawer.Then Madame Schontz announced a potential marriage opportunity. A young woman had fallen into trouble; her father, a notary, wished to marry her off quickly, offering a substantial dowry. Her previous lover had died, and the notary sought a respectable man to take her name. Madame Schontz pressed Lousteau to consider the match, stressing the financial benefits and a comfortable life, while warning about the mother-in-law—a strict woman likely to scrutinize his past. Despite reservations, she assured him the marriage would leave him free and secure.Initially hesitant, Lousteau agreed to meet the notary and his family. He flattered the notary, invoked memories of his own father, and presented himself as a man of ambition. The notary liked Lousteau’s charm and background, and he was soon introduced to the Cardot family as a prospective son-in-law. At his first dinner the atmosphere was dull, but Lousteau noticed Félicie, the notary’s daughter: shy, gracious, and genuinely grateful for his willingness to marry her. Madame Cardot regarded him as a man with promising prospects and was pleased by his apparent morality.Buoyed by these prospects, Lousteau felt a new purpose. He imagined a life of stability: a charming wife, respectable family ties, and the financial security the dowry promised. Ready to embrace this new chapter, he believed the match would open greater ambitions and opportunities.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Cardot wanted to see the prints for Gil Blas, an illustrated volume, and borrowed the book to visit her future son-in-law, Lousteau, unexpectedly, hoping to learn about his lifestyle from his bachelor rooms. Her sister-in-law, Madame Camusot, had been apprehensive about the match, having heard unflattering things about Lousteau from her husband, a Supreme Court Councillor.One January day in 1837, Madame Cardot and her daughter Félicie took a cab to return the book and inspect Lousteau’s lodgings. The porter was absent, but his daughter handed over the key after Madame Cardot slipped her a gold coin. Around noon Lousteau, returning from breakfast, saw Dinah, his former lover, arrive in a coach and was stunned when she revealed she had a child. He tried to send her away, but she insisted on staying. Pamela, the little girl in the apartment, sensed the awkwardness and told Lousteau that his mother-in-law was inside. When he entered, he found Madame Cardot and Félicie hiding in a corner.Dinah declared her love; Lousteau fretted about complications. Madame Cardot confronted him, expressing clear disapproval of him as a potential son-in-law, and left with Félicie. Dinah remained, trusting Lousteau’s reassurances. Seeing Dinah’s high-minded sacrifice, Lousteau began to imagine using her to escape the arranged match he’d been facing.Dinah, charmed by the apparent elegance of Lousteau’s rooms, did not perceive their poverty. Pamela borrowed dinnerware from Madame Schontz to help feed her, and Schontz soon learned of Lousteau’s predicament, warning him that Cardot would not press the match. Lousteau, however, was distracted by his new life with Dinah and enjoyed their time together.When Bixiou, a friend, arrived he challenged Lousteau for abandoning a wealthy marriage for Dinah. He warned that provincial women often fail to adapt to Parisian life and will bore and disappoint a man used to the city. Lousteau dismissed him, insisting he loved Dinah and valued her over any fortune. Bixiou pressed the practical side: marriage secures legitimacy and social stability, and Lousteau was risking both.Back with Dinah, who grew distressed and questioned coming to Paris, Lousteau reassured her, even as the precarious reality of their situation pressed in. Madame Schontz later informed him that Cardot had arranged Félicie’s marriage to another man, ending Lousteau’s prospects of a secure alliance.Despite losing that future, Lousteau became more entangled with Dinah, who believed in their love. They settled into a dependent domesticity: Lousteau kept Dinah entertained and reliant on him, while their passionate affair slid into the very provincial, stifling romance he had once mocked. In the end both were caught in a cycle of dependency and illusion, unprepared for the challenges ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This winter in Paris was for Madame de la Baudraye what October had been in Sancerre. Etienne, eager to introduce Dinah to Parisian life, took her to the theater, where she at first felt self-conscious and feared recognition from acquaintances in Sancerre. Lousteau, vain and ambitious, helped her shed provincial modesty by taking her to fashionable dressmakers and introducing her to Parisian society. Dinah quickly adapted to the city's vibrant life, immersing herself in its culture and entertainments.One morning a newspaper article reminded her of Sancerre, mentioning Monsieur le Baron de Clagny's promotion. Dinah joked with Lousteau about his lingering affection, revealing the depth of their passion, which still felt fresh and enchanting. They exchanged extravagant declarations, promising to die for each other if their love faded. Lousteau, however, secretly planned to deceive Dinah when he grew tired of her, viewing her as a valuable asset.Despite her love, Dinah kept her dignity and ambition. She was more than a charming companion: intelligent and well read, she could hold her own with the most sophisticated women. Lousteau perceived her worth, seeing in her the potential to be both muse and partner.As Dinah's pregnancy showed, she prepared to retreat from public life. Before doing so she attended the premiere of a play by Nathan, feeling both excitement and anxiety. The evening soured when she sat in a box beside Anna Grossetete, an old acquaintance, and Lousteau abandoned her to socialize. Dinah felt the weight of scrutiny and discomfort, unable to hide her unease.When Clagny visited her at the theater he expressed concern for her reputation and warned about the consequences of her liaison with Lousteau. Dinah remained resolute about her happiness and the sacrifices she'd made for love. Clagny's visit stirred nostalgia and regret, but she stayed committed to her choices.After the birth of her child, Dinah became a devoted mother and housekeeper, resolved to support Lousteau's career. She pawned her jewelry to help with finances, believing her sacrifices would secure a better life for them. Lousteau's financial irresponsibility and laziness soon became apparent, and Dinah bore the household burden.Despite efforts to help him, Lousteau continued to struggle with writing and debts. Dinah's love fueled her determination to help him succeed, but she felt the strain. As she worked tirelessly to keep their home, Lousteau grew increasingly indifferent and dependent on her.The arrival of a second child increased Dinah's burdens. Lousteau's debts mounted and he became more detached from domestic life, seeking solace with friends and other women. Feeling the weight of her sacrifices, Dinah began to question whether their relationship was sustainable.Tensions rose, and Dinah's jealousy and insecurity surfaced. She tried to regain control by confronting Lousteau about his behavior and their finances, but her efforts often led to conflict. She struggled to maintain dignity while managing the complexities of their love.The turning point came when Dinah's estranged husband, Monsieur de la Baudraye, reappeared seeking a power of attorney to manage an inheritance. The encounter forced her to confront her past and the reality of her situation. Clagny, now Attorney-General, intervened to protect her interests, negotiating an allowance from her husband in exchange for her cooperation.As Dinah grappled with betrayal and loss, she reached a crossroads. Lousteau's lack of ambition and commitment weighed on her, and she realized her sacrifices might not be reciprocated. The love that once felt like refuge began to feel like a burden, and she questioned whether she could keep supporting a man unwilling to fight for their future.In the end, Dinah's journey through love, sacrifice, and self-discovery revealed the complexities of her relationship with Lousteau. She learned true love requires not only passion but mutual respect and commitment. Facing motherhood and the consequences of her choices, Dinah emerged stronger, determined to reclaim her identity and find her own path in a world that often sought to define her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day on Etienne lived in luxury, and Dinah could compete with the best-dressed women in Paris. Lousteau, however, pretended despair to his friends, claiming ruin by Madame de la Baudraye and insisting no one could free him from Dinah because her love was so deep she would hurt herself if he asked. Despite this, he remained unfaithful. Monsieur de Clagny, distressed by Dinah's disgrace, warned her of betrayal; she answered, “I know it.” When Clagny asked if she still loved Lousteau, she declared passionately, “I would lose my soul for you!” — a confession that left Clagny speechless and in tears.Dinah gradually perceived Lousteau’s true nature. She described him to her mother as a poet: lazy, pleasure-seeking, yet someone she could not hate because she felt responsible for him, believing she was his only support. “Oh, my Dinah!” her mother cried, “what a hell you live in! What gives you the strength to persist?” Dinah replied simply, “I will be a mother to him!”She made compromises to endure, feeling the full weight of her dishonor only when confronted by others. She resolved to emulate Madame de Pompadour to keep his affection; love became a burden. Lousteau’s insincerity and staged devotion deepened her degradation, yet she clung to the hope his love might return and found a perverse joy in being his plaything.She often rehearsed the tragedy Le Dernier Jour d’un condamné, imagining their separation, but each word or gesture from Lousteau drew her back. She even contemplated suicide while walking in her garden, yet her devotion persisted. The romance of Adolphe guided her; she vowed not to become like Ellenore, refusing tearful reproaches.Meanwhile Monsieur de la Baudraye schemed to reclaim fortune: he manipulated his wife’s inheritance—masking its value and pocketing the difference—invested in land for future returns, and considered himself free of debt to Dinah, planning to restore her fortune later. He imagined himself prominent in Sancerre, aspiring to title and nobility.By early 1842 Dinah felt like a reserve for Lousteau, sacrificing herself for his comfort. She adopted mourning attire to signal remorse; her mother, worried, tried to intervene. Reality proved far from literary drama; it was fatigue and disillusionment.With Clagny’s help, Madame Piedefer urged Dinah to consider life apart from Lousteau, suggesting her husband would allow her to keep the children and live in Paris. Dinah left negotiations to Clagny, who assured her the husband would support her return to respectability.Unknown to Dinah, Monsieur de la Baudraye had secretly invested in a luxurious Paris residence to elevate his status, aiming for a peerage and a respectable image with a well-furnished home. He wrote to Dinah, inviting her to live with him in Paris and promising financial support for their children’s education.Now a Countess, Dinah felt the weight of her title and its expectations, preparing for social prominence that contrasted with earlier struggles. On a fateful day in May 1842 she settled her debts and invited Lousteau to dinner, signaling a decisive turn.When Lousteau arrived he was struck by her transformation. She told him of her new status and declared their separation, expressing enduring affection but insisting on a calm parting. Lousteau accused her of ruining his life; Dinah stood firm, asserting independence. The exchange turned bitter; Lousteau lamented his lost potential and Dinah refused to accept blame. Their final words mixed sorrow and defiance. Dinah returned home both liberated and heartbroken.In her new life Dinah transformed her home into a model of comfort, spending more than her husband expected. Monsieur de la Baudraye, returning to Paris, was pleased with her efforts, unaware of the emotional turmoil beneath her polished exterior. Though charming and fashionable, Dinah felt a void, missing the passion and excitement of her past.As she entered a new social circle, Dinah was haunted by memories of Lousteau. Despite success she struggled to bond with other women and felt isolated. Clagny, ever supportive, tried to introduce her to influential figures, but she remained unfulfilled.After a theater evening Dinah confided to Clagny that she still felt for Lousteau. He revealed Lousteau was living with another woman, stirring conflicting emotions in Dinah and prompting her to throw herself into society, seeking success and recognition.In March 1843 Dinah was appointed to collect donations for a charitable cause — a turning point in her public image. Her efforts were acknowledged and she began to regain social standing, though emotional scars lingered and she often reflected on her choices.Seasons changed and Dinah’s life evolved. She returned to Sancerre for the summer where her husband doted on their children; she found herself torn between past and present. Relationship dynamics shifted as she faced motherhood and social expectations.In June 1844 Monsieur de la Baudraye proudly introduced their children to a cousin, underscoring the family’s status. Dinah, composed, grappled with complexities, balancing roles as mother, Countess, and a woman shaped by sacrifice and longing.The narrative closes unresolved: Dinah inhabits a dual existence, torn between love and ambition, public success and private yearning — a portrait of passion, social pressure, and personal sacrifice that defines her journey.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Eugene-Auguste-Georges-Louis Midy de la Greneraye Surville, Royal Engineer of the Ponts et Chausses. As a testimony to the affection of his brother-in-law, De BalzacCHAPTER I. ONE OF MANY CHEVALIERS DE VALOISAcross France there were many Chevaliers de Valois; one in Alençon is the subject here. Though sharing the name, these chevaliers rarely knew one another; they accepted the Bourbon monarchy, recognizing that Henri IV succeeded because the Valois left no male heir. The Alençon chevalier was an elderly, tall, thin gentleman whose Parisian youth and amorous pursuits were interrupted by the Revolution.Respected among local aristocracy for manners, wit, and anecdotes of Louis XV’s reign, he was a regular at gatherings. At fifty-eight he insisted he was fifty, preserving a sprightlier air than his years suggested. He had a large nose and a pale complexion, reddened on one side—an odd “hot liver” explanation he used to excuse a hearty appetite.Seemingly frail but vigorous and passionate, his face showed the marks of a full life. He groomed himself carefully and upheld aristocratic standards: his clothes, though worn, were always clean. Living modestly in a rented room above Madame Lardot’s laundry, he dined out daily and depended on a small annuity. Beloved for card-playing and amiability, he conversed easily across ranks; his discretion and charm made him desirable company. Yet curiosity surrounded his private life, including rumors of a secret marriage to a woman named Césarine.Despite precarious finances, he maintained dignity and the reputation of a gentleman, navigating provincial life with grace and tact.CHAPTER II. SUSANNAH AND THE ELDERSOne spring morning the chevalier was visited by Suzanne, a young worker from Madame Lardot’s laundry. She brought news that could bring pleasure and pain, feigning indifference to his rumored link with Césarine. Ambitious and shrewd, Suzanne wanted to go to Paris to better herself.The chevalier, touched by her charm and drive, encouraged her in a kindly, paternal way and advised she court Monsieur du Bousquier, a wealthy bachelor who had shown interest. He recognized how beauty and wit could secure a favorable match and counseled her on social strategy.The narrative then turns to du Bousquier: once wealthy and influential, now back in Alençon, he hoped to marry Mademoiselle Cormon, a rich old maid, to restore his fortunes. His past as a purveyor during the Revolution had tarnished his standing; he represents brusque opportunism rather than aristocratic grace.Du Bousquier and the chevalier contrast sharply—the chevalier as the old regime’s refined elegance, du Bousquier as the new order’s opportunist. Their subtle rivalry over Mademoiselle Cormon and their interactions with Suzanne intertwine, revealing the provincial dance of desire, ambition, and social expectation, and illuminating broader themes of class, gender, and the pursuit of happiness in a changing France.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Granson, a widow living on a modest pension and on property she owned, struggled to support her son Athanase, who added a small salary from the mayor’s office. Their provincial home was simple and marked by scarcity; that poverty shaped both their daily life and their morals. Athanase, a pale, introspective twenty-three-year-old, carried a deep passion for his cousin, Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he believed to be wealthy. His feelings were complicated by social expectations and the risk of ridicule for loving a woman older than himself.What began as a practical calculation—to secure a better life for himself and his mother—quietly became genuine affection. Athanase admired Mademoiselle Cormon from afar, at once drawn to her and constrained by his circumstances. His talent and potential went largely unrecognized in their provincial setting, where private ambition too often collided with harsh realities and limited opportunity.Madame Granson, conscious of her son’s attachment, recognized the possible advantages of a match with Cormon despite Athanase’s lack of fortune and the cousin’s age. She remained hopeful and patient, waiting for a moment to advance his prospects. All the while Athanase’s thoughts were consumed by fantasies of a future that seemed blocked by class, reputation, and the small-town gaze.The arrival of Suzanne, a young woman from a more precarious background, introduced a new dynamic. She felt an immediate sympathy for Athanase, sensing his struggles and aspirations, yet her own ambitions drove her toward deception as a vehicle for escape. After a brief encounter with Madame Granson, during which she secured money and new clothes, Suzanne left town; the community lamented her departure and the scandal it implied.As Athanase prepared for a decisive dinner with Mademoiselle Cormon, his mother urged him to present himself well, hoping to improve his odds. Unaware of the full scope of his mother’s plans, he navigated love and ambition under the weight of provincial constraint. Suzanne’s flight to Paris became a turning point—her attempt to redefine herself away from limits shared by many women of the time—while Athanase remained in Alençon, grappling with unfulfilled dreams and the heavy hold of his love as their fates continued to intertwine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In many second-class prefectures of France there exists a salon that gathers influential—but not elite—members of the community. Its hosts are prominent locally and invited to events, yet they do not mix with the highest social echelons, who keep their distance and communicate by formal invitation.This salon, frequented by lesser nobility, clergy, and magistrates, wields considerable local influence. Its members are indifferent to luxury and fashion, focused on practical concerns such as land acquisition. They resist outside influences, especially from Paris, and their gossip often functions as judgment. Young women are seldom present; when they do appear it is usually to seek family and public validation.In Alençon the upper classes assembled at the house of Mademoiselle Rose-Marie-Victoire Cormon, an unmarried woman whose fortune was coveted by relatives and two old bachelors. She lived with her uncle, a former grand-vicar, and her family had long served the nobility. Through advantageous marriages they acquired a semblance of aristocracy despite a fundamentally middle-class status.Her house, dating from the time of Henri IV, provoked envy by its prime location and careful upkeep. The mansion’s courtyard with a neat flowerbed testified to her meticulousness and insistence on cleanliness. Robust architecture and decorative elements recalled its historical standing.Inside, the house was arranged for reception: a salon hung with family portraits and a dining room that exuded provincial charm. The salon was a hub of social life—card games and conversations about local affairs took place there. Mademoiselle Cormon’s circle numbered about 150 people, mostly mature, who rarely traveled and were firmly rooted in provincial life.Wednesdays were special: she hosted dinners that drew a larger assemblage. The women dressed modestly and the atmosphere was one of familiarity and routine. Conversations centered on local gossip, land disputes, and judgments about aristocratic behavior, reflecting the community’s concerns and values.Despite her wealth and standing, Mademoiselle Cormon remained unmarried largely because she wished to marry a nobleman and because of the era’s constraints. The political upheavals from 1788 to 1815 made suitable matches scarce—many eligible men were too old or too young or lacked the qualities she sought. Fear of being married merely for her fortune led her to set subtle tests for suitors, designed to expose mercenary intentions without revealing her motive.As she aged, her longing for love intensified, yet she remained oblivious to some genuine attachments, including that of Athanase Granson, a suitor whose affection she failed to recognize. Her innocence and naivety produced missed opportunities; over time her character hardened and the weight of social expectations grew heavier.Although pious and virtuous, she became a figure of local ridicule; her unmarried state turned into a proverb. She lived modestly, shunning ostentation, and her considerable fortune did not exempt her from scrutiny—attempts at marriage were viewed skeptically. Occasional returns of disbanded soldiers and officers offered glimmers of hope, but none met her standards. Her yearning for companionship and motherhood gradually became a source of despair.Mademoiselle Cormon’s story illuminates the complexities of love, social expectation, and the particular challenges faced by women in her position. Her life—marked by a deep desire for connection and a struggle against restrictive norms—serves as a poignant commentary on happiness and fulfillment in a world governed by rigid class conventions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################To complete the picture of Mademoiselle Cormon’s household, we must consider her and the Abbé de Sponde alongside their loyal servants: Jacquelin, Josette, and Mariette, who all contributed to the comfort of the uncle and niece.Jacquelin, a short, stout man with a ruddy complexion, had served the household for twenty-two years, doing everything from waiting at table to grooming the mare Pénélope. He was in love with Josette, a thirty-year-old maid whom Mademoiselle would dismiss if she married him. The couple quietly saved their wages, hoping Mademoiselle’s marriage would free them to wed. Josette, plump and practical, was said to rule her mistress. Mariette, the cook, had been with the family for fifteen years and was skilled in preparing local delicacies.Pénélope, the old brown-bay mare, attracted affection and conversation, since Mademoiselle Cormon, lacking children, directed maternal instincts toward the animal. The servants and the mare kept a steady routine that reflected long-standing roles. Mademoiselle, however, grew increasingly difficult to please; her idle mind fixated on trivial matters. She fretted over household linens and the timing of Pénélope’s meals, turning small disputes into a sense of purpose.Her life revolved around the household, her uncle, and her religious duties, which magnified the minutiae of existence. Worried about her health, she obsessed over minor ailments and dietary choices. Each season she visited her estate at Prébaudet to oversee apple trees and prepare for harvest, marking the occasion with a farewell dinner that was a notable event in Alençon.On the day of her departure the town buzzed with gossip about her and potential suitors, especially M. du Bousquier, whom she had rejected. The farewell dinner was full of social maneuvering; Mademoiselle Cormon tried to engage guests but often struggled to find the right words, her lack of wit amusing the sharper attendees.As the evening progressed conversation turned to local politics and the controversial du Bousquier, rumored to be involved with a young woman named Suzanne. In defending him, Mademoiselle made a remark that unexpectedly drew laughter and admiration, showing she could contribute—if unintentionally—to the evening’s entertainment.The dinner closed with light banter about du Bousquier’s character, revealing a town divided. Mademoiselle’s innocence and naivety were plain as she navigated social complexities; when the guests departed, talk continued of her prospects and the implications of du Bousquier’s behavior. The evening left her contemplating her role in the community and her own desires, setting the stage for future developments where love, ambition, and societal expectations would continue to shape relationships in Alençon.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day after arriving at Prebaudet in her old carriole with Josette, Mademoiselle Cormon’s plans shifted when Jacquelin brought a letter from her uncle, the Abbé de Sponde. It explained that Monsieur de Troisville, a grandson of one of the abbé’s friends, wished to stay in Alençon and required hospitality. Alarmed by the request’s urgency, she returned to town immediately despite the rain, driven by excitement and the hope of a favorable match.Their arrival in Alençon caused a stir. Josette and Jacquelin were left to manage the household while Mademoiselle Cormon focused on preparing for the viscount’s visit. News of his impending arrival spread, and gossip swirled. Worried that her old-fashioned house and appearance might disappoint, she even sacrificed prized liqueurs to make a better impression.When Monsieur de Troisville arrived he was received by the abbé and Mademoiselle Cormon. He looked distinguished, and she felt an immediate attraction. Over dinner he complimented her home and the region, making her feel noticed and desirable. The atmosphere changed, however, when the Chevalier de Valois and other curious guests arrived. During introductions the viscount casually revealed he was married. The disclosure devastated Mademoiselle Cormon; she fainted and was carried to her room by Du Bousquier while the townspeople watched and speculated.Recovering, she realized her hopes of marrying the viscount were crushed. The next day news of her fainting and his marital status swept through Alençon, provoking a blend of laughter and sympathy. Pressured by reputation and desirous of restoring her standing, she weighed her options as both Du Bousquier and the Chevalier sought her favor.In the end she accepted Du Bousquier’s proposal as a means to regain social standing, stipulating that he agree to a public lie about their engagement—a condition he readily accepted. As they prepared to announce it, the Chevalier de Valois arrived unaware of the arrangement. The evening closed with Mademoiselle Cormon feeling a mixture of triumph and uncertainty as the town buzzed over her engagement, marking a new chapter in her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The news of Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage devastated Athanase Granson, though he concealed his turmoil. He first heard it at Chief Justice du Ronceret’s house, where his mother played cards. Athanase had long pinned his hopes on Mademoiselle Cormon; the announcement felt catastrophic. Shaped by an Imperial education that bred fatalism, he drifted into indifference and despair. His mother noticed his pallor but assumed he was merely preoccupied with work.After the marriage was announced, Athanase wandered the Sarthe region, finding bitter solace in its overlooked beauty. He withdrew from politics and society, becoming a figure of little interest to townspeople who remained unaware of the depth of his suffering. His mother worried and speculated that he might be contemplating a major project, never grasping the true cause of his withdrawal.Suzanne, a young woman inspired by romantic tales and adventure, returned to Alençon intending to help Athanase financially, though their paths did not meet. The town buzzed with gossip about Mademoiselle Cormon’s match, with conflicting reports about its legitimacy. Eventually the marriage contract was signed and the wedding took place amid ominous signs — a priest accidentally read from the De Profundis — setting an uneasy tone.The marriage of Mademoiselle Cormon and du Bousquier lacked celebration; the couple soon retreated to Prebaudet. Du Bousquier, intent on modernizing the Cormon estate, began renovations that alarmed locals who feared losing their provincial charm. Athanase, consumed by grief, ultimately took his own life, leaving a letter his mother misread as evidence of newfound independence rather than the despair it expressed.Athanase’s suicide shocked Alençon. Madame Granson was left to mourn privately, grappling with the full weight of her loss that others barely comprehended. The Chevalier de Valois revealed Athanase’s unrequited love for Mademoiselle Cormon, deepening the town’s sense of tragedy. Madame Granson’s grief was profound; she alone understood the true nature of her son’s struggles and bore them in silence.Madame du Bousquier returned to Alençon and encountered Madame Granson in a meeting heavy with unspoken words. Madame Granson sought solace in the church and implored the rector for a proper burial for her son; the ceremony was conducted discreetly to avoid scandal, reflecting the community’s desire to contain the episode.As time passed, Madame du Bousquier’s marriage became gossip and cautionary tale. At first she appeared content, but the reality was different. Du Bousquier, outwardly successful and politically ambitious, proved a tyrant at home. The marriage brought social advantage but left her feeling powerless and unfulfilled; they had no children, and her longing for motherhood deepened her pain.The Chevalier de Valois, embittered by the match, sought revenge against du Bousquier, whose conduct made him a figure of ridicule. Public mockery at Mademoiselle Cormon’s misfortune gave way to growing sympathy for Madame du Bousquier as the true hardships of her life became known. The chevalier’s schemes only widened the rift between du Bousquier and the local aristocracy, intensifying animosity rather than restoring honor.Madame du Bousquier lived amid contradictions: she performed the duties of a devoted wife while inwardly resenting her husband’s oppression. The marriage, beneficial in status, left her emotionally desolate; she clung to duty even as she mourned the life she might have had.The Chevalier de Valois’s death coincided with sweeping political changes in France, marking an end of an era and creating a void for those who had known him. Suzanne, seeking to honor the past, purchased a small memento from the chevalier’s estate, a symbol of lost connections and vanished possibilities.Ultimately, Madame du Bousquier’s life illustrates the complexities of love, duty, and social expectation. She remained both pitied and admired — enduring the consequences of her choices and reflecting on what might have been — while the narrative exposes how private suffering and public reputation intertwine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Baron Von Hammer-Purgstall, Member of the Aulic Council, Author of the History of the Ottoman Empire.Dear Baron, your encouragement of my “History of French Manners in the Nineteenth Century” led me to associate your name with my work. As a representative of studious Germany, your approval lends credibility to my efforts. Your dedication to research sustained me in my labor on modern civilization. I hope this token of respect finds you well in Dobling, and reminds you of your sincere admirer and friend.DE BALZAC.THE COLLECTION OF ANTIQUITIESIn a small French town stands the house called the Hotel d’Esgrignon, home of the old Marquis Charles Marie Victor Ange Carol. What began as a jest in naming became earnest over time. The d’Esgrignon family, descended from powerful Northmen, preserved a proud lineage and provincial nobility, refusing titles that would diminish their status and never bowing to kings or commoners. Revered locally, they maintained dignity despite long neglect by the court.During the Revolution the d’Esgrignons were imperiled. The Marquis, respected in the countryside, avoided emigration but went into hiding. Family lands were sold; yet Mlle. d’Esgrignon managed to save some property through the family steward, Chesnel. After the Terror the Marquis returned to a dilapidated castle, mourning lost heritage. The old Hotel d’Esgrignon had been demolished; Chesnel used the last of the Marquis’s money to buy a new home for the family, wryly named the Hotel d’Esgrignon.Around 1800 émigrés returned, including the Baron de Nouastre and his daughter, whom the Marquis married. She died in childbirth, leaving a son to carry the family name. Grief compounded the Marquis’s sense of diminished circumstances. Later, when a local contractor proposed to Mlle. d’Esgrignon, she and her brother found the offer insulting; this incident drove a wedge between them and Chesnel, who had acted on the contractor’s behalf.Chesnel, a respected notary, was far more than a servant: confidant, manager, and protector of family interests. The altered dynamics troubled him; the Marquis regarded Chesnel as a loyal companion, while Chesnel felt the strain of fidelity to a declining house. Politically the era shifted—some nobles aligned with Napoleon while others remained monarchist—and the d’Esgrignons held fast to their principles.By 1804 many émigrés had returned, yet the d’Esgrignons’ fortunes did not recover. The Marquis became a symbol of loyalty and old nobility, a leader among remaining royalists. His salon, nicknamed the “Collection of Antiquities,” became a gathering for those clinging to the past; what was once a playful sobriquet took on grave meaning as political tides turned.The family faced challenges from the rising bourgeoisie, epitomized by du Croisier, who sought to undermine their standing. Rivalry intensified as his faction gained strength while the d’Esgrignons remained a bulwark of old-world values. By 1822 their situation remained precarious: the Marquis, now elderly, was uncertain of his role in a changing society; the Restoration had not restored prosperity, and income barely met needs. The Collection of Antiquities stood as a testament to a bygone era and the d’Esgrignons’ enduring legacy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Marquis neglected estate management, and most found the idea that the d’Esgrignons could regain their former glory absurd. Hopes centered on Victurnien, the handsome heir, marrying into nobility to restore prestige. In truth the family lacked the political connections and finances to reclaim status; their continuity had been broken over the preceding forty years.Only Chesnel, the notary, grasped the futility of such hopes. He recognized the social changes of the Revolution and the altered political landscape. Loyal to the family, he nevertheless urged adaptation; the old Marquis, however, remained convinced that fate or divine intervention would restore their fortunes.Victurnien, adored by his father and aunt, had been a spoiled child who never learned opposition. That indulgence made him selfish and reckless. The Chevalier, a relic of the old order, fed his extravagance with romantic tales of past grandeur, inflating his sense of superiority. Surrounded by flatterers and exempt from ordinary constraints, Victurnien came to regard himself as above the common people, deepening both moral and practical flaws.When he entered society, the gap between his upbringing and modern realities became clear. His behavior alienated townspeople; escapades produced legal troubles Chesnel discreetly managed. Encouraged by companions who enjoyed his company, his arrogance hardened into excess, assuming lineage would shield him from consequences.Du Croisier, a rival, sought to exploit Victurnien’s weaknesses and hasten the d’Esgrignons’ ruin.
