Meanwhile Victurnien’s vanity and appetite for pleasure pushed him into gambling and frivolity, sustained by Chesnel’s financial sacrifices, which he accepted as his due. Sent to Paris with high expectations, he soon discovered the court far less welcoming than imagined; the nobility he met were largely self-interested and indifferent to reviving the d’Esgrignon name.Undeterred, he tried to secure a place by adopting the extravagant lifestyle of Parisian elites. His charm and looks opened doors; he became a fixture in high society, most captivated by the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Enchanted by her beauty and the glitter of her world, he fell intensely in love, blind to her debts and convinced their passion was pure.As he immersed himself in Parisian pleasures his debts mounted beyond his means. Companions who enjoyed his company also recognized his precarious state and egged his spending, entertained by the prospect of his fall. Chesnel, ever loyal, continued to prop him up financially but grew increasingly alarmed. Despite warnings, Victurnien remained absorbed in fantasy, oblivious to reality.Victurnien’s passage through Parisian high society exposes the dangers of vanity, excess, and the illusion of nobility. His entanglement with the Duchesse and the accumulating deceit and financial ruin underscore the fragility of status and the consequences of living beyond one’s means, offering a cautionary tale about pride and the need to recognize limits in a changing world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1823 the Kellers had advanced Victurnien two hundred thousand francs, without Chesnel or Mlle. Armande knowing; Chesnel had also paid two thousand crowns to conceal the source. Victurnien wrote deceitful letters to his father and aunt, who remained unaware of the disaster looming over their house. Only du Croisier knew, and he took pleasure not only in ruining the d’Esgrignons but in dishonoring them; he savored his approaching revenge as Victurnien’s debts mounted.Du Croisier’s first move targeted Chesnel, who lived modestly in the Rue du Bercail. One afternoon, after dinner, Chesnel’s housekeeper announced du Croisier. The old lawyer’s heart sank as du Croisier presented a bill for two hundred and twenty-seven thousand francs, demanding payment or threatening to expose Victurnien’s obligations. Overwhelmed, Chesnel realized he could raise the money only by mortgaging his lands. Du Croisier seized the chance, proposed to buy Chesnel’s property—and did so within two days. Stripped of his lands, Chesnel was left with only his practice and his house, consumed by worry for Victurnien’s future.Chesnel dreaded telling Mlle. Armande about the family’s financial ruin. She had just received a charming, hypocritical letter from Victurnien describing his travels in Italy with the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse and remained enchanted and oblivious. Chesnel’s arrival shattered her reverie: all was lost, and Victurnien risked bringing dishonor on their house. Devastated, she realized they had given Victurnien too much freedom.The Marquis, having read Victurnien’s letter, remained unaware of the crisis. Mlle. Armande and Chesnel shared a somber understanding of their concern for the young Count. They discussed intervening to save him from his reckless lifestyle, fearing he would be left destitute. Mlle. Armande resolved to go to Paris to rescue Victurnien, masking the trip as a medical consultation to avoid gossip.Chesnel gave her his last bag of louis, and she set off, determined to confront the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Du Croisier, meanwhile, had arranged his revenge to ensure Victurnien would be cut off from further support. He wrote to Victurnien, informing him that the Kellers would advance no more money without security, timing the letter to coincide with Victurnien’s increasing desperation.Victurnien, until then living in luxury, suddenly faced the reality of his debts. Despite earlier gambling wins, he found himself unable to procure funds and felt the weight of ruin. In despair he briefly considered suicide but rejected it. Instead, he contemplated fleeing with the Duchess and forging a bill of exchange to finance their escape, though he hesitated, wanting the Duchess’s agreement first.When he confessed his financial troubles to the Duchess, she assured him money could be found and agreed to escape with him. Buoyed by her promise, Victurnien forged a bill for three hundred thousand francs and presented it to the Kellers, who advanced the money, unaware of the deceit. Du Croisier, learning of the transaction, realized his plan for revenge was bearing fruit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Victurnien received the money he gave it to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, who locked it away and suggested one last visit to the Opera. He considered confessing everything to Chesnel but, despite doubts, lingered at the Duchess's behest; her enchanting glance kept him longer than he intended.The next day he returned to the Hotel de Maufrigneuse intending to plan an escape, only to find the Duchess resolved to stay. She believed it would be more remarkable to face the storm than to flee, and Victurnien, torn between admiration and despair, realized how deeply he loved her.After their argument Diane's coldness left Victurnien distraught. He begged forgiveness, but she remained unyielding—an angelic indifference that intensified his anguish. As time passed his despair deepened; he felt lost and unable to think clearly. Meanwhile the Count had taken Diane's hand but found it lifeless, a moment that intensified the household's distress.Victurnien’s personal turmoil was compounded by the discovery that his financial misdeeds had put him in serious trouble. When Josephin informed him that a warrant for his arrest had been issued, he panicked, convinced he was ruined. Josephin urged him to flee to the Hotel du Bon la Fontaine, where Mlle. Armande waited. Clinging to his distraught but determined aunt, Victurnien discussed hiding and the possibility of slipping into Chesnel’s house unnoticed. In executing the plan he unwittingly walked into a trap.Chesnel, meanwhile, was selling his practice to a rival notary, M. Lepressoir, who hoped to profit from Victurnien's misfortune. Learning of the young man’s plight devastated Chesnel; fearing for Victurnien’s future, he even contemplated taking the blame for the forgery himself. Resolving to act, Chesnel decided to confront du Croisier, Victurnien's creditor, and negotiate a settlement.Du Croisier, however, reveled in his power over the d’Esgrignon family. He saw the affair as an opportunity to assert dominance and settle scores with the aristocracy. At a gathering at his place, news of Victurnien's arrest spread among guests who were both shocked and intrigued; the atmosphere grew tense as allies discussed the implications and the potential trial.Realizing the gravity of the situation, Chesnel sought to influence the examining magistrate, M. Camusot, by appealing to his ambitions and connections, hoping this would mitigate damage to Victurnien's reputation. As legal proceedings unfolded, Chesnel intensified his efforts and turned to Mme. de Maufrigneuse. Initially unaware of the forgery’s full extent, she felt compelled to help once she understood Victurnien's love and the consequences he faced.Negotiations with du Croisier proved difficult: he demanded not only financial compensation but political favors and social recognition—requests Chesnel could scarcely satisfy. In desperation Chesnel offered a substantial sum to have the charges withdrawn, but du Croisier’s pride and ambition made him resistant. Both sides were unwilling to yield, and the conflict escalated as the trial approached.Chesnel’s determination drove him toward morally questionable options, including lying under oath, as he realized the d’Esgrignon family’s honor hung in the balance. The legal battle thus became emblematic of broader tensions between aristocracy and bourgeoisie: its outcome would decide Victurnien's fate and signal shifting power dynamics. Throughout, Chesnel’s unwavering loyalty and willingness to navigate provincial politics highlighted the complexities of love, honor, and ambition in a world where social status could dictate destiny.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Blondet, a competent judge, was eclipsed by his passion for gardening—especially pelargoniums—which consumed his time and ambition. His marriage to a much younger woman, who had an affair with the local prefect, further clouded his career. Though he had a legitimate son, Joseph, Blondet preferred his illegitimate son Emile, whom he ultimately sent to Paris with scant support after his wife’s death.His modest brick house and neat garden reflected his horticultural devotion. Despite his favoritism, Blondet planned to leave his estate to Joseph and had disinherited Emile, whom he regarded with contempt. Emile, however, thrived in Paris thanks to help from his mother’s family, notably Mlle. de Troisville, who promised patronage and aided his literary recognition—successes Blondet never learned of, absorbed as he was in law and gardening. Blondet’s life was defined by frugality, enforced by a maid who ran the household.Joseph was being groomed for the bench and spent time with the Blandureau family, while Michu, a younger lawyer, moved through social circles and often outshone the older judges. Meanwhile the public prosecutor, a shrewd politician, became entangled in a scheme against the d’Esgrignons, producing a forgery charge against the young noble Victurnien d’Esgrignon.The President of the Tribunal, seeking advantage for du Croisier—a rival of the d’Esgrignons—manipulated events. Influenced by the President’s wife, the prosecutor pursued the case against Victurnien despite potential political costs.As the trial approached, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse intervened, providing funds to secure Victurnien’s release. With Chesnel, the family notary, supporting the defense and restoring the family’s standing, the trial exposed the truth: Victurnien was exonerated and du Croisier’s intrigues laid bare.Afterward the d’Esgrignons endured social scrutiny but retained their honor. Victurnien’s future remained uncertain as he confronted his past and lineage’s expectations. The narrative probes ambition, familial loyalty, and shifting social ranks in a changing France, with characters like Chesnel embodying fading feudal devotion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyTHE LILY OF THE VALLEYBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Monsieur J. B. Nacquart, Member of the Royal Academy of Medicine.Dear Doctor--Here is one of the most carefully hewn stones in the second course of the foundation of a literary edifice which I have slowly and laboriously constructed. I wish to inscribe your name upon it, as much to thank the man whose science once saved me as to honor the friend of my daily life.De Balzac.THE LILY OF THE VALLEYENVOIFelix de Vandenesse to Madame la Comtesse Natalie de Manerville:I yield to your wishes. It is the privilege of the women whom we love more than they love us to make the men who love them ignore the ordinary rules of common-sense. To smooth the frown upon their brow, to soften the pout upon their lips, what obstacles we miraculously overcome! We shed our blood, we risk our future!You exact the history of my past life; here it is. But remember this, Natalie; in obeying you I crush under foot a reluctance hitherto unconquerable. Why are you jealous of the sudden reveries which overtake me in the midst of our happiness? Why show the pretty anger of a petted woman when silence grasps me? Could you not play upon the contradictions of my character without inquiring into the causes of them? Are there secrets in your heart which seek absolution through a knowledge of mine? Ah! Natalie, you have guessed mine; and it is better you should know the whole truth. Yes, my life is shadowed by a phantom; a word evokes it; it hovers vaguely above me and about me; within my soul are solemn memories, buried in its depths like those marine productions seen in calmest weather and which the storms of ocean cast in fragments on the shore.The mental labor which the expression of ideas necessitates has revived the old, old feelings which give me so much pain when they come suddenly; and if in this confession of my past they break forth in a way that wounds you, remember that you threatened to punish me if I did not obey your wishes, and do not, therefore, punish my obedience. I would that this, my confidence, might increase your love.Until we meet,Felix.CHAPTER I. TWO CHILDHOODSTo what genius fed on tears shall we one day owe that most touching of elegies—the tale of tortures borne silently by souls whose tender roots find stony ground in the domestic soil, whose earliest buds are torn by rancorous hands, whose flowers are nipped at the moment of their blossoming? The tale of the child whose lips must suck a bitter breast, whose smiles are checked by a stern eye—this would contain the true history of my childhood.What vanity could I have wounded as a newborn? What moral or physical infirmity caused my mother’s coldness? Put to nurse in the country and forgotten for over three years, I returned to a parental roof where even the servants pitied me. My brother and two sisters found amusement in tormenting me. The compact among children to shield each other’s trivial faults was void in my case; often I was punished for my brother’s faults and not permitted to prove the injustice. The fawning spirit of children led them to join in persecutions to keep in the good graces of a mother they feared. Perhaps imitation, testing of power, and lack of pity combined to deprive me of fraternal sweetness.Disinherited of affection, I could love nothing though nature had made me loving. Rather than hatred, my crushed feelings condensed and hollowed a depth from which, later, they gushed upon my life. Instead of relaxing into fear, those tortures developed in me a certain strength, a habit of moral resistance. Always expecting sorrow, my being wore a sullen resignation that smothered the grace of childhood and gave me an appearance which seemed to justify my mother’s prophecies. The certainty of injustice prematurely roused my pride—fruit of reason—which checked some evil tendencies that such an upbringing fostered.Though my mother neglected me, she sometimes spoke of my education as if desirous of attending to it. Such prospect chilled me; daily intercourse with her would have been torture. I blessed the neglect that let me stay alone in the garden, play with pebbles, watch insects, and gaze at the sky. My contemplative bent began early and was fixed by an incident that illustrates my troubles. The governess, Mademoiselle Caroline, once falsely accused me of turning on a garden faucet to hide my being out after dark; I was punished and forbidden from the garden. Such tyrannical restraints intensified my passion for the forbidden star I watched; often whipped for it, I told the star of my troubles in the inward prattle of childhood. Long after I left school I still watched that star with indescribable delight—so deep are early impressions.My brother Charles, five years older, was my parents’ favorite, robust, handsome, with a tutor. I, puny and sickly, was sent at five as a day pupil to a town school. I went with a scanty lunch while my comrades had plenty, and their taunts made me feel my difference. Once, thinking a comrade generous, I reached for a dainty cunningly withdrawn, to the delight of his companions; I fought to escape persecution, but became hated and distrusted. Alone and gloomy, I was thought sullen by our master, and my mother soon transferred me to Pont-le-Voy, to an Oratorian school for “Latin steps” where torpid lads lingered.There I remained eight years, seeing my family rarely, living like a pariah. With only three francs a month pocket-money, I lacked pens, ink, and playthings. Unable to buy stilts or join games, I was driven into solitude. The distribution of prizes when I won two valuable awards—theme and translation—but neither parent present, was a profound humiliation: amid general acclamation I burst into tears and burned my crowns in the stove that night. My parents being near yet absent from that public triumph wounded me deeply. I wrote moving letters imploring their attendance, asked my sisters' help in vain. The old porter’s step called my comrades to their friends; my name never sounded. From early communion I sought refuge in religion; my confessor pointed to the heavenly palm promised for "Beati qui lugent" and I flung myself into prayer and visions that expanded my imagination. Those visions nourished a faculty of insight and a poetic sensibility.My father, doubting Oratorian influence, took me from Pont-le-Voy and sent me to Paris to the Lepitre Academy at fifteen. Examined, I was placed low despite my rhetoric-class standing. Sufferings continued in another form. Monsieur Lepitre had a small monthly aid for me, but it was inadequate. The establishment, in the old Joyeuse mansion, had a porter, Doisy, who breakfasted wealthier pupils and supplied luxuries on credit—coffee and sugar as marks of superiority. I resisted this seduction long, but ultimately succumbed; the moral struggle was intense.At length my parents visited Paris. My brother had written that all would dine at the Palais-Royal and see Talma; I was in debt to Doisy and begged my brother to mediate. My mother’s rebuke was merciless—she called me dreadful, predicted ruin, and cast me from maternal grace. Sent back to school under my brother’s charge, I lost the theatre and the Paris delights—my first interview with my mother after years was humiliating.When school ended, my father left me with Monsieur Lepitre to pursue mathematics, law, and philosophy, still under constraint. Lepitre sent a man to escort me to and from the law school; my liberty was chained. Paris alarmed my parents, and rightly in their view. My attempts to reach the Palais-Royal—a place of licentious freedom where fortunes were spent and virgin doubts dissolved—were repeatedly thwarted by petty accidents: Lepitre’s coach appearing, my mother’s unexpected arrival. Fate kept me from that hell and heaven alternately. Weariness and repression drove me to study, which had become a passion and perhaps saved me from worse dangers.Thus I reached twenty, small, thin, pale, a body childlike, a mind old from reading and thought. I stood in the frigidity between puberty and manhood—prepared to feel and to love. The journey with my mother from Paris to Tours only prolonged the cold. At Orleans I fell at her knees and begged her, but she called me an actor and condemned me a heartless child. At Blois, despair drove me to the bridge with a wish to drown myself; the parapet prevented the attempt. At home my sisters surprised me with indifference; I was given a third-floor room and only school linen. My mother, tall, spare, card-playing, egotistical, demanded duty as a religion. She met our homage as a priest receives incense. Her irony, sharpened against us who could not reply, stung constantly, yet our love for her—misguided and deep—lasted until later years when we judged her truly.To escape, I took refuge in my father's library and read every book. Study saved me from my mother's presence but deepened my morbid tendencies. My elder sister tried to comfort me. I desired to die.Political events stirred around us—the Duc d'Angouleme's tour aroused Touraine. I desired to attend the ball given by the duke. My mother first refused, then, in her theatrical way, prepared clothes for me; a bottle-blue coat, silk stockings, and a frilled shirt were provided. At the ball I was dazzled by the military music, scarlet draperies, and the shouts for the duke and king; the spectacle disgusted me and drove me inward. Swept amid the crowd, I longed, childishly, to be the duke, to taste the dominion over the masses. This sense of control dedicated me to glory.Yet in the crush, beset by heat and awkwardness and nearly suffocated, I sat at the end of a bench beside a woman I took for a sleepy child who slipped into the place like a bird. When I laid my head on the white, rounded shoulders—on the bosom veiled in gauze—I acted with the simple frenzy of a child: I kissed them. The woman cried and, seeing me, exclaimed, “Monsieur!” Had she called me "my little lad" I might have killed her; at "Monsieur!" tears sprang to my eyes. Her look, a saintly anger and noble beauty crowned with golden hair, transfixed me. She moved away with a queen’s step. Ashamed, conscious of the stolen warmth, I went home transformed.A new soul had arisen within me; descending from the blue of my star, my woman had come. The first ardor of love seized me—unique, exclusive, total. The mere thought that she might live in Touraine filled the air with sweetness; I resolved to search every chateau on foot until I found her. With a child's courage I began a pedestrian quest, and, leaving Tours by Saint-Eloy, crossed the moors toward Chinon. On the Charlemagne moors—sandy, dreary for miles—I reached the road to Sache and descended into a valley of the Indre, a splendid emerald cup sprinkled with chateaus and the winding river.Seated beneath a walnut-tree, thinking she must be here if anywhere, I saw her house across the valley, its roof and glinting cambric dress catching the sun. Love, the image of that woman, suffused the landscape: the long ribbon of water, poplars, oaks, vineyards, the river’s curve—all seemed grown about her. If you would see nature pure and virgin, come to Touraine in spring; if you would still bleeding wounds, come in autumn. That day the valley sang: mills, poplars, birds, crickets; I loved it as an artist loves art.My host at Frapesle, surprised by my walk from Tours, showed me the valley and named the first castle I had seen: Clochegourde, belonging to the Comte de Mortsauf, an ancient family whose wife was a Demoiselle de Lenoncourt. The estate belonged to her and the family never left Clochegourde. My host laughed at my fancy; yet he agreed to call there. About four o’clock we reached the little chateau, modest but effective in the landscape: terraces, orchards, a grass-plot, stables below, mansard windows, and carved escutcheons bearing family arms and mottos. The house, dainty as a flower, seemed scarcely to rest on earth—its set and proportion gave an elegance that charmed me. The day had all the circumstances to solemnize it; nature was adorned like a woman to meet her lover.We crossed the court where the servant, saying Monsieur le Comte had gone to Azay, announced that Madame la Comtesse was at home. My companion, Monsieur de Chessel, named me. I was nervous and almost fled, but Madame de Mortsauf entered. Her voice was golden; I recognized it from the ball. Her looks, at first fixed on the river, soon softened into kind words; she offered restoratives and consolation, and I blushed like a girl, thanking and declining. She asked my host to dine, and in that tone of gentle command it became settled. If I pleased her, her neighbor’s favor to Monsieur de Chessel would follow; my host hinted at this, and I drew courage.Madame de Mortsauf's conversation at dinner touched local concerns—harvests, vintages—apparent trivialities that were more restraint than indifference; her silence was often full of thought. I found advantage in listening: her voice, in its modulations, was a music that entered the soul. When she was gay it was a swallow’s note; when she spoke of grief it was a swan’s call. She spoke so that meanings were extended by the inflexions of her vowels; the “ch” a kiss, the “t” an echo of the heart. I forgave being shut out of talk to hear that voice.Her person and bearing entrance me: rounded shoulders, a bosom chastely veiled, hair smoothed over a soft neck, a face requiring an artist to paint the inward fires seen only by love. Her brow, round and prominent, held unuttered thoughts; her green-brown eyes were wan but flashed rare, consuming lights when children or intense feeling touched her. A Grecian nose, curved lips, oval face, and a skin like a camellia gave grace; she was plump yet childlike, simple yet proud, reserved yet capable of smiles that betrayed a buried happy nature. She charmed not by coquetries but by an artless, virginal revelation of soul. She was a mistress and a maiden, a child in feeling and grave from suffering.That morning she wore a rose-colored gown with narrow stripes and simple coiffure. I cannot render her completely, for her soul’s radiance was comprehensible only to the lover. Her quiet salon at Clochegourde—panelled, painted gray, a clock, porcelain vases of Cape heather, cambric curtains, tapestry, a backgammon board—was itself a statement of English cleanliness and modest taste. The scene, and the valley seen from its windows, filled me with impressions that nourished my ideas and shaped my future; that salon was one of the spots where my best thoughts and ambitions were born.If I had only seen her among her husband and children, I might have been humble; but having tasted the jasmine of her skin and the sweetness of the stolen kiss, I could not kneel. My dream had awakened a hunger for life’s joys. I wanted to climb trees, float in the river, and dwell in night’s companionship. Yet when she spoke of her husband, a new emotion arose: hatred for the obstacle and fear of the struggle—hatred that would brook no obstacle, fear of its consequences and above all of losing her.“Here is Monsieur de Mortsauf,” she said. I sprang up. She left the room; a little girl came in, “Here’s papa!”—Madeleine—pale, nine years old, slender, frail, with scant hair and hollow cheeks; her brother Jacques followed, also weak. The children’s air of heroism amid frailty revealed why their mother’s brow clouded; they seemed saved by love and care, like hot-house plants needing gentle nurture. Madame de Mortsauf kissed Madeleine’s white hair. The count entered holding his son’s hand.The count appeared awkward and uneasy at first, then, when I was explained as a fellow-Tourangeau returned from Paris with the host’s request for me to rest, his manner warmed. He had known my father’s devoted, secret service to the Bourbons; later I learned how my family’s name and past had made him receptive. For the moment, his transition to polite attention put me at ease. The children, drawn to their mother’s light, tried to follow her but she stopped them. Their voices in the other room sounded like bees about a hive—comforting, active life against my inner tumult.I watched the count, trying to read his character. Forty-five, he seemed nearer sixty; the wrecks of the late eighteenth century had aged him. A crescent of hair fringing a bald head, his aspect suggested fatigue and the weight of events—traits that only later would reveal his fortitude and the depth of his domestic grief.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though ten years of emigration and farm life had altered his physique, the count still bore traces of nobility. Even ardent liberals would have acknowledged his chivalric loyalty and steadfast convictions: a man devoted to a cause, honest in political views, yet ignorant of France’s current affairs. He was the upright sort who, unable to serve his party, might inadvertently harm it—willing to sacrifice his life rather than his money.At dinner I noticed his flaccid cheeks and furtive glances at his children, signs of a burdened mind and frail constitution he had passed to them. He refused to be judged by others; his harsh demeanor, born of self-awareness of his faults, lacked grace and heightened his family’s misery. When his wife returned with the children, unhappiness settled over the house like cold depths beneath a vault.After conversation shifted, the count introduced me to his wife and even spoke about my family in ways I had not known. He asked my age; when I answered, the countess expressed surprise at her daughter’s age, perhaps thinking me younger. Her maternal instincts instantly sparked hope that her children might thrive. She scrutinized me; in that look some barriers dissolved. She had many questions but spoke little.“If study has made you ill,” she said, “the air of our valley will soon restore you.”“Modern education harms children,” the count insisted. “We burden them with mathematics and sciences, aging them prematurely. You must stay and rest here. You are overwhelmed by the avalanche of ideas. What future will universal education have unless we return it to religious hands?” His words echoed his later election remark, “I shall always distrust men of talent.”He proposed a tour of the gardens.“Monsieur—” his wife interjected.“What is it, my dear?” he replied, revealing his desire to control his home.“Monsieur de Vandenesse walked from Tours this morning, and Monsieur de Chessel has already taken him on foot over Frapesle.”“Very imprudent of you,” the count said to me, shaking his head.The count’s rigid royalism required careful navigation. The manservant in livery announced dinner. Monsieur de Chessel offered his arm to Madame de Mortsauf, while the count took mine, and we went to the dining room.The dining room, with its white tiled floor and varnished paper decorated with floral designs, was charming though unpretentious. Dinner was plentiful but not luxurious: family silver, mismatched china, flowers on the table. I found joy in these quaint details, feeling contentment that made the obstacles of country life fade. I sat beside her, serving her wine, and felt a profound connection. That kiss bound us in a secret that filled me with both joy and shame. I endeavored to please the count, lavishing affection on the dogs and trying to engage the children, whom I hoped would accept me as one of their own. Any sign of gloom would jeopardize my place in their lives.Dinner passed in a haze of happiness: I reveled in being under her roof, ignoring her apparent coldness and the count’s polite indifference. Love, like life, has its adolescence; I was content in my feelings. I said several foolish things, driven by passion, but no one noticed their cause; even she was oblivious to love. The rest of my visit felt dreamlike as I returned across the Indre, enchanted by moonlight and nature’s sounds. That evening marked a turning point; I felt the weight of past struggles clash with the joy I had just experienced.Approaching Frapesle, I glanced at Clochegourde and saw a small boat tied to a tree—the count’s fishing boat.“Well,” Monsieur de Chessel said once we were out of earshot, “I needn’t ask if you found those shoulders; I must congratulate you on the reception Monsieur de Mortsauf gave you. You stepped into his heart at once.” His words lifted my spirits, though I sensed underlying bitterness.“How do you mean?” I asked.“He has never received anyone so well,” he replied.Monsieur de Chessel—whose real name was Durand—had abandoned his father’s merchant name to adopt an old parliamentary one. He had claimed a count’s title under Louis XVIII, a title his children enjoyed without understanding its cost. His character bore pettiness and envy; royal favor had rekindled his ambition, making him ostentatious yet kindly toward me. His interest felt paternal, a contrast to the neglect I knew; I was grateful for his hospitality.The owners of Frapesle were intertwined with the dawn of my happiness, and I cherished those memories. Monsieur de Chessel’s ostentation annoyed neighbors—fine horses, lavish entertaining—while the Comte de Mortsauf, with limited means, struggled to maintain his estate. Their interactions were polite but lacked intimacy.Jealousy alone did not explain the count’s isolation. His education had been superficial; he emigrated just as his intellect should have matured, and a long idleness followed. Hopes for a restored monarchy kept him from seeking work; life’s trials eroded his spirit. His gaiety faded into moroseness; he fell ill, prone to hypochondria. Hidden love affairs further deteriorated his health and prospects.After twelve years of hardship he returned to France under a decree allowing emigrants back. Crossing the Rhine, he cried “France! France!” like a child calling for its mother. Once wealthy, he returned impoverished and stripped of authority, burdened by his name. His unyielding opinions and past left him vulnerable to ridicule in a society that often mocked weakness. He eventually settled in Maine on a forgotten farm that gave him some stability.The Lenoncourt family, having lost their fortune, welcomed him; he stayed with them while preparing a home. Mademoiselle de Lenoncourt accepted his proposal and lived with her aunt, the Duchesse de Verneuil, who guided her life. Madame de Verneuil, a member of a devout society, profoundly influenced the countess, who remained devout. After her aunt’s death, Madame de Mortsauf received visits from Saint-Martin, who corrected his last works at Clochegourde. The duchess had endured trials and left Clochegourde to her niece, but her sudden death cast a shadow over the early years of the marriage.Monsieur de Mortsauf found some solace in the valley, but the birth of his first child, Jacques, brought despair: the doctor pronounced the child unviable, a truth the count concealed from his wife. Subsequent births confirmed his fears, deepening his sense of failure. The countess, sensing her husband’s turmoil, devoted herself to his care, striving to reconcile him with himself. She accepted his miserliness and mistrust, guiding him toward what was right, often at the expense of her own happiness. She resolved to remain at Clochegourde, fearing that leaving would harm their children. Her efforts masked the count’s incapacity, allowing him to find some peace in her presence.This brief history also illuminated Monsieur de Chessel, who, despite worldly knowledge, could not fully grasp Clochegourde’s complexities. My own understanding deepened as I spent time there and felt the weight of unspoken truths. One evening I found Madame de Mortsauf watching a sunset, and I felt a shared sorrow.“Some days are hard to bear,” I remarked.“You read my soul,” she replied, surprised.“We touch at many points,” I said, recognizing our shared experiences of joy and sorrow.She shuddered, and I felt compelled to express my understanding of our mutual suffering.“Ah! My childhood was like a long illness—”“I hear Madeleine coughing,” she interrupted, and left abruptly.The countess welcomed my visits, seeing in me an amusement for her husband. I often played backgammon with the count, enduring his harshness and criticism. The count’s tyranny was tempered by the countess’s presence; she softened my hardships. As I grew better at the game, his temper flared when I began to win. I learned to balance our matches—letting him win at first, then evening the score. The countess understood my strategy and thanked me with a grateful gaze that filled me with joy.One evening, after an intense game, his mood shifted dramatically into rage. I left the room and waited in the garden. When she joined me she revealed her husband’s condition and asked me to forget the incident.“Promise me to bury the recollection of that scene,” she implored. “I don’t ask you to swear it; just give me your word of honor.”“I will do all you wish,” I replied, “but I cannot change my opinion of him.”She acknowledged my feelings and urged me to understand his struggles.“Perhaps for my sake, you will forget what he said to you today,” she suggested. “He will wish to undo the wrong he did.”“Five days without seeing you?” I lamented.“Do not put such warmth into your manner of speaking to me,” she cautioned.We walked in silence; she eventually said, “It is late; we will part.” I wished to kiss her hand; she hesitated before allowing it.“Never take it unless I give it to you,” she warned. “Leave me my freedom.”As I departed I felt a profound connection, pledging my love in silence. The next day I reflected on our bond, feeling purpose and commitment to her. I recognized her sacrifices and strength. Our shared sufferings had forged a connection beyond friendship, binding us in a deep understanding of each other’s pain and resilience.In the days that followed, small moments deepened my affection. I was determined to support her, to be a source of strength, and to honor the bond we had formed. Navigating my feelings, I understood our connection was rare and precious, something I would cherish and protect. The love I felt for Madame de Mortsauf was more than passion; it was a commitment to stand by her, to share her struggles, and to celebrate the beauty of our shared existence.Beneath the stars I vowed to honor her spirit and nurture the love that had blossomed between us—an affection meant to endure through life’s trials.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This scene took place on a Tuesday. I had waited until Sunday and had not crossed the river. During those five days much had happened at Clochegourde. The count received his brevet as general of brigade, the cross of Saint Louis, and a pension of four thousand francs. The Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, made peer of France, regained two forests, resumed his place at court, and his wife reclaimed unsold property that had once been imperial crown lands. Consequently the Comtesse de Mortsauf became an heiress. Her mother arrived at Clochegourde bringing one hundred thousand francs saved at Givry—the amount of her dowry, which the count had never demanded despite his poverty. With his own savings the count bought two neighboring estates yielding about nine thousand francs a year. His son would inherit the grandfather’s peerage; the count now saw a way to entail the estate upon him without harming Madeleine, and the Duc de Lenoncourt would likely help secure a good marriage.These events soothed the count’s troubled mind. The presence of the Duchesse de Lenoncourt at Clochegourde was significant for the neighborhood. I reflected gloomily on her caste spirit, which my love for Henriette had hitherto hidden beneath noble sentiments. Who was I—poor, insignificant, with no future but my courage and faculties? I did not think of the Restoration’s wider implications. On Sunday morning, in the private chapel where I sat with Monsieur and Madame de Chessel and the Abbé de Quelus, I glanced eagerly toward the duchess’s chapel, where the duchess and her daughter, the count and his children, were praying. Henriette’s large straw hat did not tremble; her unconsciousness of my presence bound me to her more than our past had. She prayed with such abandonment that she seemed a religious statue, and I was deeply moved.By village custom vespers followed mass. After church Madame de Chessel suggested going to Frapesle rather than cross the Indre again in the heat. Monsieur de Chessel offered his arm to the duchess, Madame de Chessel took the count’s, and I offered mine to the countess, feeling her arm against my side for the first time. As we walked through the woods of Sache, where light filtered through the foliage, sensations and proud thoughts overwhelmed me, making my heart beat violently.“What is the matter?” she asked after a moment.“I have heard of your good fortune,” I said. “Will your new dignities change you and lessen your friendship?”“Change me!” she cried. “Oh, fie! Another such idea and I shall—not despise you, but forget you forever.”I looked at her with ecstasy.“We profit by new laws which we have neither made nor sought,” she said. “We are neither place-hunters nor beggars; neither Monsieur de Mortsauf nor I can leave Clochegourde. By my advice he has declined the command at the Maison Rouge. We are content my father should have it. This forced modesty has benefited our son. The king, whose household my father joins, will favor Jacques’ education. He must represent both Lenoncourt and Mortsauf. I can have no ambition except for him; he must live and be made worthy. Who will keep him safe in Paris? My friend,” she said, her voice breaking, “be some day his guardian and sponsor. Go to Paris; if your family will not support you, ours—especially my mother, who rules society—will help you. Put the strength of your desires into a noble ambition—”“I understand,” I interrupted. “Ambition will be my mistress. I do not need it to be wholly yours. No, I will not be rewarded for my obedience here by favors there. I will go and make my own way. From you, I would accept everything; from others, nothing.”“Child!” she murmured, pleased.“Besides, I have taken my vows,” I continued. “I am resolved to bind myself to you by ties that can never be broken.”She trembled and stopped.“What do you mean?” she asked.“First tell me how you wish me to love you.”“Love me as my aunt loved me,” she answered. “I gave you her rights when I permitted you to call me by her chosen name.”“Then I must love without hope and with absolute devotion. I will enter a seminary, become a priest, and educate your son. Jacques shall be myself in his own form; political conceptions, energy, patience—I will give him all. I will live near you; my love, enclosed in religion, can never be suspected of evil. I will consume myself in the flame and love you with a purified love.”She turned pale. “Félix, do not bind yourself in a way that might be an obstacle. I should die of grief. Do you think despairing love a life’s vocation? Wait for life’s trials. Marry neither the Church nor a woman—marry not at all—I forbid it. Remain free. You are twenty-one—My God! can I have mistaken him? I thought two months sufficed to know some souls.”“What hope have you?” I cried.“Accept our help, rise in life, make your way and your fortune, and you shall know my hope. And,” she added as if whispering, “never release the hand you are holding at this moment.”She bent to my ear as she said it.“Madeleine!” I exclaimed. “Never!”We neared a wooden gate into the park of Frapesle. Suddenly an idea—the count’s death—flashed through me, and I said, “I understand you.”“I am glad of it,” she answered, as though surprised at my thought.Her purity drew tears of admiration that mingled with the bitterness of passion. I thought she did not love me enough even to wish me liberty; love that recoils from crime seemed limited. “She does not love me,” I thought.To hide my pain, I kissed Madeleine’s hair. “I am afraid of your mother,” I said to the countess.“So am I,” she answered, playful. “Don’t forget to call her Madame la duchesse and speak to her in the third person. Young people have lost polite manners; you must learn them for my sake. It is good taste to respect women, no matter their age, and to recognize social distinctions. The respect shown to established superiority guarantees what is due to you. You have drunk the milk of the Revolution in your academy; but as you advance, you will find crude principles of liberty powerless to create happiness. Before considering what an aristocracy ought to be, my common sense tells me societies can exist only through a hierarchy. Be on our side—especially now,” she added, laughing, “when it triumphs.”I was touched by her combination of political feeling and affection. In defending her husband she had foreseen criticisms I might make on seeing the servility he showed her mother. Monsieur de Mortsauf, king in his castle, had a certain dignity; yet I was astonished at the obsequious distance he placed between himself and the duchess. Humiliated by his degradation, I understood the martyrdom of generous women yoked to petty men. I was respectful to the duchess because of my youth; but where others saw only a duchess, I saw Henriette’s mother, and that gave sanctity to my homage.At Frapesle the count presented me to the duchess, who examined me coldly. Madame de Lenoncourt was fifty-six, wonderfully preserved, erect and imposing, with a hard face. Her courtly manner reminded me of my own mother. I behaved well; as I passed the countess she said softly, “You are perfect.”The count took my hand. “You are not angry with me, Félix, are you? If I was hasty, pardon an old soldier. We shall probably stay for dinner, and I invite you on Thursday, the evening before the duchess leaves. I must go to Tours tomorrow. Don’t neglect Clochegourde. My mother-in-law is an acquaintance I advise you to cultivate. Her salon will set the tone for the faubourg St. Germain. She has all the traditions of the great world and immense social knowledge.”The count’s changed circumstances showed in his softened manner. The duchess was not patronizing; Monsieur and Madame de Chessel accepted the invitation. The duchess questioned me about my family and, hearing that the Vandenesse in diplomacy was my brother, became almost affectionate. She told me my great-aunt, the old Marquise de Listomere, was a Grandlieu, and that by a provision of the Charter my father was once more Marquis de Vandenesse.“I am but one thing, the serf of Clochegourde,” I said to the countess.The Restoration’s transformation bewildered our generation. To me, the revolution meant nothing; the least word from Madame de Mortsauf was the sole event of importance. I was ignorant of privy councils and politics; my sole ambition was to love Henriette better than Petrarch loved Laura. This indifference made the duchess take me for a child. A large company assembled at Frapesle, and to a young man unused to the world, seeing the woman he loves admired by all is intoxicating. The count, delighted, seemed almost young; his wife hoped for change; I amused myself with Madeleine, whose clever observations made others laugh. It was a happy day. A word, a hope awakened in the morning and illuminated nature. Seeing me joyous, Henriette was joyful too.“That happiness smiling on my gray and cloudy life seems good,” she said to me the next day.I spent that day at Clochegourde. The count left early for Tours. A serious disagreement arose between mother and daughter. The duchess wanted the countess to move to Paris, where she promised a place at court and where the count might accept a position. Henriette, hiding her husband’s incapacity and her own sufferings, opposed leaving Clochegourde because its air and sky calmed his excitements and soothed her children’s health. She had persuaded him to go to Tours to transact business so as to hide his condition from the duchess. I alone knew Clochegourde’s dark secret.Henriette saw that the duchess cared little for Jacques and Madeleine—a painful discovery. The duchess brooked no opposition; she used sweetness, then cold severity, then sarcasm, to force compliance. In those ten days Henriette endured all the contentions a young woman must face to establish independence. To picture their struggle, imagine a lily bruised under a polished wheel. That mother had nothing in common with her daughter; she could not even imagine the difficulties that forced Henriette to remain at Clochegourde. Families hide internal dissensions; behind scenes almost all contain deep wounds that lessen natural affections. Tortured and wounded, Henriette loved the one who did not strike her, who would build a shield around her. I was happy when she rested upon my heart; she told me of her troubles. Day by day I learned more fully what she meant by “Love me as my aunt loved me.”“Have you no ambition?” the duchess asked me at dinner.“Madame,” I replied, “I have enough in me to conquer the world; but I am only twenty-one, and I am all alone.”She looked at her daughter in astonishment. The duchess’s visit meant constraint. The countess begged me to be cautious. The great Thursday was a day of wearisome ceremonial. But the duchess returned to court pomps and Clochegourde recovered its order.My quarrel with the count had made me more at home; I could go there at all times. I clung like a climbing plant to the beautiful soul that had opened the world of shared emotions. Our fraternal marriage, founded on trust, became surer; Henriette enfolded me with maternal care, while my love for her burned like hot irons when away. I loved her with a double love—seraphic in her presence, searing when absent. At my age I was not yet man enough to torture her, who feared catastrophe to her children, who sat beside them when her husband allowed her a little rest. The mere sound of ardor shook her; she needed veiled love, support mingled with tenderness—that which she gave to others. This situation brought delightful languor and secret immolation. Her conscience and self-devotion awed me; the piety that was the bond of her virtues filled the air with spiritual incense. She gave me only the back of her hand to kiss, never the palm, as though drawing the line of sensual emotions. No two souls ever clasped each other with such ardor yet no earthly tie bound us. Later I understood why that sufficed: in youth we love the whole woman, her children and interests, as our own.I was now one of the household and knew infinite sweetness—the privilege of being the mistress’s friend in a strange family. Servants treated me well; the children divined my affection and were discreet. True passions bloom more charmingly in barren soil.But I also saw the full extent of Monsieur de Mortsauf’s failings. He scolded perpetually about nothing, complained of imaginary evils, and had an inward dissatisfaction that demanded victims. On walks he selected the way and was bored whichever way we went; at home he blamed others. He found domineering pleasure in touching his wife’s sensitive chords. Sometimes he fell into gloomy silence that alarmed her and brought tender attentions. Like petted children, he let her serve him as she did Jacques and Madeleine. I discovered his petty tyranny in household details and realized how it stifled the family, hindered management, and retarded improvement of the estate. The countess had been noble in describing these daily struggles. My admiration for her rose higher than my love; I yielded to be a voluntary victim to come nearer her. She allowed me a place beside her and permission to share her sorrows; eager to die in her arena, I became a repentant apostate seeking heaven with my brethren.“Were it not for you, I must have succumbed under this life,” Henriette said when the count had been more venomous than usual.He had gone to bed. Henriette and I stayed under the acacias with the children playing in the setting sun. Our few words revealed mutual thoughts; when language failed our silence served, and our souls seemed to enter one another. We luxuriated in pensive languor and asked by a look, “Among all our days on earth, will there be one for us?”Despite the evening’s poetry, desire crept through me. After three months of repression I was unable to accept my assigned fate. I took Henriette’s hand and caressed it. She at once withdrew it; tears from my eyes fell and she offered her hand to my lips.“You must know,” she said, “that this will cause me grief. A friendship that asks such a favor is dangerous.”Then I lost control; I reproached her, spoke of my sufferings and of a slight alleviation I dared ask. I told her that at my age the soul had a sex and that I could meet death but not silence my lips. She forced me to silence with a proud glance. Ever since the gate of Frapesle, when I attributed to her the hope that our happiness might spring from a grave, I had shunned the thought of staining her soul. She said she could never be wholly mine; if I could not be to her somewhat as her old confessor—less than a lover yet more than a brother—she must never see me again. She could die and offer her sufferings to God.“I gave you,” she said, “more than I ought, so that nothing might be left to take, and I am punished.”I calmed her, promised never to cause pain, and to love her as an old man loves his youngest child.The next day I came early; there were no flowers in her gray salon. I rushed into the fields and vineyards to make two bouquets; as I gathered them it occurred to me that color and foliage have meanings, and like melody awaken memory. I enlisted Jacques and Madeleine and arranged two bouquets on the portico steps to convey a sentiment—white roses and lilies, with blue flowers intermingled, symbolizing two purities: the child and the martyr. The countess discerned it inwardly and gave me a poignant glance that was reward enough. I pressed the theories of Père Castel into service and recovered a lost science: the language of flowers. What charm in expressing our sensations through these daughters of the sun! I communed with the flora of the fields, seeking varieties that would complete my messages.Twice a week I pursued this poetic enterprise, walking long distances to find flowers, initiating myself into pleasures unknown to the man of science—a poet studying spirit rather than form. Nature offered scenes whose significance rose to the highest moral conceptions: heather with dew like diamonds, mossy corners, anemones alone in their valleys, water-sheets with floating life, cottage gardens as images of humble existence, forest paths like cathedral naves. I gathered such images and poured them into bouquets.In September and October no bouquet cost less than three hours’ search. These floral symphonies expressed what no declaration of love could; my baffled desires found expression as Beethoven found in notes. In their presence, Madame de Mortsauf was my Henriette. She looked at them constantly; they nourished her spirit. These neutral pleasures soothed me: abstinence brings deadly exhaustion, and a few crumbs of heavenly comfort keep one from disaster. Sometimes I found her standing before the bouquets lost in agitation; she would lift her head and murmur, “Ah, how beautiful!”Throughout the month I often saw her at the window at my arrival. If I was late, she wandered on the terrace and told me she had come to meet me and tend her youngest child.Backgammon had ended. The count’s purchases engaged him inspecting his new properties. He was neither arrogant nor offensive, and our walks together were mutual joys. On one occasion the count, wishing to know what we talked about, asked; Henriette answered in words with a double meaning that satisfied him. Madeleine laughed. Our spiritual union continued.The days passed with new joys. The grape harvest—the vintage—began in late September. It is easier to gather grapes than to mow wheat. The vintage brings abundance and hilarity: coopers and carts, coopers and laughing girls, men and panniers. Classes are equal for the day; everyone shares the harvest. Jacques and Madeleine, who had never seen a vintage, were full of delight. We went to Villaines for baskets; none of us were to eat too many grapes. Jacques made me swear to stay at Clochegourde. The day in the vineyard was glorious: the children’s chatter, their racing about, the mother laughing with the joy of youth; she passed a hand round my neck and tapped my cheek, “You are melting away”—the only caress she ever gave me. I filled my basket and emptied it into the vat, moving slowly in regulated motion that kept my spirit free. I found the pleasure of external labor that regulates passion; I learned monastic discipline.For the first time in long weeks the count was neither surly nor cruel. His son’s rosy face rejoiced him. He had promised a dance in front of Clochegourde to celebrate the Bourbons’ return. As we returned, the countess leaned on my arm and whispered, “You bring us happiness.”To me, burdened with knowledge of her sleepless nights and cares, those words were more than any other woman could give.“The terrible monotony of my life is broken; all things are radiant with hope,” she said. “Oh, never leave me! Be the elder son, the protector of the younger.”This is not romantic; in youth passion restrained by obstacles can fill the crater of a volcano with pure water.We had another fête. The countess, wishing to accustom her children to practical life, gave Jacques the produce of the walnut trees and Madeleine that of the chestnuts. Gathering chestnuts and walnuts, seeing the children take pride in their small granaries, the petty joys of harvest and calculation, made charming scenes of innocent happiness amid autumn’s fading colors. Jacques’ walnuts suffered from rain; I advised him to hold back and sell later. His father urged prudence and reserve funds; the mother rejoiced to see Jacques listen. These homely scenes attached me ever more closely to Henriette.I learned how the countess had, over ten years, changed cultivation methods—“put it in fours,” rotating crops and dividing the estate into four farms on share leases. She induced the count to cultivate a model farm and rebuild two farmhouses to relet for money rents afterward. Fearing to die before her husband, she sought to secure his income and her children’s property. Fruit trees planted in the last ten years were in full bearing; hedges and elms grew well. With the new purchases and farming system, Clochegourde—divided into four farms—could bring in forty thousand francs a year, not counting vineyards and woodlands. The new road to Chinon would make tenants plentiful. The countess wished to invest fifteen thousand francs in each new estate to improve houses so they would yield better rents; this required thirty thousand francs she and her husband argued about. The fear “If I die tomorrow, what will become of them?” drove her. The count objected, but common sense carried the day. That day we went to the Cassine and the Rhetorière to decide on buildings; she spoke gently, confident of success. A carrier wanted a large farm on the new route; another honest man was ready to take la Baude; the soil would attract tenants. In two years Clochegourde might yield twenty-four thousand francs. Gravelotte in Maine was rented for seven thousand a year; his pension was four thousand. Later additions might let her go to Paris and arrange Jacques’ education.With what feeling she uttered “Paris!” I knew she wished not to be separated from her friend. I told her I would finish my education and work to be Jacques’ tutor. She looked grave.“No, Félix,” she said. “That cannot be, any more than your priesthood. I thank you as a mother, but as a woman who loves you, I can never allow you to be the victim of your attachment. Such a position would be a social discredit to you. My father would never forgive your taking such a humble place; he would refuse you his protection. You must accept society as it is; never fly in its face. This rash proposal of—”“Love,” I whispered.“No, charity,” she said, controlling tears. “This wild idea enlightens me as to your character; your heart will be your bane. I shall claim the right to teach you sometimes. Let my woman’s eye see for you. From the solitudes of Clochegourde I will share in your successes. As to a tutor, do not fear; we shall find some good old abbé, my father will devote sums to the boy’s education. Jacques is my pride; he is eleven years old.”At the Cassine Jacques, Madeleine, and I followed her like children. I left to speak with Martineau and saw how beloved she was. A laborer told me she was good and knew our hardships and treated the people well. I gave that man all the money I had.A pony arrived for Jacques; the father, an excellent horseman, would accustom him gradually. The morning of the first lesson, with Madeleine shouting and all the household clustered round, was a maternal festival. Jacques wore a blue collar embroidered by his mother and rode fearlessly. The count smiled for the first time in months; the mother burst into tears and leaned on my arm, “I think I have never suffered. Do not leave us today.” The child jumped into her arms; I and Madeleine arranged bouquets for dinner. She said, “The fifteenth of October is certainly a great day for me. Jacques has taken his first riding lesson, and I have just set the last stitch in my furniture cover.”“Then, Blanche,” the count laughed, “I must pay you for it.”He offered her his arm; an open carriage her father had sent awaited with two English horses. As we returned, she whispered anxiously, “I am too happy; happiness is like illness—it overwhelms me; I fear it may vanish.”I loved her so passionately I was jealous. She asked me what my eyes said; I told her. She was more touched than by any gift, then poured comfort into my heart. “Love me as my aunt loved me,” she said. “If I take your life, must I not be grateful?”“It was time I finished my tapestry,” she added. “Men find occupations a resource against troubles; we women need physical exercise to control inward sufferings. Each stitch rocked my thoughts; to every stitch I confided my secrets. While doing my last chair I thought much of you. What you put into your bouquets I have said in my embroidery.”Dinner was lovely. Jacques offered his garland to his mother with charming grace. In the afternoon we played backgammon; the count was charming. They accompanied me to Frapesle in the tranquil twilight. It was a day of days for the poor woman.Soon the riding lessons became a source of contention. The countess feared the father's harsh reprimands; Jacques grew thin and wore dark circles. I advised him to tell his father when tired, but the expedient failed, and the old huntsman took over. Angry reproaches resumed. Then the cost of the Cassine and Rhetorière buildings exceeded estimates by half. The news, given first to Monsieur de Mortsauf instead of his wife, became the ground of a quarrel that grew bitter until the count’s malady demanded its arrearages.That day I had gone from Frapesle to search for flowers with Madeleine. Returning, I could not find her and heard cries in the house. Madeleine ran out: “The general,” she cried, “is scolding mamma; go and defend her.”I reached the salon without being seen. Monsieur de Mortsauf was in violent tirade.“Never marry, Félix,” he said. “A woman is led by the devil; the most virtuous would invent evil if it did not exist; they are all vile.”Arguments flew. He harked back to old troubles, cursed the new farming system, declared he could have been twice as rich if he had managed Clochegourde. He sprang around, displacing furniture; then stopped, moaned his marrow was on fire, that his wife had ruined him. The countess smiled and looked upward.“Yes, Blanche,” he cried, “you are my executioner; you are killing me; you are a monster of hypocrisy. She is smiling! Do you know why she smiles, Félix?”I kept silence.“That woman denies me all happiness; she is no more to me than to you, and yet she pretends to be my wife! She fulfills none of the duties of laws, human and divine; she lies to God and man. She puts all her art into keeping me away; she has made me mad through privations; she is killing me by degrees, and she thinks herself a saint and takes the sacrament every month!”Henriette wept bitterly, answering only, “Monsieur! monsieur! monsieur!”The count’s words made me burn with shame for him and stirred my heart because they struck at chastity and delicacy—the warp and woof of first love.“She is virgin at my expense,” cried the count.At this, the countess cried, “Monsieur!”“And what mean you by your imperious ‘Monsieur!’?” he shouted. “Am I not your master? Must I teach you that I am?”He came toward her, thrusting forward his white wolf’s head; his yellow eyes looked savage. Henriette slid from her chair to avoid a blow, lay at full length, and lost consciousness. The count stopped bewildered. I took her in my arms; the count opened the door of her bedroom. We laid her, still dressed, on the bed. When she revived she motioned to unfasten her belt; the count found scissors; I made her breathe salts and she opened her eyes. The count left the room, ashamed. Two hours passed in silence. Henriette’s hand lay in mine; she pressed it but could not speak. Once she rose on her elbow and whispered, “Unhappy man!—ah! if you did but know—” then sank back. The memory of past sufferings and the present shock threw her into convulsions I calmed by an instinctive magnetism. I smoothed her hair—the first time I touched it—and sat looking at the simple room, its chintz curtains, its crucifix, her aunt’s portrait, the children’s drawings with locks of hair. What renunciations of luxury for such a woman—the chamber of a married nun, filled with holy resignation. Painful as the sight was, it exposed hidden, irreparable woe.When the children and Manette came in I left. The count waited for me; he seemed to seek me as mediator. He caught my hands, exclaiming, “Stay, stay with us, Félix!”“Unfortunately,” I said, “Monsieur de Chessel has a party, and my absence would cause remark. But after dinner I will return.”He left the house with me and took me to the lower gate.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As I read the letter, I felt the countess’s maternal heart beneath my fingers, a warmth that contrasted sharply with my own mother's coldness. I understood why she had forbidden me to open it in Touraine; she likely feared I would break down in tears.I met my brother Charles, who until then had been almost a stranger. His haughtiness stifled any brotherly affection; he lectured me on matters I already knew and treated me with condescension, presenting me in society to highlight his superiority. If not for my deep love, his arrogance would have left me humiliated. Recalling childhood sorrows, I recognized his vanity rather than affection. My solitude since returning from Clochegourde had sharpened my perception of the affections around me; happiness, I found, clarifies understanding.I often visited the Duchesse de Lenoncourt, where Henriette’s name was seldom mentioned. Only the old duke, who welcomed me warmly, seemed aware of our connection. Just as I began to shake off my shyness and enjoy social pleasures, the events of March 20th altered everything.At Henriette’s advice I followed my brother to Ghent, accompanying the Duc de Lenoncourt. The duke’s kindness became genuine protection when he saw my loyalty to the Bourbons. He introduced me to the king, who valued my devotion during difficult times. A letter from Madame de Mortsauf told of Jacques’ illness; I felt her distress and knew she needed support. Despite any rivalry with my brother, I prioritized her sorrow. I decided to leave the court to serve my true sovereign as a reliable messenger, eager to return to Clochegourde.After carrying out the king’s orders in France I was forced to flee Bonapartist authorities. Traveling through the Vendée, I reached Clochegourde to find Jacques better. The count welcomed me, and in the salon I was introduced to Henriette, surprised and joyful. She prepared my room and the count provided food; our greeting was heartfelt.At dinner news of Waterloo and Napoleon’s flight arrived, but my primary concern was Henriette. She had built an ice-house for me; her effort to provide iced water testified to her affection. Over the next week she recovered her health and spirits, and I marveled at her beauty, realizing absence had intensified my love.Henriette described nights spent at Jacques’ bedside and prayers for his life; her devotion was plain. The count’s harshness however interrupted her reflections and caused her pain; she wondered why her words never found favor with him, revealing the emotional toll of their relationship. Yet she maintained duty toward her family, urging her children to respect their father.As we discussed the estate, Henriette told me of their agricultural successes, and I explained my mission for the king. She had a remarkable faculty for foreseeing events concerning her children, which she attributed to divine intervention. I admired her wisdom and felt grateful for her guidance.Henriette encouraged me to pursue ambitions in Paris, stressing the importance of being present for the king’s needs. Our bond deepened as we shared thoughts and dreams, and I felt a growing sense of belonging in her life. Still, the count’s presence loomed and tension in their marriage was palpable.One day she confided her struggles with the count’s behavior, fearing for her children’s well-being. Torn between duty and personal happiness, she hesitated to assert herself. I urged her to be firm, but she feared the consequences.When the count’s illness worsened, Henriette and I took turns caring for him. Our shared duty drew us closer; I took joy in supporting her. His recovery was slow and uneven; at times it seemed a turning point, at others it revived his erratic, domineering ways. Henriette’s patience was tested as she struggled to maintain authority; I witnessed the toll on her.As he regained strength he often became more difficult. Henriette remained determined to care for her family, usually putting her children’s needs above her own. I admired her selflessness, though it complicated my feelings for her and the situation we shared.We found moments of happiness: intimate conversations, mutual vulnerability, and support. These strengthened our connection, even while the count’s influence shadowed us. I reassured Henriette of my unwavering support when she expressed fears about the future, and we shared a deep sense of kinship despite the burdens she bore.Eventually I returned to Paris, consumed by responsibilities but committed to Henriette. Our correspondence sustained us; her letters revealed the depth of her love and the weight she carried. Later news of Jacques’ relapse was a painful toll on her, and I felt helpless to ease it. Despite my own struggles, I remained devoted, hoping to offer what support I could.Throughout, Henriette’s resilience and maternal devotion shone. She navigated obligations with courage, often sacrificing personal happiness for her children and household duties. My loyalty to her only grew; our love was a guiding light amid adversity, a rare and precious bond I cherished and hoped would endure.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################During a deeply emotional period, I met a remarkable woman at the Elysee-Bourbon salons. Immensely wealthy, of noble lineage and married to a distinguished British peer, she was the idol of Parisian society—beautiful, witty, and ruling with a charming firmness.The English, distant and reserved, live by strict laws and rigid virtues. This upbringing produces a particular Englishwoman who, though delicate and charming, is often trapped in hypocrisy and appearances; her passion, like Shakespeare’s, is intense but restrained.You may envy them, yet they are complex, convinced that love alone suffices. My liaison with the Marchioness of Dudley became notorious. At a time when my better feelings were subdued, I was drawn to her; my fidelity to Madame de Mortsauf only increased her desire. Lady Dudley craved the chase, and my resistance inflamed her passion. Her dismissal of my relationship with Madame de Mortsauf—“I am wearied to death of these turtle-dove sighings”—should have warned me.Men have fewer means to resist women than women have to escape men. Social codes keep men from seeming harsh; women are expected to be coy. Though I loved Madame de Mortsauf, I was not immune to Lady Dudley’s beauty and rank. Her attention in society stirred me, and she often hinted she would be my mistress. After one particularly beguiling evening she made her intentions clear, provoking scandal in the British aristocracy.Seemingly fragile, Lady Dudley possessed a strong will. Her physical courage was remarkable—she could outpace any horse—and she pursued her desires relentlessly. By contrast, Madame de Mortsauf was nurturing and expansive, drawing others into her warmth; Lady Dudley was intense and consuming.English customs reflect a materialist bent, valuing comfort and order often at the expense of spiritual depth. Lady Dudley embodied that materialism: compelling physically but leaving an emptiness I did not feel with Madame de Mortsauf, whose love was spiritual and boundless.Lady Dudley’s jealousy was masked by charm; she sought to erase Madame de Mortsauf from my heart. I continued to write to Madame de Mortsauf, but my letters went unanswered, filling me with dread. When I finally returned to Clochegourde, I found the coldness my infidelity had caused.Madame de Mortsauf’s demeanor had altered; she was distant and sorrowful, her beauty dimmed by grief. I felt the weight of my betrayal as I watched her withdraw, pride and pain evident. The family gathered around me, but I perceived her retreat. The count, unaware of the turmoil, welcomed me warmly, while Madame de Mortsauf remained aloof.Witnessing her, I realized the depth of my wrongdoing. I had brought her suffering, and my heart ached for the love I had jeopardized. The count, whose health was fragile, expressed concern for his wife and confessed his fears, sharing the emotional toll he bore.Madame de Mortsauf’s health seemed delicate, and I was tormented by the thought that I was its cause. I longed to make amends, but she remained guarded, her heart closed. Our conversations were strained, full of unspoken words and lingering pain.The count’s condition declined, and I felt helpless as I watched its effect on Madame de Mortsauf. Devoted to her children, she sought solace in their well-being, yet shadows of despair crept into her life. I was torn between love for her and my entanglement with Lady Dudley, whose presence still loomed.In desperation I sought Madame de Mortsauf’s forgiveness, but she rebuffed me, insisting I had caused her too much pain. Left to grapple with guilt, I felt the weight of my actions pressing down on me.As I spent more time with the family, I observed further changes: her once-vibrant spirit dulled, her health fragile. I feared for her and resolved to seek atonement, though the path seemed fraught.The tension between us grew and the distance widened. I was caught in a web of my own making, torn between two women who represented different aspects of love: Lady Dudley’s passionate, material allure and Madame de Mortsauf’s nurturing devotion. In the end I understood that choices have consequences; my desires carried duties, and the harm I caused those I cared for weighed heavily on me.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I know nothing more destructive than an Englishwoman’s wit; it carries the pompous conviction through which the British mask their prejudices. French wit is like lace, adorning life’s joys; English wit is corrosive, stripping flesh until only bone remains. Arabella wielded it like a sultan, cutting down rivals with her words.“My angel,” she said, “I can speak morality too. I’ve questioned whether my love for you is a crime, and I find it both natural and pious. God created beauty to inspire adoration. The real crime would be not to love you. That other woman insults you by comparing you to men; morality’s laws do not apply to you, for you are above them. Am I not drawing closer to divine love by loving you? Will God punish a woman for seeking the divine? My love is a weakness, while that woman’s is strength. Don’t frown; I do not wish her ill. I adore the morality that has freed you for me—because you are mine forever, aren’t you?”“Yes.”“Forever and ever?”“Yes.”“Ah! I have found favor in my lord! I alone understand his worth! You say she knows how to cultivate her estate. I leave that to farmers; I cultivate your heart.”I try to recall this intoxicating babble to convey Arabella and the events that followed. She mixed passion with argument, attempting to efface the impressions left by Henriette’s dignified love with impetuous affection. Arabella’s fierce assault revealed fear and admiration for her rival. The next morning she was tearful, lamenting a sleepless night.“What troubles you?” I asked.“I fear my love will ruin me,” she answered. “I have given all. That woman has something you still desire. If you prefer her, forget me; I will not trouble you. I will go far and die, like a plant deprived of sunlight.”She drew reluctant declarations from me and, filled with joy, cried, drying her eyes, “Ah! I am happy. Go back to her; I do not want to owe you to the force of my love, but to your own will. If you return, I shall know you love me as much as I love you, which I always doubted.”She persuaded me to return to Clochegourde. Under her influence the false position in which I had placed myself did not strike me. Had I refused, I would have conceded Lady Dudley a triumph over Henriette. Arabella would have taken me to Paris; returning to Clochegourde now insulted Madame de Mortsauf—Arabella was sure of me. Would any loving woman forgive crimes against love? Unless she were an angel, a loving woman would rather see her lover die than know him happy with another. Thus my leaving Clochegourde for the Grenadière was fatal to my true love and advantageous to the transient affection that held me. Lady Dudley had calculated skillfully.When I met the countess that morning, she was pale and depressed, like one who had not slept. The nobility of generous hearts perceived the criminal nature of actions the world ignores. Like a child playing near a precipice, I realized she and I were separated by a universe. Henriette’s illusions were shattered; her heart had endured its passion. She refused to look at me, denying me the light that had shone on my life for six years. I felt the wrong of having brought into this roof, where pleasure was unknown, a face that had known joy. Had I let Lady Dudley depart alone and returned to Clochegourde, perhaps Henriette would not have resolved to treat me as a brother. But now she paid me ostentatious attentions that humiliated me, like caring for a sick man.“You were out walking early,” said the count. “I hope you have brought back a good appetite, you whose stomach is not yet destroyed.”That jest, which made Henriette smile, completed my sense of ridicule. Day and night I could not be at Clochegourde and Saint-Cyr. By day, becoming friends again with the woman I had long loved was difficult; the transition, though possible with time, felt like an illness. I was ashamed; I cursed the pleasure Lady Dudley gave me; I wished Henriette would demand my blood. I could not tear her rival apart before her, for she avoided speaking of Arabella. Had I mentioned her, Henriette would have despised me. After five years of delightful intercourse, we had nothing to say; our words had no connection with our thoughts; we hid intolerable pain from each other.Henriette feigned cheerfulness, but she was sad. She called herself my sister, yet we had no common ground and remained in constrained silence.
