She increased my misery by pretending to be the only victim.“I suffer more than you,” I said at one point.“How so?” she asked haughtily.“Because I am the one to blame.”Eventually her manner grew so cold that I resolved to leave Clochegourde. That evening on the terrace I bade farewell. As I took my horse, she said, “Let us walk down the avenue together, alone.” We walked slowly. At the grove marking the boundary, she stopped.“Farewell, my friend,” she said, resting her head on my chest and wrapping her arms around me. “Farewell, we shall never meet again. God has given me the sad power to see the future. Do you remember the terror when you first returned, so young, so handsome? I saw you turn your back on me as you do now. Once again, I have seen into the future. Friend, we speak for the last time. Death has seized something in me. You have taken the mother from her children; I ask you to take her place. You can; Jacques and Madeleine love you, as if you had always made them suffer.”“Die!” I cried, frightened at the fire in her eyes. “Henriette, I command you to live. Swear to me you will send for Origet and obey him in everything.”“Would you oppose God’s mercy?” she cried, misreading me.“You do not love me enough to obey blindly, as that miserable Lady Dudley does?”“Yes, yes, I will do all you ask,” she cried, goaded by jealousy.“Then I stay,” I said, kissing her eyelids.Frightened, she escaped my arms and walked home without looking back. I followed her; she was weeping and praying. We reached the lawn; I took and respectfully kissed her hand. This submission touched her.“I am yours—forever, and as you will,” I said; “for I love you as your aunt loved you.”She trembled and wrung my hand.“One look,” I begged, “one last of our old looks! The woman who gives herself wholly,” I cried, “gives less of life and soul than I have now received. Henriette, thou art my best-beloved—my only love.”“I shall live!” she said; “but cure yourself as well.”That look erased Arabella’s memory. Thus I was the plaything of two irreconcilable passions; I was influenced alternately. I loved an angel and a demon; two women equally beautiful—one adorned with virtues we decry through hatred of our imperfections, the other with vices we deify through selfishness. Returning down the avenue, seeing Madame de Mortsauf surrounded by her children waving handkerchiefs, I felt pride in being arbiter of two such destinies; proud of inspiring such great passions that death must come to whichever I abandoned. That passing conceit was doubly punished.I do not know what demon prompted me to remain with Arabella and await the moment the count’s death might give me Henriette forever; her harshness, tears, remorse, and Christian resignation testified to a feeling that could not be erased. Walking down that avenue, I was no longer twenty-five; I was fifty. A man passes quickly from youth to middle age. Perhaps these evil thoughts came from the Tuileries, from the king’s cabinet. Who could resist the polluting spirit of Louis XVIII?When I reached the end of the avenue, I rushed back—Henriette was still there, alone. I went to bid her a last farewell, bathed in repentant tears, given, though unconsciously, to noble loves forever lost, to virgin emotions that cannot bloom again. Later, a man gives nothing; he receives; he loves himself in his mistress; but in youth he loves his mistress in himself. Woe to the man who has not had his Henriette. Woe to him who has never known a Lady Dudley. Joy to him who can find the two women in one.After my return to Paris, Arabella and I grew intimate. We soon abandoned the conventional restraints I had tried to keep—the external comedies and concealments that renew desire and protect the heart. Young passions, like youth, raze their forests rather than cut timber. Arabella adopted none of these bourgeois strategies; she wished to exhibit me to all Paris as her “sposo.” She used her powers of seduction to keep me under her roof, not content with whispered scandal. Seeing her happy in an imprudence that frankly admitted her position, how could I not believe her love?But once plunged into the comforts of our illegal union, despair seized me; I saw my life bound to a course that contradicted Henriette’s ideas and counsel. From then on I lived in a rage like consumptives who, knowing death near, cannot bear scrutiny. There was no corner of my heart where I could escape suffering; an avenging spirit filled me with thoughts I dared not dwell on. My letters to Henriette depicted this moral malady and did her infinite harm. “At the cost of so many treasures lost, I wished you to be at least happy,” she wrote in the only answer I received. But I was not happy. Happiness is absolute; it admits no comparisons. Once first ardor fades, comparisons arise. All great passions first check character’s asperities; later, when lovers are accustomed, moral physiognomies reappear and natural antipathies surface. This period began with me. Less blinded, I undertook a critical examination of Lady Dudley that harmed her.First, she lacked the qualities of mind that distinguish Frenchwomen. When a Frenchwoman loves, she metamorphoses: coquetry adorns love; she abandons dangerous vanity and claims no merit but loving well. She espouses her lover’s interests and acquires experience in a day; heedless and prodigal by nature, she manages with grace. She becomes mother, adviser, doctor, giving transformations a grace that reveals infinite love. She combines other nations’ special qualities and unifies them with wit that enlivens and varies love, relieving monotony. The Frenchwoman loves always, in public or solitude; in public she speaks by silence or looks; if speech and look fail, she finds other means. The Englishwoman, by contrast, makes her love bend to the world. Educated to icy manners, she opens and shuts her heart with a British mechanism. Passionate when no eye sees her, she is cold before the world. A lover doubts his empire seeing her immobility, her aloof countenance, the calm voice with which she leaves her boudoir. Hypocrisy becomes indifference; she has forgotten all.A woman who can lay aside her love like a garment may be thought capable of changing it. What tempests in a man’s heart when he sees a woman take and drop her love like embroidery. These women, too completely mistress of themselves, never wholly belong to you; society’s influence prevents you reigning supreme. Where a Frenchwoman comforts with a look, an Englishwoman’s silence irritates and frets. They are so on their dignity that fashion often reigns over pleasures. An Englishwoman forces everything into form; Protestantism’s doubt and reasoning kill belief; it is the death of art and love. Passionate hearts flee worldliness.You can understand my shock when I found Lady Dudley could not live without the world, and the English system of two lives was natural to her. When she loved, she loved madly—no woman could compare; but when the curtain fell, she banished memory of it. In public she never answered a look or smile; she was ambassadress-like, folding her phrases and elbows; her composure outraged my heart. She degraded love to a need rather than raising it to an ideal. She expressed neither fear nor regret nor desire; then, at a given hour, tenderness reappeared like a sudden fire.Which of the two should I believe? By a thousand pin-pricks I felt the infinite differences between Henriette and Arabella. Madame de Mortsauf’s slightest departures reminded me of her: the sound of her gown, her lowered eyelids, her musical voice—a continual caress—her consistent thought. She did not halve her soul for two atmospheres. Arabella’s mind existed for the world, spent in sarcasm; she loved to rend and bite to satisfy craving. Madame de Mortsauf would have hidden her happiness; Lady Dudley chose to exhibit hers to all Paris yet kept an impenetrable English mask in public. This mixture of ostentation and dignity, love and coldness, wounded me. When I complained, she turned her tongue with triple sting, mingling boasts of love with cutting English sarcasms. In opposition she made amusement of hurting me, kneading me like dough. To any remark about keeping a medium, she caricatured my ideas. She asked if I wished her to kiss me at the opera before all Paris, seriously enough that I trembled she might. Despite passion, she was never meditative, self-contained, or reverent like Henriette; she was insatiable for excitement, noise, and celebrity; everything concerned herself, not me.Henriette’s letter you have read—proof of how this virtuous woman obeyed the genius of a Frenchwoman—shows how she had studied my prospects, political relations, moral conquests, and with what ardor she took hold of my life in permissible directions. Lady Dudley affected the reticence of an acquaintance. She never informed herself about my affairs or likings as a man. Prodigal for herself without being generous, she separated self-interest and love too decidedly. I knew, without proving it, that to save me a pang, Henriette would have sought for me what she would never seek for herself. In any great misfortune I would have gone to Henriette for counsel; I would have been dragged to prison sooner than say a word to Lady Dudley.The contrast extended to outward things. In France luxury expresses the man; it reproduces his ideas and gives attention value between lovers. English luxury, which at first allured by choiceness, proved mechanical. The thousand attentions at Clochegourde Arabella considered servants’ business; each had a duty as if horses. She never attached to servants; the death of the best would not affect her; money could replace him. Her selfishness was candid and created laughter. Crimson draperies covered an iron nature. The siren of amorous folly could make a young man forget the hard Englishwoman; only gradually I discovered the stony rock where my seeds were wasted. Madame de Mortsauf had seen this at a glance. She was right; Arabella’s love became intolerable to me. I have since remarked that many women who ride well have little tenderness. Like Amazons, they lack a breast; their hearts are hard in some direction.When weariness took possession of me, when I comprehended the sanctity true feeling imparts to love, when memories of Clochegourde brought the fragrance of roses, the warmth of the terrace, and the warble of nightingales, I received a blow whose echo still wakes in my heart.I was working in the king’s cabinet when the Duc de Lenoncourt entered. The king asked news of the countess. I raised my head hastily; the king, offended, gave me the look that precedes harsh words.“Sire, my poor daughter is dying,” replied the duke.“Will the king deign to grant me leave of absence?” I cried, tears in my eyes.“Go, my lord,” he answered, withholding reprimand in satire.More courtier than father, the duke asked no leave but got into the carriage with the king. I left without bidding Lady Dudley goodbye, leaving a note that I was sent on a mission by the king. At the Croix de Berny, I met his Majesty returning. He threw me an ironic look seeming to say: “If you mean to be anything in politics, come back; don’t parley with the dead.” The duke waved sadly. The pompous equipages rolled away to cries of “Vive le Roi!” It seemed the court had driven over the dead body of Madame de Mortsauf with utter insensibility. Though the duke was an excellent man, he would no doubt play whist with Monsieur after the king retired. The duchess had long ago given her daughter the first stab by writing of Lady Dudley.My hurried journey was like a ruined gambler’s dream; I despaired at receiving no news. Had the confessor excluded me? I accused everyone. Beyond Tours, on the poplar-bordered road to Poncher, I met Monsieur Origet. He guessed I was going to Clochegourde and told me: “I doubt if you find her living. She is dying a frightful death—of inanition. When she called me last June, no medical power could control the disease; she had the symptoms Monsieur de Mortsauf knows, for he thinks he has them. Madame la comtesse was not in a transient condition; her disease had reached a point where science is useless; it is the incurable result of grief. Her physical condition is produced by inertia of an organ as necessary as the heart. Grief has done the work of a dagger. Don’t deceive yourself; she is dying of hidden grief.”“Hidden!” I exclaimed.“No,” he said, looking at me, “and since she was so seriously attacked, Monsieur de Mortsauf ceased to torment her. I am no longer needed; Monsieur Deslandes is all-sufficient; nothing can be done; her sufferings are dreadful. Young, beautiful, and rich, to die emaciated—for she dies of hunger! During the last forty days the stomach has rejected all nourishment.”Origet pressed my hand with respect. “Courage, monsieur,” he said, lifting his eyes.These words were like poisoned arrows. I sprang into the carriage and promised a reward if I arrived in time.Despite impatience, the distance seemed short as bitter reflections crowded my soul. Dying of grief, yet her children were well? Then she died through me! My conscience arraigned me. What weakness in human justice, which avenges open deeds but forgives murderers of the soul! How many victims—Madame de Beauseant, the Duchesse de Langeais, Lady Brandon—our period teems with such events. Who does not remember the young woman who poisoned herself from jealousy? Society and science are accomplices in crimes without assizes. The world declares no one dies of grief; our scientific nomenclature provides labels that serve as passports to the coffin. Is there some law we do not know? Must the centenary pitilessly strew earth with corpses? My God! do I belong to the race of tigers?Remorse gripped me as I entered Clochegourde’s avenue on a damp October morning. Jacques, Madeleine, and the Abbé Dominis knelt at the foot of a wooden cross. I sprang from the carriage toward them, my heart aching. The old huntsman stood apart.I kissed Jacques and Madeleine, who gave a cold look. The abbé rose; I took him by the arm.“Is she still alive?” I implored. He bowed sadly and said it might be better to wait; she was greatly changed. “It is necessary to prepare her for this interview, or it might increase her suffering—death would be a blessed release.”He told me how for six months the countess had suffered more despite Origet’s care. The doctor had come every evening for two months. “To heal the body, the heart must first be healed,” the doctor had said. As the illness increased, the countess’s words grew bitter. She called on earth to keep her, then repented. Such alternations rend the heart. “She falls, but each time she stumbles she rises higher toward heaven,” he said.I cried, “Surely this pure lily will flower in heaven?”“You left her still a flower,” he answered. “You will find her consumed, purified, pure as a diamond buried in ashes.”The count, white-headed, hurried out, declaring, “She was right! He is here! ‘Felix, Felix, Felix has come!’ she kept crying. My dear friend, death is here. Why did it not take a poor madman like me?”I walked toward the house but the Abbé Birotteau stopped me. “Madame la comtesse begs you will not enter yet.” Inside, servants moved, all dumb with grief. Manette searched for Madeleine; we paced the lawn. I saw the dear “mignonne” gathering autumn flowers. Overcome, I sat, broken.“Poor Felix,” said the count, “she forbade me to write to you. She knew how much you loved her.”All was gloom. The servants wept; work was left undone; the place seemed uninhabited. The count, speaking in bursts, blamed and then wept for his wife, mixing despair with accusations. Madeleine told me her mother was ready; the Abbé Birotteau accompanied me. He warned, “I have done all to prevent this meeting. The salvation of this saint required it. I shall be present to protect you against yourself and perhaps against her. Respect her weakness. She is dying of hunger and thirst. Since morning she suffers feverish irritation. Pray God she die not in a moment of doubt.”We reached the chamber. Henriette sat in white, before the fireplace, flowers about her. The abbé’s amazement at the room’s restored tenderness showed she had prepared to receive me. Her shrunken face had the greenish pallor of a magnolia; hollow eyes shone; her forehead kept proportion; inward fires issued despite waxy features. The smile on her whitened lips resembled death’s grin. Her robe showed emaciation. She knew she was changed; thought filled her with bitterness. No longer the sublime Madame de Mortsauf, she was a nameless thing struggling against annihilation. I took her dry, burning hand to kiss. Her discolored lips tried a smile—the forced smile of one seeking to hide irony, expectation, or fury.“Ah, my poor Felix, this is death,” she said, “and you do not like death; odious death. This is the end of love; I knew it would be so. Lady Dudley will never see you thus surprised. Ah! why have I so longed for you, Felix? You have come at last, and I reward your devotion by the same horrible sight that made the Comte de Rance a Trappist. I, who hoped to remain beautiful and noble in your memory, destroy your illusions! True love cannot calculate. But stay; do not go, stay. Monsieur Origet said I was better this morning; I shall recover. Your looks will bring me back to life. When I regain strength and nourishment, I shall be beautiful again. I am scarcely thirty-five; there are many years of happiness before me—we will leave Clochegourde and go to Italy.”Tears filled my eyes; I turned to the window. She put her burning lips to my ear: “My anguish has been in not seeing thee! Did you not bid me live? I will live; I will ride on horseback; I will know life, Paris, fêtes, pleasures!”Those tones pictured a lifetime’s battles, the anguish of true love lost. The countess rose with the motion of a child. The confessor fell on his knees and prayed. She flung an arm around my neck, kissed me violently, and said, “You shall not escape me now!” She fainted; I laid her on the bed. “You carried me thus before,” she murmured.Deslandes entered, surprised at the decorations. “We must suffer much to die,” she said faintly. The doctor felt a pulse and murmured that it was the forty-second day since she had eaten or drunk. The Abbé Birotteau took me to the garden. “Let us leave her to the doctor,” he said; “they will wrap her in opium. Well, you have heard her—if indeed it is she.”“No,” I said, “it is not she.”Stupefied, I hid in a punt to be alone. I tried to detach myself from my life—torture like that which afflicts adulterers. My life was a failure. Despair suggested to die beside her or bury myself at Meilleraye among Trappists. I looked at the windows where Henriette was dying, fancying I saw the light burning the night I betrothed my soul to hers. Ought I not to have followed the simple life she created, keeping faithful while I worked in the world? Had she not bidden me become great to be saved from shameful passions? Chastity! Love as Arabella understood it disgusted me.Madeleine walked slowly and sat on a bench. When I approached, she rose and returned to the house, hating me; she fled from her mother’s murderer. At the portico Jacques sat, expressionless. Young persons carrying death are often unmoved at funerals. I longed to question Jacques.“You know, Jacques,” I said, “that in me you have a most devoted brother.”“Your friendship is useless; I shall follow my mother,” he replied with a sullen look.He showed me his handkerchief stained with blood.They each had a fatal secret. The brother and sister avoided each other. Henriette lay low; all was ruins at Clochegourde.“Madame is asleep,” Manette said, happy that the countess was out of pain. Small joys matter then. “Monsieur Deslandes ordered the flowers taken away; they excited Madame’s nerves,” Manette added. She thought the flowers caused delirium.I returned as the sunset gilded Azay’s roofs. All was calm and pure. A soft light lit the bed where my Henriette lay wrapped in opium. The body seemed annihilated; the soul reigned. Blanche and Henriette, two sublime faces of one woman, reappeared. The abbes sat at the bed. The count stood stupefied. I took a seat on the sofa. We exchanged looks of admiration mingled with tears. The Abbé Dominis and I communicated in signs: yes, angels were watching. Her face cleared; the green of suffering gave way to pure white pallor; she seemed to embrace the divine.Madeleine knelt, adoring, crying “My mother!” Jacques smiled; he would follow her. “She is entering the haven,” said the Abbé Birotteau. The Angelus rang; Nature’s poetry joined the sacred hour. We burst into tears. Even servants knelt and prayed. The doctor signaled that this sleep was the hour without pain; it was time for the last sacraments. At nine o’clock she awoke quietly with gentle eyes, and we beheld her in former beauty.“Mother! you are too beautiful to die—life and health are coming back to you!” cried Madeleine.“Dear daughter, I shall live—in thee,” she answered, smiling.They embraced. The count kissed his wife. She colored when she saw me.“Dear Felix,” she said, “this is, I think, the only grief I have ever caused you. Forget all I may have said—I, a poor creature beside myself.” She held out her hand; I took and kissed it. “As in the old days, Felix?”We left the room during the last confession. I approached Madeleine. In the presence of others she could not escape civility; but, like her mother, she looked at no one.“Dear Madeleine,” I said softly, “what have you against me? Why such coldness before death, which should reconcile us?”“I hear in my heart what my mother says,” she replied, with a look like Ingres’ “Mother of God.”“And you condemn me when your mother absolves me—if indeed I am guilty.”“You, you—always yourself!” Her voice revealed determined hatred, implacable as Corsican judgment.An hour passed in silence. The Abbé Birotteau returned after receiving the countess’s confession. We found her sitting up, dressed in the long white garment to be her shroud. She had had her letters burned—sacrifice she had delayed until this hour. She smiled with eyes moist and inward lucidity; she saw the promised land.“Dear Felix,” she said, pressing my hand, “stay with us. You must be present at my last scene; be not absent from the least painful among many things in which you are concerned.”She made a sign and the door closed. She knelt before the astonished count, then laid her head on his knees.“Though I have been a faithful wife to you,” she said faintly, “I have sometimes failed in my duty. I have given a friendship outside my family more affectionate care than to you. Perhaps I have irritated you by comparisons. I have had a deep friendship which no one—not even he who has been its object—has fully known. Though virtuous by human laws and an irreproachable wife, other thoughts, voluntary or involuntary, have crossed my mind, and in this hour I fear I welcomed them too warmly. But having loved you and continued submissive, I now pray for your blessing with a clean heart. I shall die without a bitter thought if I can hear a tender word for your Blanche, for the mother of your children—if you forgive her those things she did not forgive herself till reassured by the great tribunal which pardons all.”“Blanche, Blanche!” cried the broken man, shedding tears on her head. “Would you kill me?” He raised and kissed her solemnly. “Have I no forgiveness to ask? Have I never been harsh?”“Perhaps,” she said. “But dear friend, indulge a dying woman’s weakness; tranquillize me. Will you grant that I leave to our friend here that pledge of my affection?” She showed a letter on the mantel. “He is now my adopted son, and that is all. The heart makes bequests; my last wishes impose a duty on that dear Felix. If you do not wish to marry Madeleine, at least seek my soul’s repose by making Monsieur de Mortsauf as happy as he can be. Farewell, long farewell like that I gave last night to our valley, where I shall rest and where you will often—will you not?—return.Henriette.”I fell into abyssal reflections as the depths of her life were lighted by this expiring flame. My clouds of egotism rolled away. She had suffered as much as I—more, for she was dead. She believed others would be kind to her friend; she was blind to her daughter’s enmity. That last proof of tenderness pained me. Poor Henriette wished to give me Clochegourde and her daughter.From that dread day when I first followed my noble Henriette to the grave, the sun has been less warm, nights more gloomy, movement less agile, thought more dull. Some departed we bury in earth; others are buried within us, their memory mingling with our heartbeats; they are in us by a metempsychosis of love. A soul is within my soul. When some good thing is done by me, that soul acts and speaks. All that is good within me issues from that grave; sarcasm and bitterness are mine. Natalie, when next my eyes are darkened or my lips silent, do not ask, “Of what are you thinking?”I ceased writing for days; these memories were too bitter. I owe you an account of events that followed; they need few words. Henriette’s letter set a star of hope before me. To live at Clochegourde with Madeleine, consecrating my life to her, seemed a rescue. But it was necessary to know her true feelings. As I bade the count farewell, we walked the terrace. He spoke of his loss but soon of the future; he feared his daughter, who lacked her mother’s gentleness. Madeleine’s firm character alarmed him; he foresaw a struggle between father and daughter that would end his days in bitterness. His son was to leave; his daughter marry; what son-in-law might she have? Though talking of dying, he feared being alone without sympathy.Madeleine could see me from the terrace but would not come down. I seized a moment to beg the count to call her, saying I had a last wish of her mother to convey. The count fetched her and left us on the terrace alone.“Dear Madeleine,” I said, “if I speak to you, it should be here where your mother listened to me. I know your thoughts; but are you not condemning me without knowledge of facts? My life and happiness are bound up in this place; you know that, yet you seek to banish me by coldness instead of brotherly affection that death should have strengthened. If my mother’s name still has power, in her name I beg you never to return me to Clochegourde so long as you are in it. The sight of me causes a repugnance I cannot express, which I shall never overcome.”She stopped me.“Monsieur,” she said, voice trembling, “I know all your thoughts; but I shall not change my feelings. I would rather fling myself into the Indre than ally myself to you. If my mother’s name has power, in her name I beg you never to return while I am here. The sight of you causes repugnance I cannot overcome.”She bowed with dignity and returned to the house without looking back. The searching eye of that young girl had discovered her mother’s secrets; her hatred of the man she fancied fatal to her mother may have been increased by a sense of complicity.All before me was chaos. Madeleine hated me without considering cause or victim. The edifice of my happiness fell in ruins. I alone knew the life of that unknown woman; none had fathomed her heart. Strange truth: how many families have their Henriette! How many noble feelings leave earth with no historian to measure their depths! Mothers know their children little; so with husbands, lovers, brothers. When Madeleine disappeared, I left with a broken heart. Bidding farewell to my host at Sache, I started for Paris, following the right bank of the Indre.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############################################################################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At the story's outset, the Stanhope press and ink-distributing roller were not yet common in small provincial printing houses. In Angoulême, tied to Parisian typography by its paper mills, only wooden presses were used. “The press groans” was literal: leather ink-balls were still employed, and the pressman inked by hand. This old mechanism, cherished by Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, is central to the tale.Séchard, once a journeyman pressman, survived the Requisition of 1793 and became the sole worker in a printing house after the master’s death. Illiterate, he was granted a master printer’s license by a Representative of the People, allowing him to buy the business at a bargain. He partnered with a nobleman, M. le Comte de Maucombe, who helped print decrees while keeping a low profile from authorities.By 1795, after the Terror, Séchard needed a compositor and hired an Abbé who later became a Bishop. Despite his lack of education, Séchard learned to run the business, developing a shrewd grasp of costs and profits. His avarice grew as he saw the potential for wealth: he bought paper when prices fell and became his own landlord, acquiring the old printing house.Séchard had one son, David, whom he sent to grammar school hoping for a competent successor. David excelled academically, and Séchard—who scorned book learning—insisted he economize while studying in Paris. David completed his education without costing his father anything.The printing house held a monopoly on official printing in the region, but the Cointet Brothers sought a second printer’s license, threatening Séchard’s position. Confident in David’s abilities, Séchard looked forward to retirement, believing his son would meet the competition.Now in his seventies, Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard had developed a taste for drink, which his late wife had once kept in check. His appearance reflected the habit: a bloated face, cunning gaze, three-cornered hat, and old-fashioned clothing suited to his character.When David returned home, Séchard welcomed him with feigned warmth, eager to discuss business. Despite David’s plea to postpone serious matters, Séchard was intent on negotiating the sale of the business, viewing his son not only as offspring but as a potential rival in commercial dealings.The printing house, established since Louis XIV’s reign, was a large room with a glass door opening to the workshop. Customers often stumbled through the shop, distracted by the printing process. The premises included a yard with vines and a kitchen where inky runoff mixed with household waste, giving the place a notorious reputation.The living quarters above the office were simple and cluttered, reflecting Séchard’s greed. The dining room was sparsely furnished, and the bedroom contained old furniture unchanged since the predecessor’s time. The state of the house mirrored the old printer’s character and relentless pursuit of profit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The sitting-room had been partly modernized by the late Mme. Séchard, with powder-blue wainscoting and Oriental wallpaper, but the furniture remained sparse—lyre-backed chairs with blue leather cushions. The arched windows faced the Place du Mûrier and the room lacked a clock, candle sconces, or a mirror, for Mme. Séchard had died before finishing her plans. Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, practical to a fault, saw no value in improvements that didn’t yield money and left it as it was.Jérôme-Nicolas brought his son David into the room and pointed to a valuation of the printing plant on the table. David read the inventory aloud, noting the old presses and their valuation. Dismissing them as "only fit for firewood," he dropped the paper. Séchard, affronted, insisted they were time-tested and still valuable. He descended the stairs and, to David’s surprise, demonstrated the presses’ functionality with surprising agility. Proudly he asked, “Where’s the English press that can match this?” and argued the machines could bring a good income without a foreman. David, skeptical, thought the type’s price inflated.The inventory totaled thirty thousand francs, including license and goodwill, which David feared he could not afford. Séchard preferred a heated argument to silence and extolled the virtues of old tools, claiming they were worth more than new ones. He stressed the license’s value, citing income from advertisements and other work, and attached a partnership deed: Séchard would rent the premises to David for twelve hundred francs a year and keep a room for himself. Feeling cornered and aware that his father had once bought the business for ten thousand francs, David protested that the price was exorbitant—“Father, you’re cutting my throat!”—but, recalculating the license and goodwill, decided he might manage the debt and accepted the terms, surprising his father.The next day the apprentice moved David’s belongings to the attic, leaving him with three empty rooms and no funds to pay wages. When David asked for financial help, Séchard refused, saying he had already paid his share. Humiliated, David realized he was alone and that his father intended to profit from him rather than support him. He mentioned his mother’s fortune, but Séchard dismissed it as only her beauty and intelligence; any legal claim would demand a costly, prolonged suit. Resigned, David resolved to work hard and build his own future.Séchard mentioned a “treasure” he was leaving David—Marion, a capable country girl who managed many tasks in the printing house—and then retired to a vineyard he had bought in Marsac. Uneasy about the thirty thousand francs and suspicious of David’s management, he frequently visited the workshop, fearing his son was failing, especially as the Cointet Brothers gained ground in the trade.David, indifferent to politics, struggled to secure work. The Cointets, aligning themselves with the local clergy, began monopolizing the market and slandering him as a Liberal and untrustworthy because of his father’s reputation. Their advantageous connections and more aggressive business tactics undermined David’s clientele and opportunities.As David’s business faltered, the Cointet Brothers offered to buy the printing house. Séchard intervened, insisting David keep the paper as essential for holding their Liberal readership, and negotiated a price of sixty thousand francs for the business—ostensibly to protect David’s interests. Despite these efforts, David was ultimately forced to sell the paper to the Cointets, marking the decline of the Séchard establishment. Séchard, increasingly detached, visited less often, though he still felt a residual connection to the old printing house.David’s troubles deepened when Lucien Chardon, an old school friend reduced to poverty after his father’s death, sought his help. Lucien, struggling to find his place, accepted a position as a printer’s reader that David offered out of compassion. The two men’s friendship deepened as they exchanged dreams: Lucien aspired to be a poet, David to pursue natural science. Lucien mentioned his father’s ideas for refining sugar and making paper—ventures that intrigued David and offered a glimpse of alternative prospects.These ambitions, however sincere, distracted them from the printing house’s urgent needs. While the Cointet Brothers thrived through clerical alliances and business savvy, Séchard & Son scraped by, earning only a fraction of what was necessary. The Cointets, recognizing David’s lack of business acumen, allowed his establishment to continue operating at a loss, ensuring it would not seriously compete. Meanwhile, the physical condition of the printing house reflected years of neglect born of Séchard’s avarice: repairs were postponed, maintenance ignored, and the building grew dilapidated. The workshop itself was cluttered and grimy, a visible testament to the persevering struggle of its occupants and the decline of their enterprise.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One day in May 1821, David and Lucien stood by the window watching the yard until the men left for dinner. Uncomfortable in the office’s atmosphere, David led Lucien outside to sit under the vines, where the sunlight revealed their contrasting looks and characters.David had a robust, fighter’s build: broad shoulders, a swarthy face marked by struggle, thick black hair and deep-set eyes that suggested a wisdom mixed with melancholy and an awareness of social limits born of humble origins. Beside him, Lucien was graceful and beautiful, with a refined profile and striking blue eyes that held youthful innocence. His delicate features and slender frame gave him an almost feminine look, yet his ambition and sharp intellect were evident; he viewed society pragmatically and could contemplate morally dubious means to succeed.Though both were low in social standing, they aspired to higher ideals, growing bitter when reality fell short. Lucien’s Gascon temperament made him bold and adventurous; David, stronger physically, was more introspective and cautious. Their friendship combined admiration and imbalance: David often yielded to Lucien’s charm, seeing himself as the more ordinary man.For three years they shared a bond nourished by literature and art, immersing themselves in great writers and thinkers and dreaming of fame as an escape from harsh circumstances. One day David brought a new volume of André de Chénier’s poetry that moved them both. The verses offered solace and kinship with the poet’s struggles; as they read, the yard seemed to fill with the promise of their aspirations and the possibility of transcending their situation.Their reverie was broken by Cérizet, an apprentice who brought a stranger seeking to print a monograph. Lucien, eager to impress, engaged the author, who mentioned Mme. de Bargeton, a woman Lucien admired. He blushed and thanked the visitor for her interest, betraying his admiration.After the author left, David questioned Lucien about Mme. de Bargeton; Lucien confessed his passionate love. David warned him of social barriers, but Lucien hoped love could surmount them. He declared he would not visit Mme. de Bargeton again unless David was welcomed too, showing loyalty.As Lucien walked through Angoulême, he reflected on the distance between his class and Mme. de Bargeton’s, feeling both hope and anxiety. The city, perched on a crag, was divided between the upper town of nobility and the lower town of trade. That split bred tension: the upper class looked down on merchants and tradespeople, resisting change and viewing outsiders with suspicion, creating deep animosity between classes.Despite government efforts to modernize Angoulême, the upper town remained stagnant and attached to old values. The social hierarchy was rigid: the nobility kept their exclusivity, and the bourgeoisie resented their disdain. This animosity would later fuel social upheaval as the rising middle class sought recognition and respect.In this climate, Lucien’s desire to enter Mme. de Bargeton’s world was a major challenge. His origins as a druggist’s son made him an outsider in her circle, yet his literary ambitions and charm set him apart. Mme. de Bargeton, a woman of artistic inclination, had a past that shaped her character and influenced Lucien’s path.As Lucien navigated love and ambition, societal barriers loomed, but his determination to bridge the gap drove him. David’s mix of admiration and concern reflected the complexity of their friendship: he understood Lucien’s depth of feeling but saw the obstacles clearly. Lucien’s pledge to include David in any future visits underscored both loyalty and hope that friendship might soften social distance.Their story would unfold against an Angoulême where tradition and modernity, nobility and bourgeoisie, ambition and constraint met, shaping both their fates.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Bargeton was the great-grandson of a Bordeaux alderman ennobled under Louis XIII, but the family declined: his grandfather, “the Waster,” squandered the fortune. By 1789 the present Bargeton had only entailed lands and a house in Angoulême, most seignorial rights gone and income reduced to modest rents. In 1805 he married Mlle. Marie-Louise-Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, a noblewoman from a once-prominent family; her father lived modestly on his wife's estate, farming and distilling brandy.Mme. de Bargeton (Naïs) developed a love of music and literature under Abbé Niollant during the Revolution; he taught her languages and music, fostering independence and a certain haughty superiority. Her father’s thrift and provincial isolation reinforced this stance; she resisted marriage, valuing autonomy over submission.Seeking a match who would not challenge her intellect, her father chose M. de Bargeton: a man of breeding but limited mind. Naïs accepted, intending to pursue social ambitions within that match. She was thirty-six to his fifty-eight; despite the age gap the marriage seemed mutually advantageous.Settled in Angoulême, they awaited Naïs’s inheritance to move to Paris, but small income and provincial life stifled her intellectual yearnings and produced absurdities in her character. She became theatrical, expressing emotions with grand gestures; passions that might once have been elevated were trivialized by small-town existence.Her life turned into a cycle of unfulfilled hopes and isolation, which intensified her longing for love. The rise of the Empire and military fame rekindled romantic dreams: she fell for a young officer whose death in battle left her heartbroken, amplifying regret.For years she lived in solitude, her dignity hardening into affectation. Then M. du Châtelet arrived—charming, ambitious, and disruptive. He ingratiated himself into her circle, using music to win her favor and stirring a renewed sense of youth. Seeing her as a potential wealthy widow, he hoped to advance his aims through the connection.Du Châtelet’s presence shocked the local elite, challenging the salon’s pretensions. Despite scandal, Mme. de Bargeton welcomed him; his entrance altered her social world and exposed tensions of ambition, love, and provincial expectation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Political opinion was verbose and exaggerated; the Quotidienne showed only lukewarm loyalty, while Louis XVIII was seen as a Jacobin. Society’s women were mostly awkward and poorly dressed, lacking completeness in appearance and conversation. Châtelet found solace in company chiefly because of his interest in Mme. de Bargeton. In the countryside, a noble spirit and traditions of good breeding masked many deficiencies; their loyalty to the House of Bourbon was a mark of honor and gave them a sense of elevation, almost like the value Bambara Negroes placed on cowrie shells.Some women, flattered by Châtelet, saw in him qualities absent in their own men, soothing their vanity. They all aspired to be near the Imperial Highness. Châtelet endured insolence but kept his position by engaging the clergy and humoring the queen of Angoulême in her local foibles, sharing new literature and poetry with her. Mme. de Bargeton, enamored with the Bourbon revival, longed for Parisian genius, so Châtelet told her of a promising young poet in Angoulême.Lucien, the poet, did not expect the attention. His headmaster had praised his work, and he thrilled at the prospect of being welcomed at the Hôtel de Bargeton. His sister Eve secretly supported him, saving money for new shoes and clothes and coaching him on aristocratic behavior. As he left, she felt a mix of pride and anxiety about his future.At the Hôtel de Bargeton, Lucien found the house less grand than imagined but was enthralled by Mme. de Bargeton, who embodied the poetry he admired. She was struck by his beauty and diffidence; their meeting was full of mutual admiration. Lucien, enchanted by her, overlooked signs of faded grandeur and felt a promising connection.Sensing a rival, Châtelet tried to assert influence over the young poet, offering unsolicited advice about Angoulême’s social dynamics and declaring his feelings for Mme. de Bargeton, claiming she would soon be his. Despite Châtelet's attempts to intimidate him, Lucien continued to visit Mme. de Bargeton, initially flattered by the nobility’s condescension but later resentful of it.Lucien’s infatuation with Naïs (Mme. de Bargeton) deepened as she flattered and treated him as a project to be nurtured. Their relationship mixed patronage and tenderness: she sometimes acted as mentor and at other times showed genuine affection. Lucien experienced the tumult of first love; as their bond strengthened, Naïs addressed him more intimately, further binding them.One evening Lucien found Naïs gazing at a portrait of a deceased lover, which sparked jealousy in him. Nevertheless, he boldly declared his feelings, and their relationship progressed. Naïs encouraged his literary ambitions, introducing him to music and literature while revealing her own vulnerabilities.When their liaison became public, town gossip caused a social uproar. Châtelet, feeling threatened, tried to undermine Lucien. Mme. de Bargeton defended her poet, arguing with wit that genius could spring from humble origins and countering the nobles’ disdain.Lucien’s social presence grew as Mme. de Bargeton hosted gatherings to showcase his talent. She urged him to adopt the noble name Rubempré, promising to help him gain favor at court. The idea excited Lucien, who began to abandon earlier ideals of equality in favor of social ambition.Under Naïs's influence, Lucien’s aspirations expanded: he resolved to achieve fame and success even at the cost of some principles. He wrote a heartfelt letter to Naïs, asking her to help his friend David, which revealed his deep emotional struggles and ambitions. Torn between love and recognition, Lucien navigated complex relationships while seeking to balance artistic aims with social advancement.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Lucien descended from Angoulême he worried whether the great lady was angry and if she would receive David. He perceived the vast gulf between a queen and her favorite, aware that David could not easily traverse the same social terrain. He did not know that receiving David could ruin Mme. de Bargeton: mingling with the lower classes would bring her ostracism. Many sins are pardoned for the powerful, but breaching social law by associating with commoners was unforgivable.Lucien’s aristocratic instinct warned him of trouble. He understood that noble bearing does not always accompany noble feeling; many notable men did not fit the aristocratic mold. David lacked the social graces Lucien possessed, and Lucien feared the ridicule his friend would face in polished circles. After a poetic reverie he turned to worldly wisdom, regretting his impulsive letter to Mme. de Bargeton and foreseeing the harsh realities of success and the sacrifices required. He longed for the simpler pleasures of his past and felt it nobler to win by his own efforts than through a woman’s favor. Inspired by Napoleon’s rise from humble origins, Lucien resolved to pursue ambitions without patronage, though he felt ashamed of his father’s shop as an obstacle to his social hopes.Back home, Lucien was greeted by his father, M. Postel, who fondly recalled Lucien’s father’s past achievements; the mention stung. Lucien eagerly searched the shop for Mme. de Bargeton’s reply and found a letter expressing willingness to help David while also revealing prejudice against lower social standing. Lucien read this as a victory, convinced David would shine in her company despite the caveats.Eve, Lucien’s sister, noticed his elation and voiced hopes for his happiness. Lucien, absorbed in ambition, reassured her they would all be happy together, though Eve perceived his priorities shifting. David, however, felt unworthy of the social elevation Lucien sought and decided to decline Mme. de Bargeton’s invitation. He affirmed his commitment to his own path as a printer and emphasized the social differences between them.David’s humility and loyalty moved Lucien, who felt a deep bond with his friend and reflected on the sacrifices of those who supported him. Yet ambition began to eclipse gratitude, and he became increasingly focused on his own advancement.As Lucien prepared for his first public reading, David suggested choosing a different poem, fearing Lucien’s biblical piece might not resonate with the audience; eager to impress, Lucien agreed. Meanwhile, David and Eve shared a quiet moment that hinted at mutual affection.At the reading Lucien arrived early and encountered M. de Bargeton, socially awkward and ineffectual in conversation. His limited intellect and reliance on superficial pleasantries underscored the social dynamics at play and Lucien’s challenge in navigating high society. The evening, marked by Lucien’s ambition and the complex relationships around him, set the stage for the conflicts and aspirations that would shape his future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################There was talk of nominating the mute gentleman for a deputy, but Lucien had only briefly visited the house and had not yet fathomed this enigmatic M. de Bargeton. Reclining, he observed everything with a calm silence that awed Lucien. Imaginative, Lucien treated him with deference, half expecting a sphinx.“I am the first comer,” Lucien said, bowing more than usual.“That is natural enough,” replied M. de Bargeton.Lucien took the remark as a sly jab, pictured a jealous husband, and became self-conscious about his appearance. M. de Bargeton remarked that people who lived far away always came earlier; when Lucien asked why, he received only a brief “I don’t know,” and silence returned.Lucien tried to restart the conversation, noting Mme. de Bargeton was likely dressing. M. de Bargeton confirmed this; Lucien, awkward, fretted about the room’s preparations and his attire, then turned to a Japanese jar for something to say.“You seldom leave the city, monsieur?” he ventured.“Very seldom,” came the curt reply.M. de Bargeton watched Lucien closely, making him uneasy. Then the old servant announced “M. du Châtelet.” The Baron entered confidently, greeting M. de Bargeton and nodding at Lucien, who felt overshadowed by the Baron’s ease. Sixte du Châtelet, still bearing the faded charm of a once-stylish beau in white trousers and a black coat, inspected Lucien’s dress and deepened his discomfort.Sensing tension, M. de Bargeton asked Châtelet if there was any news.“The latest is M. Chardon,” Châtelet said with malice. “Have you brought a charming poet for us?” he added, arranging his hair.“I should have asked you whether I succeeded,” Lucien replied, feeling outclassed.“Pshaw! Just a few vaudevilles and songs,” Châtelet dismissed.Mme. de Bargeton then appeared, elaborately dressed and theatrically captivating Lucien. Châtelet showered her with compliments; she barely acknowledged Lucien, favoring Châtelet instead.Guests arrived: the Bishop and his Vicar-General, both dignified yet contrasting; Mme. de Chandour and her husband, almost fictional in their eccentricity—Amélie posing like a rival queen while her husband, M. de Chandour, drew attention with exaggerated dress. M. de Saintot, called Astolphe, came with his solemn wife Élisa; he pretended to scientific gravity that was superficial. M. de Bartas presented himself as a musical expert, hoping for praise for his singing.M. Alexandre de Brébian, an artist, and his wife came with M. de Bartas, each showing quirks. The women, Lolotte and Fifine, obsessed over fashion; their husbands appeared dowdy. Also present were M. le Comte de Senonches and his tall, striking but ill Zéphirine; her devoted family friend Francis managed their household and prompted speculation about their relationship.The wealthiest pair, the Marquis de Pimentel and his wife, arrived with the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac, provoking jealousy especially when Mme. de Bargeton paid them special attention, revealing social dynamics. The Prefect and the General arrived last, along with a nervous country gentleman who brought a treatise on silkworms and struggled to fit in, eager to discuss his work but met with indifference.Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard, noble but impoverished, were present, their attempts to make connections marked by desperation. M. de Séverac, a childless widower, engaged them about his silkworm nurseries, while Mme. du Brossard promoted her daughter’s talents.As the evening progressed, Lucien felt increasingly scrutinized. Most called him M. Chardon, not M. de Rubempré, which stung his pride. The audience seemed more absorbed in its own chatter than in his poetry, leaving him isolated.When it came time to read, Lucien’s excitement mixed with anxiety. He announced he would read works by the recently discovered André de Chénier, a choice some misinterpreted as an excuse to spare his own feelings. He began with Le Malade, which drew mild applause; his reading of L’Aveugle, however, failed to engage the room. Their lack of response made the experience painful; he sensed disinterest and impatience.Sweating from the pressure, Lucien found courage in a supportive glance from Louise and continued with Chénier’s Iambes, which finally captured some attention though many still missed the meaning. During a break, Zéphirine sent Francis to investigate the poetry, prompting the women to speculate about Lucien’s background. Amélie’s remarks about his noble name and printing connections sparked gossip and complicated perceptions of his status.Through the evening Lucien remained largely oblivious to the mounting tensions and misunderstandings surrounding his reading, absorbed in the beauty of his art while most of the audience stayed indifferent.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien tried to immortalize his love for Mme. de Bargeton in an ode he fancied rivaled Chénier. Proud under her protective gaze, he nonetheless found the audience cold: the Baron dismissed the poem as juvenile grandiloquence, the women pretended admiration while trading sarcastic remarks, and Francis, ever crafty, circulated insinuations that Lucien’s verses owed more to his mother’s influence than to genius. Even the Bishop’s well-meant praise only intensified the aristocrats’ scorn. Overwhelmed and out of place, Lucien fumbled social cues, misidentified people, and felt the weight of their disdain.As cards and music drew the company away, Mme. de Bargeton, stung by the contempt directed at Lucien, withdrew with him to her boudoir. She offered consolation and counsel: endure for the sake of future greatness, she urged, and transmute suffering into work that will secure artistic immortality. She romanticized his humiliation as a necessary precursor to lasting fame and encouraged him to bear it with hope.Elsewhere, Lucien’s sister Eve and her lover David debated his ambitions and the risks of his intimacy with Mme. de Bargeton. David feared Lucien’s soaring hopes would lead to ruin—he lacked the temperament and means for high society. Eve, more hopeful, believed they could sustain him until he succeeded. Their talk turned to themselves when David proposed. Eve hesitated over class differences but, moved by love and a wish to help Lucien, accepted. David vowed to provide for Lucien so he would not suffer alone.On the way home Lucien voiced frustration, resolved to win Mme. de Bargeton’s love and rise above the jeers; news of Eve’s engagement felt like a complication rather than comfort. David then sought his father’s blessing and met skepticism: his father, pragmatic about finances, disparaged Eve and urged a more advantageous match. David refused to relent, asserting his commitment to Eve and their plans. He left his father’s house disheartened but determined to build a life with her, even facing hardship alone. Lucien remained torn between lofty aspiration and the harsh realities of his social standing, caught between dreams and the contempt around him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Angoulême, the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s witty reply made Lucien a local celebrity. Walking through Beaulieu he drew envy and flattering remarks that fed his pride. Seeking a private moment with Louise, he told her of his sister Eve’s upcoming marriage, hoping Louise’s kindness would bring him happiness. She greeted him with passionate affection, praised his reading, and promised to create a poetic refuge for him despite the surrounding envy.Buoyed by family and friends, Lucien’s self-regard expanded. He teasingly asked Louise if she would be his Beatrice; she playfully promised future love and urged him toward greatness. Lucien confided in her about David and Eve’s attachment; Louise reassured him their bond rose above mere family ties. When M. de Bargeton arrived, Lucien hesitated, but Louise invited him to dinner and a reading, strengthening his sense of belonging.As he assumed the name M. de Rubempré, Lucien enjoyed the comforts of his relationship with Louise and the patronage of M. de Bargeton. His confidence grew and he believed his literary ambitions were near fulfillment. Eve and David postponed their wedding to support him, enchanted by his charm, though David worried Lucien might change with his new status.Their intimacy drew Châtelet’s attention; he aimed to expose them and watched for a compromising moment. Despite largely innocent interactions, provincial gossip and speculation produced misunderstandings. The constraints of small-town life made open declarations of love dangerous, and Louise felt the pressure of social expectation.Determined to declare his feelings, Lucien ran up against the barrier of their environment; Louise, pressed by circumstance, considered fleeing provincial restraints. Châtelet continued to manipulate perceptions and sow doubt about their innocence.One day Lucien’s emotional outburst created a vulnerable scene witnessed by Stanislas, who misread it. Gossip swelled and exaggerated tales circulated through Angoulême. Châtelet seized the opportunity to advance his agenda, while Stanislas reveled in the drama.Alarmed, Mme. de Bargeton felt compelled to defend her honor and confided in her husband, urging him to confront Stanislas for slander. M. de Bargeton, spurred by his wife’s distress, resolved to challenge Stanislas despite his awkwardness. Approaching Stanislas’s house, he struggled to assert himself without looking ridiculous; his quiet resolve eventually won respect. The scene was set for a confrontation that would further entangle their lives, revealing the complexities of love, honor, and social expectation in the town.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Châtelet recognized the consequences of a late-night summons, especially with M. de Bargeton already in bed, and suspected Naïs had instigated it. Using his authority in the household, he took M. de Bargeton to speak with Stanislas. The old gentleman, relieved to have a mediator, went to Amélie’s room, expecting a confrontation that could leave Mme. de Bargeton a widow and widen the rift between her and Lucien, the source of the trouble.Châtelet warned Stanislas that Bargeton would confront him about remarks concerning Naïs and advised him to remain decorous. When they returned, Bargeton demanded retraction of Stanislas’s accusations about Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré. Feeling trapped, Stanislas accepted a duel and chose pistols.The announcement of a duel altered the evening; guests split over who was right. Châtelet remained composed while Stanislas was shaken. Amélie, sensing the tension, fell ill and was taken to her room. The men debated the duel’s implications, some noting Bargeton’s past as an expert marksman.The next morning, Lucien ate with David when Mme. Chardon arrived, alarmed by news of the duel. She reported that Bargeton had nearly killed Stanislas, who accused him of compromising conduct with Mme. de Bargeton. Lucien vehemently defended her innocence. The duel’s aftermath was grim: Bargeton had shot Stanislas in the neck, leaving him wounded.While Lucien absorbed this, he received a note from Louise warning him to avoid her house because of the duel’s fallout and suggesting he spend the day with her. David, preparing for his marriage, observed that Lucien’s frequent visits to Mme. de Bargeton might need curtailing.Lucien visited Mme. de Bargeton at breakfast, where her husband and M. de Nègrepelisse scrutinized him, trying to gauge his relationship with his daughter. Afterwards Louise confided she intended to go to Paris to secure a political position for Bargeton; their leaving Angoulême was meant to blunt the duel’s consequences.Louise urged Lucien to join her in Paris, extolling the capital’s opportunities and intellectual life compared with provincial stagnation. Lucien felt drawn to that life and to Louise, but his sister Eve’s imminent wedding weighed on him. Louise dismissed his hesitation, questioning why he’d prioritize a family event over their future. Torn between family duty and prospects with Louise, Lucien felt overwhelmed.Their argument ended with Louise fainting; Lucien apologized and confessed desperation. She insisted he be discreet and be ready to leave with her the next night. He returned to David’s house troubled by the burden his departure would place on his family.Talking with Eve and their mother, he found sorrow at his planned departure, especially so close to Eve’s wedding. Lucien considered borrowing money to support himself in Paris, but Eve and their mother revealed how wedding costs had strained their finances.Overhearing, David offered to help despite his own constraints. He agreed to endorse a loan for Lucien, showing loyalty and friendship. Lucien felt guilty but determined to pursue his aspirations.As he prepared to leave, the emotional cost of severing family ties weighed on him. David and he arranged to send Lucien’s belongings to Paris discreetly, while Châtelet, suspicious of Mme. de Bargeton’s planned departure, sent someone to follow her.Lucien set out early the next morning with David, who pretended business as cover. Waiting for Mme. de Bargeton’s carriage, Lucien felt a mix of excitement and dread about Paris; David, sensing the gravity, wished him well, fearing for his future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY: the narrative's central, pivotal section in the story's progression.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################
</story>
